passage_text_markdown:
: Read the following passage and answer the four questions that follow.

The standard story of how scientific revolutions occur tells us that an established paradigm collapses
under the weight of accumulated anomalies, and a new framework, more accurate and more elegant,
replaces it. This story is reassuring because it makes the transition look rational; the old picture failed
empirically, and the community of scientists, being empirical, switched. Yet historians of science have
for some time pointed out a curious feature of major theoretical shifts. The crucial anomalies are
very often known long before the paradigm is overthrown, and the eventual replacement is frequently
no better at handling them, at least at first, than the theory it displaces. Galileo’s heliocentrism
initially required ad hoc patches to explain motions that geocentric astronomy already explained
reasonably well, and the early atomic theory could not predict valences with the precision of the
contemporary chemistry of equivalents. If anomalies alone do not topple paradigms, what does?

One tempting answer is that paradigms fall when a more elegant framework arrives. Elegance,
however, is not a property paradigms simply have or lack; it is something they acquire through the
practice of those who use them. Newtonian mechanics was thought clumsy until the calculus of
variations made it lean. Elegance, then, is partly a record of accumulated mathematical labour, and
so cannot, without circularity, be the reason a new paradigm is preferred.

A more persuasive account locates the shift in the changing distribution of authority within scientific
communities. Young researchers, looking for problems on which to make a reputation, drift toward
the new framework because it offers more open questions and fewer entrenched experts. As they

secure positions and prizes, the institutional centre of gravity moves. The transition therefore looks
empirical in retrospect, but is sustained at the time by something closer to a generational migration.
This is not to say that evidence is irrelevant. It is to say that the community will tolerate evidence
weighing against a young theory and refuse the same indulgence to an old one.

Such an account is uncomfortable, because it suggests that what is called scientific consensus is
partly an artefact of professional life. Yet to call this a critique of science would be to misread it.
The migration of attention to richer research problems is precisely what makes science productive. If
young scientists clung to a paradigm only because it was well-confirmed, no new framework, however
promising, could attract the labour needed to develop it. The scientific community’s apparent
injustice toward incumbent theories is the price it pays for being able to move at all. A theory is
overthrown not when it has been refuted but when it has stopped being interesting to those who still
have careers to build.
question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the author’s central claim?
option_a_markdown:
Scientific revolutions are driven less by the accumulation of empirical anomalies than by
shifts in where ambitious researchers choose to invest their labour.
option_b_markdown:
Scientific consensus is an artefact of professional incentives rather than a reflection of
empirical adequacy, which makes the standard story of paradigm change misleading.
option_c_markdown:
Elegance is not an intrinsic property of theories but a product of cumulative mathematical
work, which is why elegance cannot explain paradigm shifts.
option_d_markdown:
Younger scientists are biased against established paradigms because such paradigms offer
fewer open problems on which a reputation can be built.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Answer: A. The passage opens by undermining the standard “anomalies cause paradigm
change” story, dismisses elegance as a circular explanation, and then settles on the migration of
younger researchers to new frameworks as the mechanism that actually drives shifts. Option A
captures this final positive claim.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the author does say consensus is partly an artefact of
professional life. But B treats this remark as the central claim and characterises the standard
story as “misleading” in a flat sense. The author explicitly says this is not a critique of science
and that the migration is what makes science productive. B confuses concession with conclusion
and exaggerates tone.

11


CAT-plus VARC Simulation

24 Questions | 40 Minutes

• C. True but partial: the elegance argument is a sub-claim used to clear the field for the central

thesis, not the thesis itself.

• D. Distorted emphasis and tone: the author does not say younger scientists are “biased” but

that they rationally drift to richer problem spaces.
source_raw_block:
Q1. Which of the following best captures the author’s central claim?

(A) Scientific revolutions are driven less by the accumulation of empirical anomalies than by
shifts in where ambitious researchers choose to invest their labour.

(B) Scientific consensus is an artefact of professional incentives rather than a reflection of
empirical adequacy, which makes the standard story of paradigm change misleading.

(C) Elegance is not an intrinsic property of theories but a product of cumulative mathematical
work, which is why elegance cannot explain paradigm shifts.

(D) Younger scientists are biased against established paradigms because such paradigms offer
fewer open problems on which a reputation can be built.

question_text_markdown: The author refers to Newtonian mechanics and the calculus of variations primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown:
illustrate that paradigms gain explanatory power only after sustained mathematical invest-
ment.
option_b_markdown:
show that elegance, being acquired rather than intrinsic, cannot serve as an independent
reason for paradigm preference.
option_c_markdown:
support the view that Newtonian mechanics was eventually displaced because it ceased to
be a productive site of research.
option_d_markdown:
argue that the apparent clumsiness of a theory is a poor guide to its long-term empirical
promise.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Answer: B. The Newton/calculus passage appears in the paragraph that rejects elegance
as a free-standing reason for paradigm preference. The point is that elegance is acquired through
mathematical labour, so citing it to explain why a paradigm wins is circular.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because the passage does say mathematical labour gives a paradigm
its lean form. But A reads the example as making a general claim about how paradigms gain
explanatory power, whereas the example is doing the narrower job of disabling an alternative
theory of paradigm change. Confuses local function with global claim.

• C. Unsupported: the passage does not say Newtonian mechanics was displaced because it ceased

to be productive.

• D. True but irrelevant to the function of the example in context.
source_raw_block:
Q2. The author refers to Newtonian mechanics and the calculus of variations primarily in order to:

(A) illustrate that paradigms gain explanatory power only after sustained mathematical invest-
ment.

(B) show that elegance, being acquired rather than intrinsic, cannot serve as an independent
reason for paradigm preference.

(C) support the view that Newtonian mechanics was eventually displaced because it ceased to
be a productive site of research.

(D) argue that the apparent clumsiness of a theory is a poor guide to its long-term empirical
promise.

overthrown not when it has been refuted but when it has stopped being interesting to those who still

question_text_markdown: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument?
option_a_markdown:
Across several historical transitions, the new framework was adopted only after it generated
predictions the old framework could not.
option_b_markdown:
Senior scientists, rather than younger ones, have repeatedly led the adoption of new
paradigms once early evidence accumulates.
option_c_markdown:
The number of unsolved problems within an established paradigm tends to grow rather
than shrink in its final decades.
option_d_markdown:
Prizes and faculty positions are awarded to researchers working within new paradigms at
roughly the same rate as to those defending older ones.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3. Answer: A. The author’s argument rests on the claim that anomalies and predictive superiority
do not by themselves explain paradigm change. A direct counter-finding showing that new paradigms
have been adopted only after they outperformed the old in prediction would undercut the very
mechanism the passage is built on.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the author identifies young researchers as the engine of
migration; if seniors led adoption, the migration story would weaken. But the author’s claim is
about where research labour and attention move, not strictly who signs the adoption documents.
Senior leadership is compatible with the broader migration of problem-attention. A more directly
cuts the central mechanism.

• C. Compatible with the passage; growth of unsolved problems within a paradigm can be precisely

what attracts younger workers elsewhere.

• D. Would weaken only mildly, since the author’s claim concerns shifts in attention and problem

space, not crude prize counts.

(B) Senior scientists, rather than younger ones, have repeatedly led the adoption of new
paradigms once early evidence accumulates.

(C) The number of unsolved problems within an established paradigm tends to grow rather
than shrink in its final decades.

(D) Prizes and faculty positions are awarded to researchers working within new paradigms at
roughly the same rate as to those defending older ones.

question_text_markdown: The author would most likely agree with which of the following?
option_a_markdown:
A paradigm’s longevity is best measured by the volume of new research problems it continues
to generate, not by the volume of evidence supporting it.
option_b_markdown:
The history of science shows that empirical refutation, properly understood, remains the
central engine of theoretical change.
option_c_markdown:
Scientific revolutions reveal a deep irrationality at the heart of the scientific enterprise,
since they are driven by social rather than evidential factors.
option_d_markdown:
The migration of younger scientists to new frameworks is unjust to established theories,
even if it is necessary for scientific progress.

Passage 2: Read the following passage and answer the four questions that follow.

When economists describe a city as “efficient,” they usually mean that its labour and housing markets
clear quickly: workers find jobs near where they live, firms find workers near where they invest, and
rents settle at levels that reflect underlying productivity. By this measure, large Indian metropolises
perform poorly. Commute times are long, formal housing is scarce, and a substantial share of workers
live in settlements that planners refuse to recognise. The natural conclusion is that these cities are
dysfunctional, and that a programme of clearance, formalisation, and improved transport will restore
them to economic health.

This conclusion, however attractive to administrators, misreads what these settlements are doing.
An informal settlement near an industrial corridor is not a failure of the labour market; it is a
low-cost solution to a problem the formal market refuses to solve. The settlement offers proximity to
work without the rents that proximity would command in a recognised neighbourhood. It allows a
domestic worker, a small contractor, and a security guard to share infrastructure that none could
afford alone. Far from being economically irrational, these arrangements are remarkably well-tuned
to the constraint that the formal city, by withholding tenure and services, has itself imposed.

It is true that informality carries real costs. Residents face the constant possibility of eviction, cannot
use their homes as collateral, and have weaker claims on schools and clinics. To point this out,
however, is not to vindicate the standard prescription. The standard prescription assumes that the
costs of informality are caused by informality itself, when in many cases they are caused by the
refusal of the state to extend ordinary services to informal residents. A settlement does not lack a
paved road because it is informal; it lacks one because the municipality has decided that paving it
would amount to recognition. The defect, in other words, lies in the relationship between settlement
and state, not in the settlement.

Critics may respond that this reasoning romanticises poverty, treating exposure to floods and fires
as a clever adaptation. The objection has force, but it misses the analytical point. To say that
an arrangement is well-tuned to its constraints is not to praise the constraints. It is to insist that
policy take the arrangement seriously rather than treat it as rubble to be cleared. Programmes
that begin by demolishing such settlements and rehousing residents at the city’s edge do not solve
the underlying market failure; they recreate the original problem at greater distance, and in doing
so impose costs on residents that the original arrangement had ingeniously avoided. The relevant
question for policy is not how to dissolve informality but how to extend to it the recognitions that
would relieve its avoidable costs without destroying the proximities it has won.

Q5. The author’s primary purpose in the passage is to:
option_e_markdown:
argue that informal urban settlements are economically rational responses to constraints
imposed by the formal city, and that policy should adjust to that fact rather than try to eliminate
informality.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4. Answer: A. The closing claim is that a theory is overthrown “not when it has been refuted
but when it has stopped being interesting to those who still have careers to build.” Translating this
conceptually: a paradigm’s longevity is best read as its capacity to generate new problems, not as
the supply of supporting evidence.
• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the passage calls the community’s behaviour an “apparent
injustice” toward incumbent theories. But the author immediately reframes this as not really
unjust and as the price of being able to move at all. D reads “apparent injustice” as actual
injustice, confusing concession with endorsement.

• B. Directly contradicted by the passage.

• C. Descriptive-to-prescriptive shift: the passage describes a social mechanism but explicitly denies

it amounts to irrationality.

Passage 2: Informal Settlements and the Formal City
source_raw_block:
Q4. The author would most likely agree with which of the following?

(A) A paradigm’s longevity is best measured by the volume of new research problems it continues
to generate, not by the volume of evidence supporting it.

(B) The history of science shows that empirical refutation, properly understood, remains the
central engine of theoretical change.

(C) Scientific revolutions reveal a deep irrationality at the heart of the scientific enterprise,
since they are driven by social rather than evidential factors.

(D) The migration of younger scientists to new frameworks is unjust to established theories,
even if it is necessary for scientific progress.

Passage 2: Read the following passage and answer the four questions that follow.

Q5. The author’s primary purpose in the passage is to:

(A) argue that informal urban settlements are economically rational responses to constraints
imposed by the formal city, and that policy should adjust to that fact rather than try to eliminate
informality.

(B) criticise economists for using a narrow definition of efficiency that fails to capture the social
value generated by informal settlements.

(C) show that the costs commonly associated with informal settlements are not produced by
informality itself but by the state’s refusal to extend services to such settlements.

(D) defend informal settlements against the charge that they are dysfunctional by pointing to
the unaffordable rents that formal proximity to work would otherwise demand.

Q6. Which of the following best describes the function of the third paragraph in the passage?

(A) It introduces a counter-argument that the author then concedes in full, narrowing the
original claim accordingly.

(B) It acknowledges the genuine costs of informality and then reattributes the source of those
costs in a way that preserves the author’s earlier argument.

(C) It anticipates an objection from administrators and answers it by showing that the costs of
formalisation are higher than the costs of informality.

(D) It complicates the earlier paragraph by suggesting that informal settlements may, in some
respects, be irrational after all.

Q7. Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not support the author’s
central argument?

(A) Residents of informal settlements cannot use their homes as collateral.

(B) Programmes that rehouse residents at the city’s edge recreate the original market failure at
greater distance.

(C) Informal settlements allow workers in different occupations to share infrastructure that
none could afford alone.

(D) The state’s withholding of tenure and services is itself a constraint to which settlements
have adapted.

Q8. Which of the following would the author be least likely to endorse?

(A) A scheme that extends water, drainage, and electricity to recognised and unrecognised
settlements on the same terms.

(B) A policy that grants residents of informal settlements limited tenure rights without altering
the location of the settlement.

(C) A redevelopment plan that demolishes a centrally located informal settlement and provides
new housing on the urban periphery.

(D) A regulatory change that allows informal homes to be used as collateral for small loans.

Passage 3: Read the following passage and answer the four questions that follow.

It is a commonplace of contemporary criticism that the artist’s biography illuminates the work. The
thought has an obvious appeal: if a painter lost a parent in the year a canvas was finished, the
canvas’s grief becomes legible; if a novelist’s letters reveal a hidden affair, the novel’s silences begin
to speak. The biographical method promises to make the work less opaque, more humanly accessible,
and more securely tied to a verifiable life. It is, in a sense, criticism’s answer to the suspicion that
art is arbitrary.

What the method conceals, however, is that an artwork’s meanings are produced by its readers, not
retrieved from its maker. A poem written in grief and a poem written in imitation of grief can be
indistinguishable on the page, and a reader who knows nothing of either can find in it whatever
the poem’s structure permits. Biographical knowledge does not so much explain the work as direct
attention toward certain features and away from others. It is less a key than a flashlight; whatever it
illuminates, it also leaves the rest in deeper shadow. This is not a fatal objection to the method, but
it is a serious one. Critics who use biography to anchor a reading are often unaware of how much of
the work’s range they are quietly excluding.

A defender of the method may concede this and argue that some narrowing is unavoidable. Every
reading excludes something, and biographical narrowing has the virtue of being principled rather
than idiosyncratic. The reply has force, but it underestimates how arbitrary the principle in fact is.
We possess detailed biographies of some artists and almost none of others, and the relative depth of
those archives is determined by accidents of survival, prestige, and language. To privilege biography
is therefore to privilege those works whose makers happen to have been documented, and to make
criticism a hostage to bibliographic luck. The narrowing is principled in form but accidental in
distribution.

None of this implies that biography should be banished from criticism. A great deal can be
learned about a work by knowing the conventions, debates, and disappointments that surrounded its
composition. The problem is not biographical knowledge but biographical inflation, the assumption
that what the maker felt determines what the work means. A more modest use of biography would
treat the life as one context among several, useful when it illuminates a feature already visible in
the work, dispensable when it does not. Such a discipline would lose the consolation that strong
biographical readings provide, but it would also lose their characteristic vice: the steady reduction of
a work’s possibilities to the dimensions of a single life.

Q9. Which of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to make?

(A) Between biographical knowledge as a tool that directs attention and biographical knowledge
as a key that unlocks meaning.

(B) Between criticism that draws on the artist’s life and criticism that ignores it, the former
being defensible only in modest forms.

(C) Between meanings that readers construct and meanings that artists intend, the latter being
inaccessible without biographical detail.

(D) Between principled narrowing of interpretation and idiosyncratic narrowing of interpretation,
the first being justified and the second not.

Q10. The author’s attitude toward the biographical method is best described as:

(A) outright rejection, on the grounds that meaning is produced by readers rather than retrieved
from makers.

(B) qualified acceptance, allowing biography a limited role provided its application does not
collapse the work into the life.

(C) sympathetic, since principled narrowing is preferable to idiosyncratic readings even if the
principle is sometimes arbitrary.

(D) ambivalent, since the method’s benefits and costs are presented as roughly balanced and
no preference is expressed.

Q11. The author refers to “bibliographic luck” primarily in order to:

(A) show that the biographical method privileges works whose makers happen to have been
heavily documented, undermining its claim to principled neutrality.

(B) demonstrate that biographies survive selectively, and that critics should therefore distrust
the records they possess.

(C) illustrate that prestige, language, and accidents of survival distort which artists are remem-
bered at all.

(D) challenge the defender’s claim that biographical narrowing is principled by pointing out
that any narrowing involves some exclusion.

Q12. Which of the following is most consistent with the author’s view?

(A) A reading that draws on a poet’s letters to confirm an interpretation that the poem’s
imagery already supports is legitimate criticism.

(B) A critic who refuses on principle to consult an artist’s biography produces interpretations
more faithful to the work than one who does.

(C) The richer the documentation of an artist’s life, the more securely a critic can recover the
work’s intended meaning.

(D) Biographical readings should be reserved for artists whose lives are well documented, since
other readings would rest on speculation.

Passage 4: Read the following passage and answer the four questions that follow.

For most of the twentieth century, ecologists treated ecosystems as systems tending toward equilibrium.
Disturbances such as fires, storms, and outbreaks of disease were exceptions to a baseline of stability,
and the proper aim of management was to allow that stability to reassert itself. This picture had
clear policy consequences: forests were to be protected from fire, predators from hunters, watersheds
from grazing. It also had a quieter aesthetic appeal. A stable ecosystem was a complete ecosystem,
and intervention, however well-intentioned, was understood as departure from a natural state.

Field research over the past several decades has steadily eroded this picture. Many systems that
look stable are in fact maintained by the very disturbances they appear to exclude. Some grasslands
persist only because periodic fire prevents tree colonisation, and certain coral reefs maintain their
diversity only because moderate storm damage repeatedly opens space for new recruits. Where
managers, persuaded by the equilibrium model, have eliminated such disturbances, the result has
often been not greater stability but a slow simplification of the system, ending in a regime that is
harder to reverse than the one being protected.

It would be tempting to conclude that disturbance is therefore good and that managers should
engineer disturbances where they have been suppressed. This inference, however, is too quick. The
same fire that maintains a grassland in one region can convert a humid forest into degraded scrub
in another, and reefs that thrive under moderate storms collapse under intense ones. What field
research suggests is not that disturbance is uniformly beneficial but that the relevant property of
an ecosystem is its disturbance regime: a characteristic pattern of intensity, frequency, and spatial
extent that has shaped which species persist and how they interact. Disrupt that pattern, whether
by suppressing disturbance or by amplifying it, and the system shifts.

This reframing changes what management can reasonably aim for. It cannot aim for the restoration
of an undisturbed baseline, because no such baseline exists; it cannot aim for the simple presence
of disturbance, because disturbance per se is not the unit of analysis. It can aim, more modestly,
for the maintenance of historical disturbance regimes, while acknowledging that those regimes are
themselves shifting under climate change. The shift in climate threatens to push many systems

outside the disturbance regimes they evolved with, and management, in such cases, becomes less a
matter of preservation than of guiding transitions whose outcomes cannot be fully predicted. The
older language of stability, intervention, and natural state survives in policy documents, but it has
quietly ceased to describe the work that ecology now does.

Q13. The passage as a whole is primarily concerned with:

(A) tracing how the central concept of ecological management has shifted from stability to
disturbance regime, and what that shift implies for what management can hope to achieve.

(B) arguing that the equilibrium model of ecosystems is empirically inadequate and should be
replaced by one in which disturbances are central.

(C) explaining why policies that suppress fires, predation, and grazing have produced ecological
outcomes worse than those they sought to prevent.

(D) warning that climate change is pushing ecosystems beyond the disturbance regimes within
which they evolved, making prediction increasingly difficult.

Q14. The example of grasslands and coral reefs serves mainly to:

(A) show that the appearance of ecological stability can be misleading, since some apparently
stable systems depend on the disturbances they seem to exclude.

(B) establish that disturbance is generally beneficial to ecosystems, and that managers should
reintroduce it where it has been suppressed.

(C) illustrate the diversity of mechanisms by which fire and storm damage contribute to
ecological complexity.

(D) demonstrate that managers who follow the equilibrium model misidentify which species in
a system are likely to persist.

Q15. The author’s view of the policy implication that disturbance should be engineered where it
has been suppressed is best described as:

(A) endorsement, since the suppression of disturbance has produced outcomes worse than the
disturbances it was intended to prevent.

(B) resistance, because the same kind of disturbance can stabilise one system and degrade
another, and what matters is the regime rather than disturbance itself.

(C) scepticism, on the ground that climate change is likely to overwhelm any management
strategy aimed at restoring historical patterns.

(D) partial agreement, accepting the principle but doubting that managers can engineer
disturbances at the required intensity.

Q16. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author’s reframing of management
goals?

(A) A long-term study finds that in regions where historical fire frequencies were preserved,
ecosystems retained more species than in regions where fire was either suppressed or intensified.

(B) A meta-analysis shows that ecosystems with a higher absolute frequency of disturbance
tend to support more species than ecosystems with a lower one.

(C) A survey of policy documents shows that “stability” and “natural state” continue to
dominate official ecological language despite their declining scientific use.

(D) A climate model projects that storm intensity will increase in tropical regions, threatening
reef systems that depend on moderate disturbance.

Section B:

question_text_markdown:
The paragraph below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the paragraph.

Demographers and policy commentators routinely treat the falling fertility rate as a problem demanding
intervention, on the assumption that fewer young workers will eventually be unable to support a
growing elderly population. The intuition is plausible, but it conflates two distinct questions: whether
a society has too few workers, and whether it has too few children. A society can in principle
compensate for fewer children by raising labour productivity, extending working lives, or admitting
migrants, and the historical record suggests that each of these responses has been used somewhere
with some success. The presumption that fertility is the lever to pull is therefore not a conclusion
drawn from evidence but a preference for one solution over the others, and it should be presented as
such rather than dressed up as demographic necessity.
option_a_markdown:
Falling fertility rates are widely treated as a crisis, but historical evidence shows that
productivity gains, longer working lives, and immigration have each compensated for declining
births in some societies.
option_b_markdown:
Policy debates conflate the question of whether a society has too few workers with whether it
has too few children; since multiple responses to a worker shortage exist, preferring fertility-raising
policies is a choice that should be acknowledged rather than presented as a necessity.
option_c_markdown:
The argument that low fertility threatens support for the elderly rests on an intuition that
ignores alternative levers such as productivity and migration, and is therefore demographically
unjustified.
option_d_markdown:
Demographers should stop treating falling fertility as a problem and instead direct attention
to productivity, longer working lives, and immigration, which have been used to address shortages
of workers.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17. Answer: B. The paragraph’s central move is to separate two questions (workers vs children)
and to recast the demand for higher fertility as one policy preference among several. B captures both
the analytical separation and the prescriptive upshot.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because it lists the alternative levers the paragraph cites. But A’s
emphasis is on the historical record, not on the conflation of the two questions, which is the
paragraph’s analytical hinge. True but partial.

• C. Too strong: “demographically unjustified” overshoots the paragraph, which says only that the

preference should be presented as a preference rather than a necessity.

15


CAT-plus VARC Simulation

24 Questions | 40 Minutes

• D. Descriptive-to-prescriptive shift: the paragraph does not say demographers should “stop”

treating fertility as a problem.

question_text_markdown:
The paragraph below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the paragraph.

The standard defence of free speech holds that truth emerges from the open contest of opinions, and
that any restriction on what may be said therefore retards the discovery of truth. This is an attractive
picture, but it assumes that the contest takes place on terms its participants would all recognise as
fair: that arguments are met with arguments, that listeners weigh claims rather than count repetitions,
and that the medium of exchange does not itself favour some kinds of speech over others. Where
these assumptions fail, as they often do in environments designed to maximise attention, the defence
loses much of its force. It does not follow that restriction is therefore justified, but it does follow that
the standard defence cannot be invoked as if the environment of speech were neutral.
option_a_markdown:
The defence of free speech as a path to truth presupposes a fair contest of opinions; where
the medium of exchange distorts that contest, the defence cannot be straightforwardly invoked,
though restriction is not thereby justified either.
option_b_markdown:
Free speech is defended on the ground that truth emerges from open debate, but attention-
driven environments distort that debate and therefore weaken the case against restriction.
option_c_markdown:
Restrictions on speech may sometimes be justified because modern media environments fail
to satisfy the conditions under which the open contest of opinions reliably yields truth.
option_d_markdown:
The standard defence of free speech rests on assumptions about fair contest that hold in
some environments and fail in others, especially those designed to maximise attention.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18. Answer: A. The paragraph builds toward the careful claim that the failure of fair-contest
assumptions weakens the standard defence but does not by itself justify restriction. A is the only
summary that preserves this two-sided structure.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because attention-driven distortion is named in the paragraph. But
B says the case against restriction is “weakened,” which is closer to the author’s claim, yet drops
the explicit caveat that restriction is not thereby justified. True but incomplete on the central
qualification.

• C. Goes further than the paragraph allows by suggesting restrictions “may sometimes be justified.”

• D. True but partial; misses the central conclusion about what cannot be invoked.

question_text_markdown:
The paragraph below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the paragraph.

It is often said that translation is impossible, since no word in one language corresponds exactly to a
word in another, and the structures that hold words together vary even more sharply. Yet the same is
true within any single language: the word “home” means different things to different speakers, and
a sentence can shift in meaning according to context, tone, and reader. If we do not conclude on
this basis that monolingual communication is impossible, we should hesitate to draw the conclusion
for translation. What translation lacks is not the kind of fidelity ordinary communication possesses
but the illusion of such fidelity. The translator’s labour is visible, and so the inexactness translation
shares with all language is mistaken for a special failure of translation itself.
option_a_markdown:
Translation is widely thought to be impossible because of inexact correspondences between
languages, but the same inexactness exists within a single language, and translation is therefore
no more impossible than ordinary communication.
option_b_markdown:
The claim that translation is impossible misidentifies the inexactness common to all
communication as a defect peculiar to translation, an error encouraged by the visibility of the
translator’s labour.
option_c_markdown:
Translation is held to a higher standard of fidelity than monolingual communication because
the translator’s labour is more visible, and this double standard accounts for the persistent belief
in its impossibility.
option_d_markdown:
Words and structures do not correspond exactly between languages, but neither do they
correspond exactly within languages, so the impossibility of translation is overstated.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19. Answer: B. The paragraph diagnoses an error: people mistake the inexactness common to all
language for a defect peculiar to translation, an error encouraged by the visibility of the translator’s
labour. B states exactly this diagnosis.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because it parallels the paragraph’s structure (inexactness in both
contexts). But A reads the conclusion as “translation is no more impossible than monolingual
communication,” which states a true entailment but misses the explanatory move about visibility
and misattribution that is the paragraph’s main work. True but partial.

• C. Too narrow: focuses only on the visibility point and treats it as the whole argument.

• D. Restates premises without the conclusion.
source_raw_block:

question_text_markdown:
Five sentences related to a topic are given below. Four of them can be put together to form a
meaningful and coherent paragraph. Identify the odd one out and key in the number of that sentence.
option_a_markdown:
Early-twentieth-century museums of natural history were organised on the conviction that
arranging specimens by morphological similarity would make evolutionary relationships visible
to any attentive observer.
option_b_markdown:
This conviction reflected a faith, common to the period, that the right ordering of objects could

substitute for explicit theoretical commentary, which would have seemed didactic.
option_c_markdown:
Visitors were therefore presented with rows of beetles and birds whose meaning, curators

believed, would be inferred from the very fact of their arrangement.
option_d_markdown:
Modern museum design increasingly favours interactive screens that allow visitors to customise

the level of detail they encounter.
option_e_markdown:
The implicit theory of viewing was that the eye, properly guided by sequence, would discover

relationships without being told what to find.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20. Answer: 4. Sentences 1, 2, 3, and 5 form a coherent paragraph about early-twentieth-century
museum theory: the conviction that arrangement could substitute for explicit theory (1, 2), the
practical consequence (3), and the underlying theory of viewing (5). Sentence 4 is on the same broad
theme (museum design) but shifts to modern interactive screens and visitor customisation, which
belongs to a different stage of argument. The oddness is structural, not topical.
source_raw_block:

2. This conviction reflected a faith, common to the period, that the right ordering of objects could

3. Visitors were therefore presented with rows of beetles and birds whose meaning, curators

believed, would be inferred from the very fact of their arrangement.

4. Modern museum design increasingly favours interactive screens that allow visitors to customise

the level of detail they encounter.

5. The implicit theory of viewing was that the eye, properly guided by sequence, would discover

relationships without being told what to find.

question_text_markdown:
Five sentences related to a topic are given below. Four of them can be put together to form a
meaningful and coherent paragraph. Identify the odd one out and key in the number of that sentence.
option_a_markdown:
Public-health surveillance gathers detailed data about populations in order to detect disease

patterns before they become emergencies.
option_b_markdown:
The same data, however, can be repurposed in ways the original collection never contemplated,

raising questions about consent that surveillance frameworks rarely answer cleanly.
option_c_markdown:
Privacy, once treated as a residual matter, has therefore moved closer to the centre of how

public-health systems are designed and justified.
option_d_markdown:
Even where the original collection is uncontroversial, the absence of a clear rule against secondary

use weakens public confidence in subsequent rounds of data gathering.
option_e_markdown:
Vaccination programmes succeed most reliably when they combine convenient access with

sustained community engagement by trusted health workers.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q21. Answer: 5. Sentences 1, 2, 3, and 4 form a coherent paragraph about how surveillance
data, consent, and privacy are reshaping the design of public-health systems. Sentence 5 is on a
public-health topic but addresses vaccination delivery and community engagement, which lies outside
the surveillance-privacy argument. The oddness arises from a shift in scope and causal direction.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4) given below, when properly sequenced, would yield a
coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper sequencing of the order of the sentences and key in this
sequence of four numbers as your answer.
option_a_markdown:
This habit of postponement, however, is rarely an admission of inadequacy; it is usually the

mark of a discipline confident enough to refuse premature closure.
option_b_markdown:
Economists are often criticised for failing to commit themselves to clear forecasts, hedging

predictions with conditions that critics regard as evasions.
option_c_markdown:
Physicists, by contrast, are admired for predictions of remarkable precision, but they make

them only about systems whose variables they can isolate.
option_d_markdown:
A discipline that studies systems it cannot isolate must either inflate its confidence or qualify

its predictions, and qualification is the more honest path.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22. Answer: 2314. Sentence 2 introduces the critical view of economists. Sentence 3 contrasts
physicists, sharpening the picture. Sentence 1 reverses the critic’s framing (“This habit of post-
ponement, however, is rarely an admission of inadequacy”), where the demonstrative “this” points
back to qualification implicit in 2 and 3. Sentence 4 closes by articulating the general principle: a
discipline that cannot isolate variables must qualify. The conceptual sequence is critique → contrast
→ reversal of the critique → general principle. Tempting alternative 2134 puts the reversal before
the contrast, which leaves “this habit of postponement” without the sharpening that the physicist
comparison provides.
source_raw_block:

comparison provides.

Q22. Answer: 2314. Sentence 2 introduces the critical view of economists. Sentence 3 contrasts
physicists, sharpening the picture. Sentence 1 reverses the critic’s framing (“This habit of post-
ponement, however, is rarely an admission of inadequacy”), where the demonstrative “this” points
back to qualification implicit in 2 and 3. Sentence 4 closes by articulating the general principle: a
discipline that cannot isolate variables must qualify. The conceptual sequence is critique → contrast
→ reversal of the critique → general principle. Tempting alternative 2134 puts the reversal before
the contrast, which leaves “this habit of postponement” without the sharpening that the physicist
comparison provides.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4) given below, when properly sequenced, would yield a
coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper sequencing of the order of the sentences and key in this
sequence of four numbers as your answer.
option_a_markdown:
A society that loses its slang loses, with it, the chief medium through which the young distinguish

themselves from their elders.
option_b_markdown:
Slang, often dismissed as careless or coarse, performs a function for which standard language is

poorly equipped.
option_c_markdown:
The very features that make slang offend purists, its instability and its capacity to age quickly,

are exactly what allow each generation to remake the boundary on its own terms.
option_d_markdown:
It marks the boundary between insiders and outsiders, and it does so in a form light enough to

be discarded as soon as outsiders learn to mimic it.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23. Answer: 2431. Sentence 2 introduces slang and announces that it performs a function
standard language cannot. Sentence 4 specifies the function (boundary marking between insiders
and outsiders). Sentence 3 then explains why slang’s offensive features are functional. Sentence 1
closes by stating the social cost of losing slang. The conceptual sequence is claim → mechanism
→ explanation → consequence. Tempting alternative 2413 places consequence before explanation,
breaking the flow from mechanism to defence of features.

Q23. The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4) given below, when properly sequenced, would yield a
coherent paragraph. Decide on the proper sequencing of the order of the sentences and key in this
sequence of four numbers as your answer.

1. A society that loses its slang loses, with it, the chief medium through which the young distinguish

themselves from their elders.

2. Slang, often dismissed as careless or coarse, performs a function for which standard language is

poorly equipped.

are exactly what allow each generation to remake the boundary on its own terms.

4. It marks the boundary between insiders and outsiders, and it does so in a form light enough to

be discarded as soon as outsiders learn to mimic it.

question_text_markdown:
The passage given below has four blanks marked [A], [B], [C], and [D]. A standalone sentence
follows. Identify the blank where the sentence best fits.

The idea that historians have replaced narrative with analysis is largely a self-image of the profession
rather than a fair description of its work. [A] Look at any work of academic history more than two
hundred pages long, and the narrative spine is unmistakable: a beginning that establishes a problem,
a middle that brings forward causes and contingencies, and an end that closes some questions while
[B] What has changed is not the place of narrative but its visibility; analysis is
opening others.
foregrounded and narrative has been pushed into the structure of the book itself, where it is felt rather
than announced. [C] Younger historians, taught to disavow storytelling as a literary indulgence, often
produce works whose narrative architecture is the more powerful for being unacknowledged. [D] The
discipline’s denial of its own narrative form is therefore less a methodological achievement than a
vocabulary problem.

Sentence to insert: “The disavowal, in other words, has shaped how historians describe their work
but not how they actually compose it.”
option_d_markdown:
[D]

4. Detailed Solutions

Passage 1: Scientific Revolutions and Generational Migration

Q1. Answer: A. The passage opens by undermining the standard “anomalies cause paradigm
change” story, dismisses elegance as a circular explanation, and then settles on the migration of
younger researchers to new frameworks as the mechanism that actually drives shifts. Option A
captures this final positive claim.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the author does say consensus is partly an artefact of
professional life. But B treats this remark as the central claim and characterises the standard
story as “misleading” in a flat sense. The author explicitly says this is not a critique of science
and that the migration is what makes science productive. B confuses concession with conclusion
and exaggerates tone.

• C. True but partial: the elegance argument is a sub-claim used to clear the field for the central

thesis, not the thesis itself.

• D. Distorted emphasis and tone: the author does not say younger scientists are “biased” but

that they rationally drift to richer problem spaces.

Q2. Answer: B. The Newton/calculus passage appears in the paragraph that rejects elegance
as a free-standing reason for paradigm preference. The point is that elegance is acquired through
mathematical labour, so citing it to explain why a paradigm wins is circular.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because the passage does say mathematical labour gives a paradigm
its lean form. But A reads the example as making a general claim about how paradigms gain
explanatory power, whereas the example is doing the narrower job of disabling an alternative
theory of paradigm change. Confuses local function with global claim.

• C. Unsupported: the passage does not say Newtonian mechanics was displaced because it ceased

to be productive.

• D. True but irrelevant to the function of the example in context.

Q3. Answer: A. The author’s argument rests on the claim that anomalies and predictive superiority
do not by themselves explain paradigm change. A direct counter-finding showing that new paradigms
have been adopted only after they outperformed the old in prediction would undercut the very
mechanism the passage is built on.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the author identifies young researchers as the engine of
migration; if seniors led adoption, the migration story would weaken. But the author’s claim is
about where research labour and attention move, not strictly who signs the adoption documents.
Senior leadership is compatible with the broader migration of problem-attention. A more directly
cuts the central mechanism.

• C. Compatible with the passage; growth of unsolved problems within a paradigm can be precisely

what attracts younger workers elsewhere.

• D. Would weaken only mildly, since the author’s claim concerns shifts in attention and problem

space, not crude prize counts.

Q4. Answer: A. The closing claim is that a theory is overthrown “not when it has been refuted
but when it has stopped being interesting to those who still have careers to build.” Translating this
conceptually: a paradigm’s longevity is best read as its capacity to generate new problems, not as
the supply of supporting evidence.
• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the passage calls the community’s behaviour an “apparent
injustice” toward incumbent theories. But the author immediately reframes this as not really
unjust and as the price of being able to move at all. D reads “apparent injustice” as actual
injustice, confusing concession with endorsement.

• B. Directly contradicted by the passage.

• C. Descriptive-to-prescriptive shift: the passage describes a social mechanism but explicitly denies

it amounts to irrationality.

Passage 2: Informal Settlements and the Formal City

Q5. Answer: A. The passage opens with the “efficient city” framing, shows informal settlements
are well-tuned responses to constraints, concedes their costs, reattributes those costs, and closes with
the prescription that policy should extend recognition rather than clear settlements. A captures both
the analytical claim and the policy upshot.

• Runner-up: C. Tempting because the passage devotes a full paragraph to the reattribution
of costs. But this is one move within the larger argument, not the argument’s primary purpose,
which also includes the prior claim that the settlements are rational and the later prescription on
policy. True but partial.

• B. Confuses example with argument: the author uses the economist’s definition as a starting

point, not as the chief target.

• D. Too narrow: focuses only on the rent-savings claim and ignores the reattribution-of-costs move

and the policy conclusion.

Q6. Answer: B. Paragraph 3 begins by accepting the costs of informality (“It is true that
informality carries real costs”), then says this does not vindicate the standard prescription, and
then reattributes the source of those costs from informality to state withholding. The paragraph’s
function is concede-then-reattribute, preserving the central claim.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because the paragraph does open with concession. But the concession
is partial; the author does not narrow the original claim, he relocates the source of the costs.
Confuses partial concession with full concession.

• C. Unsupported: the paragraph does not compare formalisation costs with informality costs.

• D. Opposite: the paragraph defends the claim of rationality rather than complicating it.

Q7. Answer: A. The author’s central argument is that informal settlements are rational responses
to constraints and that their costs are largely caused by the state. The detail in A (that residents
cannot use homes as collateral) is mentioned in the passage but functions as one of the costs whose
source the author then reattributes; it is true according to the passage but does not in itself support
the author’s central reframing.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because it is true according to the passage. However, B directly
supports the central argument by showing that the standard prescription recreates the original
problem; it is therefore not an answer to the stem. True but central.

• C. Directly supports the rationality claim and is therefore not the stem’s answer.

• D. Directly supports the central claim that the settlements are adapted to a state-imposed

constraint.

Q8. Answer: C. The author argues repeatedly that demolishing a centrally located settlement
and rehousing residents at the periphery recreates the original market failure at greater distance.
Endorsing such a plan is what the author would least accept.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the author’s framing emphasises full recognition, and “limited
tenure rights” may sound like an incomplete reform. But the author would clearly accept a step
that begins to relieve avoidable costs without altering location; modest reform is compatible with
the central claim, while peripheral rehousing destroys the proximities the author values.

• A. Directly aligned with the author’s prescription.

• D. Directly addresses one of the costs the author identifies.

Passage 3: Biography and the Limits of Criticism

Q9. Answer: A. The author repeatedly distinguishes biography-as-key (which retrieves the work’s
meaning from the maker) from biography-as-flashlight (which directs attention to some features and
away from others). This is the precise distinction the passage is constructed around.

• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the author engages with the defender’s claim that biographical
narrowing is principled. But this is a sub-move; the larger distinction the passage is structured
around is the key-versus-flashlight contrast, not the principled-versus-idiosyncratic one.

• B. Misstates the distinction: the author does not propose abolishing biography but resisting

biographical inflation.

• C. Confuses an in-passing remark about readers producing meaning with the central distinction;
the passage’s main contrast is functional (how biography is used), not metaphysical (who produces
meaning).

Q10. Answer: B. The closing paragraph explicitly says biography “should not be banished from
criticism” and proposes a “more modest use” of it. This is qualified acceptance, allowing biography
a limited role and resisting biographical inflation.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because much of the middle of the passage criticises the biographical
method. But the closing paragraph rules out rejection outright. Confuses concession with
conclusion.

• C. Misreads the author’s response to the defender; the author treats the principled-narrowing

defence as forceful but ultimately weakened by the arbitrariness of archival depth.

• D. Unsupported: the author expresses a clear preference for the modest use of biography.

Q11. Answer: A. “Bibliographic luck” is introduced to show that the principle on which biographical
narrowing is defended is in practice applied only where archives happen to be deep, which makes
the narrowing arbitrary in distribution and therefore weakens the defender’s claim to principled
neutrality.
• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the phrase does form part of the response to the defender.
But D wrongly frames the response as a general point about all narrowing; the author’s specific
point is about the unequal distribution of biographical archives, which is what A captures.

• B. Too broad and shifts to a general distrust of biographical records.

• C. True in the passage but locates the point in a different argumentative role; the issue is not

which artists are remembered, but which works are interpretable under the method.

Q12. Answer: A. The author’s prescription in the closing paragraph is to treat biography as
“useful when it illuminates a feature already visible in the work, dispensable when it does not.” A is
a careful conceptual paraphrase of this prescription.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the passage criticises biographical inflation; a critic who
refuses biography on principle might seem to be safe from the vice. But the author explicitly
says biography “should not be banished from criticism” and proposes a more modest use, not an
abstention. B overshoots the author’s qualified position.

• C. Directly contradicts the author’s view that meanings are produced by readers, not retrieved

from makers.

• D. Confuses the modest use of biography with restricting criticism to well-documented artists;

the author opposes such gatekeeping.

Passage 4: Disturbance, Stability, and Ecological Management

Q13. Answer: A. The passage tracks a conceptual transition (from equilibrium to disturbance
regime) and closes by spelling out the implications of that transition for what management can hope
to achieve. A captures both the conceptual movement and its policy upshot.

• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the passage does dismantle the equilibrium model. But B
stops at the empirical critique and misses the constructive reframing (disturbance regime) and
the policy implications, which are the destination of the passage. True but partial.

• C. Too narrow: covers one segment of the passage.

• D. True but partial; climate change is invoked at the end as a complication, not as the primary

concern.

Q14. Answer: A. The grassland and reef examples appear at the moment when the passage is
showing that the appearance of stability is misleading, since the apparently stable systems depend
on the very disturbances they appear to exclude.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the examples might seem to be making a positive case for
disturbance. But the next paragraph explicitly warns against that very inference, saying it is
“too quick.” B confuses example with conclusion.

• C. True but irrelevant to the example’s role.

• D. Distorted emphasis: the passage is making a structural point, not one about species identifica-

tion.

Q15. Answer: B. The author explicitly says the inference that managers should engineer distur-
bances is “too quick,” and the closing argument insists that the relevant unit is the disturbance
regime, not disturbance itself.
• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the author’s objection might be misread as a practical doubt
about whether disturbances can be engineered. But the author’s objection is conceptual, not
technical: same disturbance, different regimes, different effects. D confuses conceptual with
practical resistance.

• A. Opposite of the author’s view.

• C. True later in the passage but does not describe the author’s main objection to the implication.

Q16. Answer: A. The author’s reframing centres on the maintenance of historical disturbance
regimes. A direct empirical result showing that preserving historical regimes (rather than maximising
or suppressing disturbance) supports the most species would directly strengthen this reframing.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because it appears to support the importance of disturbance. But B
measures absolute frequency, not regime; the author explicitly distinguishes the two and says
disturbance itself is not the unit of analysis. B strengthens a position the author rejects.

• C. True but irrelevant: vocabulary in policy documents does not support a scientific reframing.

• D. Strengthens only the climate-change warning at the end, not the reframing of management

goals.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24. Answer: C. The inserted sentence reads “The disavowal, in other words, has shaped how
historians describe their work but not how they actually compose it.” This is a restatement of the
paragraph’s claim that narrative has been pushed into the structure of the book itself even as analysis

is foregrounded. The sentence belongs immediately after the paragraph distinguishes appearance
(analytical) from substance (narrative), which is precisely at [C], between the visibility claim and
the example of younger historians whose narrative architecture is unacknowledged. At [A] or [B] it
would precede the conceptual material it summarises; at [D] it would duplicate the closing summary

are well-tuned responses to constraints, concedes their costs, reattributes those costs, and closes with
the prescription that policy should extend recognition rather than clear settlements. A captures both
the analytical claim and the policy upshot.

• Runner-up: C. Tempting because the passage devotes a full paragraph to the reattribution
of costs. But this is one move within the larger argument, not the argument’s primary purpose,
which also includes the prior claim that the settlements are rational and the later prescription on
policy. True but partial.

• B. Confuses example with argument: the author uses the economist’s definition as a starting

point, not as the chief target.

• D. Too narrow: focuses only on the rent-savings claim and ignores the reattribution-of-costs move

and the policy conclusion.

Q6. Answer: B. Paragraph 3 begins by accepting the costs of informality (“It is true that
informality carries real costs”), then says this does not vindicate the standard prescription, and
then reattributes the source of those costs from informality to state withholding. The paragraph’s
function is concede-then-reattribute, preserving the central claim.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because the paragraph does open with concession. But the concession
is partial; the author does not narrow the original claim, he relocates the source of the costs.
Confuses partial concession with full concession.

• C. Unsupported: the paragraph does not compare formalisation costs with informality costs.

• D. Opposite: the paragraph defends the claim of rationality rather than complicating it.

Q7. Answer: A. The author’s central argument is that informal settlements are rational responses
to constraints and that their costs are largely caused by the state. The detail in A (that residents
cannot use homes as collateral) is mentioned in the passage but functions as one of the costs whose
source the author then reattributes; it is true according to the passage but does not in itself support
the author’s central reframing.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because it is true according to the passage. However, B directly
supports the central argument by showing that the standard prescription recreates the original
problem; it is therefore not an answer to the stem. True but central.

• C. Directly supports the rationality claim and is therefore not the stem’s answer.

• D. Directly supports the central claim that the settlements are adapted to a state-imposed

constraint.

Q8. Answer: C. The author argues repeatedly that demolishing a centrally located settlement
and rehousing residents at the periphery recreates the original market failure at greater distance.
Endorsing such a plan is what the author would least accept.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the author’s framing emphasises full recognition, and “limited
tenure rights” may sound like an incomplete reform. But the author would clearly accept a step
that begins to relieve avoidable costs without altering location; modest reform is compatible with
the central claim, while peripheral rehousing destroys the proximities the author values.

• A. Directly aligned with the author’s prescription.

• D. Directly addresses one of the costs the author identifies.

Passage 3: Biography and the Limits of Criticism

Q9. Answer: A. The author repeatedly distinguishes biography-as-key (which retrieves the work’s
meaning from the maker) from biography-as-flashlight (which directs attention to some features and
away from others). This is the precise distinction the passage is constructed around.

• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the author engages with the defender’s claim that biographical
narrowing is principled. But this is a sub-move; the larger distinction the passage is structured
around is the key-versus-flashlight contrast, not the principled-versus-idiosyncratic one.

• B. Misstates the distinction: the author does not propose abolishing biography but resisting

biographical inflation.

• C. Confuses an in-passing remark about readers producing meaning with the central distinction;
the passage’s main contrast is functional (how biography is used), not metaphysical (who produces
meaning).

Q10. Answer: B. The closing paragraph explicitly says biography “should not be banished from
criticism” and proposes a “more modest use” of it. This is qualified acceptance, allowing biography
a limited role and resisting biographical inflation.
• Runner-up: A. Tempting because much of the middle of the passage criticises the biographical
method. But the closing paragraph rules out rejection outright. Confuses concession with
conclusion.

• C. Misreads the author’s response to the defender; the author treats the principled-narrowing

defence as forceful but ultimately weakened by the arbitrariness of archival depth.

• D. Unsupported: the author expresses a clear preference for the modest use of biography.

Q11. Answer: A. “Bibliographic luck” is introduced to show that the principle on which biographical
narrowing is defended is in practice applied only where archives happen to be deep, which makes
the narrowing arbitrary in distribution and therefore weakens the defender’s claim to principled
neutrality.
• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the phrase does form part of the response to the defender.
But D wrongly frames the response as a general point about all narrowing; the author’s specific
point is about the unequal distribution of biographical archives, which is what A captures.

• B. Too broad and shifts to a general distrust of biographical records.

• C. True in the passage but locates the point in a different argumentative role; the issue is not

which artists are remembered, but which works are interpretable under the method.

Q12. Answer: A. The author’s prescription in the closing paragraph is to treat biography as
“useful when it illuminates a feature already visible in the work, dispensable when it does not.” A is
a careful conceptual paraphrase of this prescription.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the passage criticises biographical inflation; a critic who
refuses biography on principle might seem to be safe from the vice. But the author explicitly
says biography “should not be banished from criticism” and proposes a more modest use, not an
abstention. B overshoots the author’s qualified position.

• C. Directly contradicts the author’s view that meanings are produced by readers, not retrieved

from makers.

• D. Confuses the modest use of biography with restricting criticism to well-documented artists;

the author opposes such gatekeeping.

Passage 4: Disturbance, Stability, and Ecological Management

Q13. Answer: A. The passage tracks a conceptual transition (from equilibrium to disturbance
regime) and closes by spelling out the implications of that transition for what management can hope
to achieve. A captures both the conceptual movement and its policy upshot.

• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the passage does dismantle the equilibrium model. But B
stops at the empirical critique and misses the constructive reframing (disturbance regime) and
the policy implications, which are the destination of the passage. True but partial.

• C. Too narrow: covers one segment of the passage.

• D. True but partial; climate change is invoked at the end as a complication, not as the primary

concern.

Q14. Answer: A. The grassland and reef examples appear at the moment when the passage is
showing that the appearance of stability is misleading, since the apparently stable systems depend
on the very disturbances they appear to exclude.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because the examples might seem to be making a positive case for
disturbance. But the next paragraph explicitly warns against that very inference, saying it is
“too quick.” B confuses example with conclusion.

• C. True but irrelevant to the example’s role.

• D. Distorted emphasis: the passage is making a structural point, not one about species identifica-

tion.

Q15. Answer: B. The author explicitly says the inference that managers should engineer distur-
bances is “too quick,” and the closing argument insists that the relevant unit is the disturbance
regime, not disturbance itself.
• Runner-up: D. Tempting because the author’s objection might be misread as a practical doubt
about whether disturbances can be engineered. But the author’s objection is conceptual, not
technical: same disturbance, different regimes, different effects. D confuses conceptual with
practical resistance.

• A. Opposite of the author’s view.

• C. True later in the passage but does not describe the author’s main objection to the implication.

Q16. Answer: A. The author’s reframing centres on the maintenance of historical disturbance
regimes. A direct empirical result showing that preserving historical regimes (rather than maximising
or suppressing disturbance) supports the most species would directly strengthen this reframing.
• Runner-up: B. Tempting because it appears to support the importance of disturbance. But B
measures absolute frequency, not regime; the author explicitly distinguishes the two and says
disturbance itself is not the unit of analysis. B strengthens a position the author rejects.

• C. True but irrelevant: vocabulary in policy documents does not support a scientific reframing.

• D. Strengthens only the climate-change warning at the end, not the reframing of management

goals.

in flatter terms.

passage_text_markdown:
When we speak of remembering, we tend to imagine a faithful retrieval, as though the mind walked
back to a previous moment and returned with a souvenir. Philosophers, however, have long noted
that nothing in the present act of recall logically requires the past it claims to address. The conviction
that an event occurred yesterday is, after all, a state of mind held today; it is constituted by present
images, feelings of familiarity, and a sense of pastness. The reality of the event remembered is not
part of the experience itself, only of its supposed reference. From this it follows that a population
whose world sprang into being five minutes ago, complete with apparent recollections, would be
experientially indistinguishable from a population genuinely continuous with its past.

This skeptical possibility is often raised to embarrass our confidence in memory. But its real
philosophical use lies elsewhere. It shows that memory-belief and the past are linked not by logical
necessity but by something looser–an inherited trust, a working assumption, perhaps a biological
design feature. We rely on this trust not because we have proved it, but because the alternative
leaves us with no usable account of ourselves. Even the doubt about memory must, ironically, draw
on memory to be intelligible: the very thought “my recollection might be illusory” is itself sustained
across a sliver of remembered time.

The point is therefore neither that memory is reliable nor that it is unreliable in a routine sense. It is
that the question of reliability cannot be settled from inside the present experience of remembering.
To ask whether a particular memory is accurate, we must already presuppose a past against which to

test it. Within the felt act of recalling, no such test is available; only further recollections, themselves
of the same logical kind. The skeptic’s five-minute hypothesis is unfalsifiable not because it is
profound but because it is parasitic on the very faculty it questions.

What follows is more interesting than the original puzzle. If memory-belief is logically self-contained,
then the rich texture of a remembered life–continuity of self, the sense of authorship over one’s
actions, the moral weight of past commitments–cannot be vindicated by appeal to the past as an
external fact. It must be sustained from within, as a stance we adopt, not a discovery we make. We
do not first verify our past and then identify with it; we identify with it, and that identification is
what gives the past its grip. Memory, on this view, is less an instrument of knowledge than an act of
allegiance. To remember is to take responsibility for a story whose evidential foundations are, strictly
speaking, always present-tense.
question_text_markdown: The author’s central claim is best expressed as:
option_a_markdown:
Memory cannot be trusted because the five-minute hypothesis can never be disproved, so claims

about the past should be treated with systematic suspicion.
option_b_markdown:
Because the truth of any memory cannot be settled from within the act of remembering, the
continuity we ascribe to our lives rests on a stance taken in the present rather than on external
verification.
option_c_markdown:
Memory is biologically designed to produce trust in the past, and although this trust cannot

be logically proved, it is the most reliable cognitive faculty available to human beings.
option_d_markdown:
Skeptical hypotheses about memory are self-defeating, since doubting memory itself requires
memory, and therefore the reliability of recollection is in fact secured by the very attempt to
question it.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Answer: (2). The passage is built around a single line of reasoning: since reliability cannot be
tested from within the act of remembering, the continuity we attribute to our lives is sustained as an
adopted stance rather than as a verified discovery. Option (2) reproduces this idea without lifting
the passage’s distinctive wording.
Runner-up: (4) is tempting because the passage does observe that even doubt about memory relies
on memory; a fast reader may mistake this remark for the central claim. It is a local observation, not
the global thesis, and it overstates the author’s position by concluding that reliability is “secured”–a

conclusion the author explicitly resists (confuses concession with conclusion).
(1) overstates skepticism (distorted emphasis). (3) imports an evaluative claim about biological design
that the passage neither endorses as central nor uses to ground reliability (too broad / unsupported ).
source_raw_block:
1. The author’s central claim is best expressed as:

(1) Memory cannot be trusted because the five-minute hypothesis can never be disproved, so claims

about the past should be treated with systematic suspicion.

(2) Because the truth of any memory cannot be settled from within the act of remembering, the
continuity we ascribe to our lives rests on a stance taken in the present rather than on external
verification.

(3) Memory is biologically designed to produce trust in the past, and although this trust cannot

be logically proved, it is the most reliable cognitive faculty available to human beings.

(4) Skeptical hypotheses about memory are self-defeating, since doubting memory itself requires
memory, and therefore the reliability of recollection is in fact secured by the very attempt to
question it.
