question_text_markdown: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s reasoning?
option_a_markdown:
Neuroscientific studies show that memories are partially reconstructed each time they are

recalled rather than retrieved intact.
option_b_markdown:
There exist features internal to a present act of remembering–qualitatively distinct from
imagining or believing–that can be shown to occur only when the recalled event actually took
place.
option_c_markdown:
Many cultures sustain elaborate ancestral narratives whose factual accuracy cannot be checked

against any surviving record.
option_d_markdown:
Philosophers have argued that doubt about memory is incoherent because the act of doubting

itself unfolds over a remembered interval.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Answer: (2). The author’s reasoning depends on the claim that no feature internal to a present
act of recall can settle whether the recalled event occurred. (2) directly attacks that linchpin by
asserting an internal marker tied to actual past occurrence. If such a marker existed, the present-tense
self-containment of memory would collapse.
Runner-up: (4) is tempting because it sounds like an objection to skepticism. But it is the author’s
own move, not a counter-move; treating it as a weakener confuses the author’s view with a view
discussed by the author.
(1) is broadly true but does not engage the logical structure (true but irrelevant). (3) is consistent
with the author’s view rather than against it.
source_raw_block:
2. Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s reasoning?

(1) Neuroscientific studies show that memories are partially reconstructed each time they are

recalled rather than retrieved intact.

(2) There exist features internal to a present act of remembering–qualitatively distinct from
imagining or believing–that can be shown to occur only when the recalled event actually took
place.

(3) Many cultures sustain elaborate ancestral narratives whose factual accuracy cannot be checked

against any surviving record.

(4) Philosophers have argued that doubt about memory is incoherent because the act of doubting

itself unfolds over a remembered interval.

passage_text_markdown:
When we speak of remembering, we tend to imagine a faithful retrieval, as though the mind walked
back to a previous moment and returned with a souvenir. Philosophers, however, have long noted
that nothing in the present act of recall logically requires the past it claims to address. The conviction
that an event occurred yesterday is, after all, a state of mind held today; it is constituted by present
images, feelings of familiarity, and a sense of pastness. The reality of the event remembered is not
part of the experience itself, only of its supposed reference. From this it follows that a population
whose world sprang into being five minutes ago, complete with apparent recollections, would be
experientially indistinguishable from a population genuinely continuous with its past.

This skeptical possibility is often raised to embarrass our confidence in memory. But its real
philosophical use lies elsewhere. It shows that memory-belief and the past are linked not by logical
necessity but by something looser–an inherited trust, a working assumption, perhaps a biological
design feature. We rely on this trust not because we have proved it, but because the alternative
leaves us with no usable account of ourselves. Even the doubt about memory must, ironically, draw
on memory to be intelligible: the very thought “my recollection might be illusory” is itself sustained
across a sliver of remembered time.

The point is therefore neither that memory is reliable nor that it is unreliable in a routine sense. It is
that the question of reliability cannot be settled from inside the present experience of remembering.
To ask whether a particular memory is accurate, we must already presuppose a past against which to

test it. Within the felt act of recalling, no such test is available; only further recollections, themselves
of the same logical kind. The skeptic’s five-minute hypothesis is unfalsifiable not because it is
profound but because it is parasitic on the very faculty it questions.

What follows is more interesting than the original puzzle. If memory-belief is logically self-contained,
then the rich texture of a remembered life–continuity of self, the sense of authorship over one’s
actions, the moral weight of past commitments–cannot be vindicated by appeal to the past as an
external fact. It must be sustained from within, as a stance we adopt, not a discovery we make. We
do not first verify our past and then identify with it; we identify with it, and that identification is
what gives the past its grip. Memory, on this view, is less an instrument of knowledge than an act of
allegiance. To remember is to take responsibility for a story whose evidential foundations are, strictly
speaking, always present-tense.
question_text_markdown: The author refers to the five-minute hypothesis primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown:
demonstrate that radical skepticism about the past is a serious empirical possibility that

working scientists ought to take into account.
option_b_markdown:
dramatize a recurring theme in philosophical history concerning the fragility of human knowledge

claims about temporal experience.
option_c_markdown:
isolate a structural feature of memory-belief–its independence from any actually occurring
past–without endorsing the skeptical conclusion the hypothesis is usually marshalled to support.
option_d_markdown:
show that, since the hypothesis is unfalsifiable, every claim about the past is equally unfalsifiable

and therefore equally suspect.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3. Answer: (3). The author uses the hypothesis to isolate a structural property–memory-belief’s
independence from any actually occurring past–while explicitly declining to draw the skeptical moral
usually attached to the hypothesis.
Runner-up: (4) is attractive because the passage does call the hypothesis unfalsifiable. But the
author rejects the inference that everything about the past is therefore equally suspect; (4) confuses
concession with conclusion.
(1) treats a thought experiment as an empirical possibility (distorted emphasis). (2) is a vague gesture
at theme rather than function (too broad ).

(1) demonstrate that radical skepticism about the past is a serious empirical possibility that

working scientists ought to take into account.

(2) dramatize a recurring theme in philosophical history concerning the fragility of human knowledge

claims about temporal experience.

(3) isolate a structural feature of memory-belief–its independence from any actually occurring
past–without endorsing the skeptical conclusion the hypothesis is usually marshalled to support.

(4) show that, since the hypothesis is unfalsifiable, every claim about the past is equally unfalsifiable

and therefore equally suspect.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following would the author be least likely to endorse?
option_a_markdown:
A person’s sense of being the author of her earlier choices is sustained by an attitude she adopts

toward her past rather than by a verification of it.
option_b_markdown:
Whether a particular recollection corresponds to an event that actually occurred is a question

that cannot be answered without leaning on further recollections.
option_c_markdown:
The moral seriousness with which we treat past commitments is a posture that is secured only

once the underlying memories have been independently authenticated.
option_d_markdown:
Doubt about the reliability of memory, when stated honestly, has to make use of the very

capacity whose reliability is in question.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4. Answer: (3). The author repeatedly denies that identification with one’s past requires prior
authentication; identification is what gives the past its grip. (3) reverses the order the author insists
on (causal reversal ).
Runner-up: (1) is the closest to a candidate for “least” because a hurried reader may think the
author rejects all talk of authorship. The passage in fact endorses it; (1) is the author’s own position,
not the one she would least endorse.
(2) and (4) restate explicit claims of the passage.

Passage II – Solutions
source_raw_block:
4. Which of the following would the author be least likely to endorse?

(1) A person’s sense of being the author of her earlier choices is sustained by an attitude she adopts

toward her past rather than by a verification of it.

(2) Whether a particular recollection corresponds to an event that actually occurred is a question

that cannot be answered without leaning on further recollections.

(3) The moral seriousness with which we treat past commitments is a posture that is secured only

once the underlying memories have been independently authenticated.

(4) Doubt about the reliability of memory, when stated honestly, has to make use of the very

capacity whose reliability is in question.

passage_text_markdown:
It is tempting to think of tribal art as the residue of an earlier, less developed imagination, charming
in its naivete but conceptually thin. Such a picture misreads the situation. What looks unstructured
is in fact organised by a different ordering principle–one in which the boundary between perceived and
imagined, animate and inanimate, present and ancestral, is deliberately porous. In tribal narrative,
oceans may swim as fish and mountains may speak as elders, not because the maker is confused
about natural categories, but because the categories themselves are not treated as the deepest layer
of reality. Below them lies a continuum of feeling that any object–star, river, ancestor–may enter
when the story requires it.

This is often described as “hallucinatory”, and the term can mislead. A hallucination, in ordinary
usage, is a private failure of perception. The tribal frame is closer to a shared convention about
which kinds of fusion are admissible. The maker is not reporting a defective sensory event; she is
exercising a craft whose rules differ from those of literate, secular representation. Within that craft,
association of feeling, rather than fidelity to outline, is the binding logic. A mountain may be sad
because the episode is sad; the figure is not a metaphor pasted onto a separate physical fact but a
single expressive unit.

A related claim is that the tribal mind privileges time over space. The argument runs as follows.
Where communities have been historically deprived of secure territorial control, the project of mastery
has migrated inward to the dimension of duration–lineage, ancestry, the long line of the dead. Ritual
conversation with carved ancestors is, in this reading, less a superstition than a strategy: a way of
asserting authority over what cannot be taken away, namely the past one is descended from. The
claim is suggestive, and it explains the remarkable mnemonic feats by which tribal communities
classify thousands of species and stories without writing. But it should not be hardened into a thesis
that tribal cultures are uninterested in space. Their handling of pictorial space is in fact distinctive,
not absent: the frame is mobile, the picture leaks into the wall, the wall into the dwelling.

What the secular tradition calls imagination is, on this account, a relatively late and self-conscious
operation, in which a creator stands apart from the world and constructs an image of it. The tribal
artist is doing something else. She is drawing on a sedimented memory–racial, sensory, communal–and
allowing it to surface through her hand. The work is therefore less an invention than an enactment.
To call it primitive is to apply the wrong measure. To call it merely traditional is to miss the
active labour involved. It is its own mode of making, with its own discipline, its own freedoms,
and–crucially–its own theory of what a representation is for.
question_text_markdown: The author refers to a mountain being “sad” primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown:
illustrate that tribal storytellers often confuse the properties of inanimate objects with the

properties of human beings.
option_b_markdown:
show that tribal narratives use metaphor in a manner broadly similar to that of modern secular

literature.
option_c_markdown:
demonstrate that, within the tribal frame, an emotional charge and the object that carries it
are treated as a single expressive unit rather than as a figurative pairing of two distinct things.
option_d_markdown:
argue that tribal art relies on emotional intensity rather than on craft conventions or rules of

representation.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q5. Answer: (3). The mountain example is introduced precisely to illustrate that emotional
charge and carrier are a single expressive unit, not a metaphor pasted onto a separate physical fact.
Runner-up: (2) is the close competitor: a fast reader may read the example as ordinary metaphor.
The passage explicitly denies this reading; (2) confuses example with argument.
(1) imports a charge of confusion the author rejects (distorted emphasis). (4) makes a broader claim
about the role of feeling vs. craft that the example was not designed to support (too broad ).
source_raw_block:
5. The author refers to a mountain being “sad” primarily in order to:

(1) illustrate that tribal storytellers often confuse the properties of inanimate objects with the

properties of human beings.

(2) show that tribal narratives use metaphor in a manner broadly similar to that of modern secular

literature.

(3) demonstrate that, within the tribal frame, an emotional charge and the object that carries it
are treated as a single expressive unit rather than as a figurative pairing of two distinct things.

(4) argue that tribal art relies on emotional intensity rather than on craft conventions or rules of

representation.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the function of the third paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
It introduces a sympathetic account of why time matters to tribal communities and then

qualifies that account so it is not overstated into a denial of spatial sensibility.
option_b_markdown:
It defends the claim that tribal communities have always been historically dispossessed of

territory and therefore turned inward to memory as compensation.
option_c_markdown:
It rejects the view that ritual conversations with ancestors are merely superstitious, and replaces

it with a strictly political reading of tribal practice.
option_d_markdown:
It catalogues the mnemonic achievements of tribal cultures in order to establish that the absence

of writing has not impaired their knowledge systems.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q6. Answer: (1). The third paragraph first develops the time-over-space thesis sympathetically
and then qualifies it so it is not hardened into a denial of spatial sensibility–an explicit concession-
plus-limit structure.
Runner-up: (3) is tempting because the paragraph does treat ancestor ritual as more than superstition.
But it does not replace the religious reading with a “strictly political” one; the political reading is
offered as “suggestive,” not exclusive (too narrow / distorted emphasis).
(2) overstates a historical claim (unsupported ). (4) takes a supporting detail for the paragraph’s main
function (true but partial ).
source_raw_block:
6. Which of the following best captures the function of the third paragraph?

(1) It introduces a sympathetic account of why time matters to tribal communities and then

qualifies that account so it is not overstated into a denial of spatial sensibility.

(2) It defends the claim that tribal communities have always been historically dispossessed of

territory and therefore turned inward to memory as compensation.

(3) It rejects the view that ritual conversations with ancestors are merely superstitious, and replaces

it with a strictly political reading of tribal practice.

(4) It catalogues the mnemonic achievements of tribal cultures in order to establish that the absence

of writing has not impaired their knowledge systems.

question_text_markdown: Which one of the following is most consistent with the author’s view?
option_a_markdown:
Tribal art is best understood as an early stage on the same developmental path that culminates

in modern secular art.
option_b_markdown:
The fluidity of categories in tribal art is a deliberate ordering principle, not a failure to observe

distinctions that secular traditions later perfected.
option_c_markdown:
The label “hallucinatory” captures the essential character of tribal art because it draws attention

to its private, individual sources.
option_d_markdown:
Tribal art is governed primarily by spontaneity and therefore resists analysis in terms of craft,

convention, or rule.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q7. Answer: (2). The author treats categorial fluidity as a deliberate ordering principle that
operates by a different logic, not as a defective version of secular distinction.
Runner-up: (4) is appealing because the passage stresses freedom and porousness. But the author
explicitly insists that tribal art has its own discipline, conventions, and theory of representation; (4)
overstates the freedom into a denial of rule.
(1) is the developmental view the passage rejects. (3) misreads “hallucinatory” against the author’s
own clarification.
source_raw_block:
7. Which one of the following is most consistent with the author’s view?

(1) Tribal art is best understood as an early stage on the same developmental path that culminates

in modern secular art.

(2) The fluidity of categories in tribal art is a deliberate ordering principle, not a failure to observe

distinctions that secular traditions later perfected.

(3) The label “hallucinatory” captures the essential character of tribal art because it draws attention

to its private, individual sources.

(4) Tribal art is governed primarily by spontaneity and therefore resists analysis in terms of craft,

convention, or rule.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not support the
author’s central claim?
option_a_markdown:
Tribal narratives admit a principle of association between emotion and motif under which a

star or a tree may enter the emotional life of the story.
option_b_markdown:
In tribal traditions, communities have developed elaborate systems for classifying species and

stories without recourse to writing.
option_c_markdown:
The tribal frame is best understood as a shared convention rather than as a private sensory

failure of the kind that the word hallucination usually denotes.
option_d_markdown:
The tribal artist’s work is an enactment of sedimented communal memory and not an invention

by a creator standing apart from the world.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q8. Answer: (2). The mnemonic feat–classifying species and stories without writing–is true per
the passage, but the author offers it as evidence for the time-thesis and even warns against hardening
that thesis. It does not in itself support the author’s broader claim about tribal art as a distinctive
mode of representation governed by its own logic.
Runner-up: (3) is tempting because, like the correct option, it is true per the passage. However, (3)
directly supports the author’s central reframing of “hallucinatory” away from private failure and
toward shared convention; it is not a peripheral fact (true but central, hence wrong for this stem).
(1) and (4) are likewise constitutive of the author’s central position.

Passage III – Solutions
source_raw_block:
8. Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not support the
author’s central claim?

(1) Tribal narratives admit a principle of association between emotion and motif under which a

star or a tree may enter the emotional life of the story.

(2) In tribal traditions, communities have developed elaborate systems for classifying species and

stories without recourse to writing.

(3) The tribal frame is best understood as a shared convention rather than as a private sensory

failure of the kind that the word hallucination usually denotes.

(4) The tribal artist’s work is an enactment of sedimented communal memory and not an invention

by a creator standing apart from the world.

passage_text_markdown:
Imagine entrusting an artificial system with the gravest moral tasks–sentencing offenders, allocating
scarce organs, brokering ceasefires. The appeal is obvious. Such a system would not tire, would not
take bribes, would not let resentment shape its rulings. To many, this looks like the very picture
of impartial reason. Yet the appeal conceals an unresolved question. Is the machine understanding
morality, or only mimicking the patterns of human moral talk? If it is the latter, the impartiality is
cosmetic; the system inherits, in a polished form, the same blind spots and cultural distortions that
shaped its training. It would not transcend our limits; it would launder them.

A deeper worry follows. Moral judgement, on most plausible accounts, depends on intuition, on
lived familiarity with the texture of a situation, and on a sensitivity to what is salient in this case as
opposed to a similar one. These qualities resist formalisation in the way poetry resists paraphrase.
To convert them into a rule book is not merely to translate ethics into a new medium; it may be to
discard what makes ethics ethics. If so, the worry about AI is not that it copies our biases but that
it can only ever operate on a flattened representation of moral life, from which the very materials of
reflective judgement have already been stripped.

Defenders of formal moral systems take this objection seriously and reply that formalisation need not
be flattening. They borrow an analogy from physics. Physics, after centuries of success, still lacks
a single theory of everything. It has many theories, each tied to a domain–quarks, solids, galaxies.
Some are mutually incompatible, yet each begins from a few postulates and unfolds rigorously from
them. The same, it is argued, can be true of ethics. Utilitarianism takes the maximisation of overall
wellbeing as foundational and derives recommendations from there. Deontology starts elsewhere–with
constraints on how persons may be treated–and derives a different but equally disciplined body of
guidance. The plurality of frameworks is not a defect; it is the structure of any serious moral inquiry.

This reply is genuine progress, but it is not the conclusion the optimists draw from it. The physics
analogy says that systems can be both formal and plural. It does not say that ethics will converge
on a single master code, or that any case can be assigned to its proper framework without judgement
of exactly the kind formalisation was supposed to bypass. The defender of AI ethics is therefore
left with a thinner achievement than is sometimes claimed: machines may execute moral reasoning
within a chosen framework, but the choice of framework, and the recognition that this case calls for
this framework, remain stubbornly human acts. The machine can be a powerful instrument within a
moral world; it cannot constitute one.
question_text_markdown: The passage as a whole is primarily concerned with:
option_a_markdown:
rejecting the use of artificial systems in moral decision-making on the grounds that any

formalisation of ethics is incoherent.
option_b_markdown:
arguing that, although formal moral systems can support disciplined reasoning, the prior
judgement of which framework fits a given case is not itself something a machine can supply.
option_c_markdown:
defending the view that ethics, like physics, will eventually converge on a small set of theories

powerful enough to handle the cases that arise in practice.
option_d_markdown:
criticising the appeal to physics as a misleading analogy that obscures the irreducibly intuitive

character of all moral reasoning.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q9. Answer: (2). The argument grants that formal moral systems can support disciplined
reasoning, but insists that the prior question–which framework applies to this case–remains a human
act.
Runner-up: (1) is tempting because the passage is critical of optimistic claims about AI ethics. But
the author does not reject formalisation in principle; she accepts that machines can “execute moral
reasoning within a chosen framework” (distorted emphasis).
(3) is the optimist position the author resists. (4) is too strong: the passage calls the analogy “genuine
progress.”
source_raw_block:
9. The passage as a whole is primarily concerned with:

(1) rejecting the use of artificial systems in moral decision-making on the grounds that any

formalisation of ethics is incoherent.

(2) arguing that, although formal moral systems can support disciplined reasoning, the prior
judgement of which framework fits a given case is not itself something a machine can supply.

(3) defending the view that ethics, like physics, will eventually converge on a small set of theories

powerful enough to handle the cases that arise in practice.

(4) criticising the appeal to physics as a misleading analogy that obscures the irreducibly intuitive

character of all moral reasoning.

of impartial reason. Yet the appeal conceals an unresolved question. Is the machine understanding
morality, or only mimicking the patterns of human moral talk? If it is the latter, the impartiality is
cosmetic; the system inherits, in a polished form, the same blind spots and cultural distortions that
shaped its training. It would not transcend our limits; it would launder them.

A deeper worry follows. Moral judgement, on most plausible accounts, depends on intuition, on
lived familiarity with the texture of a situation, and on a sensitivity to what is salient in this case as
opposed to a similar one. These qualities resist formalisation in the way poetry resists paraphrase.
To convert them into a rule book is not merely to translate ethics into a new medium; it may be to
discard what makes ethics ethics. If so, the worry about AI is not that it copies our biases but that
it can only ever operate on a flattened representation of moral life, from which the very materials of
reflective judgement have already been stripped.

Defenders of formal moral systems take this objection seriously and reply that formalisation need not
be flattening. They borrow an analogy from physics. Physics, after centuries of success, still lacks
a single theory of everything. It has many theories, each tied to a domain–quarks, solids, galaxies.
Some are mutually incompatible, yet each begins from a few postulates and unfolds rigorously from
them. The same, it is argued, can be true of ethics. Utilitarianism takes the maximisation of overall
wellbeing as foundational and derives recommendations from there. Deontology starts elsewhere–with
constraints on how persons may be treated–and derives a different but equally disciplined body of
guidance. The plurality of frameworks is not a defect; it is the structure of any serious moral inquiry.

This reply is genuine progress, but it is not the conclusion the optimists draw from it. The physics
analogy says that systems can be both formal and plural. It does not say that ethics will converge
on a single master code, or that any case can be assigned to its proper framework without judgement
of exactly the kind formalisation was supposed to bypass. The defender of AI ethics is therefore
left with a thinner achievement than is sometimes claimed: machines may execute moral reasoning
within a chosen framework, but the choice of framework, and the recognition that this case calls for
this framework, remain stubbornly human acts. The machine can be a powerful instrument within a
moral world; it cannot constitute one.
question_text_markdown: The example of physics is used in the passage chiefly to:
option_a_markdown:
suggest that ethics, like physics, will ultimately produce a single master theory that subsumes

the others.
option_b_markdown:
show that a discipline can be both rigorously formal and irreducibly plural, thereby weakening
one objection to formalising ethics without settling the question of how a framework is to be
chosen in a given case.
option_c_markdown:
demonstrate that incompatible theories within a discipline are signs of immaturity that further

work will eventually remove.
option_d_markdown:
argue that moral domains, like physical domains, have natural boundaries that any competent

reasoner can detect without further judgement.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q10. Answer: (2). The physics analogy is used to defuse one objection (formalisation = flattening)
by showing that a discipline can be formal and plural. The author explicitly refuses to extend the
analogy to settle framework-selection.
Runner-up: (1) is attractive because optimists may invoke physics to argue for convergence. The
author flags this very move and rejects it (author’s view vs. view discussed by the author ).
(3) and (4) misread the analogy in directions the passage explicitly disowns.
source_raw_block:
10. The example of physics is used in the passage chiefly to:

(1) suggest that ethics, like physics, will ultimately produce a single master theory that subsumes

the others.

(2) show that a discipline can be both rigorously formal and irreducibly plural, thereby weakening
one objection to formalising ethics without settling the question of how a framework is to be
chosen in a given case.

(3) demonstrate that incompatible theories within a discipline are signs of immaturity that further

work will eventually remove.

(4) argue that moral domains, like physical domains, have natural boundaries that any competent

reasoner can detect without further judgement.

passage_text_markdown:

question_text_markdown:
Which one of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author’s final
position?
option_a_markdown:
Several large-scale studies show that AI systems trained on moral judgements reproduce the

cultural biases of their training data.
option_b_markdown:
A panel of expert ethicists, asked to assign novel cases to the most appropriate moral framework,
disagree systematically in ways that cannot be resolved by appeal to any higher-order rule.
option_c_markdown:
Recent advances in computation have dramatically reduced the time needed to derive conse-

quences from a fixed set of moral axioms.
option_d_markdown:
Utilitarian and deontological frameworks agree, in the great majority of everyday cases, on

which action should be performed.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q11. Answer: (2). The author’s final position is that selection of framework cannot itself be
mechanised. (2) supplies evidence that even expert humans cannot resolve this selection by any
higher-order rule–exactly the gap the author claims a machine cannot fill.
Runner-up: (1) is tempting because it speaks to the bias worry from the first paragraph. But the
bias worry is the opening concern, not the final position; (1) strengthens an earlier point, not the
conclusion (true but partial ).
(3) strengthens the optimist view. (4) weakens the importance of selection rather than strengthens it.
source_raw_block:
11. Which one of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author’s final
position?

(1) Several large-scale studies show that AI systems trained on moral judgements reproduce the

cultural biases of their training data.

(2) A panel of expert ethicists, asked to assign novel cases to the most appropriate moral framework,
disagree systematically in ways that cannot be resolved by appeal to any higher-order rule.

(3) Recent advances in computation have dramatically reduced the time needed to derive conse-

quences from a fixed set of moral axioms.

(4) Utilitarian and deontological frameworks agree, in the great majority of everyday cases, on

which action should be performed.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to draw in
the final paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
Between machines that can compute moral conclusions and machines that cannot, the latter

being all that current technology provides.
option_b_markdown:
Between reasoning conducted inside a chosen moral framework, which a machine can in principle
execute, and the prior selection of the framework itself, which calls on the very judgement
formalisation was meant to dispense with.
option_c_markdown:
Between the cosmetic impartiality of an automated judge and the genuine impartiality of an

experienced human one, the former being merely a polished imitation of the latter.
option_d_markdown:
Between ethical systems that can be reduced to physics-style axioms and ethical systems that

cannot, the latter being unsuitable for any kind of mechanical implementation.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q12. Answer: (2). The closing paragraph distinguishes derivation-within-a-framework (machine-
suitable) from selection-of-framework (irreducibly judgemental).
Runner-up:
(3) is tempting because the opening paragraph contrasts “cosmetic” and genuine
impartiality. That contrast belongs to an earlier stretch of the argument, not the final paragraph’s
distinction (true but mislocated / answers a nearby but different question).
(1) is descriptively about machines, not about the conceptual distinction. (4) imports a stronger
claim than the author makes.

Passage IV – Solutions
source_raw_block:
12. Which of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to draw in
the final paragraph?

(1) Between machines that can compute moral conclusions and machines that cannot, the latter

being all that current technology provides.

(2) Between reasoning conducted inside a chosen moral framework, which a machine can in principle
execute, and the prior selection of the framework itself, which calls on the very judgement
formalisation was meant to dispense with.

(3) Between the cosmetic impartiality of an automated judge and the genuine impartiality of an

experienced human one, the former being merely a polished imitation of the latter.

(4) Between ethical systems that can be reduced to physics-style axioms and ethical systems that

cannot, the latter being unsuitable for any kind of mechanical implementation.

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, a large dam was treated almost as a self-justifying object. Its
silhouette spoke for itself: a nation rising, a river tamed, a frontier brought under management.
The benefits were named in the same breath as the structure–flood control, irrigation, hydropower,
jobs–while the costs settled quietly into other ledgers. Communities displaced from the valley moved
out of the photograph. The river below, slowed and warmed, slipped beneath public notice. The
dam belonged to the order of monuments; the harms belonged to the order of paperwork.

This asymmetry was not an accident of public attention. It was built into the way decisions about
dams were made. The same agencies that proposed the projects also evaluated them; the same
political coalitions that benefited from the contracts also drafted the standards by which performance
was measured. Independent, comprehensive review–of why a dam was built, how it actually performed
across decades, and whether the public received a fair return on the enormous investment–was, as
one major commission later concluded, almost entirely absent. The result was not so much a series
of mistakes as a system that made certain kinds of mistakes structurally unlikely to be detected.

A familiar response is that such costs were the unavoidable price of modernity. The cities downstream
needed water; the fields needed irrigation; the grid needed power. Someone had to pay, and the
displaced paid. There is something honest about this defence. It at least acknowledges that the costs
were real. But it misstates the question. The question was never whether modern infrastructure
imposes costs; it was who decides which costs are acceptable and on whose behalf. Across most of
the dam-building century, that decision was made by the beneficiaries on behalf of the dispossessed,
and almost never the other way round. To call the result a sacrifice is to imply a choice. There was,
for most of those who lost, no choice.

The picture is now changing, but not as decisively as is sometimes claimed. Opposition has grown
more articulate; alternative energy sources are increasingly viable; some long-cherished projects have
been abandoned. At the same time, ageing dams are being pushed beyond their designed lifetimes,
climate-driven droughts are reducing their output, and major new construction continues in several
large economies. It would be premature to read the recent slowdown as a turning point. What is
genuinely new is not the end of the dam-building drive but the loss of its old innocence. The benefits
can still be named confidently; the costs can no longer be tucked into other ledgers. Whatever is
built from here on will have to be defended in front of the people whom an earlier era would not
have asked.
question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the author’s central argument?
option_a_markdown:
Twentieth-century dam construction failed primarily because the engineering was poor and the

structures are now ageing badly.
option_b_markdown:
The era’s commitment to dams was sustained less by an honest reckoning of costs and benefits
than by a decision-making structure in which the costs were borne by those who had no voice
in the decision.
option_c_markdown:
Recent declines in dam construction show that the case against large dams has now been

definitively won, even if projects continue in some economies.
option_d_markdown:
The displaced communities of the dam-building era were, on the whole, willing to accept the

costs imposed on them as a fair price for modern development.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q13. Answer: (2). The argument is that the dam-building era was sustained by a decision-making
structure in which costs were imposed on those who had no say.
Runner-up: (3) is tempting because the closing paragraph notes the slowdown and opposition. But
the author explicitly resists reading the slowdown as a turning point (confuses concession with
conclusion).
(1) reduces a structural critique to an engineering complaint (too narrow ). (4) reverses the author’s
claim about consent (causal reversal ).
source_raw_block:
13. Which of the following best captures the author’s central argument?

(1) Twentieth-century dam construction failed primarily because the engineering was poor and the

structures are now ageing badly.

(2) The era’s commitment to dams was sustained less by an honest reckoning of costs and benefits
than by a decision-making structure in which the costs were borne by those who had no voice
in the decision.

(3) Recent declines in dam construction show that the case against large dams has now been

definitively won, even if projects continue in some economies.

(4) The displaced communities of the dam-building era were, on the whole, willing to accept the

costs imposed on them as a fair price for modern development.

question_text_markdown:
The author’s attitude towards the defence that dams were “the unavoidable price
of modernity” is best described as:
option_a_markdown: entirely dismissive: the defence is treated as a self-serving rationalisation with no merit.
option_b_markdown:
broadly accepting: the author concedes that the displaced did, on balance, benefit from the

modernity the dams produced.
option_c_markdown:
partially appreciative but ultimately redirecting: the author grants that the defence honestly
admits real costs, while insisting that the genuine question is who was authorised to weigh
those costs.
option_d_markdown:
neutral and analytical: the author neither endorses nor rejects the defence, merely setting it

alongside competing positions.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q14. Answer: (3). The author calls the defence “honest” in that it acknowledges real costs, then
redirects: the real question is who decides whose costs are acceptable.
Runner-up: (1) is tempting because the author ultimately rejects the defence’s framing. But “entirely

dismissive” contradicts the explicit concession that the defence at least admits the costs (too strong).
(2) reverses the author’s position. (4) understates the redirect (too narrow ).
source_raw_block:
14. The author’s attitude towards the defence that dams were “the unavoidable price
of modernity” is best described as:

(1) entirely dismissive: the defence is treated as a self-serving rationalisation with no merit.

(2) broadly accepting: the author concedes that the displaced did, on balance, benefit from the

modernity the dams produced.

(3) partially appreciative but ultimately redirecting: the author grants that the defence honestly
admits real costs, while insisting that the genuine question is who was authorised to weigh
those costs.

(4) neutral and analytical: the author neither endorses nor rejects the defence, merely setting it

alongside competing positions.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument in the
second paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
Independent post-construction audits of major twentieth-century dams routinely included
representatives of displaced communities and consistently rated those projects highly across
their full operating lives.
option_b_markdown:
Many large dams built in the mid-twentieth century are now approaching the end of their

expected service lives.
option_c_markdown:
Several governments have, in recent years, abandoned long-cherished hydroelectric projects in

the face of organised opposition.
option_d_markdown: Reservoirs created by large dams are frequently mistaken on maps for natural lakes.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q15. Answer: (1). The second paragraph’s claim is that independent, inclusive review was almost
entirely absent and that the decision-making system structurally suppressed certain kinds of feedback.
(1) directly contradicts that claim by asserting routine independent audits with displaced-community
representation and consistently high performance ratings.
Runner-up: (3) is tempting because it concerns dams. But it speaks to the present slowdown, not
to twentieth-century evaluation practice; it leaves the second paragraph’s claim intact (true but
irrelevant to the stem).
(2) is consistent with the author’s view. (4) is a side observation about cartography.

second paragraph?

(1) Independent post-construction audits of major twentieth-century dams routinely included
representatives of displaced communities and consistently rated those projects highly across
their full operating lives.

(2) Many large dams built in the mid-twentieth century are now approaching the end of their

expected service lives.

(3) Several governments have, in recent years, abandoned long-cherished hydroelectric projects in

the face of organised opposition.

(4) Reservoirs created by large dams are frequently mistaken on maps for natural lakes.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following best describes what is, according to the author, “genuinely
new” in the present moment?
option_a_markdown:
That the construction of major dams has decisively ended, after a long period of unchecked

expansion.
option_b_markdown:
That decision-makers can no longer treat the costs of new dams as a separate, quiet matter;

future projects must be justified to the people they will affect.
option_c_markdown:
That alternative energy sources have now made large dams entirely redundant in the global

energy mix.
option_d_markdown:
That ageing infrastructure has forced governments to acknowledge that the original cost-benefit

calculations were technically incorrect.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q16. Answer: (2). The closing paragraph distinguishes the slowdown (which the author does not
treat as decisive) from a genuine novelty: the costs can no longer be tucked away, and new projects
must be defended to those they affect.
Runner-up: (1) is tempting because the paragraph discusses a slowdown. The author explicitly says
it would be premature to read the slowdown as decisive (distorted emphasis).
(3) overstates the role of alternatives. (4) reduces the novelty to a technical correction.

Verbal Ability – Solutions
source_raw_block:
16. Which of the following best describes what is, according to the author, “genuinely
new” in the present moment?

(1) That the construction of major dams has decisively ended, after a long period of unchecked

expansion.

(2) That decision-makers can no longer treat the costs of new dams as a separate, quiet matter;

future projects must be justified to the people they will affect.

(3) That alternative energy sources have now made large dams entirely redundant in the global

energy mix.

(4) That ageing infrastructure has forced governments to acknowledge that the original cost-benefit

calculations were technically incorrect.

question_text_markdown:
The passage below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best
captures the essence of the passage.

The standard story about the spread of literacy treats it as the slow victory of clarity over confusion:
once people could read, they could think for themselves, evaluate evidence, and resist the spell of
rumour. The historical record is less flattering. Mass literacy in the nineteenth century coincided
not with the retreat of credulous belief but with its industrial expansion. Cheap newsprint amplified
sensational stories far more effectively than it amplified careful ones; rumours travelled faster, not
slower, once they could be set in type. What changed was not the gullibility of readers but the scale
at which their attention could be captured. Literacy did not so much immunise societies against
manipulation as create a far larger surface for it.
option_a_markdown:
Although literacy was historically expected to make populations more discerning, its actual
nineteenth-century effect was to expand the reach of sensational and unreliable information
rather than to curb it.
option_b_markdown:
Nineteenth-century newspapers were responsible for the spread of rumour, and any honest

history of literacy must therefore treat the press as a corrupting rather than civilising force.
option_c_markdown:
Literacy in the nineteenth century failed in its mission to make citizens more rational, because

publishers consistently prioritised commercial gain over public education.
option_d_markdown:
Mass literacy did not change the basic credulity of human beings; it merely allowed them to

encounter the same kinds of rumours in printed rather than oral form.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17. Answer: (1). The paragraph’s central movement is concession-and-reversal: literacy was
expected to immunise, but it expanded the surface for manipulation. (1) captures both expectation
and reversal.
Runner-up: (4) is tempting because it captures part of the story (credulity unchanged, form changed).
It misses the central scale-and-amplification point and quietly weakens the reversal (true but partial ).
(2) is too strong, framing the press as “corrupting.” (3) introduces an unsupported motive (commercial
gain).
source_raw_block:
17. The passage below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best
captures the essence of the passage.

The standard story about the spread of literacy treats it as the slow victory of clarity over confusion:
once people could read, they could think for themselves, evaluate evidence, and resist the spell of
rumour. The historical record is less flattering. Mass literacy in the nineteenth century coincided
not with the retreat of credulous belief but with its industrial expansion. Cheap newsprint amplified
sensational stories far more effectively than it amplified careful ones; rumours travelled faster, not
slower, once they could be set in type. What changed was not the gullibility of readers but the scale
at which their attention could be captured. Literacy did not so much immunise societies against
manipulation as create a far larger surface for it.

(1) Although literacy was historically expected to make populations more discerning, its actual
nineteenth-century effect was to expand the reach of sensational and unreliable information
rather than to curb it.

(2) Nineteenth-century newspapers were responsible for the spread of rumour, and any honest

history of literacy must therefore treat the press as a corrupting rather than civilising force.

(3) Literacy in the nineteenth century failed in its mission to make citizens more rational, because

publishers consistently prioritised commercial gain over public education.

(4) Mass literacy did not change the basic credulity of human beings; it merely allowed them to

encounter the same kinds of rumours in printed rather than oral form.

question_text_markdown:
The passage below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best
captures the essence of the passage.

Ecological restoration is often pictured as the return of a landscape to a previous, undisturbed condition.
Practitioners increasingly distrust this picture. The “previous condition” is usually a snapshot from
a particular human archive–an early survey, a colonial map, a settler’s memoir–and reflects the
moment at which that record happens to begin. Choosing such a baseline does not recover an objective
past; it elevates one human selection above others. A more candid framing accepts that restoration
is the deliberate construction of a future ecosystem, informed by the past but accountable to present
values and to constraints that the original landscape never faced.
option_a_markdown:
Ecological restoration cannot succeed because no objective baseline of an undisturbed landscape

can ever be reliably established from human records.
option_b_markdown:
The romantic ideal of restoring landscapes to their original state should be replaced by a more

honest practice that openly designs future ecosystems while drawing selectively on the past.
option_c_markdown:
Practitioners of ecological restoration have begun to distrust historical baselines because the

archives they rely on were produced under colonial conditions.
option_d_markdown:
Restoration projects are best evaluated by how faithfully they reproduce the conditions

documented in the earliest available record of the landscape.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18. Answer: (2). The paragraph argues that the romantic ideal of restoration to an original
state should be replaced by a candid practice of designing future ecosystems while drawing selectively
on the past.
It under-reports the
Runner-up: (3) is tempting because it captures the distrust of baselines.
constructive claim about designing the future and over-attributes the distrust to colonial archives
alone (too narrow ).
(1) is too strong (“cannot succeed”). (4) is the very position the paragraph rejects.

(1) Ecological restoration cannot succeed because no objective baseline of an undisturbed landscape

(2) The romantic ideal of restoring landscapes to their original state should be replaced by a more

question_text_markdown:
The passage below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best
captures the essence of the passage.

Economic models of consumer choice usually assume that preferences are stable and merely revealed
by the prices people pay. Behavioural research has complicated this assumption without overturning
it. People do exhibit recognisable patterns of valuation, but the order in which options are presented,
the default chosen for them, and the wording of the question all shift their stated preferences in
measurable ways. The honest conclusion is neither that preferences are illusory nor that the classical
picture is intact. It is that preferences exist, but in a more porous form: shaped, not invented, by the
architecture of the choice.
option_a_markdown:
Behavioural research has shown that consumer preferences are essentially constructed by the
choice environment and therefore cannot be treated as genuine inputs to economic modelling.
option_b_markdown:
The classical economic assumption of stable preferences has been definitively refuted by

experiments demonstrating that small changes in framing alter consumer choices.
option_c_markdown:
Consumer preferences are real but not insulated from context; the framing of choices modifies
rather than fabricates them, requiring economic models to be qualified rather than abandoned.
option_d_markdown:
Economic models should be replaced by behavioural accounts in which the architecture of the
choice, rather than any underlying preference, is the principal determinant of decisions.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19. Answer: (3). The paragraph’s qualified conclusion is that preferences are real but porous–
shaped, not invented, by choice architecture.
Runner-up: (1) is tempting because it correctly registers the behavioural finding. It overstates the
conclusion into “constructed,” which the passage explicitly rejects (change in degree of certainty).
(2) and (4) overstate in the same direction.
source_raw_block:

question_text_markdown:
Five jumbled sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5) related to a topic are given
below. Four of them can be put together to form a coherent paragraph. Identify the
odd sentence out and key in the number of that sentence as your answer. (TITA)
option_a_markdown:
Cities have long been described in metaphors of flow, with traffic, water and capital all imagined

as currents to be widened or unclogged.
option_b_markdown:
This vocabulary frames the urban question as one of removing obstacles, so that planning is

naturally cast as a labour of clearance.
option_c_markdown:
Within such a frame, neighbourhoods that resist demolition appear, almost by definition, as

blockages rather than as living arrangements with their own logic.
option_d_markdown:
Recent studies of commuter behaviour confirm that travel times in dense cities have risen faster

than population over the last two decades.
option_e_markdown:
The trouble is that the metaphor smuggles in a politics: what counts as a flow worth protecting,
and what counts as friction to be removed, has already been decided before any specific proposal
is examined.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20. Answer: 4. Sentences 1, 2, 3, 5 form a tight argument: cities are described in flow-metaphors
(1); the metaphor frames planning as clearance (2); within that frame, neighbourhoods become
“blockages” (3); the trouble is that the metaphor smuggles in a politics (5). Sentence 4 reports an
empirical finding about commute times–thematically related to cities but causally and argumentatively
disconnected from the metaphor-critique that organises the other four.
source_raw_block:

question_text_markdown:
Five jumbled sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5) related to a topic are given
below. Four of them can be put together to form a coherent paragraph. Identify the
odd sentence out and key in the number of that sentence as your answer. (TITA)
option_a_markdown:
The history of cartography is sometimes told as a steady improvement in accuracy, as if older

maps were simply less successful attempts at the same task.
option_b_markdown:
This telling obscures the fact that maps are made for purposes, and a map that fails by today’s

measure may have succeeded admirably on its own terms.
option_c_markdown:
A medieval map that places Jerusalem at the centre is not a botched piece of geography; it is a

faithful diagram of a particular cosmology.
option_d_markdown:
Modern satellite imagery has dramatically increased the resolution at which terrain can be

represented and analysed.
option_e_markdown:
To judge such a map by the standards of a Mercator projection is therefore not to detect its

errors but to misunderstand what it was for.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q21. Answer: 4. Sentences 1, 2, 3, 5 form a coherent argument that maps must be judged on their
own purposes: a steady-improvement narrative (1); a corrective principle (2); a medieval example
(3); the consequent judgement that Mercator-standards misread medieval maps (5). Sentence 4
reports a technological fact about satellite imagery–related to cartography but irrelevant to the
purposes-not-accuracy argument.
source_raw_block:

below. Four of them can be put together to form a coherent paragraph. Identify the
odd sentence out and key in the number of that sentence as your answer. (TITA)

maps were simply less successful attempts at the same task.

2. This telling obscures the fact that maps are made for purposes, and a map that fails by today’s

faithful diagram of a particular cosmology.

4. Modern satellite imagery has dramatically increased the resolution at which terrain can be

represented and analysed.

5. To judge such a map by the standards of a Mercator projection is therefore not to detect its

errors but to misunderstand what it was for.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4) below, when properly sequenced, form a
coherent paragraph. Identify the correct sequence and key in the 4-digit number as
your answer. (TITA)
option_a_markdown:
Yet the suspicion misreads what tradition does:

it is the equipment with which any later

questioning of it is conducted, not an obstacle to be cleared before thinking can begin.
option_b_markdown:
The Enlightenment habit of treating tradition as the opposite of reason has had a long and

influential afterlife in modern intellectual life.
option_c_markdown:
On this habit, a belief that is inherited is, by that very fact, suspect, and the proper task of
the thinker is to step outside the inheritance and judge it from a vantage that owes it nothing.
option_d_markdown:
Even the resolution to reason without tradition is itself a tradition, with a recognisable history

and a vocabulary that did not invent itself in the moment of its first use.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22. Answer: 2314.
Sentence 2 introduces the Enlightenment habit. Sentence 3 unpacks what that habit prescribes (“On

this habit, . . . ”). Sentence 1 turns against it (“Yet the suspicion misreads . . . ”). Sentence 4 closes
the conceptual movement (even the resolution to reason without tradition is itself a tradition). Other
orders break the concession-and-reversal arc: 2→1 prematurely overturns the habit before it has
been stated; opening with 1 lacks a referent for “the suspicion.”
source_raw_block:
22. The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4) below, when properly sequenced, form a
coherent paragraph. Identify the correct sequence and key in the 4-digit number as
your answer. (TITA)

1. Yet the suspicion misreads what tradition does:

it is the equipment with which any later

questioning of it is conducted, not an obstacle to be cleared before thinking can begin.

2. The Enlightenment habit of treating tradition as the opposite of reason has had a long and

influential afterlife in modern intellectual life.

3. On this habit, a belief that is inherited is, by that very fact, suspect, and the proper task of
the thinker is to step outside the inheritance and judge it from a vantage that owes it nothing.

4. Even the resolution to reason without tradition is itself a tradition, with a recognisable history

and a vocabulary that did not invent itself in the moment of its first use.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences (labelled 1, 2, 3, 4) below, when properly sequenced, form a
coherent paragraph. Identify the correct sequence and key in the 4-digit number as
your answer. (TITA)
option_a_markdown:
What this view overlooks is that intuition, in expert hands, is itself the residue of years of

disciplined practice, not a faculty opposed to reasoning.
option_b_markdown:
It is common in contemporary writing on decision-making to oppose careful, slow deliberation

to fast, intuitive judgement, with the implication that the former is more trustworthy.
option_c_markdown:
An experienced clinician’s snap diagnosis, then, is less a flight from analysis than analysis

compressed by repetition into something that feels immediate.
option_d_markdown:
The opposition is intuitive but misleading, because it treats intuition as if it were a separate,

primitive faculty rather than a layer of judgement built up by training.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23. Answer: 2413.
Sentence 2 introduces the contemporary opposition between deliberation and intuition. Sentence 4
diagnoses why the opposition is misleading. Sentence 1 develops the diagnosis (intuition as residue of
disciplined practice). Sentence 3 closes with the clinician example as the natural illustration of the
developed point. Beginning with 1 leaves “this view” unanchored; 2→1→4→3 misorders diagnosis
and unpacking.
source_raw_block:

coherent paragraph. Identify the correct sequence and key in the 4-digit number as
your answer. (TITA)

disciplined practice, not a faculty opposed to reasoning.

2. It is common in contemporary writing on decision-making to oppose careful, slow deliberation

to fast, intuitive judgement, with the implication that the former is more trustworthy.

3. An experienced clinician’s snap diagnosis, then, is less a flight from analysis than analysis

compressed by repetition into something that feels immediate.

4. The opposition is intuitive but misleading, because it treats intuition as if it were a separate,

primitive faculty rather than a layer of judgement built up by training.

question_text_markdown:
The paragraph below has four blanks marked [A], [B], [C], and [D]. The sentence in
italics has been removed from one of these positions. Choose the option that indicates
the most appropriate position from which the sentence has been removed.

Removed sentence: It is not the existence of the gap but the willingness to be candid
about it that distinguishes mature science from its caricature.

The popular picture of science as a steady accumulation of settled facts has always made working
scientists uncomfortable.
[A] In practice, what counts as established at any given moment is a
working consensus, constantly probed at its edges by results that do not quite fit. [B] Every active
field carries, alongside its textbook achievements, a quiet inventory of anomalies that nobody has
yet been able to explain away. [C] A discipline that pretended no such inventory existed would be
more comforting to outsiders and considerably less honest with itself. [D] The willingness to keep
the inventory open, rather than to tidy it away, is what makes further progress possible at all.

A

B

C

D
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24. Answer: 3 ([C]).
The removed sentence contrasts the existence of the gap with the willingness to be candid about it–a
contrast that requires the gap to have been mentioned and that prepares the contrast between candid
and comforting practice. After [B] the inventory of anomalies has just been introduced; [C] is the
point at which a candour-versus-comfort distinction is precisely needed, and the following sentence
about pretending no inventory exists naturally develops the candour–comfort contrast just stated.
Why other slots fail: [A] precedes the introduction of the inventory, so “the gap” has no referent;
[B] interrupts the move from working-consensus to the explicit naming of anomalies; [D] follows the
candour-versus-comfort contrast, so the sentence is no longer setting it up but redundantly restating
it.

24. The paragraph below has four blanks marked [A], [B], [C], and [D]. The sentence in
italics has been removed from one of these positions. Choose the option that indicates
the most appropriate position from which the sentence has been removed.

Removed sentence: It is not the existence of the gap but the willingness to be candid
about it that distinguishes mature science from its caricature.

The popular picture of science as a steady accumulation of settled facts has always made working
scientists uncomfortable.
[A] In practice, what counts as established at any given moment is a
working consensus, constantly probed at its edges by results that do not quite fit. [B] Every active
field carries, alongside its textbook achievements, a quiet inventory of anomalies that nobody has
yet been able to explain away. [C] A discipline that pretended no such inventory existed would be
more comforting to outsiders and considerably less honest with itself. [D] The willingness to keep
the inventory open, rather than to tidy it away, is what makes further progress possible at all.

A

B

C

D

passage_text_markdown:
To call a society’s relation to its past a matter of “memory” is already to make a quiet philo-
sophical choice. Memory, in the modern register, names a faculty that secures a private link
between a present self and an earlier self; what it stores is, by assumption, a chain of events that
did once unfold. The faculty is supposed to be answerable to a past that exists independently
of the act of remembering, and it is judged accurate or faulty against that standard. This view
feels self-evident only because it has been naturalised by centuries of secular literacy, in which
the writing down of dates and the keeping of records have come to define what counts as “what
really happened.”

Tribal communities, by contrast, are often described as having a defective or merely poetic
version of this faculty. Their songs telescope generations into a single afternoon; their genealogies
absorb foreign rivers and foreign animals into the family line; their dead speak, instruct, and
sometimes vote. Anthropologists, even sympathetic ones, have tended to treat these features
as ornaments around a residual core of “real” memory, as though under the embellishment one
would eventually find the same archive the modern subject keeps. The assumption, in other
words, is that tribal memory is modern memory plus distortion.

It is this assumption, rather than tribal practice, that deserves scrutiny. The point of a tribal
narrative is not to record a past that lies behind it but to constitute a present community by
enacting a continuity that the community needs in order to act. When a clan “remembers” an
ancestor who fought a river, the question of whether the encounter occurred in 1612 or 1812

 is not so much wrong as misplaced; the narrative is doing work for which calendrical accuracy
would be an irrelevance and, sometimes, a hindrance. The faculty at work is closer to what
we might call participation: the past is summoned in order to take part in the present, not
consulted as an external authority over it.

This is why the criterion of factual fidelity, when imported wholesale, produces such consistent
confusion. It is the wrong tool. One does not measure a prayer by its propositional content; nor
does one fault a song for failing to be an inventory. The deeper error, however, is to suppose that
the modern, archival relation to the past is itself simply the description of how memory works,
rather than one historically peculiar way of organising it. Once that supposition is loosened,
the tribal narrative ceases to look like a degraded version of modern memory and begins to look
like evidence that the relation between a community and what precedes it can be ordered along
axes the modern register does not register.

None of this requires us to romanticise. A society that “remembers” through participation can
also misremember, can also harden a useful fiction into a weapon. The point is only that the
standard by which it would be judged to misremember is not the archival one, and that to apply
the archival one is to ask a question to which the practice was never an answer.
question_text_markdown: The author refers to anthropologists who are “even sympathetic” primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: concede that scholarly opinion on tribal memory has steadily improved over time.
option_b_markdown:
observers.

suggest that the assumption being criticised is not confined to hostile or dismissive
option_c_markdown:
indicate that sympathetic scholarship has so far failed to gather adequate ethnographic

evidence.
option_d_markdown:
contrast modern scholarship with older colonial accounts that treated tribal memory as

mere distortion.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3 (RC1 — Author’s purpose in citing “even sympathetic” anthropologists)

Correct: (b). The qualifier “even sympathetic” is used to show that the assumption (tribal
memory = modern memory + distortion) survives in scholarship that is not hostile, i.e., the
problem is structural, not merely a matter of bias. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because the
contrast colonial-vs-modern is a familiar move; but the passage does not invoke colonial accounts
here. Distorted emphasis. (a) unsupported —no claim of steady improvement. (c) unsupported —
no claim about evidence adequacy.
source_raw_block:
Q3. The author refers to anthropologists who are “even sympathetic” primarily in order to:
(a) concede that scholarly opinion on tribal memory has steadily improved over time.
(b)
observers.

suggest that the assumption being criticised is not confined to hostile or dismissive

(c) indicate that sympathetic scholarship has so far failed to gather adequate ethnographic

evidence.

(d) contrast modern scholarship with older colonial accounts that treated tribal memory as

mere distortion.

question_text_markdown: Which one of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to draw?
option_a_markdown: Between a faculty that stores events and one that recovers them.
option_b_markdown:
Between memory understood as a record of an independent past and memory understood

as an act that constitutes a present community.
option_c_markdown: Between literate societies that value calendrical accuracy and oral societies that do not.
option_d_markdown: Between memory as a private function and memory as a collective one.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4 (RC1 — Distinction)

Correct: (b). The author’s distinction is between an archival faculty (memory as record of an
independent past) and a participatory one (memory as constitutive of the present community).
Runner-up: (d)—private vs. collective is a real distinction, and the passage gestures toward it,
but it is not the distinction the author is trying to draw ; true but not central. (a) too generic;
(c) reduces the distinction to a literate/oral contrast, which the passage explicitly resists.
source_raw_block:
Q4. Which one of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to draw?

(a) Between a faculty that stores events and one that recovers them.
(b) Between memory understood as a record of an independent past and memory understood

as an act that constitutes a present community.

(c) Between literate societies that value calendrical accuracy and oral societies that do not.
(d) Between memory as a private function and memory as a collective one.

passage_text_markdown:
To call a society’s relation to its past a matter of “memory” is already to make a quiet philo-
sophical choice. Memory, in the modern register, names a faculty that secures a private link
between a present self and an earlier self; what it stores is, by assumption, a chain of events that
did once unfold. The faculty is supposed to be answerable to a past that exists independently
of the act of remembering, and it is judged accurate or faulty against that standard. This view
feels self-evident only because it has been naturalised by centuries of secular literacy, in which
the writing down of dates and the keeping of records have come to define what counts as “what
really happened.”

Tribal communities, by contrast, are often described as having a defective or merely poetic
version of this faculty. Their songs telescope generations into a single afternoon; their genealogies
absorb foreign rivers and foreign animals into the family line; their dead speak, instruct, and
sometimes vote. Anthropologists, even sympathetic ones, have tended to treat these features
as ornaments around a residual core of “real” memory, as though under the embellishment one
would eventually find the same archive the modern subject keeps. The assumption, in other
words, is that tribal memory is modern memory plus distortion.

It is this assumption, rather than tribal practice, that deserves scrutiny. The point of a tribal
narrative is not to record a past that lies behind it but to constitute a present community by
enacting a continuity that the community needs in order to act. When a clan “remembers” an
ancestor who fought a river, the question of whether the encounter occurred in 1612 or 1812

 is not so much wrong as misplaced; the narrative is doing work for which calendrical accuracy
would be an irrelevance and, sometimes, a hindrance. The faculty at work is closer to what
we might call participation: the past is summoned in order to take part in the present, not
consulted as an external authority over it.

This is why the criterion of factual fidelity, when imported wholesale, produces such consistent
confusion. It is the wrong tool. One does not measure a prayer by its propositional content; nor
does one fault a song for failing to be an inventory. The deeper error, however, is to suppose that
the modern, archival relation to the past is itself simply the description of how memory works,
rather than one historically peculiar way of organising it. Once that supposition is loosened,
the tribal narrative ceases to look like a degraded version of modern memory and begins to look
like evidence that the relation between a community and what precedes it can be ordered along
axes the modern register does not register.

None of this requires us to romanticise. A society that “remembers” through participation can
also misremember, can also harden a useful fiction into a weapon. The point is only that the
standard by which it would be judged to misremember is not the archival one, and that to apply
the archival one is to ask a question to which the practice was never an answer.
question_text_markdown: The author would be least likely to endorse which of the following claims?
option_a_markdown:
A tribal narrative that absorbs foreign rivers into a family genealogy may nonetheless

be doing exactly what it is supposed to do.
option_b_markdown:
Modern archival memory and tribal participation are two historically situated ways of

relating to what precedes a community.
option_c_markdown:
Tribal narratives, once their idiom is properly understood, can be shown to encode the

same factual content as archival records, only in a different form.
option_d_markdown:
The fact that a tribal account is calendrically inaccurate is, by itself, not a serious

objection to it.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q5 (RC1 — Least likely to endorse)

Correct: (c). The author’s whole point is that translating tribal narratives into “the same
factual content. . . in a different form” is the wrong move: tribal narrative is not modern memory
in disguise. Runner-up: (b)—an attractive paraphrase of the author’s position; the author would
endorse it. Selecting (b) here would require treating the author’s relativising move as something
they reject—the opposite of the text. (a), (d) are directly endorsed by the passage.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most seriously undermine the author’s central
argument?
option_a_markdown:
Some tribal communities, on contact with literacy, switch rapidly to archival modes of

recording the past without apparent loss.
option_b_markdown:
Ethnographers have documented tribal practices in which elders are explicitly corrected
by other elders for getting dates and sequences wrong, and the community treats such corrections
 as binding.
option_c_markdown:
Modern archival societies sometimes harden useful fictions about their past into political

weapons, just as tribal societies do.
option_d_markdown:
The participatory mode of relating to the past can be found, in attenuated form, within

modern literate societies as well.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q6 (RC1 — Weaken)

Correct: (b). If communities themselves treat chronological correction as binding, then the
participatory account underestimates the role of factual fidelity from within the practice, un-
dercutting the author’s claim that the archival standard is simply external to it. Runner-up:
(a)—tempting: rapid switching to literacy looks like evidence that the modern mode is more
native than the author allows. But the passage allows that the two modes can coexist or succeed
each other historically; rapid adoption does not show that the practice being replaced was an
archival practice wit
