Q6. Which of the following, if true, would most seriously undermine the author’s central
argument?

(a) Some tribal communities, on contact with literacy, switch rapidly to archival modes of

recording the past without apparent loss.

(b) Ethnographers have documented tribal practices in which elders are explicitly corrected
by other elders for getting dates and sequences wrong, and the community treats such corrections
 as binding.

(c) Modern archival societies sometimes harden useful fictions about their past into political

weapons, just as tribal societies do.

(d) The participatory mode of relating to the past can be found, in attenuated form, within

modern literate societies as well.

passage_text_markdown:
Suppose, for a moment, that the most consequential moral decisions of a society—who is sen-
tenced, who is treated, who is admitted—were delegated to a system whose verdicts could be
derived from a small set of explicit principles. The appeal of such a prospect is not, in the first
instance, technological. It is the appeal of a judge who cannot be bribed, who does not tire,
and whose reasons can be inspected line by line. To many proponents of “machine ethics,” this
is the underlying promise: not that a machine will be more compassionate than a human, but
that it will be more answerable.

The route to such a system, the argument runs, is the route physics once took. Newton
compressed a tangle of celestial and terrestrial observations into three laws;
later theorists
compressed these further. Ethical theory, on this picture, awaits its own act of compression.
Utilitarianism, deontology and the rest are read as competing axiom systems, each generating
verdicts in particular cases; the project is to identify the system whose verdicts best survive
scrutiny, and to encode it. The expected payoff is twofold. First, judgement becomes auditable.
Second—and more quietly—the embarrassment of moral disagreement is reframed as a soluble
technical problem.

The analogy is seductive but selective. In physics, two theories that disagree about a prediction
can be brought before an experiment that, in principle, both sides accept as decisive. In ethics,
the analogue does not exist. When utilitarian and deontological verdicts diverge, no further
fact about the case settles which verdict is correct; the disagreement is, in part, about what
would even count as settling it. To suppose otherwise is not to apply the method of physics to
ethics; it is to import the appearance of that method while leaving its evidential substructure
behind.

A subtler worry is that the compression itself is a form of loss. Moral attention, as it is actually
exercised, attends to features of a case—a hesitation, a history, an unspoken obligation—that
resist statement as variables. A formal system can be designed only on features that can be
stated; whatever escapes statement is, for the system, absent. The result is not that the system
fails to capture morality but that it succeeds at capturing a sub-version of morality, and then is
taken to be morality. The danger is therefore not error in the ordinary sense. It is the gradual
substitution, in public life, of what is auditable for what is morally relevant.

None of this is an argument against machine ethics as such. There are domains—scheduling,
triage under known constraints, certain classes of administrative decision—where the gains in
consistency are real and the residual loss is modest. The argument is, rather, against the picture
in which machine ethics is the late-arriving successor of an old project, completing what physics
began. That picture flatters the project by misdescribing its difficulty. It treats the absence
of a decisive ethical experiment as a temporary embarrassment; in fact, it is the feature that
makes ethics what it is.

claim that ethical disagreement is “in principle irresoluble.” Change in degree of certainty /
overstatement.

question_text_markdown: The author’s attitude toward machine ethics is best described as:
option_a_markdown:
broadly hostile, on the grounds that delegating moral judgement to machines is intrin-

sically dangerous.
option_b_markdown:
cautiously favourable, holding that machine ethics is the natural completion of the

project physics began.

discriminating: receptive to limited applications, but opposed to the larger picture in

which machine ethics “completes” ethics.

ambivalent, citing both technical promise and unresolved disagreement as roughly equal

considerations.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q8 (RC2 — Author’s attitude)

Correct: (c). The closing paragraph explicitly allows applications where “gains in consistency
are real,” while rejecting the larger “completing what physics began” picture. Runner-up: (a)—
tempting because the bulk of the passage is critical; but “intrinsically dangerous” is stronger
than the author’s position, and ignores the qualifications in the final paragraph. Overstatement

ambivalence.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument in the fourth
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
Most everyday moral decisions in fact turn on a small set of features that can be stated

explicitly, with the unstatable residue making no systematic difference to outcomes.
option_b_markdown:
Many ordinary citizens, when asked, prefer machine-generated verdicts to human ones

in low-stakes administrative contexts.
option_c_markdown:
Even within physics, certain phenomena—turbulence, for example—resist compression

into closed-form principles.
option_d_markdown:
Formal ethical systems can be designed to flag uncertainty and refer hard cases to

human judgement.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q9 (RC2 — Weaken paragraph 4)

Correct: (a). The fourth paragraph’s argument is that formal systems necessarily lose features
that resist statement, and that this loss is morally consequential. If most decisions in fact turn
on a small set of statable features and the residue is systematically inconsequential, the author’s
loss-claim collapses. Runner-up: (c)—tempting because turbulence is a real case of in-physics
non-compressibility; but the author’s paragraph 4 argument is about ethics, not about the
physics analogy, which paragraph 3 already addressed. Answers a nearby but different question.

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, the large dam was less an engineering choice than a political
vocabulary. Nations as different as the United States, the Soviet Union, Egypt and India spoke
about their dams in the same idiom: of conquest, of awakening, of finally making the river do
useful work. The structure itself was secondary to the sentence it allowed a state to utter about
itself. A country that could pour that much concrete across that much water was, by the very
act, declaring its arrival.

It is tempting, in hindsight, to read this enthusiasm as straightforward error—an ecological
mistake corrected by later science. That reading is comforting but partial. The mistake, if
it was one, was not primarily about hydrology. The science of sediment, of fish migration, of
saline intrusion into deltas, was reasonably well understood even in the 1950s; what was new
was not the knowledge but the willingness to discount it. The dam project absorbed the costs
as the price of a larger settlement: a nation reconciled to its own ambition.

Here a distinction is worth making. To object to a particular dam on the grounds that its costs
 exceeded its benefits is one kind of argument. To object to the dam-building era on the grounds
that the cost–benefit framework was itself the wrong instrument is a different one. The first
treats the era as a series of bad calculations. The second treats it as a series of calculations from
which certain costs had been excluded in advance. The displaced cultivator, the silted estuary,
the village whose name vanishes from the new reservoir map—these did not appear as negative
entries in the ledger because they had not been admitted as entries at all.

It would be wrong, however, to present this exclusion as cynical. The planners did not, on
the whole, deceive themselves; they had a working theory under which the displaced were
temporary inconveniences to be compensated and the silted estuary was the affair of a different
ministry. The theory was coherent. It was also, in retrospect, the political form of the era:
the assumption that a sufficiently large public benefit licensed the unilateral redistribution of
private and ecological harm onto those least able to refuse it.

Critics of the dam-building era sometimes overstate the moral. The lesson is not that all large
infrastructure is suspect, nor that nature is to be left alone.
It is, more narrowly, that the
project of large infrastructure tends to recruit a particular kind of accounting—one in which
the harms most likely to be diffuse, slow, and borne by the least powerful are also the harms
least likely to be counted. Where such an accounting is the only accounting in the room, the
resulting decisions will reliably look prudent on the books and indefensible on the ground.

A serious successor to the dam-building era, then, is not a smaller dam. It is an accounting
that is harder to deceive.

option_b_markdown:
argue that the central failing of the dam-building era lay not in faulty calculation but

in the prior exclusion of certain costs from the calculation altogether.
option_c_markdown:
defend the dam-building era as a coherent expression of mid-century political aspiration,

while conceding its environmental costs.
option_d_markdown:
recommend smaller, more localised infrastructure projects as a corrective to the excesses

of large dams.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q11 (RC3 — Primary purpose)

Correct: (b). The author’s central argument is the second-paragraph turn: the failing was not
bad arithmetic but a prior exclusion of certain costs. Runner-up: (c)—tempting because the
author treats mid-century planners with restraint and grants them coherence; but the author’s
purpose is critical, not defensive. Confuses concession with conclusion. (a) reduces a normative
argument to documentation. (d) makes a prescriptive recommendation the author explicitly
resists (“a serious successor. . . is not a smaller dam”).
source_raw_block:
Q11. The author’s primary purpose in the passage is to:

(a) document the ecological harms that twentieth-century dam-building inflicted on rivers

and the communities depending on them.

(b) argue that the central failing of the dam-building era lay not in faulty calculation but

in the prior exclusion of certain costs from the calculation altogether.

(c) defend the dam-building era as a coherent expression of mid-century political aspiration,

while conceding its environmental costs.

(d) recommend smaller, more localised infrastructure projects as a corrective to the excesses

of large dams.

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, the large dam was less an engineering choice than a political
vocabulary. Nations as different as the United States, the Soviet Union, Egypt and India spoke
about their dams in the same idiom: of conquest, of awakening, of finally making the river do
useful work. The structure itself was secondary to the sentence it allowed a state to utter about
itself. A country that could pour that much concrete across that much water was, by the very
act, declaring its arrival.

It is tempting, in hindsight, to read this enthusiasm as straightforward error—an ecological
mistake corrected by later science. That reading is comforting but partial. The mistake, if
it was one, was not primarily about hydrology. The science of sediment, of fish migration, of
saline intrusion into deltas, was reasonably well understood even in the 1950s; what was new
was not the knowledge but the willingness to discount it. The dam project absorbed the costs
as the price of a larger settlement: a nation reconciled to its own ambition.

Here a distinction is worth making. To object to a particular dam on the grounds that its costs
 exceeded its benefits is one kind of argument. To object to the dam-building era on the grounds
that the cost–benefit framework was itself the wrong instrument is a different one. The first
treats the era as a series of bad calculations. The second treats it as a series of calculations from
which certain costs had been excluded in advance. The displaced cultivator, the silted estuary,
the village whose name vanishes from the new reservoir map—these did not appear as negative
entries in the ledger because they had not been admitted as entries at all.

It would be wrong, however, to present this exclusion as cynical. The planners did not, on
the whole, deceive themselves; they had a working theory under which the displaced were
temporary inconveniences to be compensated and the silted estuary was the affair of a different
ministry. The theory was coherent. It was also, in retrospect, the political form of the era:
the assumption that a sufficiently large public benefit licensed the unilateral redistribution of
private and ecological harm onto those least able to refuse it.

Critics of the dam-building era sometimes overstate the moral. The lesson is not that all large
infrastructure is suspect, nor that nature is to be left alone.
It is, more narrowly, that the
project of large infrastructure tends to recruit a particular kind of accounting—one in which
the harms most likely to be diffuse, slow, and borne by the least powerful are also the harms
least likely to be counted. Where such an accounting is the only accounting in the room, the
resulting decisions will reliably look prudent on the books and indefensible on the ground.

A serious successor to the dam-building era, then, is not a smaller dam. It is an accounting
that is harder to deceive.
question_text_markdown: The author refers to the dam as a “political vocabulary” primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: suggest that dams were always more rhetorical than functional.
option_b_markdown:
indicate that the appeal of large dams lay partly in what their construction allowed a

state to say about itself, independent of any particular engineering need.
option_c_markdown: criticise mid-century states for prioritising propaganda over development.
option_d_markdown:
draw a parallel between the language of dam-building and the language of military

conquest.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q12 (RC3 — “Political vocabulary”)

Correct: (b). The phrase is used to register that the structure mattered for what it allowed the
state to say, independent of functional need. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because the language
of “conquest” invites a military parallel; but the author uses the phrase to make a constitutive,
not a comparative, point. True but not central. (a) overstates (“always more rhetorical than
functional”); (c) makes a moral charge the author does not.

conquest.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not support the author’s
central claim?
option_a_markdown: The harms of dam-building tended to fall on those least able to refuse them.
option_b_markdown:
Mid-century planners did not, on the whole, deceive themselves about the theoretical

framework they were operating within.
option_c_markdown: The science of sediment and fish migration was reasonably well understood by the 1950s.
option_d_markdown: A serious successor to the dam-building era would be an accounting harder to deceive.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q13 (RC3 — True but not supporting the central claim)

Correct: (c). That the relevant science was already understood is true (it is in paragraph 2),
but it functions as background—the author’s claim is about the accounting, not about scientific
ignorance. Runner-up: (b)—a tempting choice because mid-century planners’ self-honesty is
also a concession; but it directly supports the author’s argument that the failure was structural
rather than self-deception. Confuses concession that supports the argument with one that is
incidental. (a) and (d) both directly support the central claim.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following would the author be most likely to agree with?
option_a_markdown:
Cost–benefit analysis is, in principle, an unsuitable tool for evaluating public infrastruc-

ture.
option_b_markdown:
A correctly performed cost–benefit analysis would have prevented most of the harms

associated with twentieth-century dams.
option_c_markdown:
Cost–benefit analyses of large infrastructure are characteristically shaped by which
harms have been admitted as costs in advance, and this prior admission, more than the arith-
metic, determines the verdict.
option_d_markdown:
Ecological harms are intrinsically more important than the economic benefits of infras-

tructure and should always be weighted accordingly.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q14 (RC3 — Author would agree)

Correct: (c). This restates the second-paragraph turn precisely: the verdict depends on which
harms are admitted as costs in advance. Runner-up: (a)—tempting because the author criticises
cost–benefit analysis as it was used; but the author’s complaint is about the admission of costs,
not about the framework’s intrinsic suitability. Scope shift from operational to principled. (b)
too strong (“would have prevented most”). (d) makes a prescriptive ranking the author does
not endorse.

 (a) Cost–benefit analysis is, in principle, an unsuitable tool for evaluating public infrastruc-

(b) A correctly performed cost–benefit analysis would have prevented most of the harms

associated with twentieth-century dams.

(c) Cost–benefit analyses of large infrastructure are characteristically shaped by which
harms have been admitted as costs in advance, and this prior admission, more than the arith-
metic, determines the verdict.

(d) Ecological harms are intrinsically more important than the economic benefits of infras-

tructure and should always be weighted accordingly.

question_text_markdown:
Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1 to 4, when arranged in
the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the four-digit sequence.
option_a_markdown:
Surveys nonetheless suggest that the share of the relevant population using “Latinx” in
self-description remains a small minority, well below the share that uses “Hispanic” or
“Latino.”
option_b_markdown:
The term “Latinx” emerged from activists who sought a self-designation that would not

impose the grammatical gender built into Spanish.
option_c_markdown:
Their objection was not that “Latino” was offensive in any direct sense but that its default-

masculine form silently absorbed everyone who did not fit a binary frame.
option_d_markdown:
Whether this gap reflects resistance to the term, unfamiliarity with it, or the slow tempo

at which any self-designation diffuses through a community remains contested.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17 (Para Jumble — Latinx)

Correct order: 2–3–1–4. Sentence 2 introduces the term and its origin. Sentence 3 elaborates
the activists’ specific objection. Sentence 1 introduces the contrasting fact (low adoption).
Sentence 4 closes by problematising the gap.
Why not 2–3–4–1: 4 (“Whether this gap reflects. . . ”) presupposes that the gap has already
been stated; the gap is stated in 1, so 1 must precede 4. 2 must open (defines the term); 3 must
follow 2 (“their objection” refers to the activists in 2).
source_raw_block:
Q17. Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1 to 4, when arranged in
the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the four-digit sequence.

1. Surveys nonetheless suggest that the share of the relevant population using “Latinx” in
self-description remains a small minority, well below the share that uses “Hispanic” or
“Latino.”

2. The term “Latinx” emerged from activists who sought a self-designation that would not

impose the grammatical gender built into Spanish.

3. Their objection was not that “Latino” was offensive in any direct sense but that its default-

masculine form silently absorbed everyone who did not fit a binary frame.

4. Whether this gap reflects resistance to the term, unfamiliarity with it, or the slow tempo

at which any self-designation diffuses through a community remains contested.

question_text_markdown:
Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1 to 5. Four of
them, in a particular order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not fit.
Enter the sentence number.
option_a_markdown:
Standard models of deep-sea chemistry have long treated the lightless seafloor as a place

where oxygen is consumed but not produced.
option_b_markdown:
Recent measurements at depths of around 4,000 metres in the Pacific, however, have de-

tected oxygen being produced in the absence of any photosynthesis.
 3. The source appears to be the polymetallic nodules that carpet parts of the abyssal floor,

whose surfaces seem to drive a slow electrochemical splitting of seawater.
option_c_markdown:
If the finding holds up under replication, it would complicate plans to mine these nodules,
since their removal would also remove a previously unsuspected source of oxygen for deep-
sea life.
option_d_markdown:
Phytoplankton near the ocean surface remain, by a large margin, the principal producers

of oceanic oxygen and the basis of most marine food webs.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18 (Odd Sentence Out — Deep-sea Oxygen)

Correct: 5. The coherent sequence is 1–2–3–4: standard view (1) → anomalous finding (2)
→ proposed mechanism (3) → practical implication (4). Sentence 5, while topically related to
oceanic oxygen, returns to the surface ecosystem and the conventional picture, and so steps
outside the paragraph’s specific argumentative movement about a surprising deep-sea source.
It is a different stage of argument (background reassurance rather than the anomalous finding’s
development).
source_raw_block:
Q18. Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1 to 5. Four of
them, in a particular order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not fit.
Enter the sentence number.

1. Standard models of deep-sea chemistry have long treated the lightless seafloor as a place

where oxygen is consumed but not produced.

2. Recent measurements at depths of around 4,000 metres in the Pacific, however, have de-

tected oxygen being produced in the absence of any photosynthesis.
 3. The source appears to be the polymetallic nodules that carpet parts of the abyssal floor,

whose surfaces seem to drive a slow electrochemical splitting of seawater.

4. If the finding holds up under replication, it would complicate plans to mine these nodules,
since their removal would also remove a previously unsuspected source of oxygen for deep-
sea life.

5. Phytoplankton near the ocean surface remain, by a large margin, the principal producers

of oceanic oxygen and the basis of most marine food webs.

question_text_markdown:
Sentence Placement. The paragraph below has four possible insertion points marked
[A], [B], [C] and [D]. Read the paragraph and the standalone sentence, and choose the insertion
point at which the sentence best fits.

Standalone sentence: “Productivity gains, once expected to feed through to broader living
standards, now primarily serve to enhance returns to wealth.”

Paragraph: Public discussion of inequality remains dominated by figures on income. [A] Yet, in
most advanced economies, the gap between high and median incomes has widened far less than
the gap between large and median holdings of wealth. [B] The reasons are partly demographic
and partly institutional: an ageing population accumulates assets, and the tax treatment of
capital has become more favourable relative to labour over recent decades.
[C] These shifts
have proved difficult to register in standard economic indicators, which were designed for an
economy in which the principal source of household prosperity was the wage. [D] If the indicator
continues to lag the phenomenon, policy will continue to address symptoms rather than causes.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19 (Sentence Placement — Inequality)

Correct: [B]. The sentence introduces a causal mechanism connecting widening wealth gaps
to where productivity gains now accrue. It fits naturally after the empirical observation (income
vs. wealth gap) and before the demographic/institutional reasons paragraph develops.
Why not [A]: too early—no claim about wealth has yet been set up.
Why not [C]: would interrupt the connected reasons (“demographic and institutional. . . ” →

14


“These shifts. . . ”).
Why not [D]: the paragraph’s closing already turns to indicators and policy; inserting a mech-
anism here

[C] These shifts

continues to lag the phenomenon, policy will continue to address symptoms rather than causes.

(a) [A]

(c) [C]
(d) [D]

question_text_markdown:
Para Summary. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the
option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Studies of a Dutch kindred in which several males exhibited episodic, severe aggression eventually
localised the responsible defect to a small region of the X chromosome. The discovery was widely
reported as the identification of a “gene for aggression,” a phrasing the original investigators
conspicuously avoided. Their reticence reflected a methodological point that the popular framing
overran: the affected males in this particular kindred shared a particular defect; from this it
did not follow that aggression in general had a single genetic source, or that other aggressive
individuals would carry the same defect, or that the defect, in a different developmental and social
setting, would produce the same behaviour. The finding was about a kindred. The headlines were
about a species.
option_a_markdown:
Aggression in humans cannot be reduced to a single genetic cause, and the Dutch study

showed this.
option_b_markdown:
The popular reporting of the Dutch study mistook a finding about one kindred for a
finding about aggression in general, and the investigators’ careful phrasing was meant to prevent
precisely this slippage.
option_c_markdown:
Genetic research on behaviour is routinely misreported, and the Dutch aggression study

is a notable example.
option_d_markdown:
The link between an X-chromosome defect and aggression holds only within the specific

developmental and social setting of the Dutch kindred.

Correct: (b). The paragraph’s whole point is the mismatch between what the study showed

(d) overstates by claiming the link holds only in the Dutch setting—the passage’s point is
methodological caution, not a settled scope claim. Change in degree of certainty. (a) restates
a true gen

passage_text_markdown:
The Indian Forest Act of 1878 was, in form, a statute about trees. In effect, it was a statute
about the relation between a centralising state and the populations that lived where the trees
stood. Drafted in the wake of a rapid expansion of railway construction, it converted vast
tracts of wooded land, much of it under customary common use, into property of the colonial
state, classified into “reserved,” “protected” and “village” categories. The categories were
administratively neat; on the ground they amounted to the same thing. The state acquired the
standing right to decide which trees could be felled, by whom, and on what terms.

Historians sometimes describe the 1878 Act as an instrument of timber extraction. This is true
but insufficient. Timber was the immediate occasion, but the more durable achievement of the
Act was the routinisation of a particular form of authority: a bureaucratic relation in which
the cultivator’s grazing route, the herbalist’s foraging path, and the cremation ground at the
edge of the village were all rendered legible—and therefore alterable—by a salaried officer who
had never met the people concerned. What was extracted, in the long run, was not principally
timber. It was discretion.

It is on this point that the 1982 draft, which sought to update the colonial framework, ran
into a controversy disproportionate to its overt provisions. The draft tightened restrictions and
increased penalties, but its substantive offence was structural: it preserved, almost intact, the
colonial assumption that forest dwellers stood in relation to the forest as trespassers on state
property unless specifically licensed otherwise. By 1982 a substantial political movement existed
that had begun to think of that assumption itself as the problem. The criticism, in other words,
was not that the 1982 draft was harsher than its predecessor, but that it was, in the relevant
sense, the same.

Defenders of the bureaucratic framework have often replied that without centralised control,
forest cover would collapse under local pressure. The reply has a real point. Decentralisation
administered carelessly has, in several regions, accelerated rather than slowed deforestation.
But this misses the question being asked. The question is not whether decentralised regimes are
uniformly successful—they are not—but whether the framework that treats local communities as
presumptive offenders against their own surroundings can ever produce sustained custodianship.
The empirical record suggests that it cannot, not because the framework lacks technical capacity
but because it lacks the participatory legitimacy without which technical capacity in this domain
corrodes.

A more honest reading of the 1982 episode, then, would treat it less as a failed updating
and more as the moment at which the legitimacy deficit of a century-old framework became
politically unignorable. What the controversy revealed was not chiefly that a particular draft
was poorly written but that the inherited template, regardless of who held the pen, could no

question_text_markdown:
The author’s statement that “what was extracted, in the long run, was not principally
timber. It was discretion” is best understood as:
option_a_markdown: a rhetorical exaggeration; the principal extraction was, in fact, timber.
option_b_markdown:
a claim that the lasting consequence of the 1878 Act was not the removal of forest
products but the relocation of routine decision-making about forests away from the people who
lived in them.
option_c_markdown:
a suggestion that the colonial state lacked the administrative capacity for sustained

timber extraction.
option_d_markdown: a complaint that bureaucratic officials made arbitrary decisions for personal gain.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22 (RC4 — “not principally timber. It was discretion”)

Correct: (b). The line claims that the durable consequence of the Act was the relocation
of routine decisions away from local actors. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because “discretion”
invites a connotation of arbitrariness; but the passage makes a structural, not a moral-character,
claim. Distorted emphasis. (a) misreads the line as hyperbole; (c) reverses the claim.

passage_text_markdown:
The Indian Forest Act of 1878 was, in form, a statute about trees. In effect, it was a statute
about the relation between a centralising state and the populations that lived where the trees
stood. Drafted in the wake of a rapid expansion of railway construction, it converted vast
tracts of wooded land, much of it under customary common use, into property of the colonial
state, classified into “reserved,” “protected” and “village” categories. The categories were
administratively neat; on the ground they amounted to the same thing. The state acquired the
standing right to decide which trees could be felled, by whom, and on what terms.

Historians sometimes describe the 1878 Act as an instrument of timber extraction. This is true
but insufficient. Timber was the immediate occasion, but the more durable achievement of the
Act was the routinisation of a particular form of authority: a bureaucratic relation in which
the cultivator’s grazing route, the herbalist’s foraging path, and the cremation ground at the
edge of the village were all rendered legible—and therefore alterable—by a salaried officer who
had never met the people concerned. What was extracted, in the long run, was not principally
timber. It was discretion.

It is on this point that the 1982 draft, which sought to update the colonial framework, ran
into a controversy disproportionate to its overt provisions. The draft tightened restrictions and
increased penalties, but its substantive offence was structural: it preserved, almost intact, the
colonial assumption that forest dwellers stood in relation to the forest as trespassers on state
property unless specifically licensed otherwise. By 1982 a substantial political movement existed
that had begun to think of that assumption itself as the problem. The criticism, in other words,
was not that the 1982 draft was harsher than its predecessor, but that it was, in the relevant
sense, the same.

Defenders of the bureaucratic framework have often replied that without centralised control,
forest cover would collapse under local pressure. The reply has a real point. Decentralisation
administered carelessly has, in several regions, accelerated rather than slowed deforestation.
But this misses the question being asked. The question is not whether decentralised regimes are
uniformly successful—they are not—but whether the framework that treats local communities as
presumptive offenders against their own surroundings can ever produce sustained custodianship.
The empirical record suggests that it cannot, not because the framework lacks technical capacity
but because it lacks the participatory legitimacy without which technical capacity in this domain
corrodes.

A more honest reading of the 1982 episode, then, would treat it less as a failed updating
and more as the moment at which the legitimacy deficit of a century-old framework became
politically unignorable. What the controversy revealed was not chiefly that a particular draft
was poorly written but that the inherited template, regardless of who held the pen, could no
longer be improved by amendment alone.
 Q21. Which of the following best captures the central claim of the passage?

(a) The 1878 Act was a colonial measure for timber extraction, while the 1982 draft sought,

unsuccessfully, to correct it.

(b) The deeper continuity between the 1878 Act and the 1982 draft lay in a shared structural
assumption about authority over forests, and it was this continuity, more than any specific
provision, that the 1982 controversy targeted.

(c) The 1982 draft failed because it imposed harsher penalties than the 1878 Act without

granting forest dwellers commensurate rights.

(d) Decentralised forest management would have prevented both colonial overreach and the

controversy of 1982.
question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument in the fourth
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
In several regions where decentralised regimes were instituted carelessly, deforestation

accelerated.
option_b_markdown:
Bureaucratic forest management is more efficient than community-based management

in producing audited annual yield figures.
option_c_markdown:
Forest cover in regions where local communities were granted long-term custodial rights
has, over recent decades, remained stable or improved relative to comparable regions under
centralised administration.
option_d_markdown:
Cases exist in which community-managed forests have suffered local overuse during

periods of acute economic stress.

(e) (Note: option not used—ignore.)

(Choose from (a) to (d).)
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23 (RC4 — Weaken paragraph 4)

Correct: (c). The fourth paragraph’s claim is that the framework that treats locals as pre-
sumptive offenders cannot produce sustained custodianship. Stable or improving forest cover un-
der long-term community custodianship is direct empirical pressure against that claim. Runner-
up: (a)—this is acknowledged by the author as a real point; it does not weaken the argument
the author is making. (b) is irrelevant (efficiency of audit is not the issue). (d) is what the
author already concedes (decentralisation is not uniformly successful).

Which of the following best describes the author’s view of the defenders of the bureau-
cratic framework discussed in the fourth paragraph?
option_a_markdown: They are wholly mistaken, since centralised control has consistently failed.
option_b_markdown:
They make a point that is partially valid but that addresses a question different from

the one the author considers central.
option_c_markdown:
They correctly identify the only realistic alternative to deforestation, even if their tone

is dismissive.
option_d_markdown:
They have been refuted by the empirical record, which shows that decentralisation

always succeeds.
 PART B:
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24 (RC4 — Defenders of the bureaucratic framework)

Correct: (b). The author writes that the defenders’ reply “has a real point” but “misses the
question being asked.” Runner-up: (a)—tempting because the author is ultimately critical; but
“wholly mistaken” overstates. Overstatement. (c) reverses by treating bureaucratic control as
the only realistic alternative, which the author resists. (d) attributes to the author a strong
empirical claim (“always succeeds”) the author does not make.

passage_text_markdown:
It has become almost a clich´e to say that memory is reconstructive rather than reproductive. The
clich´e, however, conceals a difficulty that its proponents rarely confront. To say that remembering
rebuilds the past out of present cues is not yet to say that the past as such ever exercises authority
over the present. Strictly considered, a memory-belief is a state of mind occurring now; nothing in
its phenomenology guarantees that anything corresponding to it ever occurred. One could, without
internal contradiction, imagine that the universe sprang into existence a moment ago, complete
with brains pre-loaded with vivid recollections of a wholly fictitious yesterday. The hypothesis is
absurd, but its absurdity is not a logical absurdity; it is the absurdity of a hypothesis we have no
use for.

What gives memory its grip, then, is not its inner certificate of authenticity—there is none—but
the role it plays in a wider economy of belief. We treat a memory as veridical when it coheres
with other memories, with the testimony of others, and with the physical record left behind by
events. A solitary recollection, unsupported by anything else and contradicted by everything else,
we eventually let go, however vivid it may feel. This is why the courtroom is not impressed by
certainty alone: confidence, on its own, secures nothing.

Some philosophers have concluded from this that memory is, at bottom, a form of inference, and
that the felt immediacy of recollection is an illusion painted over a hidden act of reasoning. This

view tidies things up too quickly. Inference, in the ordinary sense, is something we can interrupt
and review; we can hold our premises in suspension and ask whether the conclusion follows. With
memory, there is rarely anything to interrupt. We do not first survey present cues and then judge
that a certain past event must have occurred; we simply find ourselves convinced that it did. To
call this inference is to stretch the word until it covers any transition from a present state to a belief
about something absent—in which case the claim becomes trivially true and explains nothing.

A more careful position, and the one this essay defends, is that memory is neither a direct window
onto the past nor a disguised inference, but a habit of treating certain present states as testimonies.
The habit is fallible, of course, and sometimes spectacularly so. But its fallibility does not show
that it is a poor substitute for some better cognitive faculty we might have had. It shows only that
creatures like us have had to settle the question of the past with the materials available to creatures
like us. To complain that this settlement is not absolute is to complain that we are not a different
sort of being.
question_text_markdown: The author’s central claim is best captured by which of the following?
option_a_markdown:
Memory cannot be trusted because nothing in a recollection guarantees that the remembered

event ever took place.
option_b_markdown:
Memory should be understood as a fallible practice of treating present mental states as evidence

about the past, rather than as either direct access or hidden inference.
option_c_markdown:
Memory operates as a concealed form of inference whose felt immediacy disguises an underlying

act of reasoning from present cues.
option_d_markdown:
Memory acquires authority chiefly through coherence with other memories, with testimony,

and with the physical record left by past events.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Correct: (B). The author opens by acknowledging that memory is reconstructive but
argues that the deeper task is to characterise what kind of mental practice memory is. The fourth
paragraph names this directly: memory is “a habit of treating certain present states as testimonies,”
fallible but not a poor substitute for some better faculty. (B) restates this conceptually without
lifting wording.

Runner-up (D) is tempting because the second paragraph genuinely describes the role of coherence.
However, coherence is a ground for trusting a memory, not the author’s definition of what memory
itself is—the question asks for the central claim, not for a true supporting point. (D) is therefore
true but partial.

(A) overstates the author’s scepticism: the passage explicitly rejects the move from “no internal
certificate” to “no trust”—distorted emphasis into general distrust.

(C) is precisely the view the third paragraph rejects as “tidying things up too quickly”—author’s
view versus discussed view.

question_text_markdown:
The author refers to the hypothesis that “the universe sprang into existence a moment ago”
primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: argue that since such a scenario cannot be ruled out, no memory-belief can ever be justified.
option_b_markdown:
illustrate that the certainty we attach to memories does not, by itself, settle questions about

whether the remembered past occurred.
option_c_markdown:
ridicule sceptics who claim that the reliability of memory rests on something internal to the

act of remembering.
option_d_markdown:
show that the assumption of a real past is a practical commitment rather than a logically

demonstrable truth.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Correct: (B). The five-minute-old universe is introduced immediately after the claim that
“nothing in its phenomenology guarantees that anything corresponding to it ever occurred.” Its
role is therefore illustrative: it shows that the felt certainty of a memory cannot, by itself, settle
whether the remembered past occurred.

Runner-up (D) is the most tempting wrong answer. It is in fact a defensible reading of the wider
passage—the author does later treat the reality of the past as something we accept on practical
grounds. But the stem asks about the primary purpose of the specific reference, which is local:
to expose the gap between certainty and occurrence. (D) is true but not the question being
asked (it answers a nearby but different question).

(A) commits the causal/scope reversal the author warns against: he does not conclude from the
hypothesis that memory is unjustified.

(C) attributes a polemical aim (ridicule) absent from the calm, expository tone.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following best describes the function of the third paragraph in the passage?
option_a_markdown:
It introduces and endorses the view that memory is a concealed form of inference, building on

the second paragraph’s discussion of coherence.
option_b_markdown:
It concedes that memory resembles inference in some respects but rejects the assimilation as

either misleading or vacuous.
option_c_markdown:
It abandons the earlier discussion of coherence in order to relocate the problem of memory in

the philosophy of perception.
option_d_markdown:
It provides a transitional summary of the previous two paragraphs before introducing a new

line of argument in the fourth.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3. Correct: (B). The third paragraph reports the inferentialist view (“Some philosophers have
concluded. . . ”), credits it with tidiness, then rejects it because either inference loses its ordinary

meaning or memory does not fit. This is a paradigmatic concession followed by qualification.

Runner-up (D) is tempting because the paragraph does prepare the ground for paragraph 4. But
its dominant work is argumentative (against the inferentialist view), not transitional. (D) is too
narrow in function description.

(A) is the opposite of what the paragraph does—causal reversal.

(C) invents a topic-shift to “philosophy of perception” that the passage never makes—unsupported.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the position the author defends in the final
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
Studies show that confident eyewitnesses are frequently mistaken about the details of events

they claim to remember vividly.
option_b_markdown:
Across cultures, communities organise themselves in ways that constantly cross-check individual

recollections against shared records.
option_c_markdown:
When carefully prompted, ordinary subjects can pause mid-recollection, weigh present cues
against alternatives, and explicitly revise the resulting belief, behaving exactly as they do when
reasoning from evidence.
option_d_markdown:
Some recollections, especially of emotionally intense events, are accompanied by such over-
whelming vividness that subjects cannot bring themselves to doubt them even when shown
contradicting evidence.

Passage 2

For two centuries it has been a commonplace among economists that the division of labour increases
productive power. Adam Smith’s pin factory, in which ten men perform what one man alone could
scarcely begin, has done duty for so long as the standard illustration that few readers stop to
ask whether the moral of the story is what they have been told it is. The usual reading—that
specialisation makes us more productive, and that more productivity is unambiguously good—glides
over the fact that Smith himself, several hundred pages later, describes the very same workman as
reduced to “the stupidest and most ignorant” creature it is possible for a human being to become.
The pin factory, on a fuller reading, is not a celebration but a warning quietly tucked inside a
celebration.

Modern commentators tend to treat this tension as a quaint moralism Smith could not quite shake
off. They argue that the workman’s stunting is the price of an unprecedented expansion of wealth,
and that the expansion eventually returns to the workman in the form of cheaper goods, leisure,
and education that he could not otherwise have enjoyed. This may be true as a matter of historical
fact; certainly the descendants of pin-factory workers, in many places, now live lives their ancestors
could not have imagined. But to grant the historical claim is not to settle the philosophical one.
The question Smith raises is not whether the workman’s losses are eventually compensated, but
whether a society can describe itself as flourishing while a substantial portion of its members are
systematically deformed by the work that sustains it.

It is tempting to reply that the deformation is no longer with us; that modern work, with its
screens and meetings and abstract tasks, is mercifully different from the pin-maker’s eighteen daily
repetitions. The reply concedes more than it realises. It admits that the kind of work matters, not
merely the wealth it produces—which is precisely Smith’s point. And it raises a further question:
whether the new forms of narrowness, less visible because less physical, are nonetheless real. A life
spent answering email may not stunt the body in the way a life spent drawing wire did, but it does
not follow that nothing has been narrowed.

What the pin factory teaches, then, is not that specialisation is good or bad in itself, but that
productivity, taken on its own, is a poor measure of a working life. Smith knew this, and said so,
even as he sketched the engine of modern growth. The defenders of that engine have often quoted
the first half of his account and quietly forgotten the second.

Q5. Which of the following best captures the author’s central argument?
option_e_markdown:
Smith’s analysis of the pin factory is internally contradictory: he both praises specialisation

and laments its effects on workers, and the contradiction has never been resolved.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4. Correct: (C). The fourth paragraph defends the view that memory is not inference because
inference can be interrupted and reviewed, whereas memory “simply finds us convinced.” (C)
supplies precisely the empirical finding that would undermine this contrast: if subjects can in fact
pause, weigh, and revise, the structural distinction collapses.

Runner-up (A) is tempting because errors of confident memory are mentioned in the passage. But
the author has already conceded that memory is fallible—in fact, the final paragraph builds the
fallibility in. Showing more errors does not weaken a view that already includes them. (A) is
confuses concession with conclusion.

(B) actually strengthens the author’s framework, which depends on cross-checking—opposite.

(D) reinforces the contrast between memory and ordinary inference (overwhelming, uninterruptable
vividness)—opposite direction.

Passage 2 — The Pin Factory
source_raw_block:
Q4. Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the position the author defends in the final
paragraph?

(A) Studies show that confident eyewitnesses are frequently mistaken about the details of events

they claim to remember vividly.

(B) Across cultures, communities organise themselves in ways that constantly cross-check individual

recollections against shared records.

(C) When carefully prompted, ordinary subjects can pause mid-recollection, weigh present cues
against alternatives, and explicitly revise the resulting belief, behaving exactly as they do when
reasoning from evidence.

(D) Some recollections, especially of emotionally intense events, are accompanied by such over-
whelming vividness that subjects cannot bring themselves to doubt them even when shown
contradicting evidence.

Passage 2

For two centuries it has been a commonplace among economists that the division of labour increases
productive power. Adam Smith’s pin factory, in which ten men perform what one man alone could
scarcely begin, has done duty for so long as the standard illustration that few readers stop to
ask whether the moral of the story is what they have been told it is. The usual reading—that
specialisation makes us more productive, and that more productivity is unambiguously good—glides
over the fact that Smith himself, several hundred pages later, describes the very same workman as
reduced to “the stupidest and most ignorant” creature it is possible for a human being to become.
The pin factory, on a fuller reading, is not a celebration but a warning quietly tucked inside a
celebration.

Modern commentators tend to treat this tension as a quaint moralism Smith could not quite shake
off. They argue that the workman’s stunting is the price of an unprecedented expansion of wealth,
and that the expansion eventually returns to the workman in the form of cheaper goods, leisure,
and education that he could not otherwise have enjoyed. This may be true as a matter of historical
fact; certainly the descendants of pin-factory workers, in many places, now live lives their ancestors
could not have imagined. But to grant the historical claim is not to settle the philosophical one.
The question Smith raises is not whether the workman’s losses are eventually compensated, but
whether a society can describe itself as flourishing while a substantial portion of its members are
systematically deformed by the work that sustains it.

It is tempting to reply that the deformation is no longer with us; that modern work, with its
screens and meetings and abstract tasks, is mercifully different from the pin-maker’s eighteen daily
repetitions. The reply concedes more than it realises. It admits that the kind of work matters, not
merely the wealth it produces—which is precisely Smith’s point. And it raises a further question:
whether the new forms of narrowness, less visible because less physical, are nonetheless real. A life
spent answering email may not stunt the body in the way a life spent drawing wire did, but it does
not follow that nothing has been narrowed.

What the pin factory teaches, then, is not that specialisation is good or bad in itself, but that
productivity, taken on its own, is a poor measure of a working life. Smith knew this, and said so,
even as he sketched the engine of modern growth. The defenders of that engine have often quoted
the first half of his account and quietly forgotten the second.

Q5. Which of the following best captures the author’s central argument?

(A) Smith’s analysis of the pin factory is internally contradictory: he both praises specialisation

and laments its effects on workers, and the contradiction has never been resolved.

(B) Modern readings of Smith mistake a cautionary illustration for a straightforward endorsement,
ignoring his insistence that the quality of work, not merely its productive output, is what must
be evaluated.

(C) The historical gains produced by the division of labour are real and have eventually compensated

workers, even though early commentators failed to anticipate this outcome.

(D) Specialisation in modern economies produces narrowness of a less visible kind than in Smith’s

day, and this hidden narrowness undermines claims of social progress.

Q6. The author refers to the response that “modern work . . . is mercifully different from the
pin-maker’s eighteen daily repetitions” in order to:

(A) undermine that response by showing it inadvertently grants the author’s broader point.

(B) concede that the harshest forms of deformation described by Smith no longer apply to

contemporary economies.

(C) contrast physical labour with cognitive labour and argue that the latter is necessarily less

damaging.

(D) suggest that defenders of modern specialisation have learned from Smith’s warnings and

adjusted their position accordingly.

Q7. Which of the following would the author be least likely to endorse?

(A) An economy may achieve impressive growth while still failing, in important respects, to support

a flourishing human life.

(B) Adam Smith intended his account of the pin factory to be read as containing a critical as well

as a celebratory element.

(C) Wherever the eventual material gains of specialisation are large enough, the question of whether

work itself stunts the worker becomes a secondary concern.

(D) New forms of narrow, screen-bound work may have costs that are real even though they differ

in kind from those of eighteenth-century manual labour.

Q8. The author concedes that “the descendants of pin-factory workers, in many places, now live
lives their ancestors could not have imagined.” The function of this concession in the argument is
to:

(A) strengthen the modern commentators’ view that the historical record vindicates the division of

labour.

(B) acknowledge a strong empirical claim in order to deny that it answers the distinct philosophical

question the author is raising.

(C) prepare the reader for the author’s eventual rejection of the entire framework of historical

compensation.

(D) soften an otherwise harsh attack on modern industrial society by granting that some of its

results have been benign.

Passage 3

When ecologists first began to study the “subnivium”—the thin, dim world that opens up beneath
winter snow—they assumed they were describing a refuge. Small mammals, insects, and overwinter-
ing plants huddle in this insulated layer at a steady temperature near freezing while the air above
plunges far lower. The snowpack, by this account, is a roof; the subnivium, a room. Recent work
has complicated the picture in ways that are unwelcome to anyone who hoped that cold-region
ecosystems possessed at least one unambiguous form of resilience.

The complication is this. The room is not insulated by the roof so much as it is constituted by
it. Subnivial conditions depend on a snowpack of a particular depth, density, and duration; vary

any of these even modestly and the room ceases to exist as such. Shorter winters do not merely
give the inhabitants less of the same shelter; they expose them to a sequence of partial thaws and
refreezes that is, in many respects, more punishing than steady cold would have been. A vole that
has evolved to tolerate three months at minus one degree is not obviously better off when offered
six weeks at minus one degree interrupted by sudden episodes near zero.

It is at this point that an interesting kind of confusion enters the literature. Studies have shown
that some subnivial species can, under controlled conditions, tolerate temperatures lower than
those they normally experience. From this it is sometimes inferred that warming, by raising winter
temperatures, must be benign for them. The inference is faulty because it confuses what an
organism can survive in a laboratory test with what it can survive embedded in the rest of its
life-history. A vole that can withstand minus fifteen degrees for twenty-four hours in a chamber
may still be undone by repeated thaws that wet its fur, flood its tunnels, and force it onto the
surface where predators wait.

There is a temptation, having recognised all this, to swing to the opposite extreme and declare
the subnivium doomed. That conclusion outruns the evidence. What the evidence does support
is more modest and more disquieting. It is that a system whose stability depended on a single
environmental variable being roughly steady is unusually exposed when that variable becomes
erratic. The subnivium did not protect its inhabitants by being especially robust; it protected them
by being especially reliable. Reliability and robustness are different virtues, and the second cannot
be inferred from the first.

Q9. The author argues that earlier ecologists’ description of the subnivium as “a roof; the subnivium,
a room” is misleading chiefly because:

(A) the analogy underestimates how cold the air above the snowpack actually becomes during

winter.

(B) the conditions inside the subnivium do not survive any sustained reduction in the snowpack;

the “room” has no existence apart from the “roof.”

(C) the analogy implies a permanence that ecosystems in cold regions have, in fact, never possessed.

(D) the small mammals and insects that inhabit the subnivium are far more vulnerable than the

analogy suggests.

Q10. Which of the following best describes the “interesting kind of confusion” the author identifies
in the third paragraph?

(A) Treating laboratory tolerance under controlled stress as evidence of how organisms will fare

amid changes that affect their whole way of life.

(B) Inferring from a species’ present range of distribution that it must be capable of tolerating

much wider environmental conditions.

(C) Assuming that a species’ historical persistence in a habitat proves its resilience to any future

disturbance of that habitat.

(D) Conflating short-term physiological tolerance in one individual with the long-term evolutionary

tolerance of a population.

Q11. The author’s distinction between “reliability” and “robustness” is best understood as a
distinction between:

(A) a system that endures because it actively resists disturbance and one that endures because

disturbances rarely occur.

(B) ecological strategies favoured by short-lived species and those favoured by long-lived species.

(C) the laboratory behaviour of an organism and its behaviour in the wild.

(D) the protective effect of a thick snowpack and the protective effect of a thin but persistent one.

Q12. Which of the following statements is most consistent with the author’s overall position?

(A) The subnivium will almost certainly collapse as winters shorten, and its inhabitants will follow.

(B) Warming may, on balance, benefit subnivial species, since many can tolerate temperatures far

below those they normally experience.

(C) Subnivial communities are unusually exposed to climate variability because the protective

conditions on which they depended were narrow rather than strong.

(D) Recent studies have overstated the threat to the subnivium by treating short-term thaws as if

they were equivalent to sustained warming.

Passage 4

Photography, when it was new, was greeted with a peculiar mixture of enthusiasm and unease.
Painters worried about their livelihoods; moralists worried about the loss of imagination; some
philosophers, more interestingly, worried about what the new medium would do to memory. A
photograph, they noticed, did not merely record an event; it began, almost at once, to stand in
for the event in the minds of those who possessed the photograph. The recollection of a wedding
became, increasingly, the recollection of the wedding photographs. The image, intended as an aid,
was on its way to becoming a substitute.

It would be easy, and unfair, to dismiss this worry as nineteenth-century squeamishness about
a new technology. The worry returns, in a sharpened form, with every subsequent extension of
recording: the home movie, the camcorder, the smartphone. What the early critics noticed about
photography was a structural feature, not a peculiarity of silver salts. Whenever a medium offers an
external store of experience that is easier to consult than one’s own recollections, the recollections
themselves begin to atrophy. They are not erased; they are crowded out. The mind, sensibly, ceases
to maintain at full strength what it can offload onto the world.

Two replies are commonly made. The first is that the external store is more accurate than memory,
so its dominance is, on balance, a gain. The second is that nothing prevents the individual from
simply not consulting the photographs, and so preserving the original recollections intact. Both
replies are weaker than they appear. The first treats accuracy as the only virtue of memory, ignoring
the way unaided recollection participates in, rather than merely reports, the life of the rememberer.
The second imagines a level of self-discipline that the very existence of the technology makes harder
to sustain, since the temptation to consult is part of what owning a photograph means.

None of this amounts to an argument against photography, and the author of these lines has no
intention of putting one forward. The point is rather that the changes wrought by such technologies
are not exhausted by their stated purposes. A device offered as a way of remembering more may,
in another sense, leave us remembering less—less actively, less internally, less as a continuous part
of who we are. To notice this is not to demand that we put our cameras away. It is only to insist
that the bookkeeping be honest, and that gains in one column not be mistaken for the only entries
on the page.

Q13. Which of the following best captures the main point of the passage?

(A) Photography and its successors should be discouraged because they substitute external images

for genuine memory.

(B) Recording technologies, while not erasing memory, displace active internal recollection in ways

their stated purposes do not acknowledge.

(C) The early critics of photography correctly predicted that the medium would damage the

imagination and the moral life of its users.

(D) External stores of experience are more accurate than unaided recollection, but this gain comes

at the cost of personal continuity.

Q14. The author’s response to the reply that “nothing prevents the individual from simply not
consulting the photographs” is best understood as the claim that:

(A) such restraint is in principle possible but in practice rare among ordinary users of recording

technology.

(B) the option of not consulting the record is itself eroded by the conditions the technology creates,

so the reply assumes what it needs to defend.

(C) abstaining from the photographs would defeat the purpose for which they were made, rendering

the technology pointless.

(D) individual restraint, even when exercised, cannot undo the damage already done to collective

memory by the existence of the record.

Q15. The passage suggests that the author’s chief complaint against the common defences of
recording technology is that they:

(A) ignore historical evidence showing that memory has declined in measurable ways since the

invention of photography.

(B) rest on a narrow conception of what memory does, treating it as a mere repository rather than

as something integral to the life of the rememberer.

(C) overstate the accuracy of recording media and understate the reliability of ordinary recollection.

(D) confuse photography with later, more invasive technologies such as the smartphone, which

raise distinct problems.

Q16. Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not support the author’s
central claim?

(A) Recollections that have been displaced by photographs have been crowded out rather than

erased.

(B) The worry that recording media affect memory has recurred with each new generation of such

technologies.

(C) The author does not wish to argue that people should abandon their cameras.

(D) Devices marketed as aids to memory may, in another sense, leave their users remembering less.

Part B —

question_text_markdown:
Read the paragraph and choose the option that best captures its essence.

It is often said that translation is impossible, and almost as often said that translation
is indispensable; the two claims are usually delivered with equal confidence and rarely
placed beside each other. They do, however, belong together. The impossibility is not
the impossibility of carrying a meaning across, but of carrying it across without loss; the
indispensability is not the indispensability of perfect transfer, but of imperfect transfer
that nevertheless makes more available than it conceals. To speak of translation as

either impossible or indispensable, in isolation, is to mistake one half of the practice for
the whole.
option_a_markdown:
Translation is best understood as a paradox: it is simultaneously impossible to perform without

distortion and indispensable to cross-cultural communication.
option_b_markdown:
The common claims that translation is impossible and that it is indispensable describe

complementary aspects of a practice defined by inevitable, but useful, loss.
option_c_markdown:
Translation should not be judged by the standard of perfect transfer, since even imperfect

translation makes more available than it conceals.
option_d_markdown:
To call translation impossible is to overstate the case; in reality, it is indispensable, and its

imperfections are minor compared with its achievements.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17. Correct: (B). The paragraph insists that the impossibility-claim and indispensability-claim
“belong together” and that each is half of one practice characterised by useful, imperfect transfer.

Runner-up (A) is tempting but calls the relation a “paradox,” which the paragraph explicitly
refuses—it says the claims, properly understood, are not paradoxical but complementary. (A) is
distorted emphasis.

(C) captures only the indispensability half and drops the impossibility framing—true but partial.

(D) resolves the tension by dismissing one side, which the paragraph refuses to do.

question_text_markdown:
Read the paragraph and choose the option that best captures its essence.

A city is sometimes praised for being “walkable,” as though walkability were a property
residing in pavements, distances, and the absence of motor traffic. These things matter,
but they do not constitute walkability. A street can satisfy every measurable condition
for being walked along, and yet no one walks along it, because nothing in the street
invites the eye, rewards the lingering glance, or makes the next block feel worth reaching.
Walkability is not what the foot does; it is what the city offers the walker as a reason to
keep going.
option_a_markdown:
A walkable city is one in which the physical conditions for walking are supplemented by features

that give the walker an ongoing reason to continue.
option_b_markdown:
Walkability cannot be measured by pavements, distances, or freedom from traffic, since these

conditions are neither necessary nor sufficient for actual walking.
option_c_markdown:
Most assessments of walkability mistake the infrastructure of walking for the experience of

walking, and consequently misjudge the cities they evaluate.
option_d_markdown:
A city is walkable when its design makes the next stretch of street feel worth reaching, not

merely when it meets the measurable conditions for being walked along.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18. Correct: (D). The paragraph concludes: “Walkability is not what the foot does; it is what
the city offers the walker as a reason to keep going.” (D) captures both this central claim and the
dismissal of measurable conditions as sufficient.

Runner-up (A) is the most tempting wrong option. It is true that physical conditions matter; the
paragraph says they “do not constitute” walkability, but it does not deny their relevance. However,
(A) frames the additional features as supplements to a roughly adequate infrastructure, whereas
the paragraph reorients the very definition of walkability away from the foot toward what the city
offers. (A) is too narrow in conceptual shift (right in idea, wrong in emphasis).

(B) overstates the rejection: the paragraph does not say measurable conditions are neither necessary
nor sufficient—only that they are not constitutive.

(C) adds a sociological critique of evaluators not made in the paragraph.

question_text_markdown:
Read the paragraph and choose the option that best captures its essence.

The standard story about scientific revolutions has it that a new theory triumphs
because it explains what the old theory could not. This is true, but only in the way
that a story is true after one has decided how to tell it. At the moment of conflict, the
new theory typically explains some things the old one could not, fails to explain some
things the old one could, and stakes everything on a future that has not yet arrived. To
say afterwards that it triumphed because of its explanatory superiority is to read the
verdict into the trial.
option_a_markdown:
New scientific theories displace older ones not because of explanatory superiority but because

of social and rhetorical factors operating during the period of conflict.
option_b_markdown:
The familiar account of scientific revolutions is not so much false as retrospective: the explanatory

superiority it cites is visible only once the contest has been decided.
option_c_markdown:
Scientific revolutions are best described as wagers on a future state of knowledge, since at the

moment of conflict the contending theories typically have mixed records.
option_d_markdown:
At the point of conflict between theories, neither can claim explanatory superiority, and

historians are mistaken to suggest otherwise.

Para Jumble (Questions 20–21, TITA)
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19. Correct: (B). The author concedes that the standard story is “true,” but only retrospectively:
the explanatory verdict is read into a contest whose evidence was, at the time, mixed.

Runner-up (C) is tempting because the paragraph does describe theories as “staking everything
on a future that has not yet arrived.” But (C) characterises revolutions in general (as wagers),
whereas the paragraph’s actual point is about the historical narrative we construct afterwards. (C)
is true but partial—it captures the middle of the paragraph and misses the framing claim.

(A) smuggles in social/rhetorical factors the paragraph does not name—unsupported.

(D) overstates: the paragraph says the new theory does explain some things the old one could not,
so explanatory superiority is not flatly denied at the moment of conflict.

Para Jumble Solutions

question_text_markdown:
Four sentences labelled 1, 2, 3, 4 are given below. Arrange them in the correct logical order
and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.
option_a_markdown:
Calling such a process a discovery, as the standard textbooks do, makes it sound as though the

answer were lying there in the laboratory all along, waiting to be picked up.
option_b_markdown:
It is much closer to the truth to say that a question was constructed, slowly and against

resistance, until a fact could be made to count as its answer.
option_c_markdown:
In the everyday picture of scientific research, a difficulty is identified, an experiment is designed,

and a result is recorded, after which an answer is announced to the world.
option_d_markdown:
What the picture leaves out is that the difficulty itself is rarely available in advance; it is
shaped, refined, and sometimes invented in the very work that the experiment is meant to
settle.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20. Correct: 3142.

Sentence 3 sets up the everyday picture of research as a clean four-step sequence (difficulty →
experiment → result → announcement). It is the only sentence that frames a received view.

Sentence 1 criticises the resulting label (“calling such a process a discovery”), which presupposes
that the everyday picture has just been described. Note that 1 cannot open: “such a process”
demands an antecedent.

Sentence 4 identifies what the picture leaves out: that the difficulty is shaped in the very work.
This deepens 1’s complaint by locating the source of the misdescription.

Sentence 2 delivers the positive reformulation (“a question was constructed. . . until a fact could be
made to count as its answer”), serving as the conceptual conclusion.

Tempting alternatives: 3-2-4-1 is grammatically plausible but places the diagnosis (2) before the
analysis of what was left out (4), reversing the conceptual flow. 3-4-1-2 is also plausible at first, but
sentence 1’s “such a process” refers most naturally to the activity described in 3, not the omission
analysed in 4; placing 1 immediately after 3 makes the critique sharper, and 4-2 then forms a
structural pair (gap diagnosed → replacement offered).
source_raw_block:
Q20. Four sentences labelled 1, 2, 3, 4 are given below. Arrange them in the correct logical order
and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.

1. Calling such a process a discovery, as the standard textbooks do, makes it sound as though the

answer were lying there in the laboratory all along, waiting to be picked up.

2. It is much closer to the truth to say that a question was constructed, slowly and against

resistance, until a fact could be made to count as its answer.

3. In the everyday picture of scientific research, a difficulty is identified, an experiment is designed,

and a result is recorded, after which an answer is announced to the world.

4. What the picture leaves out is that the difficulty itself is rarely available in advance; it is
shaped, refined, and sometimes invented in the very work that the experiment is meant to
settle.

question_text_markdown:
Four sentences labelled 1, 2, 3, 4 are given below. Arrange them in the correct logical order
and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.
option_a_markdown:
The result is a politics that mistakes the noise of its own machinery for the voice of the people

it claims to represent.
option_b_markdown:
Yet the louder the channels grow, the harder it becomes to distinguish a widely held conviction

from a narrowly held one amplified by repetition.
option_c_markdown:
Democratic theorists have long held that the legitimacy of a government depends on its

responsiveness to what citizens, on reflection, actually want.
option_d_markdown:
Modern communication, with its talk-shows and trending topics, has been celebrated as the
perfection of this responsiveness, since it offers citizens unprecedented channels through which
to be heard.

Odd Sentence Out (Questions 22–23, TITA)
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q21. Correct: 3421.

Sentence 3 states the long-standing democratic principle (legitimacy depends on responsiveness to
citizens’ reflective wants). It alone offers the abstract premise on which the rest builds.

Sentence 4 introduces modern communication as the apparent perfection of that principle (“the
perfection of this responsiveness”). The phrase “this responsiveness” demands 3 immediately before.

Sentence 2 provides the structural twist (“Yet the louder the channels grow. . . ”), which presupposes
the celebration in 4 in order to qualify it.

Sentence 1 delivers the consequence (“The result is a politics that mistakes. . . ”), serving as a
conclusion only sentence 2 sets up.

Tempting alternatives: 4-3-2-1 misorders the principle and its supposed perfection: 4 names “this
responsiveness,” which has not yet been defined. 3-4-1-2 places the consequence (1) before the
qualification (2) that makes it intelligible.

Odd Sentence Out Solutions

Q22. Odd: 5.

Sentences 1–4 develop a single line of argument about gift exchange: 1 introduces the anthropological
observation that more than the object circulates; 2 specifies what circulates (something of the
giver, binding the recipient); 3 draws the consequence (the gift is never free; reciprocation is felt); 4
contrasts marketplaces, which work by stripping that surplus away. The four together move from
gift to its contrast.

Sentence 5 sounds related—it mentions commercial transactions, price, and even compares price
tags to ritual. But its conceptual role is incompatible:
it treats price as a structural analogue
of ritual in gift exchange, suggesting commerce and gift exchange share a deep structure. The
paragraph built by 1–4 argues the opposite—that the market works by stripping away what gift
exchange preserves. Sentence 5 thus belongs to a different argumentative thesis (commerce as
homologous to ritual), not merely a different topic. This is a structural—not thematic—oddness.

Q23. Odd: 4.

Sentences 1, 2, 3, 5 form a coherent argument about silent reading as a cultural arrangement
rather than a developmental endpoint: 1 sets up the older view; 2 corrects it; 3 generalises (when
supports weaken, reading reverts); 5 illustrates with everyday practice. All four move within the
same theoretical frame.

Sentence 4 stays inside the broad topic of reading and even mentions digital reading and comprehension—
features that make it look plausibly connected. But its claim concerns cognitive performance differ-
ences between media (comprehension scores in print versus digital), which is a different empirical
question from the cultural sustenance of solitary reading. It introduces a new dependent variable

(comprehension) and an inferential goal (medium effects) that the paragraph does not pursue. It is
thematically adjacent but logically incompatible with the line 1-2-3-5 develops.
