Q21. Four sentences labelled 1, 2, 3, 4 are given below. Arrange them in the correct logical order
and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.

1. The result is a politics that mistakes the noise of its own machinery for the voice of the people

it claims to represent.

2. Yet the louder the channels grow, the harder it becomes to distinguish a widely held conviction

from a narrowly held one amplified by repetition.

3. Democratic theorists have long held that the legitimacy of a government depends on its

responsiveness to what citizens, on reflection, actually want.

4. Modern communication, with its talk-shows and trending topics, has been celebrated as the
perfection of this responsiveness, since it offers citizens unprecedented channels through which
to be heard.

Odd Sentence Out (Questions 22–23, TITA)

question_text_markdown:
Five sentences labelled 1–5 are given below. Four of them, when arranged in some order,
form a coherent paragraph; one does not belong. Enter the number of the sentence that does not
belong.
option_a_markdown:
Anthropologists studying gift exchange have noted that what circulates between giver and

receiver is rarely the object alone.
option_b_markdown:
A gift carries something of the giver with it, and the recipient is bound, however lightly, by

what has been carried.
option_c_markdown:
This is why the apparently free gift is never quite free, and why the obligation to reciprocate,

though unspoken, is felt with such force.
option_d_markdown:
Marketplaces, by contrast, work precisely because they strip the object of any trace of its

previous owner, so that nothing of him follows it.
option_e_markdown:
In commercial transactions, the price tag plays a structural role analogous to that of ritual in

gift exchange, by setting a public limit to what is owed.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22. Odd: 5.

Sentences 1–4 develop a single line of argument about gift exchange: 1 introduces the anthropological
observation that more than the object circulates; 2 specifies what circulates (something of the
giver, binding the recipient); 3 draws the consequence (the gift is never free; reciprocation is felt); 4
contrasts marketplaces, which work by stripping that surplus away. The four together move from
gift to its contrast.

Sentence 5 sounds related—it mentions commercial transactions, price, and even compares price
tags to ritual. But its conceptual role is incompatible:
it treats price a

Sentence Placement (Question 24)

question_text_markdown:
The following paragraph has four insertion points marked [A], [B], [C], and [D]. Read the
paragraph and the sentence given below. Choose the insertion point at which the sentence best fits.

Cities have always lived on a tension between what they were and what their inhabitants
insisted they had become. [A] Maps, guidebooks, and official histories tend to settle this
tension prematurely, fixing on whichever version of the city the dominant institutions of
the moment find easiest to administer. [B] A neighbourhood that has been working-class
for two generations may still appear, on a city’s tourist map, as the artists’ quarter it
briefly was thirty years ago. [C] What is missing from such accounts is not detail but
movement: a recognition that the city is not a thing whose identity can be recorded but
a contest whose result must be continually renegotiated. [D]

Sentence to insert: The convenience of the fixed description, for those who must govern or sell
the place, almost always outweighs its fidelity.
option_a_markdown: at [A]
option_b_markdown: at [B]
option_c_markdown: at [C]
option_d_markdown:
at [D]

END OF QUESTION PAPER
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24. Correct: (B).

The paragraph builds a critique of fixed descriptions of cities. The inserted sentence—“The
convenience of the fixed description, for those who must govern or sell the place, almost always
outweighs its fidelity”—supplies the motive for the premature settling described just before, and it
must come before the working-class/artists’ quarter example, which depends on this motive having
been stated.

[A] is too early: at that point only the general tension has been named; there is no “fixed description”
yet to be “convenient.”

[B] is correct: it follows the introduction of fixed descriptions by maps, guidebooks, and official
histories, and supplies the explanation for their premature settling, after which the neighbourhood
example illustrates the point with full force.

[C] is the most tempting wrong placement: the inserted sentence does relate well to the neighbour-
hood example. But inserting it after the example deprives the example of its motivation and forces
the reader to re-read the example in light of a generalisation that should have preceded it. Logical
movement is broken.

[D] closes the paragraph with a generalisation that contradicts the final sentence’s reframing of the
city as a contest—it would dilute the closing claim with a return to the prior point.

passage_text_markdown:
The doctrine that memory furnishes direct knowledge of the past has had a long career in
philosophy, but it rests on a confusion that becomes visible only when one is willing to suspend the
habits of ordinary life. Whatever I now believe about yesterday is, considered as a psychological
event, something happening at this moment. The image I summon of a conversation, the
conviction that it occurred, the feeling that it was mine—each is a present state. From this
it follows that nothing in the act of remembering, taken by itself, certifies that the past it
appears to disclose ever existed. A world that came into being five minutes ago, complete with
populations whose nervous systems contained the very dispositions we call memories, would be
indistinguishable, from the inside, from the world we ordinarily take ourselves to inhabit.

This is not an argument that the past is unreal. It is an argument about the structure of
justification. We are accustomed to treating memory as evidence of two distinct things: that
something happened, and that we were present when it happened. The conventional view
runs both inferences together, as if the vividness of the recollection were itself a guarantee of
correspondence. Vividness, however, is a feature of the present image and not of the absent event;
the same brain mechanisms that produce accurate recall can produce confident invention, and
the subject finds nothing in introspection by which to tell them apart. A coherent epistemology
must therefore begin by separating the question of what memory feels like from the question of
what memory is evidence for.

if memory cannot certify the past, how does anyone manage to
A natural objection arises:
live? The answer is that we do not in fact rely on memory alone. The reliability we ascribe
to recollection is borrowed from a wider economy of cross-checks—testimony, written records,
physical traces, the convergence of independent reports. When these external supports vanish,
the confidence we place in any particular memory should diminish, even when the memory
itself feels no less vivid. What we ordinarily mistake for the authority of memory is in fact the
authority of the corroborating network in which memory is embedded.

The implication is mildly deflationary rather than skeptical. Memory remains indispensable as a
practical guide; it is simply not the foundation it has often been taken for. To insist that the
past is real is consistent with insisting that our access to it is mediated, partial, and dependent
on resources outside the remembering mind. The error of the traditional view is not that it
trusts memory too much, but that it locates that trust in the wrong place. Once the centre of
gravity is shifted from the inner episode to the surrounding web of confirmation, the appearance
of paradox dissolves, and what remains is a sober account of how creatures with present states
come to have warranted beliefs about absent ones.
question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the central argument of the passage?
option_a_markdown:
Because every memory is a present mental state, the past it claims to refer to may not exist,

and our ordinary confidence in memory is therefore misplaced.
option_b_markdown:
Memory should not be regarded as a self-sufficient source of knowledge about the past; its
warrant derives from an external network of corroboration into which it is integrated.
option_c_markdown:
Since accurate recall and confident invention feel identical from the inside, introspection

cannot, in principle, distinguish reliable memories from unreliable ones.
option_d_markdown:
The hypothesis that the world began five minutes ago, though logically unrefuted by memory,

is practically refuted by the convergence of independent records and testimony.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Answer: B. The passage opens with the observation that every memory is a present
mental state, but the final two paragraphs explicitly relocate the warrant of memory to “a
wider economy of cross-checks.” The author’s conclusion is that memory is not a foundation; its
authority is borrowed from a corroborating network.

Why the other options fail: (A) is the runner-up. It correctly registers the opening move but
stops there, treating the passage as scepticism about the past. This is true-but-partial : the
author explicitly says the implication is “mildly deflationary rather than skeptical” and that
“the past is real.” Stopping at (A) means missing the constructive shift in paragraphs three and
four. (C) captures the introspective point of paragraph two—local detail, not the central claim.
(D) reads the five-minutes hypothesis as something the author practically refutes; the author
uses it only to make a structural point about justification (confuses example with argument).

Q1.
Which of the following best captures the central argument of the passage?

(A) Because every memory is a present mental state, the past it claims to refer to may not exist,

and our ordinary confidence in memory is therefore misplaced.

(B) Memory should not be regarded as a self-sufficient source of knowledge about the past; its
warrant derives from an external network of corroboration into which it is integrated.

(C) Since accurate recall and confident invention feel identical from the inside, introspection

cannot, in principle, distinguish reliable memories from unreliable ones.

(D) The hypothesis that the world began five minutes ago, though logically unrefuted by memory,

is practically refuted by the convergence of independent records and testimony.

question_text_markdown:
The author refers to the hypothesis of a world that “sprang into being five minutes ago” primarily
in order to:
option_a_markdown:
Establish that the reality of the past cannot be proven, and that some form of scepticism

about history is therefore unavoidable.
option_b_markdown:
Illustrate that the felt qualities of remembering do not themselves carry information about

whether a past event occurred.
option_c_markdown:
Suggest that human nervous systems may contain dispositions whose origins are not what

their possessors take them to be.
option_d_markdown:
Argue that any account of memory must explain why such a hypothesis strikes ordinary

people as absurd.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Answer: B. The hypothesis is offered as a thought-experiment whose role is to separate
the felt vividness of memory from the existence of any past event. Sentence: “It is not logically
necessary . . . that the event remembered should have occurred.”

Why the other options fail: (A) is the runner-up. It reads the example as endorsing scepticism,
which the author later distances from (confuses concession with conclusion). (C) treats a passing
phrase (“nervous systems contained the very dispositions”) as the point of the example (distorted
emphasis). (D) is unsupported —the author does not undertake to explain why the hypothesis
strikes anyone as absurd.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s reasoning?
option_a_markdown:
Neuroscientific studies show that the brain regions activated during accurate recall differ
measurably from those activated during confabulation, even when the subject reports equal
vividness.
option_b_markdown:
Eyewitness testimony in legal settings has repeatedly been shown to be unreliable, with

confident witnesses often misidentifying suspects.
option_c_markdown:
Many cultures preserve elaborate oral histories whose accuracy has been confirmed centuries

later by archaeological evidence.
option_d_markdown:
Children develop the concept of a personal past gradually, only after acquiring language

and exposure to family narratives.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3. Answer: A. The author’s argument depends on the claim that introspection cannot
distinguish accurate recall from confident invention. If neuroimaging could reliably separate the
two states from the inside, the case for relocating warrant to an external network would lose
much of its force.

Why the other options fail: (B) strengthens, not weakens, by reinforcing that vividness does not
track accuracy. (C) is the runner-up: it shows memory can be accurate, which seems to push
against the author. But the author never denies that memory often is accurate; the claim is
about where its warrant resides. (C) is therefore true-but-irrelevant to the structural argument.
(D) is a developmental fact with no bearing on the epistemological thesis (unsupported ).

Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s reasoning?

(A) Neuroscientific studies show that the brain regions activated during accurate recall differ
measurably from those activated during confabulation, even when the subject reports equal

(B) Eyewitness testimony in legal settings has repeatedly been shown to be unreliable, with

(C) Many cultures preserve elaborate oral histories whose accuracy has been confirmed centuries

later by archaeological evidence.

(D) Children develop the concept of a personal past gradually, only after acquiring language

and exposure to family narratives.

question_text_markdown: The author would be least likely to endorse which of the following claims?
option_a_markdown:
A historian who relies on a single uncorroborated reminiscence is in a weaker epistemic

position than one who triangulates several independent records.
option_b_markdown:
The conviction that an event was personally witnessed is, by itself, an insufficient warrant

for asserting that the event took place.
option_c_markdown:
Because memory is part of a broader network of confirmation, the failure of that network in

a particular case should reduce the confidence attached to the memory.
option_d_markdown:
Since recollection is a present mental state, beliefs grounded in memory cannot in principle

be more justified than beliefs grounded in imagination.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4. Answer: D. The author distinguishes the practical reliability of memory from its
foundational status. (D) asserts a parity between memory and imagination that the author
explicitly resists in paragraph four: memory “remains indispensable as a practical guide.”

Why the other options fail: (A), (B), and (C) restate positions the author endorses—triangulation
strengthens warrant, vividness alone is insufficient, and confidence should track the network’s
success. (B) is the runner-up, since it is so close to the author’s view it may seem to be the
position he is rejecting; the question asks which he would least endorse, and he in fact endorses
(B). The trap is shifts from local context to universal claim: students may misread (B) as
overstating the author’s caution.

Solutions: Passage II (Machine Ethics)

Q4.
The author would be least likely to endorse which of the following claims?

(A) A historian who relies on a single uncorroborated reminiscence is in a weaker epistemic

position than one who triangulates several independent records.

(B) The conviction that an event was personally witnessed is, by itself, an insufficient warrant

for asserting that the event took place.

(C) Because memory is part of a broader network of confirmation, the failure of that network in

a particular case should reduce the confidence attached to the memory.

(D) Since recollection is a present mental state, beliefs grounded in memory cannot in principle

be more justified than beliefs grounded in imagination.

passage_text_markdown:
The hope that machines might one day be entrusted with moral decisions tends to follow a
familiar choreography. First, an engineer notes that human ethical judgement is inconsistent
and frequently biased. Next, a philosopher is recruited to translate a moral theory—usually
consequentialism, occasionally a sanitised Kantianism—into rules that can be checked by a
computer. Finally, the resulting system is presented as an improvement on human reasoning,
since it never tires, never panics, and treats like cases alike. The argument is seductive precisely
because each step seems modest. Taken together, however, the steps obscure a question the
project never quite addresses: what would it mean for a procedure to have the authority of an
ethical judgement at all?

Defenders of machine ethics often reply that this question rests on a romantic fiction. Human
beings, they observe, also apply procedures; we simply do so unreflectively. A doctor triaging
patients, a judge weighing precedents, a parent rationing attention among children—each is
running an algorithm whose inputs and weights happen to be opaque. If the procedures buried
in human practice can be made explicit, why should the explicit procedure be epistemically
inferior to the implicit one? The objection has force, and it is tempting to grant it provisionally.
Granting it, however, requires noticing what the analogy quietly drops. The doctor, the judge,
and the parent are answerable to the people their decisions affect; they can be asked for reasons,
and the reasons they offer are themselves open to revision. An automated system, by contrast,
produces outputs whose justification is exhausted by the rules that generated them. To demand
a further account is to be told, in effect, that the rules are the account.

This is not a complaint about transparency, which engineers can in principle improve. It is a
complaint about what ethical authority consists in. A judgement counts as ethical, on the view
the author wishes to defend, partly because it can be contested, qualified, and withdrawn in
light of considerations that the judging agent had not previously weighed. The very feature
that makes human ethical practice imperfect—its sensitivity to particulars that no prior rule
anticipated—is also what allows it to grow. A system that codifies ethics in advance forecloses
precisely the openness that ethical reasoning requires. It does not merely make mistakes; it
makes the wrong kind of decision.

It would be unfair to conclude that machines have no role in moral life. Where the task is genuinely
repetitive—flagging policy violations, allocating routine resources, surfacing inconsistencies in
human decisions—a well-designed system can usefully discipline human practice. The error lies
in treating such tools as substitutes for ethical reasoning rather than as scaffolding for it. To
delegate the easy cases is one thing; to delegate the authority to decide what counts as an easy
case is another. The distinction is fine, but it is the distinction on which the whole question of

machine ethics turns.
question_text_markdown:
Which of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to make in the final
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
Between machines that codify a single ethical theory and machines that combine several

theories.
option_b_markdown:
Between using automated tools to execute classifications already accepted as routine and

using them to determine which classifications count as routine.
option_c_markdown:
Between the engineering challenge of building transparent ethical systems and the philo-

sophical challenge of justifying their conclusions.
option_d_markdown:
Between the practical benefits of automation in repetitive tasks and the practical costs of

automation in novel ones.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q5. Answer: B. The final paragraph contrasts delegating easy cases with delegating the
authority to decide what counts as an easy case. This is a distinction between executing a settled
classification and producing the classification itself.

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up: it captures the practical contrast between
repetitive and novel tasks, which is true but locates the distinction at the wrong level (true-but-
partial ; misses the meta-level point about who decides). (A) is unsupported ; the passage never
compares theories. (C) is a real distinction but is not the one the author is drawing in the final
paragraph (answers a nearby but different question).

question_text_markdown:
The author’s treatment of the analogy between human and machine procedures (in the second
paragraph) is best described as:
option_a_markdown:
A dismissal of the analogy as resting on a category mistake about what algorithms can

represent.
option_b_markdown: An acceptance of the analogy that nevertheless identifies an asymmetry the analogy elides.
option_c_markdown:
A neutral exposition presented in order to contrast it with the author’s own preferred

procedural account.
option_d_markdown:
An endorsement that prepares the ground for the conclusion that explicit procedures are

epistemically superior.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q6. Answer: B. The passage explicitly grants that “the objection has force, and it is tempting
to grant it provisionally,” then identifies what the analogy “quietly drops”—the answerability of
human practitioners. This is acceptance with a structural caveat.

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up: students who skim the concessive opening

of paragraph two may read it as endorsement of procedural superiority (confuses concession
with conclusion). (A) overstates: there is no dismissal as category mistake. (C) misreads the
tone—the exposition is not neutral; the author has a stake.

(C) A neutral exposition presented in order to contrast it with the author’s own preferred

procedural account.

(D) An endorsement that prepares the ground for the conclusion that explicit procedures are

epistemically superior.

question_text_markdown:
According to the passage, which of the following best explains why an automated ethical system
“makes the wrong kind of decision”?
option_a_markdown:
It applies rules with a consistency that human beings cannot match, thereby producing

outcomes that strike observers as inhumane.
option_b_markdown:
Its outputs cannot be revised in response to considerations that fall outside the rules used

to generate them, even when those considerations are morally relevant.
option_c_markdown:
It optimises for measurable criteria such as efficiency and consistency, while ignoring criteria

such as compassion and fairness that resist quantification.
option_d_markdown:
It removes accountability from the institutional setting in which ethical decisions are

normally made, leaving affected parties without anyone to address.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q7. Answer: B. Paragraph three says: “a judgement counts as ethical . . . partly because it
can be contested, qualified, and withdrawn in light of considerations that the judging agent had
not previously weighed.” An automated system’s justification is “exhausted by the rules that
generated them.” The wrong kind of decision is one that forecloses this revisability.

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up: accountability is genuinely raised in paragraph
two, and one might think it is the core complaint. But the passage uses accountability as a route
to the deeper point about openness; the author’s final diagnosis in paragraph three is about
the structure of justification, not the institutional locus of complaint (true-but-partial, confuses
example with argument). (A) is the opposite of the author’s diagnosis. (C) is unsupported —the
passage does not invoke compassion or quantifiability.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following statements is supported by the passage but does not express its central
claim?
option_a_markdown: Human ethical reasoning is partly inconsistent and partly biased.
option_b_markdown:
Ethical judgements draw part of their authority from being open to contestation and

revision.
option_c_markdown:
Automating routine classifications is compatible with reserving harder judgements for human

agents.
option_d_markdown:
The argument for machine ethics typically proceeds through a sequence of individually

modest steps.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q8. Answer: A. The opening paragraph notes that human ethical judgement “is inconsistent
and frequently biased.” This is granted at the outset but is not what the passage argues; the
argument turns on what ethical authority consists in.

Why the other options fail: (B) is the central claim. (D) is the central claim. (C) is the central
claim’s coda. So they fail the “not the central claim” test. (B) is the runner-up, because
it sounds peripheral but is in fact the heart of the author’s positive view (author’s view vs.
discussed view ).

Solutions: Passage III (Large Dams)
source_raw_block:
Q8.
Which of the following statements is supported by the passage but does not express its central
claim?

(A) Human ethical reasoning is partly inconsistent and partly biased.

(B) Ethical judgements draw part of their authority from being open to contestation and

revision.

(C) Automating routine classifications is compatible with reserving harder judgements for human

agents.

(D) The argument for machine ethics typically proceeds through a sequence of individually

modest steps.

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, large dams were celebrated as monuments to a particular
idea of development: nature converted into kilowatts, floods regularised into irrigation, scattered
villages bound into national grids. The argument for a dam was, on the surface, an exercise
in cost-benefit accounting. Engineers tallied the megawatts to be generated, the hectares
to be irrigated, the floods to be averted. Against these gains they set the displacement of
communities, the loss of forest, the silting of estuaries. The arithmetic usually came out in
favour of construction, and the dam went up. What this style of reasoning concealed was that
the costs and the benefits were not borne by the same people, and that the difference between
bearer and beneficiary was not a contingent feature of the calculation but its hidden organising
principle.

The communities flooded by reservoirs were rarely those whose factories ran on the resulting
electricity, and the farmers whose lands were submerged were rarely those whose fields were
watered by the new canals. To call a region a “sacrifice zone” is not to describe a tragic accident
but to name an outcome that the calculation was designed to deliver: a zone whose sacrifice is
the precondition for benefits enjoyed elsewhere. The cost-benefit frame, by aggregating losers
and winners into a single ledger, makes this distributional structure invisible. It does not merely
fail to mention who pays; it ensures that the question cannot be raised within the terms of the
analysis.

It would be a mistake, however, to treat the issue as one of bad faith on the part of planners.
Even sympathetic administrators, working under genuine constraints of energy demand and
food security, found themselves bound by a calculus whose categories did not include the
things that mattered most to the dispossessed. The river as a sacred presence, the forest as
ancestral inheritance, the village as a network of obligation—none of these has a clear entry
in an engineering ledger. The problem is not that planners ignored such values but that the
framework offered no way to record them. To enter the ledger at all, a value had to be expressed
as a quantity comparable with kilowatts; otherwise it was, by definition, not a cost.

This suggests that the controversy over large dams cannot be resolved by improving the cost-
benefit calculation. Refinements in valuation may bring previously invisible costs into the ledger,
but they do so by translating those costs into the very currency whose adequacy is in question.
A genuinely different approach would treat the decision to dam a river as a political choice about
which forms of life will be sustained and which extinguished, and would acknowledge that no
aggregation can convert that choice into a technical one. The dam is not the wrong answer to a
well-posed question; it is the artefact of a question that was posed in a way that pre-empted its
own answer.
question_text_markdown: The passage as a whole is primarily concerned with:
option_a_markdown:
Documenting the human and ecological damage caused by large dams in the twentieth

century.
option_b_markdown:
Arguing that decisions about large dams should be made through political deliberation

rather than expert calculation.
option_c_markdown:
Showing that the standard analytical framework used to evaluate large dams systematically

obscures the distributional and qualitative dimensions of the choice.
option_d_markdown:
Defending the communities displaced by large dams against the charge that their objections

are merely sentimental.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q9. Answer: C. The passage moves from a description of cost-benefit accounting to a structural
critique: the framework aggregates winners and losers, cannot accommodate non-quantifiable
values, and pre-empts the political question. The whole argument is about what the framework
systematically obscures.

Why the other options fail: (B) is the runner-up: the author does gesture in paragraph four
toward a “political choice,” but the passage as a whole is a critique of a framework, not a
prescription for an alternative procedure (true-but-partial ; descriptive-vs-prescriptive shift). (A)
is too narrow —damage to communities is an illustration, not the thesis. (D) is unsupported ; the
author neither defends nor characterises objectors’ sentiments.
source_raw_block:
Q9.
The passage as a whole is primarily concerned with:

(A) Documenting the human and ecological damage caused by large dams in the twentieth

century.

(B) Arguing that decisions about large dams should be made through political deliberation

rather than expert calculation.

(C) Showing that the standard analytical framework used to evaluate large dams systematically

obscures the distributional and qualitative dimensions of the choice.

(D) Defending the communities displaced by large dams against the charge that their objections

are merely sentimental.

question_text_markdown:
The author refers to the river as a “sacred presence” and the forest as an “ancestral inheritance”
primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown:
Suggest that traditional ways of life possess a moral standing that ought to override the

demands of national development.
option_b_markdown:
Illustrate the kind of value that, by its nature, cannot be entered into a ledger whose entries

are quantities comparable with kilowatts.
option_c_markdown:
Indicate that the communities affected by dams hold beliefs that planners have historically

dismissed as pre-modern.
option_d_markdown:
Provide examples of cultural resources whose loss should be added to the cost side of the

cost-benefit calculation.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q10. Answer: B. The example appears in a paragraph whose explicit point is that some values
“had to be expressed as a quantity comparable with kilowatts; otherwise it was, by definition,
not a cost.” The river and the forest serve as instances of such non-quantifiable values.

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up:
it sounds reasonable—add them to the
ledger—but this is exactly what the author argues cannot be done without changing the values
into something else (confuses example with argument; misses the third paragraph’s point). (A) is
too strong—no moral override is claimed. (C) reads the passage as describing planners’ dismissive
attitudes, but the author explicitly says “the problem is not that planners ignored such values”
(causal reversal ).

question_text_markdown: Which of the following is most consistent with the author’s view?
option_a_markdown:
A more accurate cost-benefit analysis, one that monetised cultural and ecological losses,

would in most cases recommend against building large dams.
option_b_markdown:
Planners who proceeded with large dam projects acted in bad faith, suppressing values they

knew to be relevant to the affected communities.
option_c_markdown:
Whether to dam a river is a question about competing forms of life, and reframing it as a

calculation rules out the very considerations that ought to decide it.
option_d_markdown:
Large dams should be replaced by smaller decentralised projects whose distributional

consequences are easier to track.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q11. Answer: C. The fourth paragraph: “A genuinely different approach would treat the
decision to dam a river as a political choice about which forms of life will be sustained and
which extinguished, and would acknowledge that no aggregation can convert that choice into a
technical one.”

Why the other options fail: (A) is the runner-up:
it sounds sympathetic to the author and
seems to follow from his critique. But the author explicitly rejects the strategy of refining the
calculation: “Refinements in valuation . . . translate those costs into the very currency whose

adequacy is in question.” (A) therefore mistakes a position the author rejects for one he holds
(confuses concession with conclusion). (B) is too strong: the author rejects bad-faith readings
(“it would be a mistake . . . to treat the issue as one of bad faith”). (D) is unsupported —no such
alternative is proposed.
source_raw_block:
Q11.
Which of the following is most consistent with the author’s view?

(A) A more accurate cost-benefit analysis, one that monetised cultural and ecological losses,

would in most cases recommend against building large dams.

(B) Planners who proceeded with large dam projects acted in bad faith, suppressing values they

knew to be relevant to the affected communities.

(C) Whether to dam a river is a question about competing forms of life, and reframing it as a

calculation rules out the very considerations that ought to decide it.

(D) Large dams should be replaced by smaller decentralised projects whose distributional

consequences are easier to track.

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, large dams were celebrated as monuments to a particular
idea of development: nature converted into kilowatts, floods regularised into irrigation, scattered
villages bound into national grids. The argument for a dam was, on the surface, an exercise
in cost-benefit accounting. Engineers tallied the megawatts to be generated, the hectares
to be irrigated, the floods to be averted. Against these gains they set the displacement of
communities, the loss of forest, the silting of estuaries. The arithmetic usually came out in
favour of construction, and the dam went up. What this style of reasoning concealed was that
the costs and the benefits were not borne by the same people, and that the difference between
bearer and beneficiary was not a contingent feature of the calculation but its hidden organising
principle.

The communities flooded by reservoirs were rarely those whose factories ran on the resulting
electricity, and the farmers whose lands were submerged were rarely those whose fields were
watered by the new canals. To call a region a “sacrifice zone” is not to describe a tragic accident
but to name an outcome that the calculation was designed to deliver: a zone whose sacrifice is
the precondition for benefits enjoyed elsewhere. The cost-benefit frame, by aggregating losers
and winners into a single ledger, makes this distributional structure invisible. It does not merely
fail to mention who pays; it ensures that the question cannot be raised within the terms of the
analysis.

It would be a mistake, however, to treat the issue as one of bad faith on the part of planners.
Even sympathetic administrators, working under genuine constraints of energy demand and
food security, found themselves bound by a calculus whose categories did not include the
things that mattered most to the dispossessed. The river as a sacred presence, the forest as
ancestral inheritance, the village as a network of obligation—none of these has a clear entry
in an engineering ledger. The problem is not that planners ignored such values but that the
framework offered no way to record them. To enter the ledger at all, a value had to be expressed
as a quantity comparable with kilowatts; otherwise it was, by definition, not a cost.

This suggests that the controversy over large dams cannot be resolved by improving the cost-
benefit calculation. Refinements in valuation may bring previously invisible costs into the ledger,
but they do so by translating those costs into the very currency whose adequacy is in question.
A genuinely different approach would treat the decision to dam a river as a political choice about
which forms of life will be sustained and which extinguished, and would acknowledge that no
aggregation can convert that choice into a technical one. The dam is not the wrong answer to a
well-posed question; it is the artefact of a question that was posed in a way that pre-empted its
own answer.
question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the function of the third paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
It introduces a counter-example that complicates the author’s earlier critique of cost-benefit

reasoning.
option_b_markdown:
It blocks a particular line of objection by relocating the author’s complaint from the motives

of planners to the structure of their framework.
option_c_markdown:
It extends the central argument to a new class of cases not covered by the discussion in the

first two paragraphs.
option_d_markdown:
It concedes that some criticisms of large dam projects have rested on a misunderstanding of

how planners actually deliberate.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q12. Answer: B. The third paragraph anticipates the objection that the author is accusing
planners of malice. It removes that reading by relocating the critique from motives (bad faith)
to framework (categories that admit only kilowatt-comparable values).

Why the other options fail: (A) is the runner-up: the paragraph opens with “It would be a
mistake, however,” which can look like a counter-example. In fact it is a pre-emptive defence of
the author’s own argument, not a complication of it (confuses concession with conclusion). (C) is
too broad —no new class of cases is introduced. (D) is unsupported —no critic’s misunderstanding
is conceded.

Solutions: Passage IV (Cave Tetra)

passage_text_markdown:
The Mexican tetra is, in its surface form, an unremarkable silver fish that lives in the rivers of
northern Mexico and the southern United States. In several limestone caves cut into the same
drainage, however, populations of the same species have lost their eyes, lost their pigment, and
acquired a battery of compensating sensitivities. Their lateral lines—rows of pressure-sensitive
cells along the flank—are denser. Taste buds spread from the mouth onto the head. The brain
regions associated with vision have shrunk; those associated with olfaction and mechanoreception
have grown. Each cave population has arrived at a recognisably similar phenotype, but the
genetic routes by which they have done so often differ. The same loss has been achieved by
different means.

It is tempting to read this story as a straightforward demonstration of adaptation: vision is
useless in the dark, so selection erases it, while traits useful in the dark are amplified. The
interpretation is not wrong, but it is incomplete in a way that has begun to interest biologists.
Eyes are not simply absent in cave tetras; they begin to develop in the embryo and are then
actively dismantled. Pigment cells are produced and then suppressed. The body, in other words,
does not merely fail to build organs it no longer needs; it builds them and undoes the work.
Why an evolutionary process should retain the costly first half of a sequence whose second half
cancels it is a puzzle that simple stories of utility do not dissolve.

A more careful account begins with the recognition that traits are not free-standing units that
selection can pick off one at a time. The genes that pattern an eye also pattern parts of the jaw,
the lateral line, and the brain. A mutation that suppresses eye development may simultaneously
enlarge another structure, and what looks from the outside like the loss of a useless feature may
be, from the inside, the by-product of selection for something else entirely. The cave tetra’s
blindness is then not the goal of its evolutionary trajectory but a side-effect of changes whose
primary advantage lies in other organ systems. The fish is not adapted to the dark in the sense of
having been shaped for darkness; it is adapted in the sense of having been reshaped by pressures
that incidentally left it sightless.

This shift in emphasis matters beyond the tetra. It suggests that the vocabulary of “loss” and
“gain,” so natural in everyday descriptions of evolution, can mislead by implying that each trait
has been independently evaluated. Where developmental architectures link traits, selection acts
on bundles, and the resulting organism is best understood as a compromise among coupled
demands rather than as a portrait of its environment. To describe the cave tetra as having
“adapted to darkness” is shorthand; to take the shorthand literally is to mistake an effect of
structural coupling for evidence of a directed fit.
question_text_markdown: The author’s central purpose in the passage is to:
option_a_markdown:
Document the range of physiological changes observed in cave-dwelling populations of the

Mexican tetra.
option_b_markdown:
Argue that the apparent adaptation of cave tetras to darkness is better understood as a

side-effect of selection acting on linked developmental systems.
option_c_markdown:
Demonstrate that different genetic pathways can converge on the same phenotype, compli-

cating standard accounts of evolutionary parallelism.
option_d_markdown:
Show that the loss of vision in cave tetras is not the result of disuse but of an active

developmental process.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q13. Answer: B. Paragraph three’s pivot: “what looks from the outside like the loss of a
useless feature may be, from the inside, the by-product of selection for something else entirely.”
The whole passage builds toward this reframing.

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up: it captures a striking local observation from
paragraph two and one of the author’s premises, but it is a stepping stone to the broader
argument about structural coupling, not the argument itself (true-but-partial ). (A) is descriptive
only; the passage is argumentative (too narrow ). (C) picks up the opening paragraph’s note
about different genetic routes but is also subordinate to the central thesis (local detail, not global
argument).

question_text_markdown:
The author describes the embryonic formation and subsequent dismantling of eyes in cave tetras
primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown:
Provide evidence that simple appeals to the uselessness of vision in the dark cannot fully

explain the trajectory the trait has taken.
option_b_markdown:
Highlight an inefficiency in evolutionary processes that natural selection has been unable to

remove.
option_c_markdown:
Suggest that the same selective pressures can produce both gain and loss within a single

developmental sequence.
option_d_markdown:
Illustrate how vestigial structures persist when the cost of eliminating them outweighs the

cost of producing them.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q14. Answer: A. The passage uses the embryonic build-and-undo to puzzle the simple utility
story: “Why an evolutionary process should retain the costly first half of a sequence whose
second half cancels it is a puzzle that simple stories of utility do not dissolve.”

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up: it offers a competing explanation (vestigiality)
that may seem to fit. But the author does not invoke vestigial residue; he invokes developmental
coupling (answers a nearby but different question). (B) reads “inefficiency” into the author’s
framing—the author does not call selection inefficient (unsupported ; distorted emphasis). (C) is
distorted emphasis: the point is that the same sequence shows both, but it is offered as evidence
against simple utility, not as a general claim about selection.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author’s argument?
option_a_markdown:
In several cave tetra populations, the same genes that suppress eye development have been

shown to enhance the growth of taste buds and lateral-line cells.
option_b_markdown:
Surface tetras introduced into cave environments lose visual function within a few genera-

tions.
option_c_markdown:
Cave tetras raised in lit aquaria continue to develop and then resorb their eyes in the

embryonic stage.
option_d_markdown:
Different cave populations of tetras have arrived at similar phenotypes through different

mutations.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q15. Answer: A. Linked pleiotropic effects—one mutation suppressing eyes while enhancing
taste buds and lateral lines—are precisely the developmental-coupling mechanism the author
posits. Direct evidence for the mechanism strengthens the argument most.

Why the other options fail: (C) is the runner-up: it shows that eye resorption is internally driven,
which sounds supportive. But it speaks only to the embryonic puzzle of paragraph two, not
to the coupling thesis of paragraph three (true-but-partial ). (B) is consistent with both the
simple-utility story and the coupling story, so it does not preferentially strengthen the author
(neutral evidence). (D) is a fact about parallelism already in the passage; it does not add support
for the coupling claim (redundant; unsupported additional inference).

question_text_markdown:
The author’s attitude toward the description of the cave tetra as “adapted to darkness” is best
described as:
option_a_markdown: Approving, since the phrase usefully summarises a complex set of changes.
option_b_markdown:
Tolerant of the phrase as a convenient abbreviation, but wary of inferences drawn from

taking it at face value.
option_c_markdown: Dismissive, on the grounds that the phrase mistakes a side-effect for an adaptation.
option_d_markdown:
Neutral, since the passage is concerned with developmental mechanisms rather than with

how the result is described.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q16. Answer: B. The closing paragraph: “To describe the cave tetra as having ‘adapted
to darkness’ is shorthand; to take the shorthand literally is to mistake an effect of structural
coupling for evidence of a directed fit.” The shorthand is tolerated as a convenience; literal
inference from it is resisted.

Why the other options fail: (C) is the runner-up: “dismissive” captures the author’s resistance
to literal readings but overstates his attitude to the phrase itself, which he uses without scare

quotes earlier in the passage (too strong; distorted emphasis). (A) misses the resistance. (D)
ignores the explicit metacommentary in the final paragraph (unsupported ).

Solutions: Verbal Ability

Q17. (Para Summary) Answer: A. The paragraph’s argument has three movements: (i)
heritage protection is real; (ii) it works by regulating the unauthorised acts that produced the
neighbourhood; (iii) the result is preservation that alters what is preserved. (A) integrates all
three and includes the qualification (“no reason to abandon . . . but a reason to be modest”).

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up: it captures the central contradiction but drops
the author’s measured conclusion that protection is still worthwhile (true-but-partial ; misses the
qualification). (B) overstates: the author does not claim regulation should be abandoned. (C)
reads the passage as advising against designation, which the author explicitly resists (too strong;
descriptive-vs-prescriptive shift).

Q18. (Para Summary) Answer: A. Two strands: (i) LLMs are not more inaccurate than
humans; (ii) what is missing is the register of cues by which readers calibrate trust. (A) carries
both and notes the shift in the labour of judgement.

it captures the diagnosis but misframes
Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up:
the conclusion as being about accountability structures, a related but stronger claim than the
paragraph makes (slightly overstated ; too broad ). (B) reduces the paragraph to a comment
on the verification recommendation, missing the structural diagnosis (true-but-partial ). (C)
introduces a claim about the absence of an identifiable author—true in spirit, but specifically
the paragraph emphasises the absence of cues, of which an identifiable author is only one (too
narrow ).

Q19. (Para Summary) Answer: B. The argument is that the disappointment is not a
failure of the camera but a misplaced expectation: a still record of a surface cannot preserve the
multisensory experience whose loss readers attribute to a defect.

it captures the second half (memory has
Why the other options fail: (C) is the runner-up:
elements the photo cannot capture) but omits the corrective move about expectation (true-but-
partial ). (A) introduces a prescriptive recommendation (use written records) absent from the
paragraph (descriptive-vs-prescriptive shift). (D) repeats the technical observation but misses
the explanatory shift about expectation (distorted emphasis).

Q20. (Odd Sentence Out) Answer: 3. Sentences 1, 2, 4, and 5 form a continuous account:
the historical relation between reading aloud and silent reading, the consolidation of the silent
mode, the gains and losses that followed, and the closing reflection that the transition was not
unambiguous progress. Sentence 3 introduces an unrelated empirical finding about vocabulary
acquisition in children read to in early childhood—a topic in developmental psychology, not in
the history of reading. Although it shares the keyword “reading,” it does not fit the paragraph’s
historical-analytic movement (related-but-non-integrable claim).

Q21. (Odd Sentence Out) Answer: 4. Sentences 1, 2, 3, and 5 build a scientific narrative:
the standard picture of oxygen production by photosynthesis (1), an anomalous observation in
the deep sea (2), a candidate mechanism involving polymetallic nodules (3), and the implication
for biological assumptions (5). Sentence 4 shifts from the scientific story to an industrial-
policy observation about seabed mining—thematically adjacent (it concerns the same nodules)
but introducing a different stage of argument (economic exploitation rather than scientific
explanation). The oddness is one of stage of argument and causal direction, not of topic.

Q22. (Para Jumble) Answer: 1423.

Order: 1 → 4 → 2 → 3.

Conceptual sequence: (1) introduces photography’s original role in elections as an aid to recogni-
tion. (4) registers the shift: as the technology spread, the image came to carry suggestions of
character and policy beyond mere identification. (2) gives the consequence: the image displaces
argument. (3) closes by stepping back to a reflection on the medium, refusing nostalgia.

Why tempting alternatives fail: 1→2→4→3 looks grammatically possible because (2) reads as
a continuation of (1), but it skips the causal step in (4) (“as the technology cheapened”) that
explains how the substitution arose; (2) cannot directly follow (1) without the explanation of
how recognition turned into substitution. 4→1→2→3 is blocked because (4) presupposes the
earlier role of photography that (1) introduces. (3) must close because it is meta-commentary
on the whole shift.

Q23. (Para Jumble) Answer: 1243.

Order: 1 → 2 → 4 → 3.

Conceptual sequence: (1) introduces the term Latinx and its academic uptake. (2) presents the
counter-finding from surveys. (4) gives the defenders’ reply, treating resistance as unfamiliarity.
(3) concludes with the author’s reflection: the gap between adoption and recognition suggests
channels insulated from the named communities.

Why tempting alternatives fail: 1→4→2→3 looks possible because (4) is a reply, but a reply
presupposes the survey finding that (2) introduces, so (4) cannot precede (2). 1→2→3→4 is
blocked because (3) is the closing reflection, not the prompt for (4); placing (3) before (4) makes
the paragraph end with the defenders’ rejoinder, undercutting the author’s framing.

Q24. (Sentence Placement) Answer: B.

The standalone sentence describes how productivity gains accumulate disproportionately to
existing asset-holders and the distribution stretches even as the average improves. This is the
mechanism that explains how the same growth can lift output while widening the capital share,
which is exactly the claim made in the sentence ending at [B]. Placing the standalone at [B]
supplies the causal mechanism between the general statement (capital productivity rising faster)
and the policy discussion that follows (“the standard prescription has been to redistribute”).

Why the other blanks fail: [A] is too early: the sentence at [A] still describes the compressive case in
which capital earns “roughly what it contributes”—the standalone’s account of disproportionate
accumulation contradicts that case rather than amplifying it. [C] is tempting because it sits
before the policy turn, but inserting it there breaks the flow from the technological-change claim
to the standard prescription; the standalone is the bridge that explains why redistribution was
prescribed, so it belongs before the prescription, not between the prescription and its alternative.
[D] is too late: by [D], the paragraph has already moved to a meta-policy framing about baking
versus slicing the pie; the mechanism sentence has nothing to add at that level.

The runner-up is [C]: students may place the sentence just before the standard prescription,
reading the standalone as setting up that prescription. The decisive consideration is that the
standalone is a mechanism, not a policy prompt; mechanism precedes the consequence statement
at [B], and only after that consequence is on the table does the prescription at [C] make sense.

Q16.
The author’s attitude toward the description of the cave tetra as “adapted to darkness” is best
described as:

(A) Approving, since the phrase usefully summarises a complex set of changes.

(B) Tolerant of the phrase as a convenient abbreviation, but wary of inferences drawn from

taking it at face value.

(C) Dismissive, on the grounds that the phrase mistakes a side-effect for an adaptation.

(D) Neutral, since the passage is concerned with developmental mechanisms rather than with

how the result is described.

question_text_markdown:
The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the passage.

When a city protects an old neighbourhood by declaring it heritage, it freezes the very processes
through which neighbourhoods have always been made. The lanes that residents value were not
designed but accumulated: a workshop here, a shrine there, a balcony pushed out illegally over
a courtyard. To preserve such a place is to convert a record of small unauthorised acts into a
regulated tableau in which no further small unauthorised act is permitted. The protection is real,
but it changes the character of what is protected; the neighbourhood survives as an object of

policy precisely by ceasing to be the kind of thing it once was. This is not a reason to abandon
heritage designation, but it is a reason to be modest about what such designation can accomplish.
option_a_markdown:
Heritage protection saves old neighbourhoods from destruction, but at the cost of regulating
the small everyday improvisations through which residents once shaped them, so the
protection alters the very thing it preserves.
option_b_markdown:
Old neighbourhoods are valuable because they were built through unplanned individual
actions over long periods, and any attempt by the state to regulate them undermines the
qualities that made them worth preserving in the first place.
option_c_markdown:
Cities should be cautious about declaring neighbourhoods as heritage sites, since such
designation transforms living settlements into objects of policy and prevents the small
unauthorised acts on which their character depends.
option_d_markdown:
The protection of old neighbourhoods through heritage designation creates a contradiction
between the desire to preserve a place and the recognition that the place was originally
produced by acts that current regulations would now forbid.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17. (Para Summary) Answer: A. The paragraph’s argument has three movements: (i)
heritage protection is real; (ii) it works by regulating the unauthorised acts that produced the
neighbourhood; (iii) the result is preservation that alters what is preserved. (A) integrates all
three and includes the qualification (“no reason to abandon . . . but a reason to be modest”).

Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up: it captures the central contradiction but drops
the author’s measured conclusion that protection is still worthwhile (true-but-partial ; misses the
qualification). (B) overstates: the author does not claim regulation should be abandoned. (C)
reads the passage as advising against designation, which the author explicitly resists (too strong;
descriptive-vs-prescriptive shift).

question_text_markdown:
The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the passage.

Critics of large language models often note that the systems produce fluent but unreliable prose,
and recommend that their output be treated with suspicion until verified. The recommendation
is sensible as far as it goes, but it diagnoses the wrong problem. The difficulty is not that
the prose is sometimes wrong; human prose is also sometimes wrong, and we have evolved
means of checking it. The difficulty is that the prose is unaccompanied by the cues—hesitation,
qualification, sourcing, an author with a track record—by which we ordinarily decide where to
direct our scepticism. The model does not lie more often than people; it produces text in a register
from which the markers of unreliability have been smoothed away, and so reorganises the labour
of judgement rather than adding to it.
option_a_markdown:
Large language models are not more inaccurate than humans, but the polished register in
which they write strips out the cues readers normally use to calibrate trust, shifting the
burden of judgement rather than merely increasing the error rate.
option_b_markdown:
The recommendation that the output of large language models be verified before use is
correct in principle but underestimates the difficulty of verification when the source is
opaque.
option_c_markdown:
Although large language models occasionally produce false statements, this is not in itself
unusual; what distinguishes their output from human writing is the absence of an identifiable
author whose reliability can be assessed.
option_d_markdown:
Treating the output of large language models with suspicion misdiagnoses the real difficulty,
which is that such systems imitate the surface features of authoritative prose without sharing
its accountability structures.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18. (Para Summary) Answer: A. Two strands: (i) LLMs are not more inaccurate than
humans; (ii) what is missing is the register of cues by which readers calibrate trust. (A) carries
both and notes the shift in the labour of judgement.

it captures the diagnosis but misframes
Why the other options fail: (D) is the runner-up:
the conclusion as being about accountability structures, a related but stronger claim than the
paragraph makes (slightly overstated ; too broad ). (B) reduces the paragraph to a comment
on the verification recommendation, missing the structural diagnosis (true-but-partial ). (C)
introduces a claim about the absence of an identifiable author—true in spirit, but specifically
the paragraph emphasises the absence of cues, of which an identifiable author is only one (too
narrow ).

question_text_markdown:
The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the passage.

The traveller who returns from a long journey is often disappointed by photographs. The light is
wrong, the scale is wrong, the figures who were central to the experience are missing or out of

focus. We tend to take this as a failure of the camera, but the failure is rather of our expectation.
A photograph records what a particular surface looked like at a particular instant from a particular
point; the experience it claims to commemorate is constituted by everything that the photograph
cannot register—the heat, the smell, the slow accumulation of attention. To complain that the
picture does not match the memory is to ask the picture to be something other than a picture.
option_a_markdown:
Photographs frequently disappoint returning travellers because cameras cannot reproduce
the sensory richness of experience, and travellers should therefore rely less on photographs
and more on written records.
option_b_markdown:
The disappointment travellers feel on viewing their photographs reflects not a defect in
the camera but a misplaced expectation that an instantaneous record of a surface should
preserve the multisensory experience that gave the scene its meaning.
option_c_markdown:
Memory is constituted by elements that photographs cannot capture—heat, smell, accumu-
lated attention—and the gap between the two is the source of the dissatisfaction travellers
commonly feel.
option_d_markdown:
Photographic technology, despite its precision, fails to convey the affective qualities of travel
because it abstracts a single instant from a continuous experience and a single viewpoint
from a moving one.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19. (Para Summary) Answer: B. The argument is that the disappointment is not a
failure of the camera but a misplaced expectation: a still record of a surface cannot preserve the
multisensory experience whose loss readers attribute to a defect.

it captures the second half (memory has
Why the other options fail: (C) is the runner-up:
elements the photo cannot capture) but omits the corrective move about expectation (true-but-
partial ). (A) introduces a prescriptive recommendation (use written records) absent from the
paragraph (descriptive-vs-prescriptive shift). (D) repeats the technical observation but misses
the explanatory shift about expectation (distorted emphasis).

(A) Photographs frequently disappoint returning travellers because cameras cannot reproduce

question_text_markdown:
Five sentences related to a topic are given below. Four of them can be put together to form a
coherent paragraph. Identify the odd one out. Enter the digit corresponding to the odd sentence
as your answer (1, 2, 3, 4, or 5).
option_a_markdown:
Reading aloud, once the standard mode of engagement with a text, gave the listener a

particular relation to its rhythm and pacing that silent reading does not reproduce.
option_b_markdown:
The shift to silent reading, often dated to late antiquity but consolidated only much later,
made the text a private object whose pace was governed by the reader rather than performed
for an audience.
option_c_markdown:
Studies of comprehension suggest that children who are read to in early childhood acquire

larger vocabularies than those who are not.
option_d_markdown:
With privacy came a new freedom to skim, to reread, and to interrupt, but also a new

isolation from the social setting in which interpretation had once been negotiated.
option_e_markdown:
The history of reading is in part the history of these gains and losses, and any account that
treats the transition to silent reading as straightforward progress underdescribes what was
given up.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20. (Odd Sentence Out) Answer: 3. Sentences 1, 2, 4, and 5 form a continuous account:
the historical relation between reading aloud and silent reading, the consolidation of the silent
mode, the gains and losses that followed, and the closing reflection that the transition was not
unambiguous progress. Sentence 3 introduces an unrelated empirical finding about vocabulary
acquisition in children read to in early childhood—a topic in developmental psychology, not in
the history of reading. Although it shares the keyword “reading,” it does not fit the paragraph’s
historical-analytic movement (related-but-non-integrable claim).
source_raw_block:
Q20. (Odd Sentence Out)

Five sentences related to a topic are given below. Four of them can be put together to form a
coherent paragraph. Identify the odd one out. Enter the digit corresponding to the odd sentence
as your answer (1, 2, 3, 4, or 5).

1. Reading aloud, once the standard mode of engagement with a text, gave the listener a

particular relation to its rhythm and pacing that silent reading does not reproduce.

2. The shift to silent reading, often dated to late antiquity but consolidated only much later,
made the text a private object whose pace was governed by the reader rather than performed
for an audience.

3. Studies of comprehension suggest that children who are read to in early childhood acquire

larger vocabularies than those who are not.

4. With privacy came a new freedom to skim, to reread, and to interrupt, but also a new

isolation from the social setting in which interpretation had once been negotiated.

5. The history of reading is in part the history of these gains and losses, and any account that
treats the transition to silent reading as straightforward progress underdescribes what was
given up.

question_text_markdown:
Five sentences related to a topic are given below. Four of them can be put together to form a
coherent paragraph. Identify the odd one out. Enter the digit corresponding to the odd sentence
as your answer (1, 2, 3, 4, or 5).
option_a_markdown:
Phytoplankton in the upper ocean produce the bulk of the planet’s oxygen through photo-

synthesis, a process powered by sunlight at the surface.
option_b_markdown:
Recent measurements on the deep seafloor, however, have detected oxygen being generated

in complete darkness, at depths where photosynthesis is impossible.
option_c_markdown:
The candidate explanation involves polymetallic nodules whose surfaces appear to act as

natural electrolytic cells, splitting seawater into hydrogen and oxygen.
option_d_markdown:
Mining companies have already mapped large tracts of the seabed in preparation for the

commercial extraction of these nodules.
option_e_markdown:
If the finding holds up, it will require biologists to revise long-standing assumptions about

the conditions under which oxygenic environments can arise.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q21. (Odd Sentence Out) Answer: 4. Sentences 1, 2, 3, and 5 build a scientific narrative:
the standard picture of oxygen production by photosynthesis (1), an anomalous observation in
the deep sea (2), a candidate mechanism involving polymetallic nodules (3), and the implication
for biological assumptions (5). Sentence 4 shifts from the scientific story to an industrial-
policy observation about seabed mining—thematically adjacent (it concerns the same nodules)
but introducing a different stage of argument (economic exploitation rather than scientific
explanation). The oddness is one of stage of argument and causal direction, not of topic.
source_raw_block:
Q21. (Odd Sentence Out)

Five sentences related to a topic are given below. Four of them can be put together to form a
coherent paragraph. Identify the odd one out. Enter the digit corresponding to the odd sentence
as your answer (1, 2, 3, 4, or 5).

1. Phytoplankton in the upper ocean produce the bulk of the planet’s oxygen through photo-

synthesis, a process powered by sunlight at the surface.

2. Recent measurements on the deep seafloor, however, have detected oxygen being generated

in complete darkness, at depths where photosynthesis is impossible.

3. The candidate explanation involves polymetallic nodules whose surfaces appear to act as

natural electrolytic cells, splitting seawater into hydrogen and oxygen.

4. Mining companies have already mapped large tracts of the seabed in preparation for the

commercial extraction of these nodules.

5. If the finding holds up, it will require biologists to revise long-standing assumptions about

the conditions under which oxygenic environments can arise.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences (numbered 1, 2, 3, 4) given below, when properly sequenced, form a coherent
paragraph. Determine the proper order and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.
option_a_markdown:
Photography entered electoral politics as a tool of recognition: a way for voters to attach a

face to a name on a ballot they might otherwise read with difficulty.
option_b_markdown:
What had been an aid to identification gradually became a substitute for it, and the
candidate’s image came to do the work that argument had once been expected to do.
option_c_markdown:
This is not to mourn an earlier age in which voters scrutinised manifestos; it is to notice
that the medium through which candidates are encountered shapes the kinds of judgement
that can be made about them.
option_d_markdown:
As the technology cheapened and circulated more freely, however, the photograph began to
carry suggestions of character, competence, even policy, that no caption explicitly authorised.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22. (Para Jumble) Answer: 1423.

Order: 1 → 4 → 2 → 3.

16


CAT-plus VARC Simulation

Conceptual sequence: (1) introduces photography’s original role in elections as an aid to recogni-
tion. (4) registers the shift: as the technology spread, the image came to carry suggestions of
character and policy beyond mere identification. (2) gives the consequence: the image displaces
argument. (3) closes by stepping back to a reflection on the medium, refusing nostalgia.

Why tempting alternatives fail: 1→2→4→3 looks grammatically possible because (2) reads as
a continuation of (1), but it skips the causal step in (4) (“as the technology cheapened”) that
explains how the substitution arose; (2) cannot directly follow (1) without the explanation of
how recognition turned into substitution. 4→1→2→3 is blocked because (4) presupposes the
earlier role of photography that (1) introduces. (3) must close because it is meta-commentary
on the whole shift.

Q22. (Para Jumble)

The four sentences (numbered 1, 2, 3, 4) given below, when properly sequenced, form a coherent
paragraph. Determine the proper order and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.

1. Photography entered electoral politics as a tool of recognition: a way for voters to attach a

face to a name on a ballot they might otherwise read with difficulty.

2. What had been an aid to identification gradually became a substitute for it, and the
candidate’s image came to do the work that argument had once been expected to do.

3. This is not to mourn an earlier age in which voters scrutinised manifestos; it is to notice
that the medium through which candidates are encountered shapes the kinds of judgement
that can be made about them.

4. As the technology cheapened and circulated more freely, however, the photograph began to
carry suggestions of character, competence, even policy, that no caption explicitly authorised.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences (numbered 1, 2, 3, 4) given below, when properly sequenced, form a coherent
paragraph. Determine the proper order and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.
option_a_markdown:
The term Latinx was proposed as a gender-neutral alternative to the gendered endings of
Spanish, and was taken up enthusiastically in parts of the North American academy.
option_b_markdown:
Surveys of Spanish-speaking populations in the United States, however, have repeatedly
found that a large majority either do not use the term or do not recognise it as referring to
themselves.
option_c_markdown:
The gap suggests that the politics of naming can travel along channels that are largely

insulated from the communities the names are supposed to describe.
option_d_markdown:
Defenders of the term reply that adoption is a matter of time, and that the resistance

reported by surveys reflects unfamiliarity rather than considered rejection.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23. (Para Jumble) Answer: 1243.

Order: 1 → 2 → 4 → 3.

Conceptual sequence: (1) introduces the term Latinx and its academic uptake. (2) presents the
counter-finding from surveys. (4) gives the defenders’ reply, treating resistance as unfamiliarity.
(3) concludes with the author’s reflection: the gap between adoption and recognition suggests
channels insulated from the named communities.

Why tempting alternatives fail: 1→4→2→3 looks possible because (4) is a reply, but a reply
presupposes the survey finding that (2) introduces, so (4) cannot precede (2). 1→2→3→4 is
blocked because (3) is the closing reflection, not the prompt for (4); placing (3) before (4) makes
the paragraph end with the defenders’ rejoinder, undercutting the author’s framing.
source_raw_block:

The four sentences (numbered 1, 2, 3, 4) given below, when properly sequenced, form a coherent
paragraph. Determine the proper order and enter the four-digit sequence as your answer.

1. The term Latinx was proposed as a gender-neutral alternative to the gendered endings of
Spanish, and was taken up enthusiastically in parts of the North American academy.

2. Surveys of Spanish-speaking populations in the United States, however, have repeatedly
found that a large majority either do not use the term or do not recognise it as referring to
themselves.

3. The gap suggests that the politics of naming can travel along channels that are largely

insulated from the communities the names are supposed to describe.

4. Defenders of the term reply that adoption is a matter of time, and that the resistance

reported by surveys reflects unfamiliarity rather than considered rejection.

question_text_markdown:
The paragraph below has four positions marked [A], [B], [C], and [D]. Read the paragraph and
the standalone sentence that follows. Choose the position at which the standalone sentence best
fits.

In an economy in which productive investment yields steady but moderate returns, the distribution
of income tends to compress over time, as wages rise alongside output and capital earns roughly
what it contributes. [A] When, however, technological change persistently raises the productivity
of capital faster than that of labour, the same growth that lifts aggregate output also widens
the share captured by owners. [B] The standard prescription has been to redistribute through
taxation, on the assumption that the underlying growth pattern is fixed. [C] But if the pattern
itself is a policy variable—shaped by what societies choose to invest in, who is trained, and which
industries are favoured—then the question is no longer how to redistribute a given pie but how

to bake a pie whose slices do not require redistribution to be tolerable. [D]

Standalone sentence: “Productivity gains, in such an economy, accumulate disproportionately to
those who already hold the assets through which gains are realised, and the distribution stretches
even as the average improves.”
option_a_markdown: Position [A]
option_b_markdown: Position [B]
option_c_markdown: Position [C]
option_d_markdown: Position [D]
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24. (Sentence Placement) Answer: B.

The standalone sentence describes how productivity gains accumulate disproportionately to
existing asset-holders and the distribution stretches even as the average improves. This is the
mechanism that explains how the same growth can lift output while widening the capital share,
which is exactly the claim made in the sentence ending at [B]. Placing the standalone at [B]
supplies the causal mechanism between the general statement (capital productivity rising faster)
and the policy discussion that follows (“the standard prescription has been to redistribute”).

Why the other blanks fail: [A] is too early: the sentence at [A] still describes the compressive case in
which capital earns “roughly what it contributes”—the standalone’s account of disproportionate
accumulation contradicts that case rather than amplifying it. [C] is tempting because it sits
before the policy turn, but inserting it there breaks the flow from the technological-change claim
to the standard prescription; the standalone is the bridge that explains why redistribution was
prescribed, so it belongs before the prescription, not between the prescription and its alternative.
[D] is too late: by [D], the paragraph has already moved to a meta-policy framing about baking
versus slicing the pie; the mechanism sentence has nothing to add at that level.

The runner-up is [C]: students may place the sentence just before the standard prescription,
reading the standalone as setting up that prescription. The decisive consideration is that the
standalone is a mechanism, not a policy prompt; mechanism precedes the consequence statement
at [B], and only after that consequence is on the table does the prescription at [C] make sense.
