passage_text_markdown:
The intuition that we remember the past because the past exists is so deeply embedded that it
can seem perverse to question. Yet the dependence of memory on a past reality is, on exami-
nation, a metaphysical assumption rather than a logical necessity. What constitutes a present
act of remembering — a mental image, an emotion, a feeling of pastness — occurs entirely now.
The familiar sense that the remembered scene lies behind us, dimmed by intervening years, is
part of the present experience itself; it is not a perception of the past but a way of presently
experiencing certain contents. From this it follows that the universe could, without contradic-
tion, have come into existence a moment ago, complete with apparent ruins, fossils, and human
beings furnished with what we would call false memories of a non-existent yesterday. Such a
hypothesis is not a serious cosmological claim; it is a philosophical limit case designed to expose
what memory does and does not require.

Some philosophers respond that, even if the past is not logically necessary to memory, its
presence is the simplest and most natural explanation of why memory-images cohere as they
do. This is a strong rejoinder, but it concedes the point at issue. To say that the past best
explains the structure of present memory is to say that the past is hypothesised, not directly
perceived. We do not enjoy a special faculty that reaches into yesterday and inspects it. What
we have is a present array of beliefs — some vivid, some hesitant, some accompanied by the
peculiar conviction we call “remembering” — from which we infer a history.

This is unsettling, but the unease is itself instructive. The strength of the felt connection
between memory and past reality reveals how much of our cognitive life depends on dispositions

we do not examine. We trust certain images because they come with a phenomenological
signature of pastness, much as we trust certain perceptions because they come with a signature
of externality. Neither signature is self-validating. A vivid memory of an event that never
occurred carries the same internal credentials as a memory of an event that did.

It would be a mistake, however, to conclude that memory is therefore unreliable, or that scep-
ticism about the past should govern ordinary life. The argument is not normative but analytic:
it shows what we cannot infer with certainty, not how we should conduct ourselves. We must
live as though our memories track a past, because no alternative is workable. The philosophical
point is more modest and more disturbing: when we say “I remember,” we have described our
present state precisely, and any further claim — that the world contained a corresponding event
— is a further commitment, however natural and however justified by habit. The trust we place
in memory is, in the strictest sense, a trust extended on credit.
question_text_markdown:
The author refers to the hypothesis that the universe came into existence a moment ago
primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown:
argue that scepticism about the past is, on reflection, more defensible than common sense

allows.
option_b_markdown: demonstrate that ordinary beliefs about memory cannot survive philosophical scrutiny.
option_c_markdown:
isolate what the act of remembering itself contains, by stripping away the past it is assumed

to refer to.
option_d_markdown: suggest that the coherence of our memories is best explained by the existence of a real past.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Answer: (3). The five-minutes-ago hypothesis is introduced as a philosophical limit case.
Its function is to separate what is contained in the present act of remembering from the past it
is assumed to reference. Option (3) captures this function in conceptual terms without lifting
passage wording.

It is wrong
Runner-up: (1). Tempting because the hypothesis appears sceptical in flavour.
because the author explicitly says the hypothesis is “not a serious cosmological claim” and
later denies that the argument supports scepticism in ordinary life — a confuses example with
argument trap.

Why others fail: (2) is too broad — the author is careful to deny that the argument unsettles
ordinary memory practice.
(4) inverts the role of the hypothesis; the hypothesis is used to
question, not to support, the past-as-explanation view (causal reversal ).
source_raw_block:
1. The author refers to the hypothesis that the universe came into existence a moment ago
primarily in order to:

(1) argue that scepticism about the past is, on reflection, more defensible than common sense

allows.

(2) demonstrate that ordinary beliefs about memory cannot survive philosophical scrutiny.

(3) isolate what the act of remembering itself contains, by stripping away the past it is assumed

to refer to.

(4) suggest that the coherence of our memories is best explained by the existence of a real past.

question_text_markdown:
The author’s attitude toward the response that “the past is the simplest explanation of
present memory” is best described as:
option_a_markdown: dismissive, since the response merely restates the assumption the author rejects.
option_b_markdown:
sympathetic, treating the response as forceful but as conceding the deeper claim being

made.
option_c_markdown: neutral, presenting the response as one possible view among several with no clear preference.
option_d_markdown: endorsing, since the response provides a satisfactory reply to the sceptical worry.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Answer: (2). The author calls the rejoinder “strong” but immediately observes that it
“concedes the point at issue,” since explanation by inference is still not direct perception. The

runner-up (4) is tempting because the author grants the rejoinder some force; it is wrong because
the author treats it as conceding, not settling, the issue (confuses concession with conclusion).

Why others fail: (1) overstates the author’s resistance — the response is taken seriously, not
dismissed. (3) is too neutral; the author has a clear view of the rejoinder’s structure.
source_raw_block:
2. The author’s attitude toward the response that “the past is the simplest explanation of
present memory” is best described as:

(1) dismissive, since the response merely restates the assumption the author rejects.

(2) sympathetic, treating the response as forceful but as conceding the deeper claim being

made.

(3) neutral, presenting the response as one possible view among several with no clear preference.

(4) endorsing, since the response provides a satisfactory reply to the sceptical worry.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following would the author be least likely to endorse?
option_a_markdown: The conviction that one is remembering belongs to the present, not to the moment recalled.
option_b_markdown: A philosophical limit case can illuminate the structure of an ordinary mental act.
option_c_markdown:
Because memory cannot be guaranteed to track a real past, one ought to suspend everyday

reliance on it.
option_d_markdown:
The feeling that memory carries us back to a past event is a phenomenological signature

rather than a perception.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3. Answer: (3). The author explicitly distinguishes the analytic point from a normative rec-
ommendation, writing that “we must live as though our memories track a past.” A prescription
to suspend everyday reliance on memory is precisely the move the author refuses (descriptive
versus prescriptive shift).

Runner-up: (4). Tempting because the phrase “phenomenological signature” is unusual and
a careless reader may suspect it of overreaching. It is in fact directly supported by the third
paragraph and is the correct kind of claim for the author to endorse.

Why others fail: (1) and (2) are straightforwardly endorsed by the passage.
source_raw_block:
3. Which of the following would the author be least likely to endorse?

(1) The conviction that one is remembering belongs to the present, not to the moment recalled.

(2) A philosophical limit case can illuminate the structure of an ordinary mental act.

(3) Because memory cannot be guaranteed to track a real past, one ought to suspend everyday

reliance on it.

(4) The feeling that memory carries us back to a past event is a phenomenological signature

rather than a perception.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the function of the final paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
It softens the radical implications of the preceding argument by acknowledging that ordinary

practice need not change.
option_b_markdown:
It abandons the analytic stance of the earlier paragraphs and offers a practical recommen-

dation about how to live with memory.
option_c_markdown:
It restates the central claim of the passage in stronger form, asserting that memory is

fundamentally untrustworthy.
option_d_markdown:
It withdraws a key concession made earlier, in order to reinforce the argument against the

reality of the past.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4. Answer: (1). The final paragraph withdraws the suspicion that the analytic argument
should reshape conduct, by insisting that ordinary memory practice must continue. This is the
classic “softening of radical implications” move.

Runner-up: (2). Tempting because the paragraph does discuss living as though memories track
a past, which sounds practical. It is wrong because the author insists the argument is analytic,
not normative — the practical remark is a clarification of scope, not a recommendation about
how to live (distorted emphasis).

Why others fail: (3) overstates — the author denies that the conclusion is that memory is
unreliable. (4) misreads the paragraph; nothing is withdrawn.

Passage 2 — The Tribal Imagination
source_raw_block:
4. Which of the following best captures the function of the final paragraph?

(1) It softens the radical implications of the preceding argument by acknowledging that ordinary

practice need not change.

(2) It abandons the analytic stance of the earlier paragraphs and offers a practical recommen-

dation about how to live with memory.

(3) It restates the central claim of the passage in stronger form, asserting that memory is

fundamentally untrustworthy.

(4) It withdraws a key concession made earlier, in order to reinforce the argument against the

reality of the past.

passage_text_markdown:
Once a society accepts a secular mode of creativity, in which the human maker rather than a god
occupies the centre of the imaginative act, the relationship between artist and material turns
reflective. The image is no longer a vessel through which other orders of being pass; it is an
object made, judged, and signed. By contrast, the tribal imagination, where it survives, is still in
important respects dreamlike. Its forms permit fusions that the secular mind treats as category
errors: oceans take flight as birds, mountains swim, animals speak the speech of human beings,
and the dead converse without strain with the living. To read these as picturesque flourishes is
to miss their cognitive content. They register a world in which time and species and substance
have not yet been fixed into the separate compartments that literate societies inherit and police.

It is tempting to call such an imagination childlike, but the description, though sympathetic, is
misleading. A child’s confusions are unsystematic; they thin as the child matures. The tribal
imagination is not a thinned-down version of the secular imagination but a different organisation
of the world, one in which memory rather than authorship carries the chief cognitive load. Songs,
narratives, and ritual sequences are not primarily inventions to be credited to a maker; they
are inheritances to be carried forward intact. What the secular tradition calls originality is, in
this setting, slightly suspect: to depart from the received form is to risk severing the line that
connects the present community to its predecessors.

This does not mean that the tribal imagination is static. Variation occurs constantly, but it is
variation within a frame that the singer or storyteller does not openly claim as her own. The
result is an art whose centre of gravity lies outside the individual — in the line of transmission,
in the ancestral presence the performance summons, in the obligations that follow from being
temporarily in possession of a form one did not invent. The dead are not merely commemorated;
they are participants whose claims on the living are honoured through the careful handling of
inherited material.

A common error in writing about such traditions is to praise their wholeness and to lament
its loss without noticing the cost at which the wholeness was maintained. The tribal frame
demands that the individual subordinate the impulse to alter, to sign, to depart. This is not
a deprivation if one does not value such impulses; but to romanticise the frame while enjoying
the freedoms that lie outside it is to take both sides of an exchange that the tribal world itself
does not offer.
question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the central argument of the passage?
option_a_markdown:
The tribal imagination represents an earlier and less developed stage of the secular imagi-

nation, marked by category confusions that mature thought outgrows.
option_b_markdown:
The tribal imagination is governed by a distinct organisation of experience in which memory
and inheritance take the place that authorship and originality occupy in secular art.
option_c_markdown:
The tribal imagination should be admired because its wholeness contrasts favourably with

the fragmented condition of modern creative life.
option_d_markdown:
The tribal imagination differs from the secular imagination chiefly in its tolerance of fan-

tastical imagery and its disregard for spatial and temporal order.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q5. Answer: (2). The central claim is that the tribal imagination is a different organisa-
tion of experience, with memory and inheritance occupying the place taken by authorship and
originality in secular art.

Runner-up: (3). Tempting because the passage does discuss wholeness. It is wrong because the
author explicitly criticises the romantic move that admires wholeness while ignoring its costs
(distorted emphasis, true but partial ).

Why others fail:
(1) is the developmental reading the author rejects (author’s view versus
discussed view ). (4) reduces the argument to the level of imagery, missing the structural claim
about memory and authorship.
source_raw_block:
5. Which of the following best captures the central argument of the passage?

(1) The tribal imagination represents an earlier and less developed stage of the secular imagi-

nation, marked by category confusions that mature thought outgrows.

(2) The tribal imagination is governed by a distinct organisation of experience in which memory
and inheritance take the place that authorship and originality occupy in secular art.

(3) The tribal imagination should be admired because its wholeness contrasts favourably with

the fragmented condition of modern creative life.

(4) The tribal imagination differs from the secular imagination chiefly in its tolerance of fan-

tastical imagery and its disregard for spatial and temporal order.

question_text_markdown:
The author’s reference to the childlike character of the tribal imagination serves primarily
to:
option_a_markdown: endorse a description that conveys the warmth and sincerity of tribal forms.
option_b_markdown: introduce a sympathetic comparison only to reject it as a category mistake.
option_c_markdown: draw a parallel between cognitive development in individuals and in cultures.
option_d_markdown: criticise scholars who have condescended to tribal societies through such labels.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q6. Answer: (2). The childlike comparison is introduced sympathetically (“tempting”),
then rejected as misleading because children’s confusions thin with maturation while the tribal
organisation does not.

Runner-up: (3). Tempting because the passage mentions cognitive development. It is wrong
because the parallel is precisely what the author denies — the tribal imagination is not a less
developed version of anything.

Why others fail: (1) misreads; the author rejects the description. (4) imports a critique of
scholars that the text does not make.
source_raw_block:
6. The author’s reference to the childlike character of the tribal imagination serves primarily
to:

(1) endorse a description that conveys the warmth and sincerity of tribal forms.

(2) introduce a sympathetic comparison only to reject it as a category mistake.

(3) draw a parallel between cognitive development in individuals and in cultures.

(4) criticise scholars who have condescended to tribal societies through such labels.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s claim about the role of
originality in tribal artistic practice?
option_a_markdown:
Field recordings show that tribal singers introduce small variations in pitch and ornamen-

tation across performances of the same song.
option_b_markdown:
Several tribal communities maintain elaborate rituals through which a performer is publicly

recognised as the author of a new song that then enters the inherited repertoire.
option_c_markdown:
Anthropologists report that tribal audiences criticise performers who deviate noticeably

from the accepted version of a narrative.
option_d_markdown:
Some tribal traditions include stories whose imagery resembles that of secular literary works

produced in distant societies.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q7. Answer: (2). If tribal communities formally recognise individual authorship of new songs
that enter the inherited repertoire, the central claim about the displacement of authorship by
inheritance is directly undermined.

It is wrong because the author
Runner-up: (1). Tempting because variation is mentioned.
already acknowledges variation within an inherited frame; option (1) is fully compatible with
the author’s view and so cannot weaken it.

Why others fail: (3) supports the author’s view. (4) is a peripheral observation about imagery,
not authorship.
source_raw_block:
7. Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s claim about the role of
originality in tribal artistic practice?

(1) Field recordings show that tribal singers introduce small variations in pitch and ornamen-

tation across performances of the same song.

(2) Several tribal communities maintain elaborate rituals through which a performer is publicly

recognised as the author of a new song that then enters the inherited repertoire.

(3) Anthropologists report that tribal audiences criticise performers who deviate noticeably

from the accepted version of a narrative.

(4) Some tribal traditions include stories whose imagery resembles that of secular literary works

produced in distant societies.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not capture its central
claim?
option_a_markdown:
Tribal narratives include fusions of species, substances, and times that secular thought

treats as errors.
option_b_markdown: In the tribal frame, departure from the received form is regarded with a degree of suspicion.
option_c_markdown:
Romantic admiration of tribal wholeness can overlook the constraints under which that

wholeness is sustained.
option_d_markdown: The tribal imagination is organised around memory and inheritance rather than authorship.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q8. Answer: (1). The fusion of species, substances, and times is true according to the
passage but is presented as evidence of a deeper organisational difference, not as the central
claim itself — a textbook true but partial / true but not central item.

Runner-up: (2). Tempting because suspicion of departure also seems peripheral. It is closer to
the central claim — it is a direct expression of the displacement of authorship by inheritance
— so it captures the central machinery more nearly than (1) does.

Why others fail: (3) is true but is a corollary of the central argument. (4) is the central claim.

Passage 3 — Automated Ethics
source_raw_block:
8. Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not capture its central
claim?

(1) Tribal narratives include fusions of species, substances, and times that secular thought

treats as errors.

(2) In the tribal frame, departure from the received form is regarded with a degree of suspicion.

(3) Romantic admiration of tribal wholeness can overlook the constraints under which that

wholeness is sustained.

(4) The tribal imagination is organised around memory and inheritance rather than authorship.

passage_text_markdown:
Suppose we could entrust to a sufficiently sophisticated artificial system the most consequen-
tial ethical decisions of public life: which patients receive a scarce organ, which prisoners are
released, which neighbourhoods are policed more closely. The appeal is intelligible. Human
decision-makers are uneven, distracted, and unequally exposed to the costs of their mistakes.
An impartial mechanism, trained on vast historical data, promises uniformity where there is
now caprice. The promise rests, however, on a tacit assumption: that ethical reasoning is the
kind of activity that yields, in principle, to formal modelling.

This assumption is rarely defended; more often it is borrowed by analogy from the natural
sciences. Physics, after all, models phenomena that once seemed to resist quantification. Why
should ethics be different? The analogy is seductive, and it is also misleading. A physical
model succeeds when its predictions match independent observations; the criterion of success

lies outside the model. Ethical judgement has no comparable external check. The judgement
of a thoughtful person is not measured against an ethical fact awaiting discovery, but emerges
from a sensitivity to circumstance, history, and consequence that cannot be exhaustively listed
in advance.

Defenders of automation reply that this sensitivity is itself a pattern, and that a sufficiently
rich pattern recogniser will eventually capture it. There is something to this. Some ethical
regularities can be made explicit, and where they can, there is no reason to prefer human
inconsistency to machine constancy. But the cases in which the question of automation matters
most are not these. They are the cases in which moral perception involves recognising that the
rule one had been applying is the wrong rule for the situation at hand. To recognise this is not
to apply a meta-rule; it is to revise the framework within which rules operate. A system trained
It can reproduce yesterday’s wisdom
on previous decisions inherits the previous framework.
efficiently. It cannot easily produce the unsettling judgement that yesterday’s wisdom was, in
this case, mistaken.

The point is not that automated ethical decision-making must be rejected. In narrow domains,
where moral considerations are stable and the costs of human inconsistency are high, mechani-
sation may be defensible. The point is that the appeal of impartiality conceals an unresolved
question: whether the discipline whose impartiality we admire — ethical reflection at its best
— is the same activity as the procedure that machines can be trained to perform. To assume
that it is, because it would be convenient if it were, is to allow the attractions of administrative
neatness to settle a philosophical question that has not yet been argued through.
question_text_markdown:
The author’s overall stance toward the use of artificial systems in ethical decision-making is
best described as:
option_a_markdown:
categorical rejection, on the ground that mechanised judgement cannot replicate moral

perception.
option_b_markdown:
conditional caution, accepting that narrow applications may be defensible while resisting

the more ambitious claim that automation can replace ethical reflection.
option_c_markdown:
qualified endorsement, accepting the broad case for automation while raising practical con-

cerns about training data.
option_d_markdown:
ironic detachment, treating both the enthusiasts and their critics as overstating their re-

spective cases.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q9. Answer: (2). The author explicitly accepts that “in narrow domains, where moral
considerations are stable and the costs of human inconsistency are high, mechanisation may be
defensible,” while resisting the broader assumption that ethics is the kind of activity machines
can be trained to perform.

Runner-up: (1). Tempting because the analytic weight of the passage falls on the limits of
automation. It is wrong because it overstates — the author does not reject the use of automated
systems categorically.

Why others fail: (3) inverts the balance. (4) imports a tone the passage does not carry.
source_raw_block:
9. The author’s overall stance toward the use of artificial systems in ethical decision-making is
best described as:

(1) categorical rejection, on the ground that mechanised judgement cannot replicate moral

perception.

(2) conditional caution, accepting that narrow applications may be defensible while resisting

the more ambitious claim that automation can replace ethical reflection.

(3) qualified endorsement, accepting the broad case for automation while raising practical con-

cerns about training data.

(4) ironic detachment, treating both the enthusiasts and their critics as overstating their re-

spective cases.

question_text_markdown: The author refers to the analogy with physics primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: illustrate a successful method that ethical theorists ought to adopt.
option_b_markdown: show how a comparison that lends plausibility to automated ethics fails on closer inspection.
option_c_markdown: argue that ethics, like physics, will eventually admit of precise mathematical treatment.
option_d_markdown: suggest that proponents of automated ethics have misread the history of physics.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q10. Answer: (2). The physics analogy is introduced as a tempting borrowed argument and
then shown to fail, since physical models have an external check that ethical models do not.

Runner-up: (3). Tempting because the passage discusses the possibility of mathematical treat-
ment.
It is wrong because the author is criticising, not endorsing, that hope (author’s view
versus discussed view ).

Why others fail: (1) inverts the author’s stance. (4) introduces a claim about misreading the
history of physics that the passage does not make.
source_raw_block:
10. The author refers to the analogy with physics primarily in order to:

(1) illustrate a successful method that ethical theorists ought to adopt.

(2) show how a comparison that lends plausibility to automated ethics fails on closer inspection.

(3) argue that ethics, like physics, will eventually admit of precise mathematical treatment.

(4) suggest that proponents of automated ethics have misread the history of physics.

question_text_markdown:
According to the passage, which of the following best captures the limitation of a system
trained on previous ethical decisions?
option_a_markdown: It tends to amplify the biases present in the historical data on which it is trained.
option_b_markdown: It applies ethical rules consistently but slowly, making it unsuitable for urgent cases.
option_c_markdown:
It can reproduce the framework within which past judgements were made but is poorly

placed to recognise when that framework itself needs revision.
option_d_markdown:
It treats ethical judgement as a matter of external verification, despite the absence of any

check comparable to the one used in physics.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q11. Answer: (3). The decisive paragraph identifies the limitation as the inability to
recognise that a previously applied framework is itself wrong for the present case.

Runner-up: (1). Tempting because bias in training data is a well-known critique of machine
learning. The passage, however, does not pursue this bias-amplification critique; it pursues a
different (framework-revision) critique. (1) is therefore true but irrelevant to the stem.

Why others fail: (2) introduces speed considerations not in the passage. (4) confuses ethics
with physics in a way the author explicitly resists.
source_raw_block:
11. According to the passage, which of the following best captures the limitation of a system
trained on previous ethical decisions?

(1) It tends to amplify the biases present in the historical data on which it is trained.

(2) It applies ethical rules consistently but slowly, making it unsuitable for urgent cases.

(3) It can reproduce the framework within which past judgements were made but is poorly

placed to recognise when that framework itself needs revision.

(4) It treats ethical judgement as a matter of external verification, despite the absence of any

check comparable to the one used in physics.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following best captures the distinction the author draws in the final paragraph?
option_a_markdown: Between ethical decisions that affect few people and those that affect many.
option_b_markdown:
Between the impartiality we value in ethical reflection and the procedural uniformity a

machine can supply.
option_c_markdown: Between supporters and opponents of automated decision-making.
option_d_markdown:
Between domains where ethical considerations are stable and domains where they shift

unpredictably.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q12. Answer: (2). The final paragraph distinguishes the impartiality we admire in ethical
reflection from the procedural uniformity machines can supply, and warns against assuming
these are the same activity.

Runner-up: (4). Tempting because the paragraph mentions narrow, stable domains. It is wrong
because the distinction in the paragraph is between two senses of impartiality, not between two
kinds of domains (too narrow ).

Why others fail: (1) is unsupported. (3) misreads the paragraph as a political division.

Passage 4 — Dams and Their Afterlives
source_raw_block:
12. Which of the following best captures the distinction the author draws in the final paragraph?

(1) Between ethical decisions that affect few people and those that affect many.

(2) Between the impartiality we value in ethical reflection and the procedural uniformity a

machine can supply.

(3) Between supporters and opponents of automated decision-making.

(4) Between domains where ethical considerations are stable and domains where they shift

unpredictably.

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, the dam was an emblem of optimism. To impound a river was
to convert an unruly natural force into electricity, irrigation, and flood control — a triple promise
that few governments, of whatever ideological complexion, could resist. The Soviet Union built
dams; so did the United States, India, Egypt, and Brazil. The structures themselves came to
stand for modern statehood, photographed against the sky as proof that the nation had passed
some threshold of competence. To question dam-building, in such a climate, was to question
development as such.

The reckoning, when it came, was uneven. By the late twentieth century, the costs were
no longer easy to overlook: displaced communities, drowned forests, fisheries cut from their
breeding grounds, sediment trapped behind walls of concrete and starved from the floodplains
and deltas that depended on it. Yet the reaction was not, as one might expect, a wholesale
abandonment of the dam. In wealthy countries, where most of the desirable sites had already
been used, the rhetoric shifted toward decommissioning and ecological restoration. In countries
still in the middle of their developmental ascent, dam-building continued, often at greater scale,
sometimes under explicit reference to the precedents set by countries that were now removing
their own dams.

It would be tidy to read this divergence as hypocrisy. Tidiness, however, is a poor guide to the
politics of infrastructure. The countries dismantling dams were not, in most cases, repudiating
the developmental case for them; they were responding to the marginal value of any additional
dam in a landscape already saturated with them. The countries still building were not blind
to the ecological costs; they were weighing those costs against energy poverty and the political
pressure of supplying power to populations whose patience was thinning. The same instrument
was being assessed within different curves of marginal benefit, and so produced different verdicts.

What is striking, then, is not the inconsistency but the persistence of a particular logic. Whether
one is building a dam or removing one, the underlying assumption is that the river is an object
of management, to be configured for human ends, with the configuration revised when the
ends change. The shift from construction to restoration, in this light, is not a reversal of the
developmental impulse but its continuation by gentler means. Even the language of ecological
repair — of returning a river to its natural state — conceals a planning decision: which past
state, at which moment in the river’s history, counts as the one to be restored. The dam, in

this sense, leaves an intellectual residue more durable than its concrete: the habit of treating
the river as something whose course is properly settled by us.
question_text_markdown: The author’s central claim in the passage is best described as the view that:
option_a_markdown:
the ecological costs of dam-building have been systematically underestimated and now re-

quire urgent correction worldwide.
option_b_markdown:
the divergent attitudes of wealthy and developing countries toward dams reflect a hypocrisy

that ought to be acknowledged.
option_c_markdown:
construction and decommissioning of dams, despite appearing opposed, share a common

assumption about the river as an object of human configuration.
option_d_markdown:
the developmental case for dams was always weaker than its proponents believed, as the

late-twentieth-century reckoning has shown.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q13. Answer: (3). The central claim, made most clearly in the final paragraph, is that
construction and decommissioning, despite their surface opposition, share the assumption that
the river is to be configured for human ends.

Runner-up: (2). Tempting because hypocrisy is mentioned. It is wrong because the author
explicitly rejects hypocrisy as a reading of the divergence (confuses concession with conclusion).

Why others fail: (1) overstates the ecological claim and misses the structural argument. (4)
inverts the author’s careful treatment of the developmental case.
source_raw_block:
13. The author’s central claim in the passage is best described as the view that:

(1) the ecological costs of dam-building have been systematically underestimated and now re-

quire urgent correction worldwide.

(2) the divergent attitudes of wealthy and developing countries toward dams reflect a hypocrisy

that ought to be acknowledged.

(3) construction and decommissioning of dams, despite appearing opposed, share a common

assumption about the river as an object of human configuration.

(4) the developmental case for dams was always weaker than its proponents believed, as the

late-twentieth-century reckoning has shown.

question_text_markdown: The author refers to the language of ecological repair in the final paragraph mainly to:
option_a_markdown: illustrate the difficulty of choosing a baseline against which restoration is to be measured.
option_b_markdown:
show that even an apparently corrective vocabulary preserves the planning attitude the

passage is examining.
option_c_markdown:
criticise ecologists who have failed to specify the past state to which rivers should be re-

turned.
option_d_markdown:
commend the move from construction to restoration as a meaningful change in environmen-

tal thinking.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q14. Answer: (2). The language-of-repair example shows that even corrective vocabulary
continues to presuppose a planning attitude — a choice of baseline.

Runner-up: (1). Tempting because the baseline-choice point is genuinely made. It is wrong
because the author’s interest in the baseline issue is instrumental:
it is used to illustrate the
persistence of planning, not for its own sake (true but partial ).

Why others fail: (3) imports a critique of ecologists that the passage does not make. (4) inverts
the author’s view.
source_raw_block:
14. The author refers to the language of ecological repair in the final paragraph mainly to:

(1) illustrate the difficulty of choosing a baseline against which restoration is to be measured.

(2) show that even an apparently corrective vocabulary preserves the planning attitude the

passage is examining.

(3) criticise ecologists who have failed to specify the past state to which rivers should be re-

turned.

(4) commend the move from construction to restoration as a meaningful change in environmen-

tal thinking.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author’s argument?
option_a_markdown:
Some recent dam-removal projects have failed to restore fisheries to anything resembling

their pre-dam condition.
option_b_markdown:
In several countries undertaking dam removal, the resulting river basins have been re-
engineered for recreational use, flood management, and tourism, requiring continued active
intervention.
option_c_markdown:
Wealthy countries have begun exporting expertise in dam decommissioning to developing

countries that are themselves still building dams.
option_d_markdown:
A growing scientific literature suggests that the ecological costs of dams were underestimated

during the twentieth century.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q15. Answer: (2). The author’s central claim is that planning persists across construction
and removal. Evidence that removed-dam basins are re-engineered for further human ends
directly supports the persistence-of-planning thesis.

Runner-up: (3). Tempting because the export of decommissioning expertise sounds relevant.
It is wrong because it speaks to the diffusion of practice across borders, not to whether removal
preserves the planning attitude — the central claim. It would strengthen a different, weaker
argument.

Why others fail: (1) is consistent with the author but does not address the central structural
point. (4) supports a peripheral premise, not the central claim.
source_raw_block:
15. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the author’s argument?

(1) Some recent dam-removal projects have failed to restore fisheries to anything resembling

their pre-dam condition.

(2) In several countries undertaking dam removal, the resulting river basins have been re-
engineered for recreational use, flood management, and tourism, requiring continued active
intervention.

(3) Wealthy countries have begun exporting expertise in dam decommissioning to developing

countries that are themselves still building dams.

(4) A growing scientific literature suggests that the ecological costs of dams were underestimated

during the twentieth century.

It would be tidy to read this divergence as hypocrisy. Tidiness, however, is a poor guide to the
politics of infrastructure. The countries dismantling dams were not, in most cases, repudiating
the developmental case for them; they were responding to the marginal value of any additional
dam in a landscape already saturated with them. The countries still building were not blind
to the ecological costs; they were weighing those costs against energy poverty and the political
pressure of supplying power to populations whose patience was thinning. The same instrument
was being assessed within different curves of marginal benefit, and so produced different verdicts.

What is striking, then, is not the inconsistency but the persistence of a particular logic. Whether
one is building a dam or removing one, the underlying assumption is that the river is an object
of management, to be configured for human ends, with the configuration revised when the
ends change. The shift from construction to restoration, in this light, is not a reversal of the
developmental impulse but its continuation by gentler means. Even the language of ecological
repair — of returning a river to its natural state — conceals a planning decision: which past
state, at which moment in the river’s history, counts as the one to be restored. The dam, in

this sense, leaves an intellectual residue more durable than its concrete: the habit of treating
the river as something whose course is properly settled by us.
question_text_markdown:
The author’s treatment of the divergence between dam-removing and dam-building coun-
tries is best described as:
option_a_markdown:
exposing it as a moral failing on the part of countries that built and now dismantle dams

while others go on building.
option_b_markdown:
explaining it as a difference in the marginal value attached to additional dams within oth-

erwise comparable assessments.
option_c_markdown:
interpreting it as evidence that the developmental case for dams has finally been refuted in

wealthy countries.
option_d_markdown: attributing it to ideological differences between the political systems in question.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q16. Answer: (2). The author rejects the hypocrisy reading and explains the divergence
through a marginal-benefit analysis — the same instrument assessed within different curves of
marginal benefit.

Runner-up: (1). Tempting because hypocrisy is discussed at some length. It is wrong because
the author explicitly resists the hypocrisy reading (author’s view versus discussed view ).

Why others fail: (3) overstates the implication of the dismantling trend. (4) introduces ideology
where the author rejects it.

Verbal Ability
source_raw_block:
16. The author’s treatment of the divergence between dam-removing and dam-building coun-
tries is best described as:

(1) exposing it as a moral failing on the part of countries that built and now dismantle dams

while others go on building.

(2) explaining it as a difference in the marginal value attached to additional dams within oth-

erwise comparable assessments.

(3) interpreting it as evidence that the developmental case for dams has finally been refuted in

wealthy countries.

(4) attributing it to ideological differences between the political systems in question.

question_text_markdown:
Para Summary. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the
option that best captures the essence of the passage.

The mindful Indian shopper is no longer impressed by the proposition that a global brand offers
convenience, dependability, and a uniform experience across cities. What once functioned as
a quiet promise — that a labelled garment from a recognised chain would absolve the buyer
of judgement — now reads as a limitation. The newer demand is for clothing that registers
individual choice, that fits the body and the budget without theatrical pricing, and that does not
require the buyer to accept whatever the global supply chain has decided this season to make
available. The shift is less a rejection of global brands than a refusal of the older bargain in
which uniformity was treated as a virtue.
option_a_markdown:
Indian shoppers are abandoning global brands because they have grown sceptical of the

dependability and uniformity those brands once promised.
option_b_markdown:
Indian shoppers now expect clothing that reflects personal choice and is reasonably priced,

having ceased to regard the uniformity offered by global brands as a virtue in itself.
option_c_markdown:
The decisive factor in the shift in Indian shopping behaviour is price: global brands have

become unaffordable, pushing buyers toward customised alternatives.
option_d_markdown:
Indian shoppers have begun to reject the global supply chain because it dictates seasonal

choices to which they have no meaningful response.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17. Answer: (2). The paragraph’s central claim is the refusal of the older bargain (unifor-
mity as virtue), combined with the new demand for individual choice and fair pricing. Option
(2) captures both.

Runner-up: (1). Tempting because abandonment of global brands seems implied. It is wrong
because the paragraph explicitly says the shift is “less a rejection of global brands than a refusal
of the older bargain” (distorted emphasis).

Why others fail: (3) is too narrow — it isolates price as decisive, which the paragraph does not
claim. (4) is true but partial.
source_raw_block:
17. Para Summary. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the
option that best captures the essence of the passage.

The mindful Indian shopper is no longer impressed by the proposition that a global brand offers
convenience, dependability, and a uniform experience across cities. What once functioned as
a quiet promise — that a labelled garment from a recognised chain would absolve the buyer
of judgement — now reads as a limitation. The newer demand is for clothing that registers
individual choice, that fits the body and the budget without theatrical pricing, and that does not
require the buyer to accept whatever the global supply chain has decided this season to make
available. The shift is less a rejection of global brands than a refusal of the older bargain in
which uniformity was treated as a virtue.

(1) Indian shoppers are abandoning global brands because they have grown sceptical of the

dependability and uniformity those brands once promised.

(2) Indian shoppers now expect clothing that reflects personal choice and is reasonably priced,

having ceased to regard the uniformity offered by global brands as a virtue in itself.

(3) The decisive factor in the shift in Indian shopping behaviour is price: global brands have

become unaffordable, pushing buyers toward customised alternatives.

(4) Indian shoppers have begun to reject the global supply chain because it dictates seasonal

choices to which they have no meaningful response.

question_text_markdown:
Para Summary.

Music is sometimes described as a universal human practice, but the description hides as much
as it reveals. If by “universal” we mean that every known society has some form of organised
sound used in social settings, the claim is unobjectionable. If we mean that the cognitive and
emotional uses of music are shared across cultures, the evidence is far less secure. What looks
like a single phenomenon may be a family of activities — ceremonial, identity-forming, infant-
soothing, ecstatic — bundled together under one word because our own culture happens to bundle
them. The interesting question is not whether music is universal but which of its functions travel
and which do not.
option_a_markdown:
Music’s claim to universality is misleading, since cognitive and emotional uses of music

differ sharply across cultures.
option_b_markdown:
Although music exists in every known society, treating it as a single universal phenomenon

obscures the question of which of its varied functions are actually shared across cultures.
option_c_markdown:
Music is best understood as a family of activities — ceremonial, identity-forming, infant-

soothing, and ecstatic — rather than as a single practice.
option_d_markdown:
The notion of musical universality is a projection of our own culture’s habit of grouping

diverse activities under a single word.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18. Answer: (2). The central move of the paragraph is the disambiguation: universality
in the trivial sense is granted; universality of cognitive and emotional uses is doubted; the
interesting question is which functions travel.

Runner-up: (1). Tempting because it correctly identifies that universality is challenged. It is
wrong because it states the cognitive-emotional differences as established, while the paragraph
says only that the evidence is “far less secure” (changes probability into certainty).

Why others fail: (3) is true but partial. (4) captures a side-observation, not the conclusion.
source_raw_block:
18. Para Summary.

Music is sometimes described as a universal human practice, but the description hides as much
as it reveals. If by “universal” we mean that every known society has some form of organised
sound used in social settings, the claim is unobjectionable. If we mean that the cognitive and
emotional uses of music are shared across cultures, the evidence is far less secure. What looks
like a single phenomenon may be a family of activities — ceremonial, identity-forming, infant-
soothing, ecstatic — bundled together under one word because our own culture happens to bundle
them. The interesting question is not whether music is universal but which of its functions travel
and which do not.

(1) Music’s claim to universality is misleading, since cognitive and emotional uses of music

differ sharply across cultures.

(2) Although music exists in every known society, treating it as a single universal phenomenon

obscures the question of which of its varied functions are actually shared across cultures.

(3) Music is best understood as a family of activities — ceremonial, identity-forming, infant-

soothing, and ecstatic — rather than as a single practice.

(4) The notion of musical universality is a projection of our own culture’s habit of grouping

diverse activities under a single word.

question_text_markdown:
Para Summary.

Productivity gains in modern economies have not, on the whole, raised the share of national
income accruing to labour. For several decades, output per worker has risen while the wage bill
has lagged behind, with the difference accruing to the owners of capital. Standard accounts treat

this as a transitional friction — the consequence of technological adjustment, of skill mismatches,
of unfinished labour market reform — and predict eventual convergence. The persistence of
the gap, across countries with very different labour institutions and at very different stages of
technological adoption, makes such accounts increasingly difficult to sustain. What looked like
a transitional friction begins to look like a feature.
option_a_markdown:
Productivity gains have failed to lift wages in modern economies, and standard accounts
that treat the gap as transitional are increasingly hard to defend given its persistence across
diverse settings.
option_b_markdown:
The owners of capital have appropriated the productivity gains of recent decades, and labour

institutions in most countries have been too weak to prevent this outcome.
option_c_markdown:
Although wages have lagged productivity, the gap is best understood as a transitional

friction that will close as skills and technologies converge.
option_d_markdown:
Labour markets in modern economies suffer from skill mismatches and unfinished reforms,

which together account for the gap between productivity and wages.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19. Answer: (1). The paragraph contrasts the standard transitional account with the
persistence of the gap, and concludes that the transitional reading is increasingly difficult to
sustain.

Runner-up: (2). Tempting because the capital-labour framing is present. It is wrong because
it overstates the institutional argument (the paragraph notes the gap persists across diverse
institutions, not because of weak ones) — too narrow / distorted emphasis.

Why others fail: (3) endorses the view the paragraph rejects (author’s view versus discussed
view ). (4) lists factors the paragraph specifically calls insufficient.
source_raw_block:
19. Para Summary.

Productivity gains in modern economies have not, on the whole, raised the share of national
income accruing to labour. For several decades, output per worker has risen while the wage bill
has lagged behind, with the difference accruing to the owners of capital. Standard accounts treat

this as a transitional friction — the consequence of technological adjustment, of skill mismatches,
of unfinished labour market reform — and predict eventual convergence. The persistence of
the gap, across countries with very different labour institutions and at very different stages of
technological adoption, makes such accounts increasingly difficult to sustain. What looked like
a transitional friction begins to look like a feature.

(1) Productivity gains have failed to lift wages in modern economies, and standard accounts
that treat the gap as transitional are increasingly hard to defend given its persistence across
diverse settings.

(2) The owners of capital have appropriated the productivity gains of recent decades, and labour

institutions in most countries have been too weak to prevent this outcome.

(3) Although wages have lagged productivity, the gap is best understood as a transitional

friction that will close as skills and technologies converge.

(4) Labour markets in modern economies suffer from skill mismatches and unfinished reforms,

which together account for the gap between productivity and wages.

question_text_markdown:
Sentence Placement. The following paragraph has four blank positions marked [A], [B],
[C], and [D]. Choose the position at which the given sentence best fits the logical flow of the
paragraph.

Sentence to be inserted: “The risk, however, is not that the new methods fail outright, but
that they succeed narrowly — delivering measurable improvements on the questions they were
designed to answer while leaving the deeper questions untouched.”

Paragraph: For two decades, empirical methods have remade the field of development economics.
[A] Where earlier generations argued from theory and from cross-country regressions of uncertain
interpretation, the new approach insists on identifying clean variation and reading effects from
that variation alone.
[B] The yield has been impressive: a stock of well-identified findings on
which programmes can be designed and revised. [C] A randomised evaluation can establish that
a particular intervention raises school attendance by a measurable amount, but it cannot, by
itself, tell us why some societies invest in schooling at all while others do not. [D] The question
of attendance is answered with precision; the question of investment is left where it was.

A

B

C

D
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20. Answer: (3). The inserted sentence introduces the risk of narrow success — precisely
the bridge from the celebration of yield ([B] precedes) to the example about attendance versus
investment ([D] follows). Position [C] is the only blank at which the logical movement from
praise to qualification to example is preserved.

Runner-up: [B]. Tempting because the sentence carries a contrastive “however,” which seems
to follow the celebratory “yield has been impressive.” It fails because the immediately following
sentence at [B] then becomes the attendance example without any intervening articulation of
the risk-of-narrow-success principle that the inserted sentence states.

Why others fail: [A] is too early — nothing yet to qualify. [D] is too late — the example has
already done the work the inserted sentence is meant to introduce.

question_text_markdown:
Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1, 2, 3, and 4, when arranged
in the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the correct order as a 4-digit number.
option_a_markdown:
A landscape produced in this way may be ecologically poorer than the one it replaced, but
it is also more legible, and it is the legibility, not the ecology, that the planner is rewarded
for delivering.
option_b_markdown:
Modern land-use planning prefers landscapes that can be summarised on a map: tracts of
single-purpose use, edges that align with administrative boundaries, mixtures that can be
classified rather than described.
option_c_markdown:
The mosaic of fields, hedgerows, fallows, and common grazing that characterised much of
the European countryside was illegible by this standard, since each parcel did several things
at once and the relations among parcels resisted neat tabulation.
option_d_markdown:
What looked like inefficiency from the planner’s vantage was, on closer examination, a way

of distributing risk and labour across a landscape that no single use could exhaust.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q21. Answer: 2314. Sentence 2 introduces the planner’s preference for legibility. Sentence 3
illustrates with the European mosaic, which fails the legibility test. Sentence 4 reinterprets the
apparent inefficiency of the mosaic as a distribution of risk and labour. Sentence 1 concludes by
stating the cost: the planner is rewarded for legibility, not ecology. Alternate orderings (e.g.,
2341) place the verdict before the reinterpretation, breaking the conceptual movement from
preference → example → reinterpretation of example → verdict.
source_raw_block:

question_text_markdown:
Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1, 2, 3, and 4, when arranged
in the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the correct order as a 4-digit number.
option_a_markdown:
To dismiss it as nostalgia is to miss the more interesting point: the past is being recruited
not because it is loved but because the future no longer supplies a workable vocabulary.
option_b_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, political rhetoric ran on the assumption that the future

was the natural location of value, and that the task of politics was to bring it nearer.
option_c_markdown:
As that assumption has weakened, an older grammar of restoration, return, and inheritance

has begun to fill the space left behind.
option_d_markdown:
The result is a political idiom that looks backward while remaining, in its emotional structure,

every bit as restless as the idiom it has displaced.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22. Answer: 2314. Sentence 2 establishes the twentieth-century assumption (future as
locus of value). Sentence 3 reports the weakening of that assumption and the rise of an older
grammar. Sentence 1 responds to a possible misreading (“nostalgia”) and identifies the real
reason. Sentence 4 states the result: a backward-looking idiom that retains the restlessness of
the displaced one. The opening cannot be 1 (“it” lacks antecedent) or 4 (“the result” presupposes
what has not yet been established). 3 before 2 inverts the temporal-conceptual order.
source_raw_block:

in the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the correct order as a 4-digit number.

not because it is loved but because the future no longer supplies a workable vocabulary.

2. For most of the twentieth century, political rhetoric ran on the assumption that the future

was the natural location of value, and that the task of politics was to bring it nearer.

3. As that assumption has weakened, an older grammar of restoration, return, and inheritance

has begun to fill the space left behind.

4. The result is a political idiom that looks backward while remaining, in its emotional structure,

every bit as restless as the idiom it has displaced.

question_text_markdown:
Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5.
Four of them, in some order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not
fit and enter its number.
option_a_markdown:
Cities have long been studied as engines of economic growth, drawing labour and capital

toward concentrated sites of production.
option_b_markdown:
A more recent literature has argued that the productivity of cities is best explained not by

their physical size but by the density of interactions they support.
option_c_markdown:
On this view, what matters is less the number of people in a city than the frequency and

variety of the encounters those people have with one another.
option_d_markdown:
Empirical studies tracking patent citations and start-up formation have produced evidence

consistent with this interaction-based account.
option_e_markdown:
Air pollution in dense urban areas has been linked to measurable declines in respiratory

health among long-term residents.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23. Answer: 5. Sentences 1–4 build a coherent argument: cities studied as growth engines
→ recent literature foregrounding interaction density → articulation of the interaction view →
empirical support from patents and start-ups. Sentence 5 is thematically close (urban density)
but moves to health effects, which is a different causal direction and a different evaluative
register. Its oddness is not topical novelty but a shift of causal frame.

question_text_markdown:
Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5.
Four of them, in some order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not
fit and enter its number.
option_a_markdown:
Reading aloud was the default mode of reading in much of antiquity, and silent reading was

sufficiently unusual to be remarked upon when encountered.
option_b_markdown:
The shift toward silent reading, which gathered pace in the medieval period, altered the social
setting of the activity: reading became something one could do alone, in private, without
disturbing others.
option_c_markdown:
It also changed the cognitive relationship between reader and text, since the voice no longer

mediated between the eye and the meaning.
option_d_markdown:
A reader silently absorbed in a manuscript was, in this new mode, in a kind of conversation

that left no audible trace.
option_e_markdown:
Manuscript production in monastic scriptoria peaked in the twelfth century before declining

with the spread of new copying techniques.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24. Answer: 5. Sentences 1–4 develop a coherent account of the shift from oral to silent
reading and its consequences for the reader-text relation. Sentence 5 introduces manuscript
production volumes in scriptoria — thematically adjacent (medieval reading culture) but ori-
ented toward production economics rather than the cognitive and social shift the other sentences
trace. The oddness arises from a shift of explanatory level.

2. The shift toward silent reading, which gathered pace in the medieval period, altered the social

passage_text_markdown:
To call a society’s relation to its past a matter of “memory” is already to make a quiet philo-
sophical choice. Memory, in the modern register, names a faculty that secures a private link
between a present self and an earlier self; what it stores is, by assumption, a chain of events that
did once unfold. The faculty is supposed to be answerable to a past that exists independently
of the act of remembering, and it is judged accurate or faulty against that standard. This view
feels self-evident only because it has been naturalised by centuries of secular literacy, in which
the writing down of dates and the keeping of records have come to define what counts as “what
really happened.”

Tribal communities, by contrast, are often described as having a defective or merely poetic
version of this faculty. Their songs telescope generations into a single afternoon; their genealogies
absorb foreign rivers and foreign animals into the family line; their dead speak, instruct, and
sometimes vote. Anthropologists, even sympathetic ones, have tended to treat these features
as ornaments around a residual core of “real” memory, as though under the embellishment one
would eventually find the same archive the modern subject keeps. The assumption, in other
words, is that tribal memory is modern memory plus distortion.

It is this assumption, rather than tribal practice, that deserves scrutiny. The point of a tribal
narrative is not to record a past that lies behind it but to constitute a present community by
enacting a continuity that the community needs in order to act. When a clan “remembers” an
ancestor who fought a river, the question of whether the encounter occurred in 1612 or 1812

 is not so much wrong as misplaced; the narrative is doing work for which calendrical accuracy
would be an irrelevance and, sometimes, a hindrance. The faculty at work is closer to what
we might call participation: the past is summoned in order to take part in the present, not
consulted as an external authority over it.

This is why the criterion of factual fidelity, when imported wholesale, produces such consistent
confusion. It is the wrong tool. One does not measure a prayer by its propositional content; nor
does one fault a song for failing to be an inventory. The deeper error, however, is to suppose that
the modern, archival relation to the past is itself simply the description of how memory works,
rather than one historically peculiar way of organising it. Once that supposition is loosened,
the tribal narrative ceases to look like a degraded version of modern memory and begins to look
like evidence that the relation between a community and what precedes it can be ordered along
axes the modern register does not register.

None of this requires us to romanticise. A society that “remembers” through participation can
also misremember, can also harden a useful fiction into a weapon. The point is only that the
standard by which it would be judged to misremember is not the archival one, and that to apply
the archival one is to ask a question to which the practice was never an answer.
question_text_markdown: The author refers to anthropologists who are “even sympathetic” primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: concede that scholarly opinion on tribal memory has steadily improved over time.
option_b_markdown:
observers.

suggest that the assumption being criticised is not confined to hostile or dismissive
option_c_markdown:
indicate that sympathetic scholarship has so far failed to gather adequate ethnographic

evidence.
option_d_markdown:
contrast modern scholarship with older colonial accounts that treated tribal memory as

mere distortion.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3 (RC1 — Author’s purpose in citing “even sympathetic” anthropologists)

Correct: (b). The qualifier “even sympathetic” is used to show that the assumption (tribal
memory = modern memory + distortion) survives in scholarship that is not hostile, i.e., the
problem is structural, not merely a matter of bias. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because the
contrast colonial-vs-modern is a familiar move; but the passage does not invoke colonial accounts
here. Distorted emphasis. (a) unsupported —no claim of steady improvement. (c) unsupported —
no claim about evidence adequacy.

passage_text_markdown:
To call a society’s relation to its past a matter of “memory” is already to make a quiet philo-
sophical choice. Memory, in the modern register, names a faculty that secures a private link
between a present self and an earlier self; what it stores is, by assumption, a chain of events that
did once unfold. The faculty is supposed to be answerable to a past that exists independently
of the act of remembering, and it is judged accurate or faulty against that standard. This view
feels self-evident only because it has been naturalised by centuries of secular literacy, in which
the writing down of dates and the keeping of records have come to define what counts as “what
really happened.”

Tribal communities, by contrast, are often described as having a defective or merely poetic
version of this faculty. Their songs telescope generations into a single afternoon; their genealogies
absorb foreign rivers and foreign animals into the family line; their dead speak, instruct, and
sometimes vote. Anthropologists, even sympathetic ones, have tended to treat these features
as ornaments around a residual core of “real” memory, as though under the embellishment one
would eventually find the same archive the modern subject keeps. The assumption, in other
words, is that tribal memory is modern memory plus distortion.

It is this assumption, rather than tribal practice, that deserves scrutiny. The point of a tribal
narrative is not to record a past that lies behind it but to constitute a present community by
enacting a continuity that the community needs in order to act. When a clan “remembers” an
ancestor who fought a river, the question of whether the encounter occurred in 1612 or 1812

 is not so much wrong as misplaced; the narrative is doing work for which calendrical accuracy
would be an irrelevance and, sometimes, a hindrance. The faculty at work is closer to what
we might call participation: the past is summoned in order to take part in the present, not
consulted as an external authority over it.

This is why the criterion of factual fidelity, when imported wholesale, produces such consistent
confusion. It is the wrong tool. One does not measure a prayer by its propositional content; nor
does one fault a song for failing to be an inventory. The deeper error, however, is to suppose that
the modern, archival relation to the past is itself simply the description of how memory works,
rather than one historically peculiar way of organising it. Once that supposition is loosened,
the tribal narrative ceases to look like a degraded version of modern memory and begins to look
like evidence that the relation between a community and what precedes it can be ordered along
axes the modern register does not register.

None of this requires us to romanticise. A society that “remembers” through participation can
also misremember, can also harden a useful fiction into a weapon. The point is only that the
standard by which it would be judged to misremember is not the archival one, and that to apply
the archival one is to ask a question to which the practice was never an answer.
question_text_markdown: Which one of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to draw?
option_a_markdown: Between a faculty that stores events and one that recovers them.
option_b_markdown:
Between memory understood as a record of an independent past and memory understood

as an act that constitutes a present community.
option_c_markdown: Between literate societies that value calendrical accuracy and oral societies that do not.
option_d_markdown: Between memory as a private function and memory as a collective one.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4 (RC1 — Distinction)

Correct: (b). The author’s distinction is between an archival faculty (memory as record of an
independent past) and a participatory one (memory as constitutive of the present community).
Runner-up: (d)—private vs. collective is a real distinction, and the passage gestures toward it,
but it is not the distinction the author is trying to draw ; true but not central. (a) too generic;
(c) reduces the distinction to a literate/oral contrast, which the passage explicitly resists.

as an act that constitutes a present community.

(c) Between literate societies that value calendrical accuracy and oral societies that do not.
(d) Between memory as a private function and memory as a collective one.

question_text_markdown: The author would be least likely to endorse which of the following claims?
option_a_markdown:
A tribal narrative that absorbs foreign rivers into a family genealogy may nonetheless

be doing exactly what it is supposed to do.
option_b_markdown:
Modern archival memory and tribal participation are two historically situated ways of

relating to what precedes a community.
option_c_markdown:
Tribal narratives, once their idiom is properly understood, can be shown to encode the

same factual content as archival records, only in a different form.
option_d_markdown:
The fact that a tribal account is calendrically inaccurate is, by itself, not a serious

objection to it.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q5 (RC1 — Least likely to endorse)

Correct: (c). The author’s whole point is that translating tribal narratives into “the same
factual content. . . in a different form” is the wrong move: tribal narrative is not modern memory
in disguise. Runner-up: (b)—an attractive paraphrase of the author’s position; the author would
endorse it. Selecting (b) here would require treating the author’s relativising move as something
they reject—the opposite of the text. (a), (d) are directly endorsed by the passage.

same factual content as archival records, only in a different form.

(d) The fact that a tribal account is calendrically inaccurate is, by itself, not a serious

objection to it.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most seriously undermine the author’s central
argument?
option_a_markdown:
Some tribal communities, on contact with literacy, switch rapidly to archival modes of

recording the past without apparent loss.
option_b_markdown:
Ethnographers have documented tribal practices in which elders are explicitly corrected
by other elders for getting dates and sequences wrong, and the community treats such corrections
 as binding.
option_c_markdown:
Modern archival societies sometimes harden useful fictions about their past into political

weapons, just as tribal societies do.
option_d_markdown:
The participatory mode of relating to the past can be found, in attenuated form, within

modern literate societies as well.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q6 (RC1 — Weaken)

Correct: (b). If communities themselves treat chronological correction as binding, then the
participatory account underestimates the role of factual fidelity from within the practice, un-
dercutting the author’s claim that the archival standard is simply external to it. Runner-up:
(a)—tempting: rapid switching to literacy looks like evidence that the modern mode is more
native than the author allows. But the passage allows that the two modes can coexist or succeed
each other historically; rapid adoption does not show that the practice being replaced was an
archival practice wit
source_raw_block:
Q6. Which of the following, if true, would most seriously undermine the author’s central
argument?

(a) Some tribal communities, on contact with literacy, switch rapidly to archival modes of

recording the past without apparent loss.

(b) Ethnographers have documented tribal practices in which elders are explicitly corrected
by other elders for getting dates and sequences wrong, and the community treats such corrections
 as binding.

(c) Modern archival societies sometimes harden useful fictions about their past into political

weapons, just as tribal societies do.

(d) The participatory mode of relating to the past can be found, in attenuated form, within

modern literate societies as well.

passage_text_markdown:
Suppose, for a moment, that the most consequential moral decisions of a society—who is sen-
tenced, who is treated, who is admitted—were delegated to a system whose verdicts could be
derived from a small set of explicit principles. The appeal of such a prospect is not, in the first
instance, technological. It is the appeal of a judge who cannot be bribed, who does not tire,
and whose reasons can be inspected line by line. To many proponents of “machine ethics,” this
is the underlying promise: not that a machine will be more compassionate than a human, but
that it will be more answerable.

The route to such a system, the argument runs, is the route physics once took. Newton
compressed a tangle of celestial and terrestrial observations into three laws;
later theorists
compressed these further. Ethical theory, on this picture, awaits its own act of compression.
Utilitarianism, deontology and the rest are read as competing axiom systems, each generating
verdicts in particular cases; the project is to identify the system whose verdicts best survive
scrutiny, and to encode it. The expected payoff is twofold. First, judgement becomes auditable.
Second—and more quietly—the embarrassment of moral disagreement is reframed as a soluble
technical problem.

The analogy is seductive but selective. In physics, two theories that disagree about a prediction
can be brought before an experiment that, in principle, both sides accept as decisive. In ethics,
the analogue does not exist. When utilitarian and deontological verdicts diverge, no further
fact about the case settles which verdict is correct; the disagreement is, in part, about what
would even count as settling it. To suppose otherwise is not to apply the method of physics to
ethics; it is to import the appearance of that method while leaving its evidential substructure
behind.

A subtler worry is that the compression itself is a form of loss. Moral attention, as it is actually
exercised, attends to features of a case—a hesitation, a history, an unspoken obligation—that
resist statement as variables. A formal system can be designed only on features that can be
stated; whatever escapes statement is, for the system, absent. The result is not that the system
fails to capture morality but that it succeeds at capturing a sub-version of morality, and then is
taken to be morality. The danger is therefore not error in the ordinary sense. It is the gradual
substitution, in public life, of what is auditable for what is morally relevant.

None of this is an argument against machine ethics as such. There are domains—scheduling,
triage under known constraints, certain classes of administrative decision—where the gains in
consistency are real and the residual loss is modest. The argument is, rather, against the picture
in which machine ethics is the late-arriving successor of an old project, completing what physics
began. That picture flatters the project by misdescribing its difficulty. It treats the absence
of a decisive ethical experiment as a temporary embarrassment; in fact, it is the feature that
makes ethics what it is.

Q7 (RC2 — Function of paragraph 3)

Correct: (b). The paragraph identifies a structural disanalogy: in physics, disagreements can
be brought before mutually accepted experiments; in ethics, they cannot. This blocks the picture
in which ethics merely awaits its Newtonian compression. Runner-up: (d)—the paragraph does
suggest moral disagreement is not settled by further fact, but it does not commit to the strong
claim that ethical disagreement is “in principle irresoluble.” Change in degree of certainty /
overstatement.

question_text_markdown: The author’s attitude toward machine ethics is best described as:
option_a_markdown:
broadly hostile, on the grounds that delegating moral judgement to machines is intrin-

sically dangerous.
option_b_markdown:
cautiously favourable, holding that machine ethics is the natural completion of the

project physics began.
option_c_markdown:
discriminating: receptive to limited applications, but opposed to the larger picture in

which machine ethics “completes” ethics.
option_d_markdown:
ambivalent, citing both technical promise and unresolved disagreement as roughly equal

considerations.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q8 (RC2 — Author’s attitude)

Correct: (c). The closing paragraph explicitly allows applications where “gains in consistency
are real,” while rejecting the larger “completing what physics began” picture. Runner-up: (a)—
tempting because the bulk of the passage is critical; but “intrinsically dangerous” is stronger
than the author’s position, and ignores the qualifications in the final paragraph. Overstatement

12


/ scope. (b) reverses the author’s position; (d) flattens a structured view into a balance-sheet
ambivalence.
