question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument in the fourth
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
Most everyday moral decisions in fact turn on a small set of features that can be stated

explicitly, with the unstatable residue making no systematic difference to outcomes.
option_b_markdown:
Many ordinary citizens, when asked, prefer machine-generated verdicts to human ones

in low-stakes administrative contexts.
option_c_markdown:
Even within physics, certain phenomena—turbulence, for example—resist compression

into closed-form principles.
option_d_markdown:
Formal ethical systems can be designed to flag uncertainty and refer hard cases to

human judgement.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q9 (RC2 — Weaken paragraph 4)

Correct: (a). The fourth paragraph’s argument is that formal systems necessarily lose features
that resist statement, and that this loss is morally consequential. If most decisions in fact turn
on a small set of statable features and the residue is systematically inconsequential, the author’s
loss-claim collapses. Runner-up: (c)—tempting because turbulence is a real case of in-physics
non-compressibility; but the author’s paragraph 4 argument is about ethics, not about the
physics analogy, which paragraph 3 already addressed. Answers a nearby but different question.

in which machine ethics is the late-arriving successor of an old project, completing what physics
began. That picture flatters the project by misdescribing its difficulty. It treats the absence
of a decisive ethical experiment as a temporary embarrassment; in fact, it is the feature that
makes ethics what it is.

option_a_markdown: demonstrating that machine ethics is technically infeasible.
option_b_markdown:
contrasting the auditability of formal systems with the opacity of human moral judge-

ment.
option_c_markdown:
arguing that an attractive analogy between physics and ethics misdescribes the difficulty

of ethics and risks confusing what is auditable with what is morally relevant.
option_d_markdown: defending moral disagreement as a positive social good in pluralist democracies.

Q10 (RC2 — Central concern)

Correct: (c). The passage’s structural movement is: appeal of machine ethics → physics
analogy → disanalogy → compression-as-loss → a more honest picture. (c) captures both the
analogy critique and the auditability-vs-relevance worry. Runner-up: (a)—tempting, since the
passage is critical; but it makes a feasibility claim the author conspicuously avoids. The author
objects to a picture of the project, not to its technical possibility. Distorted emphasis. (b)
gets the contrast backwards (auditability is what both promise; the worry is that auditability
supplants relevan

Q10. The passage as a whole is primarily concerned with:

(a) demonstrating that machine ethics is technically infeasible.
(b) contrasting the auditability of formal systems with the opacity of human moral judge-

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, the large dam was less an engineering choice than a political
vocabulary. Nations as different as the United States, the Soviet Union, Egypt and India spoke
about their dams in the same idiom: of conquest, of awakening, of finally making the river do
useful work. The structure itself was secondary to the sentence it allowed a state to utter about
itself. A country that could pour that much concrete across that much water was, by the very
act, declaring its arrival.

It is tempting, in hindsight, to read this enthusiasm as straightforward error—an ecological
mistake corrected by later science. That reading is comforting but partial. The mistake, if
it was one, was not primarily about hydrology. The science of sediment, of fish migration, of
saline intrusion into deltas, was reasonably well understood even in the 1950s; what was new
was not the knowledge but the willingness to discount it. The dam project absorbed the costs
as the price of a larger settlement: a nation reconciled to its own ambition.

Here a distinction is worth making. To object to a particular dam on the grounds that its costs
 exceeded its benefits is one kind of argument. To object to the dam-building era on the grounds
that the cost–benefit framework was itself the wrong instrument is a different one. The first
treats the era as a series of bad calculations. The second treats it as a series of calculations from
which certain costs had been excluded in advance. The displaced cultivator, the silted estuary,
the village whose name vanishes from the new reservoir map—these did not appear as negative
entries in the ledger because they had not been admitted as entries at all.

It would be wrong, however, to present this exclusion as cynical. The planners did not, on
the whole, deceive themselves; they had a working theory under which the displaced were
temporary inconveniences to be compensated and the silted estuary was the affair of a different
ministry. The theory was coherent. It was also, in retrospect, the political form of the era:
the assumption that a sufficiently large public benefit licensed the unilateral redistribution of
private and ecological harm onto those least able to refuse it.

Critics of the dam-building era sometimes overstate the moral. The lesson is not that all large
infrastructure is suspect, nor that nature is to be left alone.
It is, more narrowly, that the
project of large infrastructure tends to recruit a particular kind of accounting—one in which
the harms most likely to be diffuse, slow, and borne by the least powerful are also the harms
least likely to be counted. Where such an accounting is the only accounting in the room, the
resulting decisions will reliably look prudent on the books and indefensible on the ground.

A serious successor to the dam-building era, then, is not a smaller dam. It is an accounting
that is harder to deceive.
question_text_markdown: The author’s primary purpose in the passage is to:
option_a_markdown:
document the ecological harms that twentieth-century dam-building inflicted on rivers

and the communities depending on them.
option_b_markdown:
argue that the central failing of the dam-building era lay not in faulty calculation but

in the prior exclusion of certain costs from the calculation altogether.
option_c_markdown:
defend the dam-building era as a coherent expression of mid-century political aspiration,

while conceding its environmental costs.
option_d_markdown:
recommend smaller, more localised infrastructure projects as a corrective to the excesses

of large dams.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q11 (RC3 — Primary purpose)

Correct: (b). The author’s central argument is the second-paragraph turn: the failing was not
bad arithmetic but a prior exclusion of certain costs. Runner-up: (c)—tempting because the
author treats mid-century planners with restraint and grants them coherence; but the author’s
purpose is critical, not defensive. Confuses concession with conclusion. (a) reduces a normative
argument to documentation. (d) makes a prescriptive recommendation the author explicitly
resists (“a serious successor. . . is not a smaller dam”).
source_raw_block:

(a) document the ecological harms that twentieth-century dam-building inflicted on rivers

and the communities depending on them.

(b) argue that the central failing of the dam-building era lay not in faulty calculation but

in the prior exclusion of certain costs from the calculation altogether.

(c) defend the dam-building era as a coherent expression of mid-century political aspiration,

while conceding its environmental costs.

(d) recommend smaller, more localised infrastructure projects as a corrective to the excesses

of large dams.

question_text_markdown: The author refers to the dam as a “political vocabulary” primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: suggest that dams were always more rhetorical than functional.
option_b_markdown:
indicate that the appeal of large dams lay partly in what their construction allowed a

state to say about itself, independent of any particular engineering need.
option_c_markdown: criticise mid-century states for prioritising propaganda over development.
option_d_markdown:
draw a parallel between the language of dam-building and the language of military

conquest.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q12 (RC3 — “Political vocabulary”)

Correct: (b). The phrase is used to register that the structure mattered for what it allowed the
state to say, independent of functional need. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because the language
of “conquest” invites a military parallel; but the author uses the phrase to make a constitutive,
not a comparative, point. True but not central. (a) overstates (“always more rhetorical than
functional”); (c) makes a moral charge the author does not.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not support the author’s
central claim?
option_a_markdown: The harms of dam-building tended to fall on those least able to refuse them.
option_b_markdown:
Mid-century planners did not, on the whole, deceive themselves about the theoretical

framework they were operating within.
option_c_markdown: The science of sediment and fish migration was reasonably well understood by the 1950s.
option_d_markdown: A serious successor to the dam-building era would be an accounting harder to deceive.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q13 (RC3 — True but not supporting the central claim)

Correct: (c). That the relevant science was already understood is true (it is in paragraph 2),
but it functions as background—the author’s claim is about the accounting, not about scientific
ignorance. Runner-up: (b)—a tempting choice because mid-century planners’ self-honesty is
also a concession; but it directly supports the author’s argument that the failure was structural
rather than self-deception. Confuses concession that supports the argument with one that is
incidental. (a) and (d) both directly support the central claim.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following would the author be most likely to agree with?
option_a_markdown:
Cost–benefit analysis is, in principle, an unsuitable tool for evaluating public infrastruc-

ture.
option_b_markdown:
A correctly performed cost–benefit analysis would have prevented most of the harms

associated with twentieth-century dams.
option_c_markdown:
Cost–benefit analyses of large infrastructure are characteristically shaped by which
harms have been admitted as costs in advance, and this prior admission, more than the arith-
metic, determines the verdict.
option_d_markdown:
Ecological harms are intrinsically more important than the economic benefits of infras-

tructure and should always be weighted accordingly.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q14 (RC3 — Author would agree)

Correct: (c). This restates the second-paragraph turn precisely: the verdict depends on which
harms are admitted as costs in advance. Runner-up: (a)—tempting because the author criticises
cost–benefit analysis as it was used; but the author’s complaint is about the admission of costs,
not about the framework’s intrinsic suitability. Scope shift from operational to principled. (b)
too strong (“would have prevented most”). (d) makes a prescriptive ranking the author does
not endorse.
source_raw_block:
Q14. Which of the following would the author be most likely to agree with?
 (a) Cost–benefit analysis is, in principle, an unsuitable tool for evaluating public infrastruc-

ture.

(b) A correctly performed cost–benefit analysis would have prevented most of the harms

associated with twentieth-century dams.

(c) Cost–benefit analyses of large infrastructure are characteristically shaped by which
harms have been admitted as costs in advance, and this prior admission, more than the arith-
metic, determines the verdict.

(d) Ecological harms are intrinsically more important than the economic benefits of infras-

tructure and should always be weighted accordingly.

question_text_markdown:
Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1 to 4, when arranged in
the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the four-digit sequence.
option_a_markdown:
Surveys nonetheless suggest that the share of the relevant population using “Latinx” in
self-description remains a small minority, well below the share that uses “Hispanic” or
“Latino.”
option_b_markdown:
The term “Latinx” emerged from activists who sought a self-designation that would not

impose the grammatical gender built into Spanish.
option_c_markdown:
Their objection was not that “Latino” was offensive in any direct sense but that its default-

masculine form silently absorbed everyone who did not fit a binary frame.
option_d_markdown:
Whether this gap reflects resistance to the term, unfamiliarity with it, or the slow tempo

at which any self-designation diffuses through a community remains contested.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17 (Para Jumble — Latinx)

Correct order: 2–3–1–4. Sentence 2 introduces the term and its origin. Sentence 3 elaborates
the activists’ specific objection. Sentence 1 introduces the contrasting fact (low adoption).
Sentence 4 closes by problematising the gap.
Why not 2–3–4–1: 4 (“Whether this gap reflects. . . ”) presupposes that the gap has already
been stated; the gap is stated in 1, so 1 must precede 4. 2 must open (defines the term); 3 must
follow 2 (“their objection” refers to the activists in 2).
source_raw_block:
Q17. Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1 to 4, when arranged in
the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the four-digit sequence.

1. Surveys nonetheless suggest that the share of the relevant population using “Latinx” in
self-description remains a small minority, well below the share that uses “Hispanic” or
“Latino.”

2. The term “Latinx” emerged from activists who sought a self-designation that would not

impose the grammatical gender built into Spanish.

3. Their objection was not that “Latino” was offensive in any direct sense but that its default-

masculine form silently absorbed everyone who did not fit a binary frame.

4. Whether this gap reflects resistance to the term, unfamiliarity with it, or the slow tempo

at which any self-designation diffuses through a community remains contested.

question_text_markdown:
Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1 to 5. Four of
them, in a particular order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not fit.
Enter the sentence number.
option_a_markdown:
Standard models of deep-sea chemistry have long treated the lightless seafloor as a place

where oxygen is consumed but not produced.
option_b_markdown:
Recent measurements at depths of around 4,000 metres in the Pacific, however, have de-

tected oxygen being produced in the absence of any photosynthesis.
 3. The source appears to be the polymetallic nodules that carpet parts of the abyssal floor,

whose surfaces seem to drive a slow electrochemical splitting of seawater.
option_c_markdown:
If the finding holds up under replication, it would complicate plans to mine these nodules,
since their removal would also remove a previously unsuspected source of oxygen for deep-
sea life.
option_d_markdown:
Phytoplankton near the ocean surface remain, by a large margin, the principal producers

of oceanic oxygen and the basis of most marine food webs.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18 (Odd Sentence Out — Deep-sea Oxygen)

Correct: 5. The coherent sequence is 1–2–3–4: standard view (1) → anomalous finding (2)
→ proposed mechanism (3) → practical implication (4). Sentence 5, while topically related to
oceanic oxygen, returns to the surface ecosystem and the conventional picture, and so steps
outside the paragraph’s specific argumentative movement about a surprising deep-sea source.
It is a different stage of argument (background reassurance rather than the anomalous finding’s
development).
source_raw_block:
Q18. Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1 to 5. Four of
them, in a particular order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not fit.
Enter the sentence number.

1. Standard models of deep-sea chemistry have long treated the lightless seafloor as a place

where oxygen is consumed but not produced.

2. Recent measurements at depths of around 4,000 metres in the Pacific, however, have de-

tected oxygen being produced in the absence of any photosynthesis.
 3. The source appears to be the polymetallic nodules that carpet parts of the abyssal floor,

whose surfaces seem to drive a slow electrochemical splitting of seawater.

4. If the finding holds up under replication, it would complicate plans to mine these nodules,
since their removal would also remove a previously unsuspected source of oxygen for deep-
sea life.

5. Phytoplankton near the ocean surface remain, by a large margin, the principal producers

of oceanic oxygen and the basis of most marine food webs.

question_text_markdown:
Sentence Placement. The paragraph below has four possible insertion points marked
[A], [B], [C] and [D]. Read the paragraph and the standalone sentence, and choose the insertion
point at which the sentence best fits.

Standalone sentence: “Productivity gains, once expected to feed through to broader living
standards, now primarily serve to enhance returns to wealth.”

Paragraph: Public discussion of inequality remains dominated by figures on income. [A] Yet, in
most advanced economies, the gap between high and median incomes has widened far less than
the gap between large and median holdings of wealth. [B] The reasons are partly demographic
and partly institutional: an ageing population accumulates assets, and the tax treatment of
capital has become more favourable relative to labour over recent decades.
[C] These shifts
have proved difficult to register in standard economic indicators, which were designed for an
economy in which the principal source of household prosperity was the wage. [D] If the indicator
continues to lag the phenomenon, policy will continue to address symptoms rather than causes.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19 (Sentence Placement — Inequality)

Correct: [B]. The sentence introduces a causal mechanism connecting widening wealth gaps
to where productivity gains now accrue. It fits naturally after the empirical observation (income
vs. wealth gap) and before the demographic/institutional reasons paragraph develops.
Why not [A]: too early—no claim about wealth has yet been set up.
Why not [C]: would interrupt the connected reasons (“demographic and institutional. . . ” →

14


“These shifts. . . ”).
Why not [D]: the paragraph’s closing already turns to indicators and policy; inserting a mech-
anism here
source_raw_block:

the gap between large and median holdings of wealth. [B] The reasons are partly demographic

capital has become more favourable relative to labour over recent decades.
[C] These shifts

continues to lag the phenomenon, policy will continue to address symptoms rather than causes.

(a) [A]
(b) [B]
(c) [C]
(d) [D]

question_text_markdown:
Para Summary. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the
option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Studies of a Dutch kindred in which several males exhibited episodic, severe aggression eventually
localised the responsible defect to a small region of the X chromosome. The discovery was widely
reported as the identification of a “gene for aggression,” a phrasing the original investigators
conspicuously avoided. Their reticence reflected a methodological point that the popular framing
overran: the affected males in this particular kindred shared a particular defect; from this it
did not follow that aggression in general had a single genetic source, or that other aggressive
individuals would carry the same defect, or that the defect, in a different developmental and social
setting, would produce the same behaviour. The finding was about a kindred. The headlines were
about a species.
option_a_markdown:
Aggression in humans cannot be reduced to a single genetic cause, and the Dutch study

showed this.
option_b_markdown:
The popular reporting of the Dutch study mistook a finding about one kindred for a
finding about aggression in general, and the investigators’ careful phrasing was meant to prevent
precisely this slippage.
option_c_markdown:
Genetic research on behaviour is routinely misreported, and the Dutch aggression study

is a notable example.
option_d_markdown:
The link between an X-chromosome defect and aggression holds only within the specific

developmental and social setting of the Dutch kindred.

detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20 (Para Summary — Genetic Aggression)

Correct: (b). The paragraph’s whole point is the mismatch between what the study showed
(a kindred-specific defect) and how it was reported (a gene for aggression in general), with the
investigators’ careful phrasing aimed at preventing exactly that slippage. Runner-up: (d)—
tempting because the paragraph does suggest the finding was restricted to its setting; but
(d) overstates by claiming the link holds only in the Dutch setting—the passage’s point is
methodological caution, not a settled scope claim. Change in degree of certainty. (a) restates
a true gen

Q20. Para Summary. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the
option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Studies of a Dutch kindred in which several males exhibited episodic, severe aggression eventually
localised the responsible defect to a small region of the X chromosome. The discovery was widely
reported as the identification of a “gene for aggression,” a phrasing the original investigators
conspicuously avoided. Their reticence reflected a methodological point that the popular framing
overran: the affected males in this particular kindred shared a particular defect; from this it
did not follow that aggression in general had a single genetic source, or that other aggressive
individuals would carry the same defect, or that the defect, in a different developmental and social
setting, would produce the same behaviour. The finding was about a kindred. The headlines were
about a species.

(a) Aggression in humans cannot be reduced to a single genetic cause, and the Dutch study

showed this.

(b) The popular reporting of the Dutch study mistook a finding about one kindred for a
finding about aggression in general, and the investigators’ careful phrasing was meant to prevent
precisely this slippage.

(c) Genetic research on behaviour is routinely misreported, and the Dutch aggression study

is a notable example.

(d) The link between an X-chromosome defect and aggression holds only within the specific

developmental and social setting of the Dutch kindred.

passage_text_markdown:
The Indian Forest Act of 1878 was, in form, a statute about trees. In effect, it was a statute
about the relation between a centralising state and the populations that lived where the trees
stood. Drafted in the wake of a rapid expansion of railway construction, it converted vast
tracts of wooded land, much of it under customary common use, into property of the colonial
state, classified into “reserved,” “protected” and “village” categories. The categories were
administratively neat; on the ground they amounted to the same thing. The state acquired the
standing right to decide which trees could be felled, by whom, and on what terms.

Historians sometimes describe the 1878 Act as an instrument of timber extraction. This is true
but insufficient. Timber was the immediate occasion, but the more durable achievement of the
Act was the routinisation of a particular form of authority: a bureaucratic relation in which
the cultivator’s grazing route, the herbalist’s foraging path, and the cremation ground at the
edge of the village were all rendered legible—and therefore alterable—by a salaried officer who
had never met the people concerned. What was extracted, in the long run, was not principally
timber. It was discretion.

It is on this point that the 1982 draft, which sought to update the colonial framework, ran
into a controversy disproportionate to its overt provisions. The draft tightened restrictions and
increased penalties, but its substantive offence was structural: it preserved, almost intact, the
colonial assumption that forest dwellers stood in relation to the forest as trespassers on state
property unless specifically licensed otherwise. By 1982 a substantial political movement existed
that had begun to think of that assumption itself as the problem. The criticism, in other words,
was not that the 1982 draft was harsher than its predecessor, but that it was, in the relevant
sense, the same.

Defenders of the bureaucratic framework have often replied that without centralised control,
forest cover would collapse under local pressure. The reply has a real point. Decentralisation
administered carelessly has, in several regions, accelerated rather than slowed deforestation.
But this misses the question being asked. The question is not whether decentralised regimes are
uniformly successful—they are not—but whether the framework that treats local communities as
presumptive offenders against their own surroundings can ever produce sustained custodianship.
The empirical record suggests that it cannot, not because the framework lacks technical capacity
but because it lacks the participatory legitimacy without which technical capacity in this domain
corrodes.

A more honest reading of the 1982 episode, then, would treat it less as a failed updating
and more as the moment at which the legitimacy deficit of a century-old framework became
politically unignorable. What the controversy revealed was not chiefly that a particular draft
was poorly written but that the inherited template, regardless of who held the pen, could no
longer be improved by amendment alone.
 Q21. Which of the following best captures the central claim of the passage?

(a) The 1878 Act was a colonial measure for timber extraction, while the 1982 draft sought,

unsuccessfully, to correct it.

(b) The deeper continuity between the 1878 Act and the 1982 draft lay in a shared structural
assumption about authority over forests, and it was this continuity, more than any specific
provision, that the 1982 controversy targeted.

(c) The 1982 draft failed because it imposed harsher penalties than the 1878 Act without

granting forest dwellers commensurate rights.

(d) Decentralised forest management would have prevented both colonial overreach and the

controversy of 1982.
question_text_markdown:
The author’s statement that “what was extracted, in the long run, was not principally
timber. It was discretion” is best understood as:
option_a_markdown: a rhetorical exaggeration; the principal extraction was, in fact, timber.
option_b_markdown:
a claim that the lasting consequence of the 1878 Act was not the removal of forest
products but the relocation of routine decision-making about forests away from the people who
lived in them.
option_c_markdown:
a suggestion that the colonial state lacked the administrative capacity for sustained

timber extraction.
option_d_markdown: a complaint that bureaucratic officials made arbitrary decisions for personal gain.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22 (RC4 — “not principally timber. It was discretion”)

Correct: (b). The line claims that the durable consequence of the Act was the relocation
of routine decisions away from local actors. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because “discretion”
invites a connotation of arbitrariness; but the passage makes a structural, not a moral-character,
claim. Distorted emphasis. (a) misreads the line as hyperbole; (c) reverses the claim.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument in the fourth
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
In several regions where decentralised regimes were instituted carelessly, deforestation

accelerated.
option_b_markdown:
Bureaucratic forest management is more efficient than community-based management

in producing audited annual yield figures.
option_c_markdown:
Forest cover in regions where local communities were granted long-term custodial rights
has, over recent decades, remained stable or improved relative to comparable regions under
centralised administration.
option_d_markdown:
Cases exist in which community-managed forests have suffered local overuse during

periods of acute economic stress.

(e) (Note: option not used—ignore.)

(Choose from (a) to (d).)
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23 (RC4 — Weaken paragraph 4)

Correct: (c). The fourth paragraph’s claim is that the framework that treats locals as pre-
sumptive offenders cannot produce sustained custodianship. Stable or improving forest cover un-
der long-term community custodianship is direct empirical pressure against that claim. Runner-
up: (a)—this is acknowledged by the author as a real point; it does not weaken the argument
the author is making. (b) is irrelevant (efficiency of audit is not the issue). (d) is what the
author already concedes (decentralisation is not uniformly successful).

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following best describes the author’s view of the defenders of the bureau-
cratic framework discussed in the fourth paragraph?
option_a_markdown: They are wholly mistaken, since centralised control has consistently failed.
option_b_markdown:
They make a point that is partially valid but that addresses a question different from

the one the author considers central.
option_c_markdown:
They correctly identify the only realistic alternative to deforestation, even if their tone

is dismissive.
option_d_markdown:
They have been refuted by the empirical record, which shows that decentralisation

always succeeds.
 PART B:
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24 (RC4 — Defenders of the bureaucratic framework)

Correct: (b). The author writes that the defenders’ reply “has a real point” but “misses the
question being asked.” Runner-up: (a)—tempting because the author is ultimately critical; but
“wholly mistaken” overstates. Overstatement. (c) reverses by treating bureaucratic control as
the only realistic alternative, which the author resists. (d) attributes to the author a strong
empirical claim (“always succeeds”) the author does not make.

passage_text_markdown:
To call the tribal imagination ‘hallucinatory’ is convenient shorthand, but the term carries a
polemical risk. It implies a deficit–a failure to reach the orderly daylight of secular, sequential
reasoning–when what is in fact at issue is a dierent settlement between consciousness and its
two great encounters: space and time. The image-making faculty, that genetic endowment by
which the mind packages space into pictures, is shared by all human cultures. What diers is
the relative weight cultures place upon imagination and upon memory. In societies that have
accepted a secular mode of creativity–in which the maker, no longer a vessel for transcendent
forces, replaces God–imaginative transactions become self-conscious, framed, and accountable
to fixed conventions of plot, perspective, and proportion. The tribal storyteller works under
a dierent settlement. Lacking the inducement to dominate territorial space–a dominion that
history seems early to have placed beyond reach–tribal communities turned, perhaps obsessively,
toward dominion over time. The ritual conversation with ancestors, the painted clay figure that
becomes momentarily inhabited, the trance through which the dead are heard: these are not
picturesque survivals but disciplined practices for collapsing temporal distance.

This is why it is misleading to describe tribal narration as primitive.
It is, on the contrary,
the product of a long, exact training of memory. Over generations, an astonishingly precise
 classificatory system–of plants, soils, weather signs, dialects of animal call–has been carried
not in archives but in the rehearsed bodies and voices of singers. Where the modern reader
instinctively distinguishes the literal from the figurative, the tribal narrator does not: a star
may grow like a vine, a mountain swim like a fish, an animal speak in the dialect of a neighbour,
because the narrative motif and the emotion it carries are bound to each other before they are
bound to any ontological commitment about kind. To call this a fusion of planes is accurate; to
call it a confusion is to import the wrong test.

It does not follow that tribal art has no conventions. The frame, however, is unusually elastic.
An epic may open from a trifling domestic incident; a painting may continue across the wall of
a hut, the post of a granary, the lid of a pot–the boundary between art and non-art becoming,
on principle, invisible. Episodes follow not the logic of sequence but the logic of association: the
chaotic geometry of dreams. The grammar of painting and the syntax of language draw nearer
to each other than they do in the literate tradition, as if the same impulse produced painted
words at one end and a song of images at the other. To insist that this constitutes the absence
of rule is to mistake an unfamiliar grammar for none at all.

[Q1 | RC — Main Argument | Hard | MCQ]

The author’s central contention in the passage is that:
question_text_markdown:
tribal narratives are governed by a flexible but principled grammar that critics misread

because it is unfamiliar.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Correct: Option 1

The passage moves from rebutting ‘hallucinatory’ as a deficit-label, through the contrast between
dominion-over-time and dominion-over-space, to the explicit closing claim that ‘to insist that
this constitutes the absence of rule is to mistake an unfamiliar grammar for none at all.’ The
central contention is therefore that tribal narration has its own grammar, which only looks
lawless from outside.

Why the others fail.
• Option 2 is true in the passage but operates one level down–it is a supporting historical

point, not the central contention. Scope: too narrow.

• Option 3 ranks the two modes as inferior/superior, a comparison the author explicitly avoids:

‘a dierent settlement,’ not a deficient one. Distortion.

• Option 4 over-generalises from ‘tribal’ to ‘all non-modern cultures.’ Scope: too broad.

1. tribal narratives are governed by a flexible but principled grammar that critics misread

because it is unfamiliar.

passage_text_markdown:
To call the tribal imagination ‘hallucinatory’ is convenient shorthand, but the term carries a
polemical risk. It implies a deficit–a failure to reach the orderly daylight of secular, sequential
reasoning–when what is in fact at issue is a dierent settlement between consciousness and its
two great encounters: space and time. The image-making faculty, that genetic endowment by
which the mind packages space into pictures, is shared by all human cultures. What diers is
the relative weight cultures place upon imagination and upon memory. In societies that have
accepted a secular mode of creativity–in which the maker, no longer a vessel for transcendent
forces, replaces God–imaginative transactions become self-conscious, framed, and accountable
to fixed conventions of plot, perspective, and proportion. The tribal storyteller works under
a dierent settlement. Lacking the inducement to dominate territorial space–a dominion that
history seems early to have placed beyond reach–tribal communities turned, perhaps obsessively,
toward dominion over time. The ritual conversation with ancestors, the painted clay figure that
becomes momentarily inhabited, the trance through which the dead are heard: these are not
picturesque survivals but disciplined practices for collapsing temporal distance.

This is why it is misleading to describe tribal narration as primitive.
It is, on the contrary,
the product of a long, exact training of memory. Over generations, an astonishingly precise
 classificatory system–of plants, soils, weather signs, dialects of animal call–has been carried
not in archives but in the rehearsed bodies and voices of singers. Where the modern reader
instinctively distinguishes the literal from the figurative, the tribal narrator does not: a star
may grow like a vine, a mountain swim like a fish, an animal speak in the dialect of a neighbour,
because the narrative motif and the emotion it carries are bound to each other before they are
bound to any ontological commitment about kind. To call this a fusion of planes is accurate; to
call it a confusion is to import the wrong test.

It does not follow that tribal art has no conventions. The frame, however, is unusually elastic.
An epic may open from a trifling domestic incident; a painting may continue across the wall of
a hut, the post of a granary, the lid of a pot–the boundary between art and non-art becoming,
on principle, invisible. Episodes follow not the logic of sequence but the logic of association: the
chaotic geometry of dreams. The grammar of painting and the syntax of language draw nearer
to each other than they do in the literate tradition, as if the same impulse produced painted
words at one end and a song of images at the other. To insist that this constitutes the absence
of rule is to mistake an unfamiliar grammar for none at all.

passage_text_markdown:
Imagine that we delegated our gravest moral choices–sentencing, triage, the apportionment of
scarce organs–to a system unfatigued by hunger, unswayed by friendship, and indierent to
the social standing of those it judged. The picture is seductive precisely because it promises to
subtract from moral decision the very features we most resent in human judges: their irritability,
their selective attention, their susceptibility to flattery. Yet the seduction is also the snare. A
system that subtracts the human element will, at best, replicate our judgments with greater
consistency.
It will not improve them. To improve a moral judgment is to bring to it some
quality the original lacked; mere consistency is a form of fidelity, and faithful reproduction of a
flawed pattern is not its correction.

The deeper worry, however, is not about bias. It is about the formalisability of moral reasoning
itself. To formalise a domain is to identify a small set of axioms and to derive from them, by
rule, an indefinitely large set of consequences. Physics has lived on this discipline for centuries.
Its theories disagree–Aristotelian natural motion is incompatible with Newtonian gravitation,
and both are incomplete relative to general relativity–but each theory shares the architectural
 pattern: postulates above, consequences below, the upper level determining the lower. Ethics,
the analogy runs, is similarly structured. Utilitarianism takes the maximisation of aggregate well-
being as its foundational claim, and from this derives subsidiary principles about distribution,
sacrifice, and the assessment of consequences. Other systems take other axioms–the inviolability
of persons, the primacy of duties, the priority of the worst-o–and derive their own corollaries.
Each can be programmed; each, given enough computation, can be carried into territory that
no single human ethicist could reason through unaided.

But the analogy with physics is precisely what should make us cautious. Physical theories
describe a domain whose objects are unaected by the description; an electron behaves as it
does whether or not we have correctly formalised its behaviour. Moral judgments concern
human acts, and human acts are answerable not only to principles but to context, history, and
the particular face of the person before us. The features that resist formalisation–intuition
seasoned by exposure, the capacity to recognise that a given case is, in a way that matters,
unlike the case before it–are not ornaments around the rational core of ethics. They may be
that core. If they are, then to formalise ethics is not to extend its reach but to amputate the
very faculty that made moral reflection possible. The risk is not that a formalised ethics will
mirror our limitations; it is that, in mirroring only what can be formalised, it will fail to mirror
our practice at all.

[Q1 | RC — Main Argument | Hard | MCQ]

The author’s central contention in the passage is that:
question_text_markdown:
tribal communities have refined memory and ritual to compensate for an inability to control

physical territory.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Correct: Option 3

The author’s central claim about tribal narration is that it is the disciplined product of a long
training of memory. To weaken this, we need evidence that the apparent features of tribal
narration are not the output of a stable internal grammar.

Why Option 3 is the strongest weakener. If the fusion-of-planes feature disappears when-
ever a non-tribal audience is present, the feature is contingent on audience rather than inter-
nalised as discipline. That directly cuts against the author’s claim.

Why the others fail.
• Option 1 (variation across communities) is consistent with locally trained but disciplined

traditions; it does not undermine the discipline claim.
 • Option 2 (written archives co-existing with oral) supplements rather than undermines–it

would suggest the system is even more robust.

• Option 4 (associative structure in some secular works) attacks the contrast between tribal
and secular, not the discipline claim about tribal narration specifically. True-but-irrelevant
trap.

2. tribal communities have refined memory and ritual to compensate for an inability to control

physical territory.

passage_text_markdown:
To call the tribal imagination ‘hallucinatory’ is convenient shorthand, but the term carries a
polemical risk. It implies a deficit–a failure to reach the orderly daylight of secular, sequential
reasoning–when what is in fact at issue is a dierent settlement between consciousness and its
two great encounters: space and time. The image-making faculty, that genetic endowment by
which the mind packages space into pictures, is shared by all human cultures. What diers is
the relative weight cultures place upon imagination and upon memory. In societies that have
accepted a secular mode of creativity–in which the maker, no longer a vessel for transcendent
forces, replaces God–imaginative transactions become self-conscious, framed, and accountable
to fixed conventions of plot, perspective, and proportion. The tribal storyteller works under
a dierent settlement. Lacking the inducement to dominate territorial space–a dominion that
history seems early to have placed beyond reach–tribal communities turned, perhaps obsessively,
toward dominion over time. The ritual conversation with ancestors, the painted clay figure that
becomes momentarily inhabited, the trance through which the dead are heard: these are not
picturesque survivals but disciplined practices for collapsing temporal distance.

This is why it is misleading to describe tribal narration as primitive.
It is, on the contrary,
the product of a long, exact training of memory. Over generations, an astonishingly precise
 classificatory system–of plants, soils, weather signs, dialects of animal call–has been carried
not in archives but in the rehearsed bodies and voices of singers. Where the modern reader
instinctively distinguishes the literal from the figurative, the tribal narrator does not: a star
may grow like a vine, a mountain swim like a fish, an animal speak in the dialect of a neighbour,
because the narrative motif and the emotion it carries are bound to each other before they are
bound to any ontological commitment about kind. To call this a fusion of planes is accurate; to
call it a confusion is to import the wrong test.

It does not follow that tribal art has no conventions. The frame, however, is unusually elastic.
An epic may open from a trifling domestic incident; a painting may continue across the wall of
a hut, the post of a granary, the lid of a pot–the boundary between art and non-art becoming,
on principle, invisible. Episodes follow not the logic of sequence but the logic of association: the
chaotic geometry of dreams. The grammar of painting and the syntax of language draw nearer
to each other than they do in the literate tradition, as if the same impulse produced painted
words at one end and a song of images at the other. To insist that this constitutes the absence
of rule is to mistake an unfamiliar grammar for none at all.

passage_text_markdown:
Imagine that we delegated our gravest moral choices–sentencing, triage, the apportionment of
scarce organs–to a system unfatigued by hunger, unswayed by friendship, and indierent to
the social standing of those it judged. The picture is seductive precisely because it promises to
subtract from moral decision the very features we most resent in human judges: their irritability,
their selective attention, their susceptibility to flattery. Yet the seduction is also the snare. A
system that subtracts the human element will, at best, replicate our judgments with greater
consistency.
It will not improve them. To improve a moral judgment is to bring to it some
quality the original lacked; mere consistency is a form of fidelity, and faithful reproduction of a
flawed pattern is not its correction.

The deeper worry, however, is not about bias. It is about the formalisability of moral reasoning
itself. To formalise a domain is to identify a small set of axioms and to derive from them, by
rule, an indefinitely large set of consequences. Physics has lived on this discipline for centuries.
Its theories disagree–Aristotelian natural motion is incompatible with Newtonian gravitation,
and both are incomplete relative to general relativity–but each theory shares the architectural
 pattern: postulates above, consequences below, the upper level determining the lower. Ethics,
the analogy runs, is similarly structured. Utilitarianism takes the maximisation of aggregate well-
being as its foundational claim, and from this derives subsidiary principles about distribution,
sacrifice, and the assessment of consequences. Other systems take other axioms–the inviolability
of persons, the primacy of duties, the priority of the worst-o–and derive their own corollaries.
Each can be programmed; each, given enough computation, can be carried into territory that
no single human ethicist could reason through unaided.

But the analogy with physics is precisely what should make us cautious. Physical theories
describe a domain whose objects are unaected by the description; an electron behaves as it
does whether or not we have correctly formalised its behaviour. Moral judgments concern
human acts, and human acts are answerable not only to principles but to context, history, and
the particular face of the person before us. The features that resist formalisation–intuition
seasoned by exposure, the capacity to recognise that a given case is, in a way that matters,
unlike the case before it–are not ornaments around the rational core of ethics. They may be
that core. If they are, then to formalise ethics is not to extend its reach but to amputate the
very faculty that made moral reflection possible. The risk is not that a formalised ethics will
mirror our limitations; it is that, in mirroring only what can be formalised, it will fail to mirror
our practice at all.

which the mind packages space into pictures, is shared by all human cultures. What diers is
the relative weight cultures place upon imagination and upon memory. In societies that have
accepted a secular mode of creativity–in which the maker, no longer a vessel for transcendent
forces, replaces God–imaginative transactions become self-conscious, framed, and accountable
to fixed conventions of plot, perspective, and proportion. The tribal storyteller works under
a dierent settlement. Lacking the inducement to dominate territorial space–a dominion that
history seems early to have placed beyond reach–tribal communities turned, perhaps obsessively,
toward dominion over time. The ritual conversation with ancestors, the painted clay figure that
becomes momentarily inhabited, the trance through which the dead are heard: these are not
picturesque survivals but disciplined practices for collapsing temporal distance.

This is why it is misleading to describe tribal narration as primitive.
It is, on the contrary,
the product of a long, exact training of memory. Over generations, an astonishingly precise
 classificatory system–of plants, soils, weather signs, dialects of animal call–has been carried
not in archives but in the rehearsed bodies and voices of singers. Where the modern reader
instinctively distinguishes the literal from the figurative, the tribal narrator does not: a star
may grow like a vine, a mountain swim like a fish, an animal speak in the dialect of a neighbour,
because the narrative motif and the emotion it carries are bound to each other before they are
bound to any ontological commitment about kind. To call this a fusion of planes is accurate; to
call it a confusion is to import the wrong test.

It does not follow that tribal art has no conventions. The frame, however, is unusually elastic.
An epic may open from a trifling domestic incident; a painting may continue across the wall of
a hut, the post of a granary, the lid of a pot–the boundary between art and non-art becoming,
on principle, invisible. Episodes follow not the logic of sequence but the logic of association: the
chaotic geometry of dreams. The grammar of painting and the syntax of language draw nearer
to each other than they do in the literate tradition, as if the same impulse produced painted
words at one end and a song of images at the other. To insist that this constitutes the absence
of rule is to mistake an unfamiliar grammar for none at all.

passage_text_markdown:
Imagine that we delegated our gravest moral choices–sentencing, triage, the apportionment of
scarce organs–to a system unfatigued by hunger, unswayed by friendship, and indierent to
the social standing of those it judged. The picture is seductive precisely because it promises to
subtract from moral decision the very features we most resent in human judges: their irritability,
their selective attention, their susceptibility to flattery. Yet the seduction is also the snare. A
system that subtracts the human element will, at best, replicate our judgments with greater
consistency.
It will not improve them. To improve a moral judgment is to bring to it some
quality the original lacked; mere consistency is a form of fidelity, and faithful reproduction of a
flawed pattern is not its correction.

The deeper worry, however, is not about bias. It is about the formalisability of moral reasoning
itself. To formalise a domain is to identify a small set of axioms and to derive from them, by
rule, an indefinitely large set of consequences. Physics has lived on this discipline for centuries.
Its theories disagree–Aristotelian natural motion is incompatible with Newtonian gravitation,
and both are incomplete relative to general relativity–but each theory shares the architectural
 pattern: postulates above, consequences below, the upper level determining the lower. Ethics,
the analogy runs, is similarly structured. Utilitarianism takes the maximisation of aggregate well-
being as its foundational claim, and from this derives subsidiary principles about distribution,
sacrifice, and the assessment of consequences. Other systems take other axioms–the inviolability
of persons, the primacy of duties, the priority of the worst-o–and derive their own corollaries.
Each can be programmed; each, given enough computation, can be carried into territory that
no single human ethicist could reason through unaided.

But the analogy with physics is precisely what should make us cautious. Physical theories
describe a domain whose objects are unaected by the description; an electron behaves as it
does whether or not we have correctly formalised its behaviour. Moral judgments concern
human acts, and human acts are answerable not only to principles but to context, history, and
the particular face of the person before us. The features that resist formalisation–intuition
seasoned by exposure, the capacity to recognise that a given case is, in a way that matters,
unlike the case before it–are not ornaments around the rational core of ethics. They may be
that core. If they are, then to formalise ethics is not to extend its reach but to amputate the
very faculty that made moral reflection possible. The risk is not that a formalised ethics will
mirror our limitations; it is that, in mirroring only what can be formalised, it will fail to mirror
our practice at all.

To call the tribal imagination ‘hallucinatory’ is convenient shorthand, but the term carries a
polemical risk. It implies a deficit–a failure to reach the orderly daylight of secular, sequential
reasoning–when what is in fact at issue is a dierent settlement between consciousness and its
two great encounters: space and time. The image-making faculty, that genetic endowment by
which the mind packages space into pictures, is shared by all human cultures. What diers is
the relative weight cultures place upon imagination and upon memory. In societies that have
accepted a secular mode of creativity–in which the maker, no longer a vessel for transcendent
forces, replaces God–imaginative transactions become self-conscious, framed, and accountable
to fixed conventions of plot, perspective, and proportion. The tribal storyteller works under
a dierent settlement. Lacking the inducement to dominate territorial space–a dominion that
history seems early to have placed beyond reach–tribal communities turned, perhaps obsessively,
toward dominion over time. The ritual conversation with ancestors, the painted clay figure that
becomes momentarily inhabited, the trance through which the dead are heard: these are not
picturesque survivals but disciplined practices for collapsing temporal distance.

This is why it is misleading to describe tribal narration as primitive.
It is, on the contrary,
the product of a long, exact training of memory. Over generations, an astonishingly precise
 classificatory system–of plants, soils, weather signs, dialects of animal call–has been carried
not in archives but in the rehearsed bodies and voices of singers. Where the modern reader
instinctively distinguishes the literal from the figurative, the tribal narrator does not: a star
may grow like a vine, a mountain swim like a fish, an animal speak in the dialect of a neighbour,
because the narrative motif and the emotion it carries are bound to each other before they are
bound to any ontological commitment about kind. To call this a fusion of planes is accurate; to
call it a confusion is to import the wrong test.

It does not follow that tribal art has no conventions. The frame, however, is unusually elastic.
An epic may open from a trifling domestic incident; a painting may continue across the wall of
a hut, the post of a granary, the lid of a pot–the boundary between art and non-art becoming,
on principle, invisible. Episodes follow not the logic of sequence but the logic of association: the
chaotic geometry of dreams. The grammar of painting and the syntax of language draw nearer
to each other than they do in the literate tradition, as if the same impulse produced painted
words at one end and a song of images at the other. To insist that this constitutes the absence
of rule is to mistake an unfamiliar grammar for none at all.

passage_text_markdown:
Imagine that we delegated our gravest moral choices–sentencing, triage, the apportionment of
scarce organs–to a system unfatigued by hunger, unswayed by friendship, and indierent to
the social standing of those it judged. The picture is seductive precisely because it promises to
subtract from moral decision the very features we most resent in human judges: their irritability,
their selective attention, their susceptibility to flattery. Yet the seduction is also the snare. A
system that subtracts the human element will, at best, replicate our judgments with greater
consistency.
It will not improve them. To improve a moral judgment is to bring to it some
quality the original lacked; mere consistency is a form of fidelity, and faithful reproduction of a
flawed pattern is not its correction.

The deeper worry, however, is not about bias. It is about the formalisability of moral reasoning
itself. To formalise a domain is to identify a small set of axioms and to derive from them, by
rule, an indefinitely large set of consequences. Physics has lived on this discipline for centuries.
Its theories disagree–Aristotelian natural motion is incompatible with Newtonian gravitation,
and both are incomplete relative to general relativity–but each theory shares the architectural
 pattern: postulates above, consequences below, the upper level determining the lower. Ethics,
the analogy runs, is similarly structured. Utilitarianism takes the maximisation of aggregate well-
being as its foundational claim, and from this derives subsidiary principles about distribution,
sacrifice, and the assessment of consequences. Other systems take other axioms–the inviolability
of persons, the primacy of duties, the priority of the worst-o–and derive their own corollaries.
Each can be programmed; each, given enough computation, can be carried into territory that
no single human ethicist could reason through unaided.

But the analogy with physics is precisely what should make us cautious. Physical theories
describe a domain whose objects are unaected by the description; an electron behaves as it
does whether or not we have correctly formalised its behaviour. Moral judgments concern
human acts, and human acts are answerable not only to principles but to context, history, and
the particular face of the person before us. The features that resist formalisation–intuition
seasoned by exposure, the capacity to recognise that a given case is, in a way that matters,
unlike the case before it–are not ornaments around the rational core of ethics. They may be
that core. If they are, then to formalise ethics is not to extend its reach but to amputate the
very faculty that made moral reflection possible. The risk is not that a formalised ethics will
mirror our limitations; it is that, in mirroring only what can be formalised, it will fail to mirror
our practice at all.

[Q6 | RC — Principal Argument | Hard | MCQ]

question_text_markdown:
Consistency in moral judgment, though desirable, is less important than fidelity to context

and individual circumstance.

quality the original lacked; mere consistency is a form of fidelity, and faithful reproduction of a
flawed pattern is not its correction.

The deeper worry, however, is not about bias. It is about the formalisability of moral reasoning
itself. To formalise a domain is to identify a small set of axioms and to derive from them, by
rule, an indefinitely large set of consequences. Physics has lived on this discipline for centuries.
Its theories disagree–Aristotelian natural motion is incompatible with Newtonian gravitation,
and both are incomplete relative to general relativity–but each theory shares the architectural
 pattern: postulates above, consequences below, the upper level determining the lower. Ethics,
the analogy runs, is similarly structured. Utilitarianism takes the maximisation of aggregate well-
being as its foundational claim, and from this derives subsidiary principles about distribution,
sacrifice, and the assessment of consequences. Other systems take other axioms–the inviolability
of persons, the primacy of duties, the priority of the worst-o–and derive their own corollaries.
Each can be programmed; each, given enough computation, can be carried into territory that
no single human ethicist could reason through unaided.

But the analogy with physics is precisely what should make us cautious. Physical theories
describe a domain whose objects are unaected by the description; an electron behaves as it
does whether or not we have correctly formalised its behaviour. Moral judgments concern
human acts, and human acts are answerable not only to principles but to context, history, and
the particular face of the person before us. The features that resist formalisation–intuition
seasoned by exposure, the capacity to recognise that a given case is, in a way that matters,
unlike the case before it–are not ornaments around the rational core of ethics. They may be
that core. If they are, then to formalise ethics is not to extend its reach but to amputate the
very faculty that made moral reflection possible. The risk is not that a formalised ethics will
mirror our limitations; it is that, in mirroring only what can be formalised, it will fail to mirror
our practice at all.

[Q10 | RC — Tone | Medium-Hard | MCQ]

The author’s tone in the passage is best described as:

passage_text_markdown:
Imagine that we delegated our gravest moral choices–sentencing, triage, the apportionment of
scarce organs–to a system unfatigued by hunger, unswayed by friendship, and indierent to
the social standing of those it judged. The picture is seductive precisely because it promises to
subtract from moral decision the very features we most resent in human judges: their irritability,
their selective attention, their susceptibility to flattery. Yet the seduction is also the snare. A
system that subtracts the human element will, at best, replicate our judgments with greater
consistency.
It will not improve them. To improve a moral judgment is to bring to it some
quality the original lacked; mere consistency is a form of fidelity, and faithful reproduction of a
flawed pattern is not its correction.

itself. To formalise a domain is to identify a small set of axioms and to derive from them, by

passage_text_markdown:
(Questions 1–5)

Any account of what we call memory must begin with an awkward concession: the only place a
memory is ever located is the present. Whatever the experience of remembering feels like from
the inside, every fibre of that experience — the felt vividness, the conviction of pastness, the
sense of being a witness to something that has already happened — is constituted now. The
remembered episode, on this view, plays no active role in the present act of recollection; it is, at
most, a hypothesis we attach to the experience to explain its character. From this it follows,
with uncomfortable rigour, that nothing internal to a memory can guarantee that the event it
appears to recall ever occurred at all.

This is more than a sceptical curiosity.
If a person’s nervous system were assembled five
minutes ago in the exact state it now occupies, complete with the standing dispositions, mental
images, and felt convictions that compose what we call their past, that person would have, by
every available criterion, an entire life of memories — memories of a past that, by hypothesis,
never existed. There is no contradiction in the supposition; the present-tense facts about the
rememberer are logically detachable from any facts about earlier times, because no chain of
present evidence can secure a non-present event.

The argument is often misread as a claim that memory is unreliable, but that is not its point.
Unreliability is a comparative notion: a memory is unreliable when it fails to match a past it
is taken to represent. The present argument is more radical: it does not say that memories
misrepresent the past; it says that the past, considered as a logically independent thing, never
enters into the description of what a memory actually is. Whatever sustains our practice
of treating memories as windows onto earlier times must therefore lie outside the memories
themselves — in convergence between rememberers, in the public record, in causal regularities
our science accepts. Without those external scaffolds, the felt pastness of a memory is mute
about whether any past exists for it to point to.

[Type: Reading Comprehension — Difficulty: Hard — Format: MCQ]
question_text_markdown: Which one of the following best captures the author’s principal claim about memory?
option_a_markdown:
Human memory is unreliable because the present-tense experience of remembering frequently

fails to correspond to the actual past.
option_b_markdown:
The felt experience of remembering is, in itself, logically silent on whether any past event ever

occurred.
option_c_markdown:
Memories cannot be trusted unless they are corroborated by external sources such as public

records and the testimony of others.
option_d_markdown:
Since the past is logically independent of the present, the practice of treating memories as evidence

of past events is philosophically incoherent.

[Type: Reading Comprehension — Difficulty: Very Hard — Format: MCQ]
detailed_solution_markdown:
Question 1 — Answer: (2)

The passage’s central thesis, stated explicitly in the third paragraph, is that the felt experience
of remembering does not, by itself, entail anything about a past. Option (2) captures exactly
this — the internal silence of memory on the existence of a past.

Why (1) is wrong: The author explicitly distances the argument from claims about unreliability.
Unreliability assumes a real past against which a memory can be measured. This option mistakes
a different (lesser) claim for the author’s.
Why (3) is wrong: The passage notes that external scaffolds in practice support our treatment
of memories as evidence, but it does not prescribe “don’t trust memories without corroboration.”
This is a behavioural recommendation the passage does not make.
Why (4) is wrong: “Philosophically incoherent” overstates the author’s claim. The author
says the past does not enter into what a memory is, not that the practice of treating memories as
evidence is incoherent — in fact he locates the legitimacy of that practice in external scaffolds.

Why this is CAT-level:
behavioural over-extension; (4) is an extreme version of the author’s stance.

(1) is the classic “true-but-not-the-author’s-claim” trap; (3) is a

1. Which one of the following best captures the author’s principal claim about memory?

(1) Human memory is unreliable because the present-tense experience of remembering frequently

fails to correspond to the actual past.

(2) The felt experience of remembering is, in itself, logically silent on whether any past event ever

occurred.

(3) Memories cannot be trusted unless they are corroborated by external sources such as public

records and the testimony of others.

(4) Since the past is logically independent of the present, the practice of treating memories as evidence

of past events is philosophically incoherent.

[Type: Reading Comprehension — Difficulty: Very Hard — Format: MCQ]

[Type: Reading Comprehension — Difficulty: Hard — Format: MCQ]
question_text_markdown:
The author distinguishes his argument from claims about “unreliable memory” primarily in order
to:
option_a_markdown:
demonstrate that memories often misrepresent the past in subtle ways that empirical science has

not yet detected.
option_b_markdown:
suggest that the unreliability of memory is a separate, lesser problem that can be solved through

better external corroboration.
option_c_markdown: show that his concern is not with how accurately a memory represents the past, but with whether
detailed_solution_markdown:
Question 2 — Answer: (3)

The author writes that the argument “is often misread” as a claim about unreliability and then
explicitly states what the argument is and is not. The function of this distinction is to clarify
scope: he is not weighing accuracy; he is asking whether the past figures in the description of a
memory at all. Option (3) names exactly this scope shift.

Why (1) is wrong: The author does not commit to misrepresentation, undetected or otherwise.
He brackets the question of accuracy.
Why (2) is wrong: The author does not call unreliability “lesser” or recommend external
corroboration as a fix. This conflates his diagnostic move with a prescriptive solution he never
offers.
Why (4) is wrong: The author is not ranking two scepticisms. He is distinguishing his
position from one form of scepticism, not endorsing a competing one.

Why this is CAT-level: The question demands recognition of authorial function (why the
distinction is made), not restatement.
(1) and (2) are partial-truth distortions; (4) is an
unsupported ranking.

question_text_markdown:
The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the passage.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Question 11 — Answer: (1)

The paragraph contrasts two modes of imagination:
the secular/self-conscious and the
tribal/fused, and characterises tribal imagination as one that does not separate planes (mountain,
star, dead/living). Option (1) preserves the central contrast and the asymmetry between the
two modes.

Why (2) is wrong: “More imaginative than” is an evaluative ranking the paragraph does not
make; the paragraph describes a difference of kind, not of degree.
Why (3) is wrong: Reverses direction: makes a claim about secular art’s deficiency that the
paragraph does not endorse; the paragraph offers no comparative deficit, only a characterisation.
Why (4) is wrong: Confuses cause with absence. The paragraph does not say tribal art
abandons time/space because tribal societies lack literate forms; it describes tribal imagination
as operating in a mode prior to that separation. Causal direction is misrepresented.

Why this is CAT-level: (2) is the ranking trap; (3) is the sign-flip; (4) is the causal-direction
distortion. All three are the classic para-summary wrong-option archetypes.
source_raw_block:
11. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best captures
the essence of the passage.

passage_text_markdown:
Directions for Questions 1–5: Read the passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.

[Paragraph 1] Once a culture begins to treat the artist as the originator of meaning, its imaginative
life acquires a peculiar self-consciousness. The maker stands apart from what is made, and the act
of making becomes an object of reflection. The imaginative life of forest communities, by contrast,
retains a quality that to literate observers can seem dreamlike—a willingness to let categories bleed
into one another. In their stories, hills weep, rivers remember, and the dead speak through the
mouths of birds. One should not mistake this for a failure of distinction. The artist of the forest is
not confused about whether a hill can in fact weep; the convention permits the hill to weep because
the narrative is organised around feeling rather than around the orderly partition of phenomena. It
is in this sense that one might say such art draws on something closer to ancestral memory than to
imagination in the modern sense.

[Paragraph 2] To press the contrast further, one needs to distinguish imagination from memory.
Consciousness, on entering the world, encounters two refractory facts: space and time. We make
space inhabitable by populating it with images—a faculty that is, in some elementary sense, a
biological inheritance. Time, however, is mastered through memory, by which we recognise that
the self of yesterday is the self of today. Communities that long ago ceased to expect mastery
over territory have, it seems, redirected their effort toward mastery over time. This is why the most
striking rituals of such communities are addressed not to the conquest of land but to the conversation
of the living with the dead, often through small carved or moulded figures kept in household shrines.

 The annual approach to these ancestors is not an act of nostalgia; it is a technique for holding time
in place.

[Paragraph 3] What follows from this orientation is a distinctive grammar of representation. The
frame that separates an image from the surface on which it rests becomes porous. A wall painting
may continue into the floor; an epic may begin from an episode no different in scale from a passing
moment in a domestic morning. Sequence, in the sense familiar to literate audiences, is treated
as an option rather than a requirement. To the outside reader or viewer, the result may appear
chaotic, but the chaos is at most the chaos of a dream, in which the absence of sequence does not
signify the absence of order. In the most expressive of these works, the boundary between what is
said and what is shown almost dissolves: the words are painted, and the pictures sing.
question_text_markdown:
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: MCQ Difficulty: Medium-Hard

The author’s purpose in drawing a distinction between imagination and memory in the second
paragraph is primarily to:
option_a_markdown: argue that imagination is biologically inherited while memory has to be deliberately cultivated.
option_b_markdown: explain why certain communities have channelled their cultural effort toward holding time rather
option_c_markdown: show that literate societies are less skilled at memory than forest communities.
option_d_markdown: correct the common error of treating memory and imagination as identical faculties of the mind.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Answer: Option 2.

Reasoning. Paragraph 2 introduces the imagination/memory distinction not as a stand-alone
philosophical exercise but as the conceptual support for a specific empirical claim that follows
directly: communities that “ceased to expect mastery over territory have, it seems, redirected
their effort toward mastery over time”. The distinction therefore functions to explain why these
communities have channelled cultural effort toward holding time rather than commanding space,
which is precisely Option 2.

Why the others fail. Option 1 distorts the passage:
imagination is described as a “biological
inheritance,” but the passage does not say memory must be deliberately cultivated. Option 3
introduces a comparative claim about “skill” that is absent from the text. Option 4 is partially
true at the surface (the author does distinguish the two faculties) but mistakes the function: the
distinction is presented to support a larger argument, not as a corrective definition.

Why this is CAT-level. The trap lies in Option 4, which is true-but-not-the-purpose—a common
CAT distractor that exploits the difference between a passage’s local claim and its global function.

passage_text_markdown:
Directions for Questions 1–5: Read the passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.

[Paragraph 1] Once a culture begins to treat the artist as the originator of meaning, its imaginative
life acquires a peculiar self-consciousness. The maker stands apart from what is made, and the act
of making becomes an object of reflection. The imaginative life of forest communities, by contrast,
retains a quality that to literate observers can seem dreamlike—a willingness to let categories bleed
into one another. In their stories, hills weep, rivers remember, and the dead speak through the
mouths of birds. One should not mistake this for a failure of distinction. The artist of the forest is
not confused about whether a hill can in fact weep; the convention permits the hill to weep because
the narrative is organised around feeling rather than around the orderly partition of phenomena. It
is in this sense that one might say such art draws on something closer to ancestral memory than to
imagination in the modern sense.

[Paragraph 2] To press the contrast further, one needs to distinguish imagination from memory.
Consciousness, on entering the world, encounters two refractory facts: space and time. We make
space inhabitable by populating it with images—a faculty that is, in some elementary sense, a
biological inheritance. Time, however, is mastered through memory, by which we recognise that
the self of yesterday is the self of today. Communities that long ago ceased to expect mastery
over territory have, it seems, redirected their effort toward mastery over time. This is why the most
striking rituals of such communities are addressed not to the conquest of land but to the conversation
of the living with the dead, often through small carved or moulded figures kept in household shrines.

 The annual approach to these ancestors is not an act of nostalgia; it is a technique for holding time
in place.

[Paragraph 3] What follows from this orientation is a distinctive grammar of representation. The
frame that separates an image from the surface on which it rests becomes porous. A wall painting
may continue into the floor; an epic may begin from an episode no different in scale from a passing
moment in a domestic morning. Sequence, in the sense familiar to literate audiences, is treated
as an option rather than a requirement. To the outside reader or viewer, the result may appear
chaotic, but the chaos is at most the chaos of a dream, in which the absence of sequence does not
signify the absence of order. In the most expressive of these works, the boundary between what is
said and what is shown almost dissolves: the words are painted, and the pictures sing.

question_text_markdown:
explain why certain communities have channelled their cultural effort toward holding time rather

than commanding space.
option_a_markdown: Tribal storytellers, when questioned by ethnographers, insist that the events in their stories are
option_b_markdown: Tribal epics in some regions follow strict chronological sequence and distinguish carefully between
option_c_markdown: The frame separating a wall painting from the surrounding architecture is rigidly enforced in
option_d_markdown: Literate cultures also produce works in which animals speak and mountains feel.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Answer: Option 4.

Reasoning. The author’s central account contains three load-bearing claims: (a) forest narrators
are not confused by the fusion of categories, (b) sequence is treated as optional, and (c) the frame
between image and surround is porous.

• Option 1 directly contradicts claim (a) by recasting the storytellers as literal believers; it

weakens.

• Option 2 directly contradicts claim (b) by asserting strict sequence and categorical separation;

it weakens.

• Option 3 directly contradicts claim (c) by asserting rigid framing; it weakens.

• Option 4 reports that literate cultures also produce works in which animals speak and moun-
tains feel. The author never claims that this organising principle is exclusive to forest art;
only that it is characteristic of it. Hence Option 4 leaves the author’s account intact.

Option 4 is therefore the EXCEPT.

Why this is CAT-level. The exception is the option that engages a claim of exclusivity that the
author never actually made. Students who confuse “X is characteristic of A” with “X is unique to
A” will mark the wrong option.

detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3. Answer: Option 2.

Reasoning. The relevant sentence is: “the chaos is at most the chaos of a dream, in which the
absence of sequence does not signify the absence of order.” The author concedes the appearance
of chaos to the outsider while denying that it is genuine disorder, locating the organising principle
elsewhere than in sequence. Option 2 captures this exactly.

Why the others fail. Option 1 accepts the chaos as real, which the author does not. Option 3
endorses the very misconception the author rejects when he writes “the artist of the forest is not
confused”. Option 4 imports a motive (provocation aimed at outsiders) that the passage nowhere
supports.

Why this is CAT-level. Three of the four options use the word “chaos” from the passage,

9


so keyword matching is useless. Only reasoning about the author’s defence of order under non-
sequential arrangement isolates Option 2.
