striking rituals of such communities are addressed not to the conquest of land but to the conversation
of the living with the dead, often through small carved or moulded figures kept in household shrines.

 The annual approach to these ancestors is not an act of nostalgia; it is a technique for holding time
in place.

[Paragraph 3] What follows from this orientation is a distinctive grammar of representation. The
frame that separates an image from the surface on which it rests becomes porous. A wall painting
may continue into the floor; an epic may begin from an episode no different in scale from a passing
moment in a domestic morning. Sequence, in the sense familiar to literate audiences, is treated
as an option rather than a requirement. To the outside reader or viewer, the result may appear
chaotic, but the chaos is at most the chaos of a dream, in which the absence of sequence does not
signify the absence of order. In the most expressive of these works, the boundary between what is
said and what is shown almost dissolves: the words are painted, and the pictures sing.

question_text_markdown: correct the common error of treating memory and imagination as identical faculties of the mind.
option_a_markdown: The porousness of the frame in tribal art is a direct consequence of these communities having
option_b_markdown: A work in which sequence is absent should not, on that ground alone, be judged disorderly.
option_c_markdown: Literate art is incapable of conveying the same range of emotion as the art of forest communities.
option_d_markdown: Memory and imagination are mutually exclusive faculties, only one of which any given culture
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4. Answer: Option 2.

Reasoning. Option 2 is a tight paraphrase of the explicit sentence “the absence of sequence does
not signify the absence of order”—which entails that a non-sequential work should not, on that
ground alone, be called disorderly. The author is therefore committed to Option 2.

Why the others fail. Option 1 sounds plausible because it uses the passage’s own vocabulary
(“porousness,” “mastery over space”), but the passage never says the porousness of the frame is
caused by the renunciation of territorial mastery. The renunciation explains the focus on time and
ancestors; the porous frame is offered as a feature of representational grammar. Asserting a causal
link is a step the passage does not take. Option 3 makes a comparative claim about literate art’s
emotional range that the passage neither states nor implies. Option 4 overstates the distinction
between memory and imagination as mutual exclusivity, whereas the passage merely says forest art
draws more on memory than on imagination “in the modern sense.”

Why this is CAT-level. The Very-Hard trap is Option 1, an inviting but unwarranted causal
inference that uses passage vocabulary. The student must distinguish what the passage states from
what merely sounds like it follows.

passage_text_markdown:
Directions for Questions 1–5: Read the passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.

[Paragraph 1] Once a culture begins to treat the artist as the originator of meaning, its imaginative
life acquires a peculiar self-consciousness. The maker stands apart from what is made, and the act
of making becomes an object of reflection. The imaginative life of forest communities, by contrast,
retains a quality that to literate observers can seem dreamlike—a willingness to let categories bleed
into one another. In their stories, hills weep, rivers remember, and the dead speak through the
mouths of birds. One should not mistake this for a failure of distinction. The artist of the forest is
not confused about whether a hill can in fact weep; the convention permits the hill to weep because
the narrative is organised around feeling rather than around the orderly partition of phenomena. It
is in this sense that one might say such art draws on something closer to ancestral memory than to
imagination in the modern sense.

[Paragraph 2] To press the contrast further, one needs to distinguish imagination from memory.
Consciousness, on entering the world, encounters two refractory facts: space and time. We make
space inhabitable by populating it with images—a faculty that is, in some elementary sense, a
biological inheritance. Time, however, is mastered through memory, by which we recognise that
the self of yesterday is the self of today. Communities that long ago ceased to expect mastery
over territory have, it seems, redirected their effort toward mastery over time. This is why the most
striking rituals of such communities are addressed not to the conquest of land but to the conversation
of the living with the dead, often through small carved or moulded figures kept in household shrines.

 The annual approach to these ancestors is not an act of nostalgia; it is a technique for holding time
in place.

[Paragraph 3] What follows from this orientation is a distinctive grammar of representation. The
frame that separates an image from the surface on which it rests becomes porous. A wall painting
may continue into the floor; an epic may begin from an episode no different in scale from a passing
moment in a domestic morning. Sequence, in the sense familiar to literate audiences, is treated
as an option rather than a requirement. To the outside reader or viewer, the result may appear
chaotic, but the chaos is at most the chaos of a dream, in which the absence of sequence does not
signify the absence of order. In the most expressive of these works, the boundary between what is
said and what is shown almost dissolves: the words are painted, and the pictures sing.
question_text_markdown:
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: TITA Difficulty: Hard

In which paragraph (enter the paragraph number 1, 2, or 3) does the author most directly address the
misconception that forest narrators are confused about the difference between literal and figurative
reality?

Your Answer:

Reading Comprehension –
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q5. Answer: 1.

Reasoning. The misconception that forest narrators are confused about the literal/figurative
boundary is addressed in Paragraph 1, in the sentence: “The artist of the forest is not confused
about whether a hill can in fact weep; the convention permits the hill to weep because the narrative
is organised around feeling rather than around the orderly partition of phenomena.” Paragraphs 2
and 3 build on the contrast established earlier; only Paragraph 1 directly rejects the misconception.

Why this is CAT-level. A weaker student will recognise the theme as relevant to the whole
passage and guess Paragraph 3. The question tests whether the student can locate the precise
sentence where a specific dialectical move is performed.

passage_text_markdown:
Directions for Questions 6–10: Read the passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.

[Paragraph 1] For roughly a century, the construction of large dams was treated less as a question
of engineering than as a measure of national seriousness. The capacity to dam a major river served,
for governments of widely different ideological persuasions, as a visible token that nature had been
brought into productive alignment with the state. Concrete poured into a gorge marked, on the
maps and in the speeches, the arrival of modernity. That a smaller class of structures—barrier dams
of modest height, weirs, and run-of-river works—was meanwhile choking the world’s middling rivers
at a scale far greater than the celebrated megaprojects went largely unnoticed.

[Paragraph 2] The standard defence of dams enumerates services: flood control, irrigation, elec-
tricity. Each is real. But each also obscures a settlement of accounts that has rarely been put
before those who pay the costs. Reservoirs displace; channels silt; nutrients arrested behind walls
do not arrive on flood plains downstream, and fisheries quietly collapse. Above all, the long arc
of construction has tended to fall on populations whose claim on the land predates the state that
requisitioned it. The phrase “sacrifice zone” is sometimes used to name the territory immediately
upstream of a wall; the same words could be applied to many of the communities downstream, who
lose a river and gain, in its place, a managed flow they did not request.

[Paragraph 3] There is a tempting story in which the era of mega-dams has ended. Resistance from
displaced communities, the rise of decentralised renewables, and the simple exhaustion of suitable
sites are routinely cited as forces narrowing the field. The story is not wholly wrong. It is, however,
premature. Construction continues, at considerable pace, in those parts of the world where rivers
descend rapidly out of high mountain ranges into densely populated plains; the geographic axis has
shifted, not the underlying impulse. Meanwhile, the mid-century cohort of dams in older industrial
 economies is reaching the end of its design life under climatic conditions for which it was not built.
Sediment loads, drought regimes, and peak flows have all departed from the assumptions on which
the original calculations were made. The most pressing question is no longer whether to build but
whether to keep, and on what terms.
question_text_markdown:
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: MCQ Difficulty: Medium-Hard

Which of the following best captures the central argument of the passage?
option_a_markdown:
Although dams provide real services, the supposed end of the dam-building era is premature,
and the central question has shifted from whether to build to whether to keep older dams under
changed conditions.
option_b_markdown:
Dams have consistently failed to deliver the services they promise and should now be dismantled

in light of climate change.
option_c_markdown:
The geographic centre of dam construction has shifted from older industrial economies to moun-

tainous regions feeding densely populated plains.
option_d_markdown:
Mid-century dams in industrial economies have outlived their design lives and must be replaced

by decentralised renewable infrastructure.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q6. Answer: Option 1.

Reasoning. The central argument runs across all three paragraphs: dams have been treated as
more than engineering (a marker of state seriousness); their services are real but their costs poorly
accounted; the supposed end of the era is premature, and the operative question has shifted to
retention under changed climatic conditions. Option 1 captures all three strands: real services,
premature ending, shift of question.

Why the others fail. Option 2 contradicts the passage, which calls the services “real” and
never advocates dismantling. Option 3 lifts only one strand (the geographic shift) and ignores the
larger argument. Option 4 inserts a prescription (“must be replaced by decentralised renewable
infrastructure”) that the passage does not make; renewables appear only as one cited factor in
resistance to new construction.

Why this is CAT-level. The strong distractor is Option 3, which is fully consistent with the
passage but is a partial summary. Para-summary discipline requires choosing the option that
captures the whole argument, not merely a true statement drawn from it.

question_text_markdown:
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: MCQ Difficulty: Hard

The author’s discussion of “sacrifice zones” in the second paragraph primarily functions to:
option_a_markdown: document the technical failures of large-dam engineering.
option_b_markdown:
extend an existing critical concept to a category of affected communities not usually counted

within it, thereby broadening the scope of the harm.
option_c_markdown: argue that the supposed benefits of dams are wholly imagined.
option_d_markdown: contrast the colonial and post-colonial uses of dam infrastructure.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q7. Answer: Option 2.

Reasoning. The phrase “sacrifice zone” enters the passage as the conventional label for territory
immediately upstream of a dam wall. The author’s move is to extend this label to communities

10


downstream—“the same words could be applied”—thereby widening the population counted as
harmed. The function is conceptual extension, which Option 2 names precisely.

Why the others fail. Option 1 mislabels the discussion as about technical engineering failure;
the paragraph is about distribution of costs. Option 3 contradicts the author’s explicit concession
that the services are real. Option 4 invokes a colonial/post-colonial axis that this passage does not
develop.

Why this is CAT-level. The question tests recognition of a specific rhetorical move—extension
of an existing concept—rather than restatement of content.

question_text_markdown:
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: MCQ Difficulty: Hard

Which of the following can be MOST reliably inferred from the passage?
option_a_markdown:
Smaller barrier dams and weirs have, taken together, blocked a larger expanse of riverine systems

than the most celebrated megaprojects.
option_b_markdown:
National governments across ideological divides have historically agreed that dam construction

is purely a matter of engineering.
option_c_markdown: Climatic change is the principal reason older dams are now being decommissioned.
option_d_markdown: The era of mega-dams should be treated as essentially concluded in most parts of the world.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q8. Answer: Option 1.

Reasoning. The passage states that a smaller class of structures has been “choking the world’s
middling rivers at a scale far greater than the celebrated megaprojects.” Option 1 is a precise
paraphrase: in aggregate, smaller barriers have blocked more riverine systems than the mega-dams.

Why the others fail. Option 2 reverses the passage, which says dam construction was treated as
“less of a question of engineering than as a measure of national seriousness.” Option 3 over-specifies
a causal claim about decommissioning that the passage does not make. The passage notes that
older dams are reaching end of design life under unanticipated climatic conditions; it does not say
they are being decommissioned principally for climate reasons. Option 4 contradicts the explicit
verdict “premature.”

Why this is CAT-level. Option 4 is a particularly attractive trap because it restates a story the
author entertains before dismissing as premature; the student must register the dismissal.

question_text_markdown:
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: MCQ Difficulty: Very Hard

Which one of the following statements is LEAST consistent with the position taken by the author?
 1. Dams continue to be built even where their original justification has weakened, because the

impulse behind dam-building has outlasted the conditions that produced it.
option_a_markdown:
The benefits commonly attributed to dams—irrigation, electricity, flood control—are largely

illusory.
option_b_markdown:
Communities downstream of major dams may suffer harms that are comparable in kind, though

different in cause, from those suffered by communities displaced upstream.
option_c_markdown:
As the design assumptions underlying older dams diverge from present hydrological conditions,

the question of maintenance has acquired a new urgency.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q9. Answer: Option 2.

Reasoning. The question asks which statement is LEAST consistent with the author’s position.
• Option 1 paraphrases “the geographic axis has shifted, not the underlying impulse”: consis-

tent.

• Option 2 calls the benefits “largely illusory.” The passage states explicitly: “Each is real.”

This option therefore contradicts the author’s explicit concession.

• Option 3 restates the extension of “sacrifice zone” to downstream communities: consistent.

• Option 4 paraphrases the closing observation that design assumptions have diverged from

present hydrological conditions and that maintenance is now urgent: consistent.

Option 2 is therefore the least consistent.

Why this is CAT-level. The author’s overall stance is critical of dams, which tempts the careless
reader to mark Option 2 as consistent with that critical mood. The Very-Hard discrimination is
between the author’s overall stance and a specific concession he makes within it.

question_text_markdown:
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: MCQ Difficulty: Hard

The author’s overall attitude toward the contemporary debate on dams is best described as:
option_a_markdown: polemical and dismissive, calling for the immediate abandonment of all dam infrastructure.
option_b_markdown:
sceptical of the comforting narrative that the era of large dams has ended, while attentive to the

new questions raised by ageing infrastructure.
option_c_markdown: neutral and descriptive, drawing no normative conclusions from the evidence presented.
option_d_markdown:
nostalgic for an earlier phase of dam-building in which costs and benefits were weighed demo-

cratically.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q10. Answer: Option 2.

Reasoning. The author rejects the comforting narrative that the dam era has ended (“premature”),
while turning attention to the new questions raised by ageing mid-century infrastructure (“the most
pressing question is no longer whether to build but whether to keep”). Option 2 captures both
halves.

Why the others fail. Option 1 is too extreme: the author does not call for abandonment of all dam
infrastructure. Option 3 calls the passage neutral, but the passage takes clear normative positions.
Option 4 invents a nostalgic register; the author explicitly notes that costs and benefits “were

11


rarely weighed thoughtfully and decided democratically” over the twentieth century, so nostalgia is
impossible.

Why this is CAT-level. The tone question forces the student to distinguish a sustained critical
analysis from a polemical denunciation; the temptation is to overshoot to Option 1.
source_raw_block:
Q10.
Section: Reading Comprehension Type: MCQ Difficulty: Hard

The author’s overall attitude toward the contemporary debate on dams is best described as:

1. polemical and dismissive, calling for the immediate abandonment of all dam infrastructure.

2. sceptical of the comforting narrative that the era of large dams has ended, while attentive to the

new questions raised by ageing infrastructure.

3. neutral and descriptive, drawing no normative conclusions from the evidence presented.

4. nostalgic for an earlier phase of dam-building in which costs and benefits were weighed demo-

cratically.

question_text_markdown: Section:
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q11. Answer: Option 2.

Reasoning. The paragraph’s logical spine is: (i) the standard claim that automation liberates
workers is long-standing but simplistic; (ii) the work left behind by automation is often the harder
residue (exception-handling, monitoring); (iii) therefore liberation may amount to a reallocation of
difficulty, not its removal. Option 2 captures all three components.

Why the others fail. Option 1 overshoots: the passage says the pattern is “more complicated,”
not that automation has “consistently failed.” Option 3 reports a true subsidiary observation about
routine versus residue but omits the central re-framing of the liberation claim. Option 4 introduces
“forgetfulness” across generations, which the passage does not mention as a cause of the recurring
claim.

Why this is CAT-level. Option 3 is the true-but-incomplete trap typical of CAT para-summary;
only Option 2 reaches the final re-framing in the last sentence.
source_raw_block:
Q11.
Section:

passage_text_markdown:
To call a society’s relation to its past a matter of “memory” is already to make a quiet philo-
sophical choice. Memory, in the modern register, names a faculty that secures a private link
between a present self and an earlier self; what it stores is, by assumption, a chain of events that
did once unfold. The faculty is supposed to be answerable to a past that exists independently
of the act of remembering, and it is judged accurate or faulty against that standard. This view
feels self-evident only because it has been naturalised by centuries of secular literacy, in which
the writing down of dates and the keeping of records have come to define what counts as “what
really happened.”

Tribal communities, by contrast, are often described as having a defective or merely poetic
version of this faculty. Their songs telescope generations into a single afternoon; their genealogies
absorb foreign rivers and foreign animals into the family line; their dead speak, instruct, and
sometimes vote. Anthropologists, even sympathetic ones, have tended to treat these features
as ornaments around a residual core of “real” memory, as though under the embellishment one
would eventually find the same archive the modern subject keeps. The assumption, in other
words, is that tribal memory is modern memory plus distortion.

It is this assumption, rather than tribal practice, that deserves scrutiny. The point of a tribal
narrative is not to record a past that lies behind it but to constitute a present community by
enacting a continuity that the community needs in order to act. When a clan “remembers” an
ancestor who fought a river, the question of whether the encounter occurred in 1612 or 1812

 is not so much wrong as misplaced; the narrative is doing work for which calendrical accuracy
would be an irrelevance and, sometimes, a hindrance. The faculty at work is closer to what
we might call participation: the past is summoned in order to take part in the present, not
consulted as an external authority over it.

This is why the criterion of factual fidelity, when imported wholesale, produces such consistent
confusion. It is the wrong tool. One does not measure a prayer by its propositional content; nor
does one fault a song for failing to be an inventory. The deeper error, however, is to suppose that
the modern, archival relation to the past is itself simply the description of how memory works,
rather than one historically peculiar way of organising it. Once that supposition is loosened,
the tribal narrative ceases to look like a degraded version of modern memory and begins to look
like evidence that the relation between a community and what precedes it can be ordered along
axes the modern register does not register.

None of this requires us to romanticise. A society that “remembers” through participation can
also misremember, can also harden a useful fiction into a weapon. The point is only that the
standard by which it would be judged to misremember is not the archival one, and that to apply
the archival one is to ask a question to which the practice was never an answer.
question_text_markdown: The author refers to anthropologists who are “even sympathetic” primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: concede that scholarly opinion on tribal memory has steadily improved over time.
option_b_markdown:
observers.

suggest that the assumption being criticised is not confined to hostile or dismissive
option_c_markdown:
indicate that sympathetic scholarship has so far failed to gather adequate ethnographic

evidence.
option_d_markdown:
contrast modern scholarship with older colonial accounts that treated tribal memory as

mere distortion.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3 (RC1 — Author’s purpose in citing “even sympathetic” anthropologists)

Correct: (b). The qualifier “even sympathetic” is used to show that the assumption (tribal
memory = modern memory + distortion) survives in scholarship that is not hostile, i.e., the
problem is structural, not merely a matter of bias. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because the
contrast colonial-vs-modern is a familiar move; but the passage does not invoke colonial accounts
here. Distorted emphasis. (a) unsupported —no claim of steady improvement. (c) unsupported —
no claim about evidence adequacy.

did once unfold. The faculty is supposed to be answerable to a past that exists independently
of the act of remembering, and it is judged accurate or faulty against that standard. This view
feels self-evident only because it has been naturalised by centuries of secular literacy, in which
the writing down of dates and the keeping of records have come to define what counts as “what
really happened.”

Tribal communities, by contrast, are often described as having a defective or merely poetic
version of this faculty. Their songs telescope generations into a single afternoon; their genealogies
absorb foreign rivers and foreign animals into the family line; their dead speak, instruct, and
sometimes vote. Anthropologists, even sympathetic ones, have tended to treat these features
as ornaments around a residual core of “real” memory, as though under the embellishment one
would eventually find the same archive the modern subject keeps. The assumption, in other
words, is that tribal memory is modern memory plus distortion.

It is this assumption, rather than tribal practice, that deserves scrutiny. The point of a tribal
narrative is not to record a past that lies behind it but to constitute a present community by
enacting a continuity that the community needs in order to act. When a clan “remembers” an
ancestor who fought a river, the question of whether the encounter occurred in 1612 or 1812

 is not so much wrong as misplaced; the narrative is doing work for which calendrical accuracy
would be an irrelevance and, sometimes, a hindrance. The faculty at work is closer to what
we might call participation: the past is summoned in order to take part in the present, not
consulted as an external authority over it.

This is why the criterion of factual fidelity, when imported wholesale, produces such consistent
confusion. It is the wrong tool. One does not measure a prayer by its propositional content; nor
does one fault a song for failing to be an inventory. The deeper error, however, is to suppose that
the modern, archival relation to the past is itself simply the description of how memory works,
rather than one historically peculiar way of organising it. Once that supposition is loosened,
the tribal narrative ceases to look like a degraded version of modern memory and begins to look
like evidence that the relation between a community and what precedes it can be ordered along
axes the modern register does not register.

None of this requires us to romanticise. A society that “remembers” through participation can
also misremember, can also harden a useful fiction into a weapon. The point is only that the
standard by which it would be judged to misremember is not the archival one, and that to apply
the archival one is to ask a question to which the practice was never an answer.
question_text_markdown: Which one of the following best captures the distinction the author is trying to draw?
option_a_markdown: Between a faculty that stores events and one that recovers them.
option_b_markdown:
Between memory understood as a record of an independent past and memory understood

as an act that constitutes a present community.
option_c_markdown: Between literate societies that value calendrical accuracy and oral societies that do not.
option_d_markdown: Between memory as a private function and memory as a collective one.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4 (RC1 — Distinction)

Correct: (b). The author’s distinction is between an archival faculty (memory as record of an
independent past) and a participatory one (memory as constitutive of the present community).
Runner-up: (d)—private vs. collective is a real distinction, and the passage gestures toward it,
but it is not the distinction the author is trying to draw ; true but not central. (a) too generic;
(c) reduces the distinction to a literate/oral contrast, which the passage explicitly resists.

question_text_markdown: The author would be least likely to endorse which of the following claims?
option_a_markdown:
A tribal narrative that absorbs foreign rivers into a family genealogy may nonetheless

be doing exactly what it is supposed to do.
option_b_markdown:
Modern archival memory and tribal participation are two historically situated ways of

relating to what precedes a community.
option_c_markdown:
Tribal narratives, once their idiom is properly understood, can be shown to encode the

same factual content as archival records, only in a different form.
option_d_markdown:
The fact that a tribal account is calendrically inaccurate is, by itself, not a serious

objection to it.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q5 (RC1 — Least likely to endorse)

Correct: (c). The author’s whole point is that translating tribal narratives into “the same
factual content. . . in a different form” is the wrong move: tribal narrative is not modern memory
in disguise. Runner-up: (b)—an attractive paraphrase of the author’s position; the author would
endorse it. Selecting (b) here would require treating the author’s relativising move as something
they reject—the opposite of the text. (a), (d) are directly endorsed by the passage.

same factual content as archival records, only in a different form.

(d) The fact that a tribal account is calendrically inaccurate is, by itself, not a serious

objection to it.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most seriously undermine the author’s central
argument?
option_a_markdown:
Some tribal communities, on contact with literacy, switch rapidly to archival modes of

recording the past without apparent loss.
option_b_markdown:
Ethnographers have documented tribal practices in which elders are explicitly corrected
by other elders for getting dates and sequences wrong, and the community treats such corrections
 as binding.
option_c_markdown:
Modern archival societies sometimes harden useful fictions about their past into political

weapons, just as tribal societies do.
option_d_markdown:
The participatory mode of relating to the past can be found, in attenuated form, within

modern literate societies as well.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q6 (RC1 — Weaken)

Correct: (b). If communities themselves treat chronological correction as binding, then the
participatory account underestimates the role of factual fidelity from within the practice, un-
dercutting the author’s claim that the archival standard is simply external to it. Runner-up:
(a)—tempting: rapid switching to literacy looks like evidence that the modern mode is more
native than the author allows. But the passage allows that the two modes can coexist or succeed
each other historically; rapid adoption does not show that the practice being replaced was an
archival practice wit
source_raw_block:
Q6. Which of the following, if true, would most seriously undermine the author’s central
argument?

(a) Some tribal communities, on contact with literacy, switch rapidly to archival modes of

recording the past without apparent loss.

(b) Ethnographers have documented tribal practices in which elders are explicitly corrected
by other elders for getting dates and sequences wrong, and the community treats such corrections
 as binding.

(c) Modern archival societies sometimes harden useful fictions about their past into political

weapons, just as tribal societies do.

(d) The participatory mode of relating to the past can be found, in attenuated form, within

modern literate societies as well.

passage_text_markdown:
Suppose, for a moment, that the most consequential moral decisions of a society—who is sen-
tenced, who is treated, who is admitted—were delegated to a system whose verdicts could be
derived from a small set of explicit principles. The appeal of such a prospect is not, in the first
instance, technological. It is the appeal of a judge who cannot be bribed, who does not tire,
and whose reasons can be inspected line by line. To many proponents of “machine ethics,” this
is the underlying promise: not that a machine will be more compassionate than a human, but
that it will be more answerable.

The route to such a system, the argument runs, is the route physics once took. Newton
compressed a tangle of celestial and terrestrial observations into three laws;
later theorists
compressed these further. Ethical theory, on this picture, awaits its own act of compression.
Utilitarianism, deontology and the rest are read as competing axiom systems, each generating
verdicts in particular cases; the project is to identify the system whose verdicts best survive
scrutiny, and to encode it. The expected payoff is twofold. First, judgement becomes auditable.
Second—and more quietly—the embarrassment of moral disagreement is reframed as a soluble
technical problem.

The analogy is seductive but selective. In physics, two theories that disagree about a prediction
can be brought before an experiment that, in principle, both sides accept as decisive. In ethics,
the analogue does not exist. When utilitarian and deontological verdicts diverge, no further
fact about the case settles which verdict is correct; the disagreement is, in part, about what
would even count as settling it. To suppose otherwise is not to apply the method of physics to
ethics; it is to import the appearance of that method while leaving its evidential substructure
behind.

A subtler worry is that the compression itself is a form of loss. Moral attention, as it is actually
exercised, attends to features of a case—a hesitation, a history, an unspoken obligation—that
resist statement as variables. A formal system can be designed only on features that can be
stated; whatever escapes statement is, for the system, absent. The result is not that the system
fails to capture morality but that it succeeds at capturing a sub-version of morality, and then is
taken to be morality. The danger is therefore not error in the ordinary sense. It is the gradual
substitution, in public life, of what is auditable for what is morally relevant.

None of this is an argument against machine ethics as such. There are domains—scheduling,
triage under known constraints, certain classes of administrative decision—where the gains in
consistency are real and the residual loss is modest. The argument is, rather, against the picture
in which machine ethics is the late-arriving successor of an old project, completing what physics
began. That picture flatters the project by misdescribing its difficulty. It treats the absence
of a decisive ethical experiment as a temporary embarrassment; in fact, it is the feature that
makes ethics what it is.

Q7 (RC2 — Function of paragraph 3)

Correct: (b). The paragraph identifies a structural disanalogy: in physics, disagreements can
be brought before mutually accepted experiments; in ethics, they cannot. This blocks the picture
in which ethics merely awaits its Newtonian compression. Runner-up: (d)—the paragraph does
suggest moral disagreement is not settled by further fact, but it does not commit to the strong
claim that ethical disagreement is “in principle irresoluble.” Change in degree of certainty /
overstatement.
(c)
miscasts the disanalogy as predictive-vs-normative; the passage’s distincti

question_text_markdown: The author’s attitude toward machine ethics is best described as:
option_a_markdown:
broadly hostile, on the grounds that delegating moral judgement to machines is intrin-

sically dangerous.
option_b_markdown:
cautiously favourable, holding that machine ethics is the natural completion of the

project physics began.
option_c_markdown:
discriminating: receptive to limited applications, but opposed to the larger picture in

which machine ethics “completes” ethics.
option_d_markdown:
ambivalent, citing both technical promise and unresolved disagreement as roughly equal

considerations.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q8 (RC2 — Author’s attitude)

Correct: (c). The closing paragraph explicitly allows applications where “gains in consistency
are real,” while rejecting the larger “completing what physics began” picture. Runner-up: (a)—
tempting because the bulk of the passage is critical; but “intrinsically dangerous” is stronger
than the author’s position, and ignores the qualifications in the final paragraph. Overstatement

12


/ scope. (b) reverses the author’s position; (d) flattens a structured view into a balance-sheet
ambivalence.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument in the fourth
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
Most everyday moral decisions in fact turn on a small set of features that can be stated

explicitly, with the unstatable residue making no systematic difference to outcomes.
option_b_markdown:
Many ordinary citizens, when asked, prefer machine-generated verdicts to human ones

in low-stakes administrative contexts.
option_c_markdown:
Even within physics, certain phenomena—turbulence, for example—resist compression

into closed-form principles.
option_d_markdown:
Formal ethical systems can be designed to flag uncertainty and refer hard cases to

human judgement.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q9 (RC2 — Weaken paragraph 4)

Correct: (a). The fourth paragraph’s argument is that formal systems necessarily lose features
that resist statement, and that this loss is morally consequential. If most decisions in fact turn
on a small set of statable features and the residue is systematically inconsequential, the author’s
loss-claim collapses. Runner-up: (c)—tempting because turbulence is a real case of in-physics
non-compressibility; but the author’s paragraph 4 argument is about ethics, not about the
physics analogy, which paragraph 3 already addressed. Answers a nearby but different question.

passage_text_markdown:
For most of the twentieth century, the large dam was less an engineering choice than a political
vocabulary. Nations as different as the United States, the Soviet Union, Egypt and India spoke
about their dams in the same idiom: of conquest, of awakening, of finally making the river do
useful work. The structure itself was secondary to the sentence it allowed a state to utter about
itself. A country that could pour that much concrete across that much water was, by the very
act, declaring its arrival.

It is tempting, in hindsight, to read this enthusiasm as straightforward error—an ecological
mistake corrected by later science. That reading is comforting but partial. The mistake, if
it was one, was not primarily about hydrology. The science of sediment, of fish migration, of
saline intrusion into deltas, was reasonably well understood even in the 1950s; what was new
was not the knowledge but the willingness to discount it. The dam project absorbed the costs
as the price of a larger settlement: a nation reconciled to its own ambition.

Here a distinction is worth making. To object to a particular dam on the grounds that its costs
 exceeded its benefits is one kind of argument. To object to the dam-building era on the grounds
that the cost–benefit framework was itself the wrong instrument is a different one. The first
treats the era as a series of bad calculations. The second treats it as a series of calculations from
which certain costs had been excluded in advance. The displaced cultivator, the silted estuary,
the village whose name vanishes from the new reservoir map—these did not appear as negative
entries in the ledger because they had not been admitted as entries at all.

It would be wrong, however, to present this exclusion as cynical. The planners did not, on
the whole, deceive themselves; they had a working theory under which the displaced were
temporary inconveniences to be compensated and the silted estuary was the affair of a different
ministry. The theory was coherent. It was also, in retrospect, the political form of the era:
the assumption that a sufficiently large public benefit licensed the unilateral redistribution of
private and ecological harm onto those least able to refuse it.

Critics of the dam-building era sometimes overstate the moral. The lesson is not that all large
infrastructure is suspect, nor that nature is to be left alone.
It is, more narrowly, that the
project of large infrastructure tends to recruit a particular kind of accounting—one in which
the harms most likely to be diffuse, slow, and borne by the least powerful are also the harms
least likely to be counted. Where such an accounting is the only accounting in the room, the
resulting decisions will reliably look prudent on the books and indefensible on the ground.

A serious successor to the dam-building era, then, is not a smaller dam. It is an accounting
that is harder to deceive.
question_text_markdown: The author’s primary purpose in the passage is to:
option_a_markdown:
document the ecological harms that twentieth-century dam-building inflicted on rivers

and the communities depending on them.
option_b_markdown:
argue that the central failing of the dam-building era lay not in faulty calculation but

in the prior exclusion of certain costs from the calculation altogether.
option_c_markdown:
defend the dam-building era as a coherent expression of mid-century political aspiration,

while conceding its environmental costs.
option_d_markdown:
recommend smaller, more localised infrastructure projects as a corrective to the excesses

of large dams.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q11 (RC3 — Primary purpose)

Correct: (b). The author’s central argument is the second-paragraph turn: the failing was not
bad arithmetic but a prior exclusion of certain costs. Runner-up: (c)—tempting because the
author treats mid-century planners with restraint and grants them coherence; but the author’s
purpose is critical, not defensive. Confuses concession with conclusion. (a) reduces a normative
argument to documentation. (d) makes a prescriptive recommendation the author explicitly
resists (“a serious successor. . . is not a smaller dam”).
source_raw_block:
Q11. The author’s primary purpose in the passage is to:

(a) document the ecological harms that twentieth-century dam-building inflicted on rivers

and the communities depending on them.

(b) argue that the central failing of the dam-building era lay not in faulty calculation but

in the prior exclusion of certain costs from the calculation altogether.

(c) defend the dam-building era as a coherent expression of mid-century political aspiration,

while conceding its environmental costs.

(d) recommend smaller, more localised infrastructure projects as a corrective to the excesses

of large dams.

question_text_markdown: The author refers to the dam as a “political vocabulary” primarily in order to:
option_a_markdown: suggest that dams were always more rhetorical than functional.
option_b_markdown:
indicate that the appeal of large dams lay partly in what their construction allowed a

state to say about itself, independent of any particular engineering need.
option_c_markdown: criticise mid-century states for prioritising propaganda over development.
option_d_markdown:
draw a parallel between the language of dam-building and the language of military

conquest.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q12 (RC3 — “Political vocabulary”)

Correct: (b). The phrase is used to register that the structure mattered for what it allowed the
state to say, independent of functional need. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because the language
of “conquest” invites a military parallel; but the author uses the phrase to make a constitutive,
not a comparative, point. True but not central. (a) overstates (“always more rhetorical than
functional”); (c) makes a moral charge the author does not.

(d) draw a parallel between the language of dam-building and the language of military

conquest.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following is true according to the passage but does not support the author’s
central claim?
option_a_markdown: The harms of dam-building tended to fall on those least able to refuse them.
option_b_markdown:
Mid-century planners did not, on the whole, deceive themselves about the theoretical

framework they were operating within.
option_c_markdown: The science of sediment and fish migration was reasonably well understood by the 1950s.
option_d_markdown: A serious successor to the dam-building era would be an accounting harder to deceive.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q13 (RC3 — True but not supporting the central claim)

Correct: (c). That the relevant science was already understood is true (it is in paragraph 2),
but it functions as background—the author’s claim is about the accounting, not about scientific
ignorance. Runner-up: (b)—a tempting choice because mid-century planners’ self-honesty is
also a concession; but it directly supports the author’s argument that the failure was structural
rather than self-deception. Confuses concession that supports the argument with one that is
incidental. (a) and (d) both directly support the central claim.

question_text_markdown: Which of the following would the author be most likely to agree with?
option_a_markdown:
Cost–benefit analysis is, in principle, an unsuitable tool for evaluating public infrastruc-

ture.
option_b_markdown:
A correctly performed cost–benefit analysis would have prevented most of the harms

associated with twentieth-century dams.
option_c_markdown:
Cost–benefit analyses of large infrastructure are characteristically shaped by which
harms have been admitted as costs in advance, and this prior admission, more than the arith-
metic, determines the verdict.
option_d_markdown:
Ecological harms are intrinsically more important than the economic benefits of infras-

tructure and should always be weighted accordingly.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q14 (RC3 — Author would agree)

Correct: (c). This restates the second-paragraph turn precisely: the verdict depends on which
harms are admitted as costs in advance. Runner-up: (a)—tempting because the author criticises
cost–benefit analysis as it was used; but the author’s complaint is about the admission of costs,
not about the framework’s intrinsic suitability. Scope shift from operational to principled. (b)
too strong (“would have prevented most”). (d) makes a prescriptive ranking the author does
not endorse.
source_raw_block:
Q14. Which of the following would the author be most likely to agree with?
 (a) Cost–benefit analysis is, in principle, an unsuitable tool for evaluating public infrastruc-

ture.

(b) A correctly performed cost–benefit analysis would have prevented most of the harms

associated with twentieth-century dams.

(c) Cost–benefit analyses of large infrastructure are characteristically shaped by which
harms have been admitted as costs in advance, and this prior admission, more than the arith-
metic, determines the verdict.

(d) Ecological harms are intrinsically more important than the economic benefits of infras-

tructure and should always be weighted accordingly.

question_text_markdown:
Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1 to 4, when arranged in
the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the four-digit sequence.
option_a_markdown:
Surveys nonetheless suggest that the share of the relevant population using “Latinx” in
self-description remains a small minority, well below the share that uses “Hispanic” or
“Latino.”
option_b_markdown:
The term “Latinx” emerged from activists who sought a self-designation that would not

impose the grammatical gender built into Spanish.
option_c_markdown:
Their objection was not that “Latino” was offensive in any direct sense but that its default-

masculine form silently absorbed everyone who did not fit a binary frame.
option_d_markdown:
Whether this gap reflects resistance to the term, unfamiliarity with it, or the slow tempo

at which any self-designation diffuses through a community remains contested.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17 (Para Jumble — Latinx)

Correct order: 2–3–1–4. Sentence 2 introduces the term and its origin. Sentence 3 elaborates
the activists’ specific objection. Sentence 1 introduces the contrasting fact (low adoption).
Sentence 4 closes by problematising the gap.
Why not 2–3–4–1: 4 (“Whether this gap reflects. . . ”) presupposes that the gap has already
been stated; the gap is stated in 1, so 1 must precede 4. 2 must open (defines the term); 3 must
follow 2 (“their objection” refers to the activists in 2).
source_raw_block:
Q17. Para Jumble (TITA). The four sentences below, labelled 1 to 4, when arranged in
the correct order, form a coherent paragraph. Enter the four-digit sequence.

1. Surveys nonetheless suggest that the share of the relevant population using “Latinx” in
self-description remains a small minority, well below the share that uses “Hispanic” or
“Latino.”

2. The term “Latinx” emerged from activists who sought a self-designation that would not

impose the grammatical gender built into Spanish.

3. Their objection was not that “Latino” was offensive in any direct sense but that its default-

masculine form silently absorbed everyone who did not fit a binary frame.

4. Whether this gap reflects resistance to the term, unfamiliarity with it, or the slow tempo

at which any self-designation diffuses through a community remains contested.

question_text_markdown:
Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1 to 5. Four of
them, in a particular order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not fit.
Enter the sentence number.
option_a_markdown:
Standard models of deep-sea chemistry have long treated the lightless seafloor as a place

where oxygen is consumed but not produced.
option_b_markdown:
Recent measurements at depths of around 4,000 metres in the Pacific, however, have de-

tected oxygen being produced in the absence of any photosynthesis.
 3. The source appears to be the polymetallic nodules that carpet parts of the abyssal floor,

whose surfaces seem to drive a slow electrochemical splitting of seawater.
option_c_markdown:
If the finding holds up under replication, it would complicate plans to mine these nodules,
since their removal would also remove a previously unsuspected source of oxygen for deep-
sea life.
option_d_markdown:
Phytoplankton near the ocean surface remain, by a large margin, the principal producers

of oceanic oxygen and the basis of most marine food webs.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18 (Odd Sentence Out — Deep-sea Oxygen)

Correct: 5. The coherent sequence is 1–2–3–4: standard view (1) → anomalous finding (2)
→ proposed mechanism (3) → practical implication (4). Sentence 5, while topically related to
oceanic oxygen, returns to the surface ecosystem and the conventional picture, and so steps
outside the paragraph’s specific argumentative movement about a surprising deep-sea source.
It is a different stage of argument (background reassurance rather than the anomalous finding’s
development).
source_raw_block:
Q18. Odd Sentence Out (TITA). Five sentences are given below, labelled 1 to 5. Four of
them, in a particular order, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the sentence that does not fit.
Enter the sentence number.

1. Standard models of deep-sea chemistry have long treated the lightless seafloor as a place

where oxygen is consumed but not produced.

2. Recent measurements at depths of around 4,000 metres in the Pacific, however, have de-

tected oxygen being produced in the absence of any photosynthesis.
 3. The source appears to be the polymetallic nodules that carpet parts of the abyssal floor,

whose surfaces seem to drive a slow electrochemical splitting of seawater.

4. If the finding holds up under replication, it would complicate plans to mine these nodules,
since their removal would also remove a previously unsuspected source of oxygen for deep-
sea life.

5. Phytoplankton near the ocean surface remain, by a large margin, the principal producers

of oceanic oxygen and the basis of most marine food webs.

question_text_markdown:
Sentence Placement. The paragraph below has four possible insertion points marked
[A], [B], [C] and [D]. Read the paragraph and the standalone sentence, and choose the insertion
point at which the sentence best fits.

Standalone sentence: “Productivity gains, once expected to feed through to broader living
standards, now primarily serve to enhance returns to wealth.”

Paragraph: Public discussion of inequality remains dominated by figures on income. [A] Yet, in
most advanced economies, the gap between high and median incomes has widened far less than
the gap between large and median holdings of wealth. [B] The reasons are partly demographic
and partly institutional: an ageing population accumulates assets, and the tax treatment of
capital has become more favourable relative to labour over recent decades.
[C] These shifts
have proved difficult to register in standard economic indicators, which were designed for an
economy in which the principal source of household prosperity was the wage. [D] If the indicator
continues to lag the phenomenon, policy will continue to address symptoms rather than causes.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19 (Sentence Placement — Inequality)

Correct: [B]. The sentence introduces a causal mechanism connecting widening wealth gaps
to where productivity gains now accrue. It fits naturally after the empirical observation (income
vs. wealth gap) and before the demographic/institutional reasons paragraph develops.
Why not [A]: too early—no claim about wealth has yet been set up.
Why not [C]: would interrupt the connected reasons (“demographic and institutional. . . ” →

14


“These shifts. . . ”).
Why not [D]: the paragraph’s closing already turns to indicators and policy; inserting a mech-
anism here
source_raw_block:

Paragraph: Public discussion of inequality remains dominated by figures on income. [A] Yet, in
most advanced economies, the gap between high and median incomes has widened far less than
the gap between large and median holdings of wealth. [B] The reasons are partly demographic
and partly institutional: an ageing population accumulates assets, and the tax treatment of
capital has become more favourable relative to labour over recent decades.
[C] These shifts
have proved difficult to register in standard economic indicators, which were designed for an
economy in which the principal source of household prosperity was the wage. [D] If the indicator
continues to lag the phenomenon, policy will continue to address symptoms rather than causes.

(a) [A]
(b) [B]
(c) [C]
(d) [D]

question_text_markdown:
Para Summary. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the
option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Studies of a Dutch kindred in which several males exhibited episodic, severe aggression eventually
localised the responsible defect to a small region of the X chromosome. The discovery was widely
reported as the identification of a “gene for aggression,” a phrasing the original investigators
conspicuously avoided. Their reticence reflected a methodological point that the popular framing
overran: the affected males in this particular kindred shared a particular defect; from this it
did not follow that aggression in general had a single genetic source, or that other aggressive
individuals would carry the same defect, or that the defect, in a different developmental and social
setting, would produce the same behaviour. The finding was about a kindred. The headlines were
about a species.
option_a_markdown:
Aggression in humans cannot be reduced to a single genetic cause, and the Dutch study

showed this.
option_b_markdown:
The popular reporting of the Dutch study mistook a finding about one kindred for a
finding about aggression in general, and the investigators’ careful phrasing was meant to prevent
precisely this slippage.
option_c_markdown:
Genetic research on behaviour is routinely misreported, and the Dutch aggression study

is a notable example.
option_d_markdown:
The link between an X-chromosome defect and aggression holds only within the specific

developmental and social setting of the Dutch kindred.

detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20 (Para Summary — Genetic Aggression)

Correct: (b). The paragraph’s whole point is the mismatch between what the study showed
(a kindred-specific defect) and how it was reported (a gene for aggression in general), with the
investigators’ careful phrasing aimed at preventing exactly that slippage. Runner-up: (d)—
tempting because the paragraph does suggest the finding was restricted to its setting; but
(d) overstates by claiming the link holds only in the Dutch setting—the passage’s point is
methodological caution, not a settled scope claim. Change in degree of certainty. (a) restates
a true gen

Q20. Para Summary. The passage given below is followed by four summaries. Choose the
option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Studies of a Dutch kindred in which several males exhibited episodic, severe aggression eventually
localised the responsible defect to a small region of the X chromosome. The discovery was widely
reported as the identification of a “gene for aggression,” a phrasing the original investigators
conspicuously avoided. Their reticence reflected a methodological point that the popular framing
overran: the affected males in this particular kindred shared a particular defect; from this it
did not follow that aggression in general had a single genetic source, or that other aggressive
individuals would carry the same defect, or that the defect, in a different developmental and social
setting, would produce the same behaviour. The finding was about a kindred. The headlines were
about a species.

(a) Aggression in humans cannot be reduced to a single genetic cause, and the Dutch study

showed this.

(b) The popular reporting of the Dutch study mistook a finding about one kindred for a
finding about aggression in general, and the investigators’ careful phrasing was meant to prevent
precisely this slippage.

(c) Genetic research on behaviour is routinely misreported, and the Dutch aggression study

is a notable example.

(d) The link between an X-chromosome defect and aggression holds only within the specific

developmental and social setting of the Dutch kindred.

passage_text_markdown:
The Indian Forest Act of 1878 was, in form, a statute about trees. In effect, it was a statute
about the relation between a centralising state and the populations that lived where the trees
stood. Drafted in the wake of a rapid expansion of railway construction, it converted vast
tracts of wooded land, much of it under customary common use, into property of the colonial
state, classified into “reserved,” “protected” and “village” categories. The categories were
administratively neat; on the ground they amounted to the same thing. The state acquired the
standing right to decide which trees could be felled, by whom, and on what terms.

Historians sometimes describe the 1878 Act as an instrument of timber extraction. This is true
but insufficient. Timber was the immediate occasion, but the more durable achievement of the
Act was the routinisation of a particular form of authority: a bureaucratic relation in which
the cultivator’s grazing route, the herbalist’s foraging path, and the cremation ground at the
edge of the village were all rendered legible—and therefore alterable—by a salaried officer who
had never met the people concerned. What was extracted, in the long run, was not principally
timber. It was discretion.

It is on this point that the 1982 draft, which sought to update the colonial framework, ran
into a controversy disproportionate to its overt provisions. The draft tightened restrictions and
increased penalties, but its substantive offence was structural: it preserved, almost intact, the
colonial assumption that forest dwellers stood in relation to the forest as trespassers on state
property unless specifically licensed otherwise. By 1982 a substantial political movement existed
that had begun to think of that assumption itself as the problem. The criticism, in other words,
was not that the 1982 draft was harsher than its predecessor, but that it was, in the relevant
sense, the same.

Defenders of the bureaucratic framework have often replied that without centralised control,
forest cover would collapse under local pressure. The reply has a real point. Decentralisation
administered carelessly has, in several regions, accelerated rather than slowed deforestation.
But this misses the question being asked. The question is not whether decentralised regimes are
uniformly successful—they are not—but whether the framework that treats local communities as
presumptive offenders against their own surroundings can ever produce sustained custodianship.
The empirical record suggests that it cannot, not because the framework lacks technical capacity
but because it lacks the participatory legitimacy without which technical capacity in this domain
corrodes.

A more honest reading of the 1982 episode, then, would treat it less as a failed updating
and more as the moment at which the legitimacy deficit of a century-old framework became
politically unignorable. What the controversy revealed was not chiefly that a particular draft
was poorly written but that the inherited template, regardless of who held the pen, could no
longer be improved by amendment alone.
 Q21. Which of the following best captures the central claim of the passage?

(a) The 1878 Act was a colonial measure for timber extraction, while the 1982 draft sought,

unsuccessfully, to correct it.

(b) The deeper continuity between the 1878 Act and the 1982 draft lay in a shared structural
assumption about authority over forests, and it was this continuity, more than any specific
provision, that the 1982 controversy targeted.

(c) The 1982 draft failed because it imposed harsher penalties than the 1878 Act without

granting forest dwellers commensurate rights.

(d) Decentralised forest management would have prevented both colonial overreach and the

controversy of 1982.
question_text_markdown:
The author’s statement that “what was extracted, in the long run, was not principally
timber. It was discretion” is best understood as:
option_a_markdown: a rhetorical exaggeration; the principal extraction was, in fact, timber.
option_b_markdown:
a claim that the lasting consequence of the 1878 Act was not the removal of forest
products but the relocation of routine decision-making about forests away from the people who
lived in them.
option_c_markdown:
a suggestion that the colonial state lacked the administrative capacity for sustained

timber extraction.
option_d_markdown: a complaint that bureaucratic officials made arbitrary decisions for personal gain.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22 (RC4 — “not principally timber. It was discretion”)

Correct: (b). The line claims that the durable consequence of the Act was the relocation
of routine decisions away from local actors. Runner-up: (d)—tempting because “discretion”
invites a connotation of arbitrariness; but the passage makes a structural, not a moral-character,
claim. Distorted emphasis. (a) misreads the line as hyperbole; (c) reverses the claim.

question_text_markdown:
Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s argument in the fourth
paragraph?
option_a_markdown:
In several regions where decentralised regimes were instituted carelessly, deforestation

accelerated.
option_b_markdown:
Bureaucratic forest management is more efficient than community-based management

in producing audited annual yield figures.
option_c_markdown:
Forest cover in regions where local communities were granted long-term custodial rights
has, over recent decades, remained stable or improved relative to comparable regions under
centralised administration.
option_d_markdown:
Cases exist in which community-managed forests have suffered local overuse during

periods of acute economic stress.

(e) (Note: option not used—ignore.)

(Choose from (a) to (d).)
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23 (RC4 — Weaken paragraph 4)

Correct: (c). The fourth paragraph’s claim is that the framework that treats locals as pre-
sumptive offenders cannot produce sustained custodianship. Stable or improving forest cover un-
der long-term community custodianship is direct empirical pressure against that claim. Runner-
up: (a)—this is acknowledged by the author as a real point; it does not weaken the argument
the author is making. (b) is irrelevant (efficiency of audit is not the issue). (d) is what the
author already concedes (decentralisation is not uniformly successful).

passage_text_markdown:
There is a familiar story about science in which observation comes first and theory follows: the
patient naturalist watches, records, classifies, and only later proposes an account. Philosophers of
science have, for nearly a century, taken pains to point out that this story is at best a half-truth.
Observation, it turns out, is not a neutral inspection of the world but an activity already steeped in
the theoretical commitments of the observer. To see a tracing on a screen as evidence of a tumour is
to bring to that tracing a whole grammar of categories, expectations and prior cases. The lay person
sees a smudge; the radiologist sees a stage.
This dependence of seeing on framing has tempted some commentators to conclude that scientific
observation is therefore arbitrary, or merely a reflection of the prevailing consensus. The conclusion
does not follow. That observation is theory-laden does not entail that any theory will do; a chest
X-ray will not, on any responsible reading, disclose a flowering shrub. The grammar of categories that
the radiologist brings is itself answerable to public criteria, to comparison cases, to error, and to the
awkward arrival of phenomena that the existing categories cannot accommodate. The dependence is
real; the arbitrariness is not.
What the framing-thesis does insist on, however, is that disagreements at the level of observation
often turn out, on inspection, to be disagreements at the level of categories. Two clinicians looking
at the same tracing may report different findings not because either is careless but because they are
operating, perhaps unwittingly, with subtly different schemes. Progress in such cases is not a matter
of one of them looking harder. It comes from making the schemes themselves visible – naming the
categories, defending them, weighing their costs against rival schemes – so that what looked like a
quarrel about what is there becomes a more tractable quarrel about how best to carve it up.
This recasting has a sobering implication for the appeal, common in popular writing about science,
to “letting the data speak”. The data, in any interesting case, do not speak; they are spoken about,
and the vocabulary used to speak about them is doing a great deal of the work. To insist on the
silence of the data is not, of course, to license vocabulary by fiat; it is rather to acknowledge that
vocabularies must themselves be defended, and that some of the most important scientific arguments
are arguments not about findings but about the terms in which findings are to be reported.
question_text_markdown:
[Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Author’s main concern | Difficulty: Hard]

Which one of the following best captures the central claim the author is most concerned to defend?
option_a_markdown:
Scientific observation is so deeply shaped by the observer’s training that disagreements be-

tween scientists are, in the end, irresolvable without an appeal to consensus.
option_b_markdown:
Because observation is shaped by prior categories, much scientific disagreement is, on inspec-
tion, disagreement about the categories themselves, and is best addressed by making those
categories explicit.
option_c_markdown:
The lay-expert gap in interpreting evidence shows that scientific findings should be reported

in language that ordinary readers, and not only specialists, are equipped to evaluate.
option_d_markdown:
Although “letting the data speak” is a misleading slogan, scientific progress still depends

primarily on the steady accumulation of careful, theory-free observations.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q1. Author’s main concern – Answer: (2)

The passage develops a single line of argument: observation depends on categories; this dependence
does not entail arbitrariness; the practical upshot is that apparent disagreements about findings
are often disagreements about categories, best handled by making the categories explicit. Option
(2) reproduces that line.

• (1) Distorted – “irresolvable without consensus” is the very view the author rejects (“the de-

pendence is real; the arbitrariness is not”).

• (3) True-but-irrelevant – the lay/expert contrast is illustrative, not the central claim; the author

never recommends lay-accessible reporting.

8


• (4) Opposite – the author explicitly denies that observations are “theory-free”.
source_raw_block:
Q1. [Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Author’s main concern | Difficulty: Hard]

Which one of the following best captures the central claim the author is most concerned to defend?

(1) Scientific observation is so deeply shaped by the observer’s training that disagreements be-

tween scientists are, in the end, irresolvable without an appeal to consensus.

(2) Because observation is shaped by prior categories, much scientific disagreement is, on inspec-
tion, disagreement about the categories themselves, and is best addressed by making those
categories explicit.

(3) The lay-expert gap in interpreting evidence shows that scientific findings should be reported

in language that ordinary readers, and not only specialists, are equipped to evaluate.

(4) Although “letting the data speak” is a misleading slogan, scientific progress still depends

primarily on the steady accumulation of careful, theory-free observations.

question_text_markdown:
[Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Function of an example | Difficulty: Hard]

The author refers to the chest X-ray that “will not, on any responsible reading, disclose a flowering
shrub” principally in order to:
option_a_markdown:
illustrate that scientific observation, though guided by theory, still encounters a world that

constrains what can be seen.
option_b_markdown:
concede a point to commentators who hold that observation merely reflects prevailing con-

sensus among experts.
option_c_markdown:
suggest that radiology, unlike botany, depends on highly specialised categories not available

to the lay observer.
option_d_markdown:
argue that the data, properly attended to, can in fact “speak” once the observer’s schemes

are stripped away.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q2. Function of the chest X-ray / flowering shrub example – Answer: (1)

The clause appears immediately after the author has noted the temptation to slide from “theory-
laden” to “arbitrary”. Its function is to mark the limit: the world resists certain readings. The
remainder of the paragraph reinforces this with “public criteria”, “comparison cases”, “error”.

• (2) Reversed – the example is offered against those commentators, not in concession to them.

• (3) True-but-irrelevant – the example is about constraint by the world, not about specialisation.

• (4) Distorted – the author rejects “letting the data speak”; the example does not support

stripping schemes away.

question_text_markdown:
[Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Inference / scope | Difficulty: Very Hard]

Which one of the following can be most safely inferred from the passage?
option_a_markdown:
Once two clinicians reach the same finding from a tracing, they can be assumed to be operating

with the same underlying categories.
option_b_markdown:
A scientific disagreement that resists resolution at the level of looking more carefully is, on
the author’s view, likely to be a disagreement that has not yet been correctly diagnosed as
one about categories.
option_c_markdown:
The framing-thesis, in the form the author endorses, treats consensus among trained observers

as the ultimate criterion of whether a category is well-formed.
option_d_markdown:
Disputes about the vocabulary in which findings are reported are, on the author’s account,

less important than the empirical disputes they are sometimes mistaken for.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q3. Inference / scope – Answer: (2)

The author argues that when looking-harder fails, the disagreement is typically located at the
level of categories, and resolution comes from making schemes explicit. (2) restates this in infer-
ential form (“likely to be a disagreement that has not yet been correctly diagnosed as one about
categories”).

• (1) Reversed inference – agreement on a finding does not establish identity of underlying cat-
egories; the passage warns the opposite (subtly different schemes can co-exist with surface
agreement, and divergence reveals them).

• (3) Distorted – the author appeals to “public criteria, comparison cases, error”, explicitly not

to consensus as the ultimate criterion.

• (4) Opposite – the passage’s closing sentence treats vocabulary-arguments as among the most

important scientific arguments, not as less important.

categories, defending them, weighing their costs against rival schemes – so that what looked like a
quarrel about what is there becomes a more tractable quarrel about how best to carve it up.
This recasting has a sobering implication for the appeal, common in popular writing about science,
to “letting the data speak”. The data, in any interesting case, do not speak; they are spoken about,
and the vocabulary used to speak about them is doing a great deal of the work. To insist on the
silence of the data is not, of course, to license vocabulary by fiat; it is rather to acknowledge that
vocabularies must themselves be defended, and that some of the most important scientific arguments
are arguments not about findings but about the terms in which findings are to be reported.
question_text_markdown:
[Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Strengthen/Weaken | Difficulty: Hard]

Which one of the following, if true, would most weaken the author’s position?
option_a_markdown:
In a controlled study, radiologists from very different training backgrounds, when shown
ambiguous tracings, sometimes return findings that no available scheme of categories can
reconcile.
option_b_markdown:
In a controlled study, radiologists from very different training backgrounds, when asked to
make their categories explicit and to argue over them, consistently converge on the same
finding for ambiguous tracings.
option_c_markdown:
Lay viewers, when given a brief tutorial on the relevant categories, are still consistently

outperformed by trained radiologists on the same tracings.
option_d_markdown:
Historical episodes show that scientific vocabularies, once entrenched, are abandoned only

under great institutional pressure, not in response to argument.

Q5. [Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Author’s attitude / tone | Difficulty: Medium-Hard]

The author’s attitude toward the slogan “letting the data speak” is best described as:
option_e_markdown: dismissive: the slogan is a piece of rhetoric without intellectual content.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q4. Most weakening – Answer: (1)

The author’s positive prescription is that making categories explicit converts an intractable obser-
vational dispute into a tractable category-dispute. The weakener is therefore the option in which
exactly this recasting fails. Option (1) describes precisely such a case: even when findings are
recast at the level of available schemes, “no available scheme can reconcile” them. The author’s
recommended path does not, in this scenario, deliver tractability.

• (2) Strengthens, not weakens – this is the convergence the author predicts when categories are

made explicit.

• (3) True-but-irrelevant – the lay/expert performance gap is consistent with the author’s view;

the author never claims that tutorials erase expertise.

• (4) True-but-irrelevant – the sociology of vocabulary change is not what is at stake; the author’s

claim concerns the structure of disagreement, not the speed of vocabulary turnover.
source_raw_block:
Q4. [Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Strengthen/Weaken | Difficulty: Hard]

ambiguous tracings, sometimes return findings that no available scheme of categories can
reconcile.

(2) In a controlled study, radiologists from very different training backgrounds, when asked to
make their categories explicit and to argue over them, consistently converge on the same
finding for ambiguous tracings.

Q5. [Section: RC – Passage 1 | Type: MCQ – Author’s attitude / tone | Difficulty: Medium-Hard]

The author’s attitude toward the slogan “letting the data speak” is best described as:

(1) dismissive: the slogan is a piece of rhetoric without intellectual content.

(2) critical but qualified: the slogan misdescribes the work that vocabularies do, though the

author does not propose that vocabularies may be chosen at will.

(3) sympathetic: the slogan is a useful reminder, even if its formulation is loose.

(4) neutral: the slogan is reported only to set up a discussion of clinicians’ disagreements.

question_text_markdown:
The passage below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best
captures the essence of the passage.

Critics often charge that the consumer who buys “ethically” is engaged in a private gesture of no
public consequence: each fair-trade purchase, after all, is a drop in an ocean of commerce. The
charge is not without force, but it misidentifies the mechanism. An ethical purchase is not significant
because of what it transfers to the producer; the sums involved are usually trivial. It is significant
because it commits the buyer, however slightly, to a description of herself under which certain other
purchases become awkward. The drops do not raise the ocean. They reshape the drinker.
option_a_markdown:
Ethical consumption is criticized as a private gesture, but its real importance lies in the
cumulative transfer of resources from buyers to producers in fair-trade arrangements.
option_b_markdown:
The case for ethical consumption rests not on the material gains it delivers to producers but

on the way it commits the buyer to a self-description that constrains future choices.
option_c_markdown:
Critics are right that ethical consumption has no public consequence, since the sums transferred

to producers are too small to alter the larger economy.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q17. Answer: (B). The passage explicitly rejects the transfer-to-producer mechanism (“the sums
involved are usually trivial”) and locates significance in self-commitment (“commits the buyer ... to
a description of herself”). The final two sentences clinch this: the drops do not raise the ocean;
they reshape the drinker.
Runner-up (D) is tempting because it mentions self-fashioning. But (D) smuggles in a forecast (“may
eventually ... alter the wider economy”) that the passage explicitly denies. The passage’s whole
point is that the importance does not depend on aggregate economic effect (smuggled implication;
distorted emphasis).
(A) inverts the passage’s claim about transfers. (C) reproduces the critics’ charge that the passage
moves to reframe.
source_raw_block:

because of what it transfers to the producer; the sums involved are usually trivial. It is significant
