question_text_markdown:
The passage below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best
captures the essence of the passage.

Open-plan offices were sold to firms on the promise of unscripted collaboration: brilliance, it was
thought, needed only proximity. The evidence has been less obliging. Studies repeatedly show that
when partitions are removed, face-to-face exchange falls and electronic messaging rises. The result is
not the spontaneous conversation the design imagined but a quieter avoidance, conducted on screens,
of the very colleagues now within arm’s reach. The design did not fail to influence behaviour; it
influenced it in the opposite direction.
option_a_markdown:
Open-plan offices fail because employees prefer electronic communication to face-to-face

conversation, regardless of the physical setting.
option_b_markdown:
Despite their stated aim of fostering collaboration, open-plan offices in practice reduce direct
interaction and substitute mediated exchange, achieving the opposite of what they were
designed to achieve.
option_c_markdown:
Open-plan offices were promoted on a theory of proximity-driven collaboration that empirical

studies have failed to confirm.
option_d_markdown:
The shift from face-to-face conversation to electronic messaging in open-plan offices reflects a

broader cultural retreat from direct interaction.

Format: MCQ
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q18. Answer: (B). The passage states a stated aim (collaboration), then reports an empirical
reversal (electronic messaging rises), and concludes that the design influenced behaviour in the
 Verbal Ability & Reading Comprehension

opposite direction. (B) captures all three.
Runner-up (C) is tempting because empirical disconfirmation is mentioned. But (C) stops at “failed
to confirm,” missing the stronger claim that the design produced the opposite of its intended effect
(true but partial).
(A) projects a fixed employee preference the passage does not assert; the design itself does the work.
(D) generalizes to a cultural retreat that the passage does not authorize.

question_text_markdown:
The passage below is followed by four summaries. Choose the option that best
captures the essence of the passage.

A surprising feature of expertise is that its possessors are often poor explainers of what they know.
The chess master cannot reconstruct the search that led to her move; the experienced clinician
cannot articulate the cue that prompted her diagnosis. This is not modesty or evasion. The very
compression that makes expert judgment fast also makes it opaque to introspection. Asking the
expert to slow down and verbalize her reasoning typically produces, not a window onto the original
process, but a plausible after-the-fact narrative. Expertise, in short, is not a body of stated rules
waiting to be uttered. It is a structure of perception whose workings are largely hidden from the one
who exercises it.
option_a_markdown:
Experts cannot explain their reasoning because the speed of expert judgment compresses
the underlying process beyond the reach of introspection, making expertise a structure of
perception rather than a statable set of rules.
option_b_markdown:
Experts often fail to verbalize their reasoning, and when pressed to do so they tend to invent

plausible narratives that misrepresent the original process.
option_c_markdown:
Because expertise is a structure of perception rather than a body of rules, it cannot be taught

through explicit instruction and must instead be acquired through practice.
option_d_markdown:
The opacity of expert judgment is a serious obstacle to the study of expertise, since the

experts themselves are unreliable witnesses to their own reasoning.

Format: MCQ
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q19. Answer: (A). (A) integrates the compression of expert judgment, its consequent opacity to
introspection, and the resulting redescription of expertise as a structure of perception. All three
are stitched into a single argumentative line in the passage.
Runner-up (B) is tempting because the passage does say verbalization produces “a plausible after-
the-fact narrative.” But (B) treats this as the main point, missing the structural redescription of
expertise as perception rather than rules (true but partial; mistakes a step for the conclusion).
(C) introduces a pedagogical claim (“cannot be taught through explicit instruction”) that the
passage does not make. (D) shifts the topic to research methodology, which is not the passage’s
concern.
source_raw_block:

question_text_markdown:
Five sentences related to a topic are given below in a jumbled order. Four of
them, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the odd sentence
and enter its number as the answer.
option_a_markdown:
Cities, viewed from the air at night, present themselves as constellations of light, suggesting a

uniform brilliance throughout.
option_b_markdown:
On the ground, however, the same city resolves into pockets of intense illumination separated

by stretches of comparative dimness.
option_c_markdown:
The contrast follows the contours of older inequalities, with the brightest blocks corresponding

closely to the wealthiest postal codes.
option_d_markdown:
Excessive artificial light has been shown to disrupt the circadian rhythms of nocturnal insects,

with cascading effects up the food chain.
option_e_markdown:
Lighting, in this sense, is not a neutral service distributed across an urban map but a record,

written in lumens, of who has been seen as worth illuminating.

Format: TITA. Enter the number (1–5) of the odd sentence.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q20. Answer: 4. The four coherent sentences are 1, 2, 3, 5, in order.
Sentence 1 sets the aerial view of uniform light. Sentence 2 introduces the “however” that reveals
pockets of brightness and dimness on the ground. Sentence 3 explains that the contrast tracks
wealth. Sentence 5 concludes that lighting is a record of who is seen as worth illuminating.
Sentence 4 (the disruption of insect circadian rhythms) shares the topic of urban light but belongs to
a different argument — ecological harms of artificial light, not the distributive politics of illumination.
It cannot be integrated into the social-inequality flow without breaking the paragraph’s movement.

question_text_markdown:
Five sentences related to a topic are given below in a jumbled order. Four of
them, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Identify the odd sentence
and enter its number as the answer.
option_a_markdown:
The earliest paper currencies were not, as is often assumed, designed to replace metal coins

for ordinary transactions.
option_b_markdown:
They were instruments by which long-distance merchants could transfer claims without

physically moving heavy bullion across hostile terrain.
option_c_markdown:
Their value rested on the credibility of the issuing house, not on any intrinsic property of the

paper itself.
option_d_markdown:
Indeed, for centuries paper notes circulated alongside coin, addressed to a narrower commercial

public.
option_e_markdown:
Modern central banks rely on a similar foundation, since the legal-tender status of a banknote

is ultimately a public assurance about the issuer.

Format: TITA. Enter the number (1–5) of the odd sentence.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q21. Answer: 5. The four coherent sentences are 1, 4, 2, 3.
Sentence 1 introduces the historical claim that paper currencies were not designed for ordinary
transactions. Sentence 4 supplies the “indeed” that confirms paper notes circulated alongside coin,
addressed to a narrower public. Sentence 2 explains what they were for (long-distance merchant
transfers). Sentence 3 explains the basis of their value (credibility of the issuing house).
Sentence 5 leaps from historical paper notes to modern central banking, importing a contemporary
institutional analogue. It is thematically adjacent but breaks the historical-explanatory arc; it
belongs to a separate paragraph that draws the modern parallel.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences given below, when arranged in the correct order, form
a coherent paragraph. Determine the proper order and enter the corresponding
four-digit sequence as the answer.

1. Such a defence, however, mistakes habituation for legitimacy: a practice does not justify itself

by having lasted.
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q22. Answer: 3214.
Sentence 3 opens: it states the broad fact that reform proposals are dismissed because the institution
has functioned for a long time. Sentence 2 names the defenders’ specific reply (“it has, whatever
its flaws, endured”), specifying the move only sketched in 3. Sentence 1 introduces the author’s
countermove (“Such a defence, however, mistakes habituation for legitimacy”). Sentence 4 completes
the countermove by spelling out what endurance actually shows (only tolerance by the powerful).
The chain 3 → 2 (general claim, then defenders’ specific reply) → 1 (author’s rebuttal) → 4 (rebuttal
completed) is the only order in which both the defenders’ position and the author’s response are
properly staged.

a coherent paragraph. Determine the proper order and enter the corresponding
four-digit sequence as the answer.

1. Such a defence, however, mistakes habituation for legitimacy: a practice does not justify itself

by having lasted.

question_text_markdown:
The four sentences given below, when arranged in the correct order, form
a coherent paragraph. Determine the proper order and enter the corresponding
four-digit sequence as the answer.
option_a_markdown:
The puzzle vanishes once one notices that translation has never been an exchange of words

for words but of uses for uses.
option_b_markdown:
Languages, it is sometimes said, are mutually translatable in principle but never quite in

practice.
option_c_markdown:
A term whose use is embedded in a particular form of life cannot be carried, intact, into

another form of life merely by being matched with a foreign synonym.
option_d_markdown:
This is taken to be a deep puzzle about meaning, as though some residue always escapes the

crossing between tongues.

Format: TITA. Enter the four-digit sequence (e.g., 2413).
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q23. Answer: 2431.
Sentence 2 opens by stating the received view: languages are translatable in principle but not in
practice. Sentence 4 names this as the standard puzzle (“This is taken to be a deep puzzle about
meaning”). Sentence 3 elaborates the source of the puzzle, that a term embedded in a form of
life resists synonym-matching. Sentence 1 dissolves the puzzle (“The puzzle vanishes once one
notices...”) by redescribing translation as an exchange of uses for uses.
The conceptual flow is: state the view (2) → label it a puzzle (4) → explain the source of the
puzzle (3) → dissolve it with the author’s reframing (1). Placing 1 anywhere earlier would dissolve
a puzzle that has not yet been set up.

question_text_markdown:
The paragraph below has four possible insertion points marked [A], [B], [C], and
[D]. Read the paragraph and decide where the following sentence best fits.

Sentence to insert: “Yet this very confidence is what the practice was designed to produce, not
what it presupposes.”

Paragraph: Standardized testing is often defended on the ground that its results are objective in
a way that teacher assessments are not.
[A] A score on a national examination, the argument
goes, means the same thing in every district, and its meaning does not depend on the affections or
prejudices of any particular examiner. [B] Employers, universities and parents, accordingly, treat
such scores as reliable signals of ability, comparable across regions and over time. [C] Long before
any pupil sits the paper, the test has been calibrated, scaled and equated through procedures whose
entire purpose is to produce comparable numbers from materially different performances. [D] To
treat the resulting comparability as evidence of underlying objectivity is to mistake an output of
the machinery for a property of the world.

A

B

C

D

Format: MCQ
detailed_solution_markdown:
Q24. Answer: (D). The inserted sentence speaks of “this very confidence” as something produced
 Verbal Ability & Reading Comprehension

by the practice, not presupposed by it. The referent of “this confidence” is the treatment of test
scores as reliable signals (described in sentences before [C]) and, more specifically, the comparability
produced by calibration and equating (described between [C] and [D]). Only at [D] has the machinery
of producing comparability been introduced, so only at [D] can the author say that the confidence
is an output of that machinery rather than a property of the world.
Placement at [A] is too early — the practice of treating scores as objective has only just been
stated; there is nothing yet for “the practice was designed to produce” to refer to.
Placement at [B] is tempting because it follows the strongest statement of confidence in scores, and
a reader scanning for “confidence” may stop here. But at [B] the technical apparatus (calibration,
equating) has not been introduced, so the claim that the confidence is produced by that apparatus
has no anchor.
Placement at [C] is also tempting because it precedes the description of the apparatus, but the
inserted sentence is a verdict on that apparatus; it has to come after the apparatus has been
described, not before.
